[Slide 3] But, like, what IS philosophy, man? Let me start today by trying to tackling a very common (and actually quite good) question. I don’t think that the average person has any idea what it is I do as a philosopher and MAYBE not even what philosophy is, as an academic discipline. So let me try to give a crude answer to that question.

[Slide 4] Some questions are neither important nor interesting Let me start answering that question by making a few observations. (These are very obvious observations, but sometimes you can get to pretty interesting places by combining the right obvious observations.)

[Slide 15] So. All of that stuff is not-philosophy. The uninteresting, the easy, and the scientifically tractable.

[Slide 16] Here’s one pass at explaining what philosophers do... Philosophers try to answer the really difficult and really important questions that science doesn’t seem equipped to answer . It’s worth noting, if you ask 10 philosophers to explain what philosophy is and what philosophers do, you’re likely to get 10 different answers. So, feel free to take this answer with an appropriate amount of reservation.

[Slide 15] So. All of that stuff is not-philosophy. The uninteresting, the easy, and the scientifically tractable.

[Slide 16] Here’s one pass at explaining what philosophers do... Philosophers try to answer the really difficult and really important questions that science doesn’t seem equipped to answer . It’s worth noting, if you ask 10 philosophers to explain what philosophy is and what philosophers do, you’re likely to get 10 different answers. So, feel free to take this answer with an appropriate amount of reservation.

[Slide 17] The history of philosophy also supports this way of thinking about the discipline – especially the contrast with science. If you go back and look at ancient Greek philosophy, you’ll quickly notice that a lot of it is actually proto-physics. People like aristotle and Epicurus (who was one of the first to believe in atoms) were all trying to do physics by just, like, thinking really hard. Then, eventually, we realized that you can learn about physics better by doing science about it, by conducting experiments. Thus, physics splintered off from philosophy and became it’s own scientific discipline. (For the most part…) Philosophers no longer do physics from the armchair, and physicists are no longer doing philosophy.

[Slide 18] We see this same pattern in the history of Psychology. Until around the turn of the 20th century, most of the best psychologists were just philosophers: they were trying to figure out how the human mind works by just thinking about it really hard. But, again, we eventually realized that psychology can be studied scientifically – with experimentation and carefully manipulated real-world observation. Thus, psychology splintered off from philosophy. It became it’s own discipline. (For the most part…) philosophers are no longer doing psychology, and psychologists are no longer considered philosophers.

Thus, physics splintered off from philosophy and became it’s own scientific discipline. [Slide 18] We see this same pattern in the history of Psychology. Until around the turn of the 20th century, most of the best psychologists were just philosophers: they were trying to figure out how the human mind works by just thinking about it really hard. But, again, we eventually realized that psychology can be studied scientifically – with experimentation and carefully manipulated real-world observation. Thus, psychology splintered off from philosophy. It became it’s own discipline. (For the most part…) philosophers are no longer doing psychology, and psychologists are no longer considered philosophers.

[Slide 19] Here’s one pass at explaining what philosophers do... Philosophers try to answer the really difficult and really important questions that science doesn’t seem equipped to answer . So, again, this is a helpful way of understanding the field. One benefit of this way of describing philosophy is that it makes clear that the boundary around what counts as philosophy may change as science progresses and become better-able to answer various sorts of questions.

[Slide 1] Intro to Arguments Arguments are the bread-and-butter of philosophy. Almost everything we do in this class will be focused on particular arguments – arguments about all sorts of things. Since our goal will be to understand and grapple with these arguments, it’s worth spending some time at the outset of the course familiarizing ourselves with what arguments are and what makes them good or bad.

[Slide 2] Arguments & their parts On propositions Definition: argument Metrics of Goodness: Validity & Soundness

[Slide 3] An Argument, Dissected

[Slide 4] If the Earth were flat, we could visit the under-Earth We can’t visit the under-Earth Therefore, the Earth is not flat

[Slide 3] An Argument, Dissected

[Slide 4] If the Earth were flat, we could visit the under-Earth We can’t visit the under-Earth Therefore, the Earth is not flat

[Slide 5] If the Earth were flat, we could visit the under-Earth We can’t visit the under-Earth The Earth is not flat Conclusion The conclusion is the claim that the argument is an argument for. An argument’s reason-for-existing is getting you to accept its conclusion .

[Slide 6] If the Earth were flat, we could visit the under-Earth We can’t visit the under-Earth The Earth is not flat Premises A premise is a claim introduced to get you to accept the conclusion . The premises are evidence for the conclusion . Premises give you reasons for thinking the conclusion is true.

[Slide 5] If the Earth were flat, we could visit the under-Earth We can’t visit the under-Earth The Earth is not flat Conclusion The conclusion is the claim that the argument is an argument for. An argument’s reason-for-existing is getting you to accept its conclusion .

[Slide 6] If the Earth were flat, we could visit the under-Earth We can’t visit the under-Earth The Earth is not flat Premises A premise is a claim introduced to get you to accept the conclusion . The premises are evidence for the conclusion . Premises give you reasons for thinking the conclusion is true.

[Slide 7] Every argument is essentially saying, “ Look at these premises . We agree that they’re true. And look: if the premises are true, then the conclusion should be true, too. Thus, the fact that you accept the premises means you should also accept the conclusion . ”

[Slide 8] Observation Philosophers call such statements, ‘ propositions ’ The premises and conclusion of every argument must have truth values. If the truth of the premises is intended to provide evidence for the truth of the conclusion, then the premises and conclusion both better *be the sorts of things that can be true*

[Slide 7] Every argument is essentially saying, “ Look at these premises . We agree that they’re true. And look: if the premises are true, then the conclusion should be true, too. Thus, the fact that you accept the premises means you should also accept the conclusion . ”

[Slide 8] Observation Philosophers call such statements, ‘ propositions ’ The premises and conclusion of every argument must have truth values. If the truth of the premises is intended to provide evidence for the truth of the conclusion, then the premises and conclusion both better *be the sorts of things that can be true*

[Slide 9] On propositions

[Slide 10] Proposition =df a statement that bears a truth-value – either true or false You can think of propositions as sentences that claim something about the world – about the way it is, was, will be, should be, or might be. A proposition is TRUE just in case the world actually is (was/will be) that way. A proposition is FALSE just in case the world is NOT that way. By “the world” I just mean, like, REALITY. The universe and everything that actually exists in it. You’ll notice, there’s nothing particular spooky or mystical or special about Truth on this definition. That’s a good thing! To say that something is “true” is just to say that there is a correspondence between what we say about the world and the way the world actually is.

To say that something is “true” is just to say that there is a correspondence between what we say about the world and the way the world actually is.

[Slide 19] What makes an argument good ?

[Slide 21] Argument =df a collection of propositions, some of them (the premises ) being offered as evidence for the truth of another one of them (the conclusion ) In essence, a good argument is one that does a good job making its conclusion seem true. That’s what an argument is. But in this course, one of our primary tasks will be assessing arguments. So we should ask: What makes an argument a good one?

[Slide 19] What makes an argument good ?

[Slide 21] Argument =df a collection of propositions, some of them (the premises ) being offered as evidence for the truth of another one of them (the conclusion ) In essence, a good argument is one that does a good job making its conclusion seem true. That’s what an argument is. But in this course, one of our primary tasks will be assessing arguments. So we should ask: What makes an argument a good one?

[Slide 22] The two most common metrics of goodness are validity and soundness An argument is valid iff the truth of the premises is sufficient for (or guarantees ) the truth of the conclusion. An argument is sound iff it is valid and has true premises. These are genuinely tough concepts. But they’re also pretty important. Put differently, a valid argument is one’s whose premises were they true, would guarantee the truth of the conclusion. N.B.: That means, a valid argument can have false premises! Validity isn’t about truth or falsity per se. It’s about the relationship between the truth of the premises and the truth of the conconclusion. This can be a bit challenging to wrap one’s head around, so we’ll look at a bunch of examples. Soundness is easier than validity. Soundness is just validity + true premises. (thus, all invalid argument are unsound) Soundness is, hopefully you can see, basically the metric of success. If an argument is sound, then you should accept its conclusion. (If you agree that an argument is sound, you agree that all its premises are true and that the premises guarantee the truth of the conclusion. Thus, you must accept the conclusion.)

[Slide 23] P1) All humans die eventually P2) Madonna is a human C) Madonna will die eventually [Work through this verbally myself] Valid? (We ask: is there any way for the premises to be true and the conclusion false?) Sound? (We ask: Is it valid and are the premises all true?)

[Slide 24] P1) All humans die eventually P2) Seabiscuit the Racehorse is a human C) Seabiscuit will die eventually Valid? (We ask: is there any way for the premises to be true and the conclusion false?) Sound? (Notice: The fact that P2 is false doesn’t affect the validity!)

[Slide 26] An Elaborate Analogy Imagine you live in a town surrounded on all sides by manure lagoons (i.e. big pits where livestock feces is stored.) Your town needs fresh, clean water, but all the available water sources are on the far side of the manure lagoons. So pipes must be constructed to carry water from a distant spring, through the pits, to the town. Obviously, you won’t be willing to drink the water unless you’re sure that the pipes are perfectly tight, perfectly shit-excluding, and perfectly good at preserving the cleanliness of the water. That’s not enough, though! For the town’s water to be drinkable, you also need assurances that the original spring water actually is clean. (After all, if you put nasty water in the World’s Best Pipes, you’ll get nasty water out the other end.) In short, good, drinkable water requires both good pipes and good source water.

[Slide 27] Validity is like good pipes . Good pipes are cleanliness-preserving :: A valid argument is truth-preserving Shit can’t slip into good pipes :: Falsity can’t slip into a valid argument Good pipes aren’t enough to guarantee drinkable water :: Validity isn’t enough to guarantee a true conclusion

But it doesn’t affect the argument’s validity.)

[Slide 33] Why does this matter?

[Slide 34] Philosophers almost exclusively work with valid arguments . This makes philosophy simple. To decide if an argument is sound , we simply ask, Are all of the premises true? We will do the same. In this course, I’ll by-and-large present valid arguments for our consideration, and our question will be just that, Are all of the premises true?

[Slide 1] The Ontological Argument

[Slide 2] Unit Overview Ingredients for the Ontological Argument Two distinctions : Possible vs. impossible beings, actual vs. possible beings Definition: God Definition: Great-making properties The Ontological Argument

[Slide 3] Unit 1 – Metaphysics: God, Evil, and the Afterlife Week 1: The Ontological Argument Week 2: The Fine-tuning Argument Week 3: The Problem of Evil Week 4: Personal Identity and the Afterlife

[Slide 4] The Ontological Argument was first suggested by Saint Anselm of Canterbury (1033 - 1109 C.E.) He argues that God’s very properties entail that She must exist – i.e. it’s incoherent to imagine God as non-existent.

[Slide 3] Unit 1 – Metaphysics: God, Evil, and the Afterlife Week 1: The Ontological Argument Week 2: The Fine-tuning Argument Week 3: The Problem of Evil Week 4: Personal Identity and the Afterlife

[Slide 4] The Ontological Argument was first suggested by Saint Anselm of Canterbury (1033 - 1109 C.E.) He argues that God’s very properties entail that She must exist – i.e. it’s incoherent to imagine God as non-existent.

[Slide 5] Two Distinctions

[Slide 14] Defining God

[Slide 15] Does God exist? So keep in mind: we’re searching of an answer to this question. There are a couple ways we could go about our search. 1) We could start by looking around at stuff and try to see if anything looks promising as a God-candidates. (“Guys, I’ve found something but I’m not sure it’s God.”) 2) We could start by specifying in detail what God must be like and ONLY THEN going out to look at things, to see if there’s anything like that. I submit to you: Method 1 is not very efficient. Suppose for instance that I have it in mind vaguely that God is awesome.

[Slide 16] God? So I go out into the world and start looking for awesome things. I notice: Tiger Woods is awesome… is he god?

[Slide 15] Does God exist? So keep in mind: we’re searching of an answer to this question. There are a couple ways we could go about our search. 1) We could start by looking around at stuff and try to see if anything looks promising as a God-candidates. (“Guys, I’ve found something but I’m not sure it’s God.”) 2) We could start by specifying in detail what God must be like and ONLY THEN going out to look at things, to see if there’s anything like that. I submit to you: Method 1 is not very efficient. Suppose for instance that I have it in mind vaguely that God is awesome.

[Slide 16] God? So I go out into the world and start looking for awesome things. I notice: Tiger Woods is awesome… is he god?

[Slide 17] God? ALSO Ice cream cake is awesome. Is ice cream cake God?

[Slide 18] God? ALSO this Chinese water deed is kinda awesome… Is it God? Notice: We CAN in each case give good reasons why each thing isn’t God. But what do those reasons amount to? It seems like we’re going to argue that each of these being isn’t God because it lacks certain properties that we think God should have. Chinese water deer are too stupid to be God. They aren’t sufficiently “God-like.” But what does that mean “God-like”? What is God like? Intelligent, apparently – at least, smarter than deer. What else, though? Maybe we should talk about that first before we go wandering around in the world point at cake.

[Slide 19] Let’s first agree on a description of God , then we can ask, “ Is there anything like that? ” Here’s a proposal.

[Slide 20] The Greatest Being God God is the greatest possible being – the being than which none greater can be conceived . Here’s the definition that we’ll be working with today. 3 points need to made here. By “great”, we mean “better” or “more admirable” or “more perfect” – God is the perfectest being. Also bear in mind that what’s relevant here is possibility, rather than actuality. So the class of things than which God is greater is all the possible things; all the things not in category 3, in other words. I cannot stress this enough: This is NOT THE ONLY POSSIBLE WAY OF UNDERSTANDING GOD. I am not claiming that this is THE definition of God. I am not claiming this is the best definition of God. Rather, this is how we’ll define God for the purposes of the Ontological Argument . This is what St. Anselm has in mind when he says ‘God’. When Anselm says ‘God exists’, he means ‘the greatest possible being exists.’ A s we’ll see in future lectrues, there are other good ways of defining God that are genuinely distinct from this definition. Other people might say ‘God exists’ and mean something like, ‘there was a powerful being who created our universe – that being exists.’

[Slide 23] Great-making Properties

[Slide 24] Let’s define a great-making property as follows, a great-making property is such that when we consider adding this property to a being which lacks it, we invariably make that being greater. By “great” here, we mean something very generic like “more admirable” or “more valuable” or “improved” Thus, we could also describe a great-making property is a property that is always an improvement, always an upgrade when you add that property to beings which lack it. I know that’s a bit telescopic, so we’ll look at some examples.

[Slide 23] Great-making Properties

[Slide 24] Let’s define a great-making property as follows, a great-making property is such that when we consider adding this property to a being which lacks it, we invariably make that being greater. By “great” here, we mean something very generic like “more admirable” or “more valuable” or “improved” Thus, we could also describe a great-making property is a property that is always an improvement, always an upgrade when you add that property to beings which lack it. I know that’s a bit telescopic, so we’ll look at some examples.

[Slide 25] Is greenness a great-making property ? Greenness is a property. Some objects are green; they ahve this property. Other objects are not green; they lack this property.

[Slide 26] Is greenness a great-making property ? Imagine a chunk of gray basalt. Would it be greater if it were green? Seemingly not.

[Slide 31] Is kindness a great-making property ? Of course, we don’t ahve to restrict ourselves to actual things. Great-making properties are suppose to improve all beings which lack them – not just the real ones. So think about Severus Snape. He’s a douche. Isn’t he greater when we imagine adding the property of kindness to him?

[Slide 32] Is kindness a great-making property ? You might be wondering, “What about ketchup again? Surely kind ketchup is no better than regular ketchup? And if that’s right, doesn’t that mean kindness isn’t great-making? Because it doesn’t always make things greater?” That’s an interesting case. Here’s what I think we should say. When we imagine adding the property of kindness to ketchup… we fail. The thing that we imagine is just plain old ketchup. It doesn’t do anything on it’s own, so it doesn’t do any kind or mean things. Since we aren’t actually imagining kind ketchup, it’s not the case that we’re imagining kind ketchup as no greater than plain ketchup. (COMPARE: Imagine there was a walkin, talking bottle of ketchup who’s kind of of douche. Now it seems like we CAN imagine a version of that ketchup who’s kind. But, then, doesn’t it also seem like the kind ketchup-man is better than the unkind one?)

[Slide 47] P1) God is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) God is not logically impossible C1) God is not a Category 3 entity (b/c we can imagine God!) P3) By definition, Category 2 beings don’t have the property of existence P4) Existence is a great-making property C2) Thus, all Category 2 beings lack at least one great-making property P5) By definition, God is the greatest possible being C3) God is not a Category 2 entity (else he would lack a great-making property ) C4) So God must be a Category 1 entity (i.e. God actually exists. ) The Ontological Argument What happens next is exactly what you expect: Anselm argues that God is not in Category 2 either. On P3) to be in Cat 2 is to be a possible thing that could exist but doesn’t actually exist. Centaurs are Cat 2. They could exist but don’t actually. That’s true by definition of all Cat 2 beings. On P5): You can think of this as the claim the God is unimprovable : he’s the greatest possible being so he must already be at peak performance, perfect in every way, completely and utterly unimprovable.

[Slide 5] The Greatest Being God God is the greatest possible being – the being than which none greater can be conceived . Here’s the definition that we’ll be working with in the Ontological Argument. 2 points need to made here. By “great”, we mean “better” or “more admirable” or “more perfect” – God is the perfectest being. Also bear in mind that what’s relevant here is possibility, rather than actuality. So the class of things than which God is greater is all the possible things; all the things not in category 3, in other words.

[Slide 6] Let’s define a great-making property as follows, a great-making property is such that when we consider adding this property to a being which lacks it, we invariably make that being greater.

The Ontological Argument Of those 6 options, only 3 of them seem actively viable. P1 seems like a logical truth. (Assuming we’ve done our possible being metaphysics correctly) P3 & P5 are both definitions. It typically doesn’t make sense to say that a definition is false, unless the definition is reporting on the world – unless the definition has an empirical truth value. The def in P3 is a logical entailment. An analytic truth. Nothing to argue with there. What about P5? In general I think, here and in other cases, it just doesn’t make a ton of sense to argue with a definition.

[Slide 33] Kant thinks this is incoherent. Existence cannot be conceptually added or subtracted from an entity. Existence is rather a precondition for an entity’s having properties .

[Slide 34] Suppose existence is a property . If so, non-existence must be one too . That means we can say things like, “Sherlock has the property of non-existence .” But is that coherent? Sherlock doesn’t exist! How can he have anything at all? Try this. That’s Kant’s objection. I’m not sure this one succeeds, but it’s been very influential historically.

[Slide 35] The Sneaky Definition Obj

[Slide 36] Consider the leprechaun. A clear Category 2 entity.

[Slide 35] The Sneaky Definition Obj

[Slide 36] Consider the leprechaun. A clear Category 2 entity.

[Slide 37] Let’s define a new kind of leprechaun. A real-leprechaun , we’ll say, is a leprechaun that isn’t just merely possible but that actually exists. (an R.L. for short)

[Slide 38] P1) An R.L. is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) An R.L. is not logically impossible (we can imagine an R.L.!) C1) An R.L. is not a Category 3 entity P3) By definition, Category 2 beings don’t have the property of existence P4) By definition, an R.L. is a leprechaun that actually exists. C2) An R.L. is not a Category 2 entity (b/c no Category 2 entity actually exists) C4) So an R.L. must be a Category 1 entity – i.e. real-leprechauns exist . The Ontological Argument – Leprechaun Edition So…. obviously that’s bullshit. Something’s gone wrong. This argument is defective. But WHY? What’s the defect? Where did we screw up?

[Slide 39] The mistake seems to be this. We also know they aren’t in Category 1 . (SPOILER: Leprechauns don’t exist) Thus, they’re impossible. R.L.s are in Category 3 . By definition, real-leprechauns don’t belong in Category 2 . It helps to try to assemble our facts about Real Leprechauns in a different order. Fact #1: Real Leprechauns are by definition not in Category 2 Fact #2: There are no leprechauns in reality. So they aren’t in Category 1 Conclusion: Real Leprechauns are in Category 3: the are impossible beings.

[Slide 43] So too with the Ontological Arg. By Anselm’s def, God isn’t merely possible . (at least, if we accept that existence is great-making .) That is, by definition God is either Category 1 or Category 3 . (either real or impossible) But then, we should not accept P2 . It looks innocent, but it’s not. (b/c Anselm’s definition is sneaky .) Anselm is saying, “Let’s define God as real or impossible. Since we can imagine God, God is not impossible. So, She’s real.”

[Slide 44] P1) God is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) God is not logically impossible (is not Category 3 ) P3) By definition, God is not Category 2 C) So, God is in Category 1 , is real The Ontological Argument (un-sneaky’d) Not a good argument! No one should accept P2, given the definition in P3. What the R.L. case teaches us is that just because we can imagine something doesn’t mean that thing is possible. We seem to be able to imagine Real Leprechauns, even though they are impossible. (b/c they don’t actually exist and by definition aren’t merely possible.) Likewise, just because we seem to be able to imagine God as the greatest possible being, that doesn’t mean God – as Anselm defines Her – isn’t impossible.

[Slide 1] The Fine-tuning Argument

[Slide 2] Ingredients for the Fine-tuning Argument An analogy The evidence for fine-tuning An easy principle about evidence The Fine-tuning Argument

[Slide 3] The Ontological Argument : Consider the properties that define God. Surely we must conclude that a non-existent God is impossible. Thus, God exists. The Fine-tuning Argument : Look at the universe. See how elegant and fragile it is? It seems very, very unlikely that such a thing could exist by accident. The best explanation is that the universe was carefully designed by God.

[Slide 4] You’re walking along the beach when you stumble across this really exquisite bit of driftwood. It’s a beautifully gnarled thing, all sharp curves and jagged spirals. Its surface is mottled in streaks of browns and blacks and greys, worn down to a smooth sheen. You think, WOW - What are the chances that this was created by natural processes? Well, pretty high, you realize. It IS a beautiful piece of wood, but nothing about it strikes you as obviously unlikely.

[Slide 3] The Ontological Argument : Consider the properties that define God. Surely we must conclude that a non-existent God is impossible. Thus, God exists. The Fine-tuning Argument : Look at the universe. See how elegant and fragile it is? It seems very, very unlikely that such a thing could exist by accident. The best explanation is that the universe was carefully designed by God.

[Slide 4] You’re walking along the beach when you stumble across this really exquisite bit of driftwood. It’s a beautifully gnarled thing, all sharp curves and jagged spirals. Its surface is mottled in streaks of browns and blacks and greys, worn down to a smooth sheen. You think, WOW - What are the chances that this was created by natural processes? Well, pretty high, you realize. It IS a beautiful piece of wood, but nothing about it strikes you as obviously unlikely.

[Slide 5] You continue walking. Further along the beach you spy a small pocket watch glinting in the sun. You pick it up. It has a long chain comprising hundreds of tiny gold links and, inside, you can see a number of finely meshed gears, all turning in perfect rhythm. On its outer cover has been etched with a picture of wild vines. You wonder again, What are the chances that this was created by natural processes? Very very low, you judge. It’s not literally impossible, of course. Yet thousands of things would need to go just perfectly, exactly right for this watch to look and function as it does. It’s just overwhelmingly more likely that this watch is the product of a watchmaker and not natural chance.

[Slide 6] Our best physics suggests, this is what the universe is like. If we randomly selected values for the universe’s laws/constants, the chance of the universe supporting life would be very, very, tiny. Our best physics suggests that the physical universe needed to be just perfectly, exactly right for it to support life. While it’s possible that it happened by chance, it’s very very unlikely. If the physical constants had been selected at random, the chance that the universe would support life is rather like the chances of trying to hit a 1-inch dartboard with a dart while standing on the opposite side of the universe.

[Slide 19] Observation : This little object has tiny, interlocking gears H1: A person made this object H2: Natural processes made this object We ask, Which is more surprising? Tiny gears when H1 is true or tiny gears when H2 is true? Obviously, the latter. Thus, our observation is strong evidence for H1. It’s not impossible that the gears were made by nature, but damn would that be surprising.

[Slide 24] This is what the Evidence Principle is meant to capture. An observation is evidence for one hypothesis over another when there’s in difference in how surprising that observation would be under each hypothesis. The evidence principle says that the observation is evidence for whichever hypothesis makes the observation less surprising (if you like, you can think of the evidence principle as telling us to minimize the degree of mismatch between our evidence and our beliefs, or telling us to minimize surprise – we should adopt whatever beliefs are least surprising given our evidence

[Slide 29] What exactly does the Fine-tuning Argument purport to show us? Quiz QUIZ (select all that apply): God is all powerful God is at least powerful enough to create a universe God at least existed for awhile – long enough to create the universe God exists today God is morally good and cares about us God has a deep understanding of physics God is singular – i.e. there is a single being God, who made the universe.

[Slide 30] The Designer God God is the being that tailored our universe to support life What do we mean by “God” in the Fine-tuning Arg? Something like this: God is the being who fine-tuned the universe. It’s is absolutely imperative to note that this is not equivalent to the Greatest Possible Being God we talked about in conj w/ the ontological arg. The fine-tuning arg does not (and does not try to) prove that God is the greatest possible being. The fact the universe was fine-tuned does not show that God is unimprovable. It only shows that God has enough power to finely-tune a universe.

[Slide 31] What exactly does the Fine-tuning Argument purport to show us? The conclusion is rather limited: there was a fine-tuner. We don’t know much about God’s whereabouts, intentions, or powers. For all that the Fine-tuning Argument shows us, God might have up and left. Or died. Or maybe She’s still around but she hates humans and is trying to destroy us. Or maybe “God” wasn’t a single being but a committee of powerful beings who together made the universe. We don’t know! All we’ve learned is that some powerful thing at at some point fine-tuned the universe!

[Slide 3] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option Recall once more, the shape of the discussion. We have this argument, The Problem of Evil, which observes an incompatibility between the world as it actually is and the world as it should be if the 3-O God exists. Most theists want to respond to this problem by arguing that all of the suffering in the world is necessary – all of it is needed to prevent some greater evil or to secure some greater good.

[Slide 4] The Free Will Theodicy It is consistently worse to violate a human’s free will than to allow evil. Thus, the suffering that stems from human choices is necessary suffering. Last time we looked at the FreeWill Theodicy. It says, roughly, that God knows that human freedom is more valuable than stopping evil. So She lets evils occur whenever stopping evil would require violating someone’s free will. We also looked at some objections to this Theodicy and tried to keep track of all the commitments the theist must make to hold onto her view.

Last time we looked at the FreeWill Theodicy. It says, roughly, that God knows that human freedom is more valuable than stopping evil. [Slide 5] The Soul-making Theodicy Today we’ll talk about a different theodicy. This one attempts to explain the necessity of suffering not by pointing out what we’d lose through God’s intervention but by emphasizing what we gain by dealing with evil ourselves.

[Slide 6] Motivation #1: God prefers human intervention to divine intervention. I want to offer two different motivations for the soul-making theodicy. The first one picks up on a thread from our conversation last time.

[Slide 5] The Soul-making Theodicy Today we’ll talk about a different theodicy. This one attempts to explain the necessity of suffering not by pointing out what we’d lose through God’s intervention but by emphasizing what we gain by dealing with evil ourselves.

[Slide 6] Motivation #1: God prefers human intervention to divine intervention. I want to offer two different motivations for the soul-making theodicy. The first one picks up on a thread from our conversation last time.

[Slide 7] God could step in to help. But in Her moral perfection, She knows She shouldn’t. You could step in, too. Although far from morally perfect, you know that you should. (and God presumably wants you to!) How do we explain this strange asymmetry? Suppose you live next to a young couple with a baby. As best as you can tell, the couple is gone for 2-3 days at a time, leaving the baby alone at home. You hear it crying through your shared walls. You worry that it’s being severely underfed and under-nurtured. You think about calling child protective services, but you ultimately decide not to.

[Slide 8] The Free Will Theodicy cannot explain this. For both you and God, helping means violating the free choice to ignore the baby. And if freedom is really more important than infant suffering, you must not help. This is what we talked about last time. This asymmetry is a very big problem for the Free Will Theodicy. To accept the Free Will Theodicy is to accept that human freedom is, like, the most valuable thing. But if that’s right, it’s hard to understand why we’d ever be morally required to violate the freedom of others in the name of stoppping evil.

[Slide 7] God could step in to help. But in Her moral perfection, She knows She shouldn’t. You could step in, too. Although far from morally perfect, you know that you should. (and God presumably wants you to!) How do we explain this strange asymmetry? Suppose you live next to a young couple with a baby. As best as you can tell, the couple is gone for 2-3 days at a time, leaving the baby alone at home. You hear it crying through your shared walls. [Slide 8] The Free Will Theodicy cannot explain this. For both you and God, helping means violating the free choice to ignore the baby. And if freedom is really more important than infant suffering, you must not help. This is what we talked about last time. This asymmetry is a very big problem for the Free Will Theodicy. To accept the Free Will Theodicy is to accept that human freedom is, like, the most valuable thing. But if that’s right, it’s hard to understand why we’d ever be morally required to violate the freedom of others in the name of stoppping evil.

[Slide 9] Maybe God wants to and does help? She uses you as her instrument of assistance. Maybe. But that would imply that God Herself is ready to step in if you refuse. That’s visibly not how these things go IRL. (If no human helps, the baby goes unhelped.) Here’s a different possible suggestion. The problem here is the implication that God is intent on helping. She is definitely going to make sure that the baby is helped. Which,simply, isnt’ true. That is quite clearly not the world we live in.

[Slide 10] Maybe it’s not really about the baby. Maybe it’s about you. Maybe God wants you to grow as a person . (And, frankly, you really need the practice.) So, yes, God could help the baby. But then what would you learn from that? But that suggests yet another possible story. We might think: God doesn’t want to be our safety net. We need to think of our world as our responsibility. If there’s some evil to be stopped, we need to see ourselves as the only ones to stop it. There’re no training wheels in this world. If’ we’re to grow and mature, we have to struggle on our own.

[Slide 11] Soul-making Theodicy Suffering and struggle are necessary in that we need to endure them to secure the greater good of our own maturation, development, & communion with God . So this brings us to the Soul-making Theodicy... Here’s one way to motivate it We say: “All of the evils in the world are actually crises/opportunities. God is inviting us to step in and stop evil for ourselves. She is inviting us to become like her, to work together to make our world and ourselves better.” After all, the 3-O God is already morally perfect. But for you and I to become more like God, we need to practice. It’s like helping your child work on a math problem. It’s simply not very helpful if you do the problems for him. You could do them – you’re pretty good at math, look at you! – but you don’t need the practice. Your kid is the one who needs to practice; he needs to be the one who struggles.

[Slide 12] Motivation #2: Our best science suggests God evolved humans. So that’s the first motivation for the S-m theodicy,. Let me offer another.

[Slide 11] Soul-making Theodicy Suffering and struggle are necessary in that we need to endure them to secure the greater good of our own maturation, development, & communion with God . So this brings us to the Soul-making Theodicy... Here’s one way to motivate it We say: “All of the evils in the world are actually crises/opportunities. God is inviting us to step in and stop evil for ourselves. She is inviting us to become like her, to work together to make our world and ourselves better.” After all, the 3-O God is already morally perfect. But for you and I to become more like God, we need to practice. It’s like helping your child work on a math problem. It’s simply not very helpful if you do the problems for him. You could do them – you’re pretty good at math, look at you! – but you don’t need the practice. Your kid is the one who needs to practice; he needs to be the one who struggles.

[Slide 12] Motivation #2: Our best science suggests God evolved humans. So that’s the first motivation for the S-m theodicy,. Let me offer another.

[Slide 13] All suffering is the result of Original Sin Recall this (bad) theodicy from a few lectures ago. I didn’t mention this at the time, but one very good reason for rejecting this theodicy is that the story it relies on, when taken quite literally, appears to conflict with our best science. The earth and human beings appear to be far more than 6,000 years old. The Garden of Eden story cannot really explain the necessity of suffering if it’s not true So here’s another path to the Soul Making Theodicy. Think about God as making humans through the process of evolution, as described by our best science. Turns out, when we do that, the Soul-making theodicy starts to look pretty good.

[Slide 32] Examples & Applications

[Slide 33] How does the soul-making theodicy make sense of the evils of war? Well, war is a bad thing that humans do to each other. But, like all bad things, it presents opportunities for maturation and development. Politicians have a chance to develop into wiser, more restrained leaders; soldiers and civilians into more thoughtful, cautious, compassionate people. Through suffering and struggling, we have a chance to grow.

[Slide 34] Disease, likewise, is a chance for growth. Disease and poor health are hard. They suck. But through that struggle we can learn important things about ourselves. About courage and determination. About humility and compassion.

It can teach humility and kindness and courage.

[Slide 36] Objections and Implications

[Slide 36] Objections and Implications

[Slide 37] Objection 1 : Many religions claim that people can be damned – i.e. sentenced to eternal posthumous punishment . Why would the 3-O God do that to her friends? Well… She wouldn’t. I think that’s what we have to say. Hell makes exactly zero sense, if you buy the soul-making theodicy. Two problems: (1) The soul-making God would never make a person, then spend a lifetime trying to improve them, and then throw up her hands when the going gets hard and the person resists getting better. She wouldn’t simply quit on someone in the midst of soul-making. (Nor, given her Omniscience and Omnipotence, is it possible for there to even BE a human who She can’t perfect with enough time and effort. God would know in advance exactly what to do to guide this person toward moral perfection and would have the power to make that happen.) (2) The soul-making God would never make a person, try to improve them, and then torture them forever for failing. That’s nonsense. God would know in advance that the person would fail. She created that person after all – and could have made them a little different if she was worried about failure. In her perfect moral character, it’s hard to imagine Her setting someone up for failure like that.

[Slide 38] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. As we did last time, I want to make a list of all the commitments we’re forced to make in defending the Soul-making THeodicy. This is the first one. I personally don’t think this one is that big of a deal, but your mileage may vary. Some people may feel VERY CONFIDENT that there is a hell, and that individuals who go there are tortured forever. The soul-making theodicy is probably not for those people. I actually think, at that point, that the 3-O God is not for those people. If one believes that some humans are tortured infinitely by God for their finite Earthly crimes, then one believes in an afterlife that is plainly and fundamentally unjust. Thus, God is not omnibenevolent. =(

[Slide 39] Objection 2 : Some people mature more than others. What does the 3-O God do with those who die without developing any significant virtue or spiritual maturity? Suppose the world ends tomorrow in a giant extinction event – a big meteor or something. Does God say, “Well, time’s up. Fuck it. Everyone gets an A”? Presumably not. The whole point is to perfect each human. If a person fails to be perfected during her time on earth, then God must continue her perfecting efforts after the person’s death. If you don’t get it done in time, you’re going to have to stay after class.

[Slide 40] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. For many people, soul-making must continue after they die . ANother implication. Again, I don’t think this one is all that tough to swallow. But it’s still worth noting.

[Slide 41] Objection 3 : What about people who die in childhood? Do they finish soul-making in heaven? If that’s an option, then why do some of us have to soul-make on the objectively shittier Earth? Before the advent of modern medicine, about ⅓ people died before the age of 5. Obviously, no 5 year old is a morally perfected being. Thus, we need some sort of story about how those individuals finish soul-making. We have two choices: They finish in heaven They finish elsewhere It’s hard to believe that they finish in heaven. If ⅓ of people die young and get to soul-make in heaven, then the world starts to look patently unfair. ( i want to soul-make in heaven, too! Why didn’t I get to die in childhood?!) It’s hard to imagine the 3-O having such an unfair plan. Which means, those who die in childhood must soul-make elsewhere. There’s only one place we know of that God has carefully designed for soul-making: the earth. Thus, those who die early prolly continue soul-making on earth… meaning, what exactly? One obvious option is reincarnation. Those who die early are sent-back into new bodies and allowed to continue soul-making.

[Slide 42] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. For many people, soul-making must continue after they die . Children who die prematurely continue soul-making on Earth . (reincarnation??) A third implication. Now things are starting to look a little weird. But, still, the Soul-making Theodicy still hasn’t committed itself to anything, like, patently silly or stupid. These first three commitments are mostly just surprising, I think.

[Slide 43] Objection 4 : Doesn’t the cost of soul-making seem… pretty high? Like, my moral development is important, sure, but couldn’t it proceed in a world with just a little less suffering? Do we really need all of these genocides, hurricanes, and diseases?

[Slide 44] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. For many people, soul-making must continue after they die . Children who die prematurely continue soul-making on Earth . (reincarnation??) Every bad thing is absolutely, 100% necessary for soul-making. This commitment is the most troubling so far. It’s not easy in some cases to see how a particular instance of evil could plausibly contribute to soul-making. But it really does seem like the SMT is committed to this. (alternatively, we could combine the SMT with another theodicy to help take up the slack. Unfortunately, the only other promising one we’ve looked at (the Free Will Theodicy) struggles in exactly the same cases)

[Slide 45] Objection 5 : The average life today is far less painful than the average life ~300 years ago. If suffering is the path to improvement, are we now soul-making slower? Quiz Take a second and think about why this might be troubling. (There are a few good answers here.)

[Slide 46] Objection 5 : The average life today is far less painful than the average life ~300 years ago. If suffering is the path to improvement, are we now soul-making slower? Do I do a bad thing by giving my child Advil? Is it better to just let her suffer/grow? Does the improvement of the species not slow the improvement of individuals? Like, if we as a species stop fighting wars, doesn’t that mean that future people will be deprived of all the great soul-making benefits of war? Here’re two of the more troubling implications of this observation. The soul-making theodicy seems to treat all suffering as an important growth opportunity. If a morally-perfect God sees suffering that way, then a morally-perfect human should too. But that means that a genuinely morally good person may sometimes correctly refuse to alleviate the suffering of others . She will say, “Yes your tooth aches, and I could give you a root canal right now, but you’ll grow more if we wait a week, so let’s do that. I don’t want to prolong the amount of time you spend on Earth after all!” Likewise, the improvement of the species seems at odds with the improvement of the individual. Individuals need to suffer to get better while societies need to prevent suffering to get better.

[Slide 48] Objection 6 : Sociopathy? Objection 7 : Animal suffering? What do we say about Sociopaths? Almost by definition, it seems, they are unable to soul-make because they are insensitive to or unmotivated by moral reasons. Maybe those people just require lots of reincarnations? But how it that fair? It seems shitty for God to make certain people lack moral compasses and thereby force them to spend even MORE time on the shitty earth. (2) Can we really justify millions of years of animal suffering by saying “Yes, but God really wanted friends”? Think again about Rowe’s fawn, who dies over the course of 3 days all alone trapped under a tree. There aren’t any humans around, so there aren’t any souls to be made better by this suffering. Or, alternatively, maybe we now need to attribute perfectable souls to animals? “God lets the deer die of exposure trapped under the burning tree b/c the deer needs to be morally perfected” That’s a weird thing to say. But that honestly strikes me as more plausible than simply “God kills these animals for US! ”

This concludes our discussion of the problem of evil. As always in this course, there’s a lot that’s been left unsaid. There’s more to say about our two main theodicies – and other theodicies to be considered. But we’ll turn next time to a somewhat different topic in this same area of philosophy.

[Slide 5] But it doesn’t survive being knocked down. The demolition alters the smokestack in ways that are too radical and irreversible for the stack to survive.

[Slide 6] A book survives the process of being opened

[Slide 7] But it doesn’t survive the process of being burned. The fire changes some of the essential parts of the book so much that the book ceases to be.

[Slide 8] Your friend Jerry survives shoving his whole body through a pumpkin. That’s still jerry in that pumpkin. Jerry isn’t dead and gone just because he had an outfit change. But he doesn’t survive a nuclear blast. The changes that nuclear blasts write on our bodies are too severe, too irreversible, too central to our being for us to survive them

But he doesn’t survive a nuclear blast. The changes that nuclear blasts write on our bodies are too severe, too irreversible, too central to our being for us to survive them

[Slide 9] “You could never step into the same river twice” – Heraclitus All of this so far is, I take it, common sense. But that’s not to say it’s uncontroversial. The Presocratic philosopher Heraclitus famously thought that NOTHING ever survived a change – a view he famously encapsulated with this quote. (Here the thought is: by the time you go to step into the river a second time, everythings different: the banks have shifted a little, different waters swirl around your feet. And since everything’s different, it’s not really the same river. To change, he thought, is necessarily to be destroyed.

[Slide 10] But we mostly don’t think that. We say things like “Look at how this river formed that oxbow” and we MEAN it. *This* river did that! The river survived the oxbow formation.

[Slide 11] Each of these change-surviving things is said to persist through time . The later version of the thing is not literally the same as the earlier thing. (it’s been changed!) But it’s still the same thing .

[Slide 12] Suppose you paint a smokestack. After the paint job, it is not literally the same as it was before. It used to be grey all over, but now it’s red and white and grey. The smokestack is not literally the same but it is still, in a looser sense, the same smokestack. That’s what we mean when we say of an object that it survives a change: it’s different than it was before, but that difference doesn’t mean the object was destroyed.

[Slide 13] We can ask of any persisting thing, What must stay-the-same for this entity to persist through time ? Which parts are the essential parts? Today we ask this question of people .

[Slide 14] To illustrate: Here are 3 pics of Drew Barrymore. We all think, I take it, that these three photos are photos of one single person. Lots of things have changed in the time between when these photos were taken, but none of those changes caused Drew Barrymore to pass out of existence. So… why not? What part (or parts) of her were preserved such that she was able to persist? And what sorts of events, if they had happened to her, would have resulted in her ceasing to be?

[Slide 19] Some people think that sometimes when individuals die, they are sent to Hell - a place of eternal torment. Maybe! But again, that you is a very important part of the story. It has to BE YOU that God sends to Hell or else She’s torturing an innocent person for YOUR sins. Which is deeply inappropriate. God would surely never do such a terrible thing.

[Slide 20] Other religious traditions say that you’re reincarnated. Again, fine; maybe! But, also again, it’s important that it really be YOU in the new body. The story has to be this: there’s this thing (YOU) and at one point in time you were in one human body and now that very same thing (YOU) are in another body – like a bear body maybe. If that’s not the story if YOU don’t literally survive, then we don’t really have reincarnation. We have you dying at one point in time – permanently dying, ceasing to be – and then at some later we have a bear. Just a bear – not a YOU-bear, but a simple bear.

[Slide 25] What even is a soul? Of course, if you’re going to hang personal identity on this funny entity, THE SOUL, you definitely owe me a story about what that entity is – and what it’s like.

[Slide 26] Non-physical Things (have neither mass nor energy) The number one The definition of ‘dinosaur’ The letter ‘A’ Dante’s Inferno Propositions Physical Things (have mass and energy) A table Dinosaurs A copy of the letter ‘A’ written in chalk A paper copy of Dante’s Inferno Photons Roughly speaking, there are two kinds of entities in the world: There are the physical, material things – with mass and energy, composed of elementary particles and subject to the laws of physics. And there are the non-physical things – with neither mass nor energy, not composed of any physical stuff nor subject to the laws of physics. If souls exist, they presumably belong in one or the other of these categories.

[Slide 35] Non-physical souls either interact with matter, or they don’t. As soon as we start trying to imagine non-physical souls, we are confronted with a choice: We either grant that those souls interact with matter or we deny that they do.

[Slide 36] If there’s no interaction , then nothing in your soul ever changes anything in the physical world, and vice versa. That means (a) your soul doesn’t affect your body, and (b) you wouldn’t want it to. After all, your soul would have no information about what’s going on in your body. (no access to your emotions, sensations, impressions, etc.) Suppose we deny interaction. Clearly this isn’t a good way to go. If this is the view, then God, when She saves your soul, doesn’t actually save you. That’s because your soul doesn’t have any information about anything that ever happened in your life – it’s exactly the same as it was when you were born, unchanged by time and happenstance. It’s an empty ghost, indistinguishable from all of the other empty ghosts attached to all of the other people in the world.

[Slide 37] Alternatively, suppose souls do interact with our bodies. Since souls are non-physical, those must be non-physical interactions. From our perspective, that should look like uncaused brain events. Physical things interact physically with other physical things – and we basically understand those interactions. That’s what the entire discipline of physics is all about. We must now try to imagine a brand-new sort of interaction, a kind unstudied by physicists: a non-physical interaction between matter located in space and time on the one hand and immaterial, unlocated, non-physical soul-stuff on the other. Since our best science only has physical instruments with which to record and measure physical interactions, we must anticipate that these non-physical interactions will be, in themselves, undetectable. But although the interactions will be undetectable, the results won’t be. The results should look like uncaused events. If sould cause things to happen in the real world, then we should see their effects in the real world – even if we can’t directly measure the causation events. These effects should appear to us to be uncaused . And since souls are primarily thought to interact with our brains, we would expect, in particular, to observe uncaused brain events.

[Slide 38] Here’s what we’d expect. Rather than a continuous pattern of neural firings (THIS neuron fires, causing THAT neuron to fire, causing THIS OTHER neuron to fire, etc), we’d sometimes see neurons firing for literally no reason. No neighboring neurons going off. No proximate environmental stimuli. Just ZAP out of the blue without explanation: a neuron firing. With litearlly zero way to explain that using our best physics. That’s how it would have to look if the soul is causing brain events while being a non-physical thing. (If you’re imagining the soul as a visible part of the causal chain, you’re imagining one of those physical souls again. And BOY DO I HAVE SOME QUESTIONS ABOUT THOSE)

[Slide 39] The problem with uncaused brain events is that… there don’t seem to be any. Our best neuroscience has yet to find any events like that. So… that means we should feel pretty pessimistic about the prospects for a non-physical soul that somehow interacts with physical stuff.

[Slide 40] But put that aside. Maybe we just need to look harder. There’s a bigger problem. Let’s imagine a soul-swap case. We ask, Does the person survive the swap?

[Slide 39] The problem with uncaused brain events is that… there don’t seem to be any. Our best neuroscience has yet to find any events like that. So… that means we should feel pretty pessimistic about the prospects for a non-physical soul that somehow interacts with physical stuff.

[Slide 40] But put that aside. Maybe we just need to look harder. There’s a bigger problem. Let’s imagine a soul-swap case. We ask, Does the person survive the swap?

[Slide 41] Imagine a man named Tom. He’s not really doing anything with his life, so one day, God reaches down to pluck out Tom’s soul and replace it with the soul that used to be attached to Julius Caesar’s body. That’s it. No change is made to Tom’s physical body. Exactly all of his atoms remain in exactly the same place. The only difference is that now he has a new soul: the one that used to belong to Julius Caesar. What is the resulting person (call him Jom) like?

[Slide 42] Option #1: Jom acts like Tom. Option #2: Jom acts like Julius Caesar. Option 1: Like Tom. The resulting person (‘Jom’) acts exactly like Tom used to. He has all the same memories as Tom. Same personality, etc. No one notices a change. Option 2: Like Julius Caesar. The resulting person (‘Jom’) acts like Julius Caesar, talks like Julius Caesar, and remembers being Julius Caesar. None of Jom’s friends speak Latin but they are very surprised when their mild mannered friend seizes control of the government and names himself emperor.

[Slide 41] Imagine a man named Tom. He’s not really doing anything with his life, so one day, God reaches down to pluck out Tom’s soul and replace it with the soul that used to be attached to Julius Caesar’s body. That’s it. No change is made to Tom’s physical body. Exactly all of his atoms remain in exactly the same place. The only difference is that now he has a new soul: the one that used to belong to Julius Caesar. What is the resulting person (call him Jom) like?

[Slide 42] Option #1: Jom acts like Tom. Option #2: Jom acts like Julius Caesar. Option 1: Like Tom. The resulting person (‘Jom’) acts exactly like Tom used to. He has all the same memories as Tom. Same personality, etc. No one notices a change. Option 2: Like Julius Caesar. The resulting person (‘Jom’) acts like Julius Caesar, talks like Julius Caesar, and remembers being Julius Caesar. None of Jom’s friends speak Latin but they are very surprised when their mild mannered friend seizes control of the government and names himself emperor.

[Slide 43] Option #1: Jom acts like Tom. Option #2: Jom acts like Julius Caesar. If this happens, souls aren’t what matter for personal identity. If this happens, souls do matter – because they carry personal content . Option #1 just looks like a case where Tom survives the soul swap. That’s still Tom! Thus, in option #1 souls aren’t what matter. You can lose your soul and get a new one, but that doesn’t kill you, or change who you are. Seemingly, that’s because in Option #1, souls don’t have any personal content. There’s nothing about Tom in Tom’s soul, so nothing important is lost when his original soul is lost. In Option #2, it starts to look like Tom doesn’t survive. Putting Julius Caesar’s soul in Tom’s body means, i’m inclined to say, that the resulting person is now Julius Caesar. At least initially, that looks better for souls. Souls have some personal content, and so swapping souls means swapping persons.

[Slide 44] Does a person survive a soul-swap? Only if they don’t thereby lose their personal content. (e.g. memories, personality) But then… surely what matters is the persistence of that personal content , not the persistence of the soul. But there’s a problem. Think about why it’s plausible that Jom is Julius Caesar in option #2. What’s doing the heavy-lifting here? Why does Jom seem like Julius Caesar? Seemingly, it’s because he acts like and remembers being Julius Caesar. But then… surely what makes the person Julius Caeser is the fact that he has Julius’ memory/personality, rather than that he has Julius’ soul. On this view, then, it’s kinda confused to say that God needs to preserve your soul to bring you to heaven. The soul isn’t what matters; it’s just the vessel for the important stuff. Think of it this way: on Option 2 – when the resulting person is really just Julius Caesar reincarnated – the soul is no more and no less the cup that holds you. You aren’t essentially a cup; you’re essentially the content that the cup contains. And indeed, it seems like we could imagine God pouring you into a different cup. If She wanted, She could copy/paste your content into a new soul – or draw all your personal stuff out of your original soul and stuff it into a different one. For all we know, it could be that human souls are weak and crumbly like Dixie cups, so God is doing this soul-swap thing every night when we sleep! That would be ok though! The important bits would still be saved; you would be saved. We can tell because you wake up with the exact same memories and personality.

[Slide 45] Three Candidate Accounts of the Afterlife For you to survive your own death ... God must preserve your soul. God must preserve your memories and personality . God must preserve your body . So the first option didn’t look very promising.

[Slide 46] Memories & Personality Given what we just said about souls, you might be feeling the pull of this view. Maybe what god needs to do to bring you into the afterlife is make sure that there’s an entity in heaven, or wherever, who remembers being you, who act like you, and who shares all your other tendencies, values, and goals. Turns out, this view also has a very serious problem.

[Slide 45] Three Candidate Accounts of the Afterlife For you to survive your own death ... God must preserve your soul. God must preserve your memories and personality . God must preserve your body . So the first option didn’t look very promising.

[Slide 46] Memories & Personality Given what we just said about souls, you might be feeling the pull of this view. Maybe what god needs to do to bring you into the afterlife is make sure that there’s an entity in heaven, or wherever, who remembers being you, who act like you, and who shares all your other tendencies, values, and goals. Turns out, this view also has a very serious problem.

[Slide 47] Again, we can get at the problem with a case. Supose you step into a Star Trek-style teleporter – a machine can move you about the universe at the speed of light. Here’s how it works: you step in the machine and press the TELEPORT button. The machine quickly scans your body, noting the exact location and arrangement of every one of your sub-atomic parts, and then transmits that information to another teleportation machine (on say Mars). The first machine then disassembles your body into its component atoms while the second machine perfectly re-assembles your body on Mars (using a different batch of atoms of course. Your old atoms are still on Earth) BOOM – you’re on Mars now. So far so good. That seems basically coherent – if technologically unfeasible at present. But now imagine that the transporter machine malfunctions. The copy is still created on Mars BUT the machine on Earth fails to disassemble the original copy. So now there are two humans running around with the exact same arrangements of atoms, the exact same memories, personalities, etc.

[Slide 48] God could create a duplicate-you right now , in heaven. But that person wouldn’t be you . After all, you’re you. But that means sharing memories and personality is insufficient for identity . Here’s the punchline. Now imagine God doing that very same thing to you right now in heaven… In other words, the mere fact that two persons have all the same memories and personality doesn’t make them literally one single person. Since it’s possible for God to create cognitive duplicates of you (beings who have your memories/personality but aren’t you) , there must be more to you than just your personal content. (And it won’t work to simply say, “But God would never do that! She would wait until I’m dead to create a copy in heaven.” That might be true, but it’s beside the point. The point is that it’s not enough to simply create a duplicate of you in heaven. Since the duplicate isn’t you in the case where you’re still alive on Earth, surely it’s not you in the case where you’ve just died on earth.

[Slide 51] Objection 1 : The tower analogy

[Slide 52] Suppose your 3-year-old son builds a fancy block tower. He is very proud of it (and so are you!), and you leave it up in the living room to show to your spouse when she gets home. But while your son is out of the room, you accidentally knock the tower down. Panicking, you pull up a picture of the tower that you took on your phone and try to reconstruct it before your wife gets home. Suppose you get the blocks back in more-or-less the same order.

[Slide 51] Objection 1 : The tower analogy

[Slide 52] Suppose your 3-year-old son builds a fancy block tower. He is very proud of it (and so are you!), and you leave it up in the living room to show to your spouse when she gets home. But while your son is out of the room, you accidentally knock the tower down. Panicking, you pull up a picture of the tower that you took on your phone and try to reconstruct it before your wife gets home. Suppose you get the blocks back in more-or-less the same order.

[Slide 53] Plausibly, this isn’t your son’s tower. It’s a mere duplicate . Your son’s tower ceased to exist when it was knocked down. Quiz This case really relies on your having the right sort of intuitions about the tower. So: quiz question, Give me some intuitions: Do you think that the recreated tower is literally the very same tower as the one that was knocked down?

[Slide 54] Plausibly, this isn’t your son’s tower. It’s a mere duplicate . Your son’s tower ceased to exist when it was knocked down. Maybe this is how you and I work. God can’t recreate us in heaven. She can only fashion mere duplicates . ... But it gets worse!

[Slide 53] Plausibly, this isn’t your son’s tower. It’s a mere duplicate . Your son’s tower ceased to exist when it was knocked down. Quiz This case really relies on your having the right sort of intuitions about the tower. So: quiz question, Give me some intuitions: Do you think that the recreated tower is literally the very same tower as the one that was knocked down?

[Slide 54] Plausibly, this isn’t your son’s tower. It’s a mere duplicate . Your son’s tower ceased to exist when it was knocked down. Maybe this is how you and I work. God can’t recreate us in heaven. She can only fashion mere duplicates . ... But it gets worse!

[Slide 55] Also, what happens if your body and, say, Queen Elizabeth I’s body were made using the same blocks? Who gets to keep that atom? Or does God just use new blocks? We might note: Your physical brain will rot and decay in the ground when you die. We know this. You will disintegrate into dust and soil and ash and become inter-mixed with the other components of Earth’s crust. So if we like the brain view, we have to ask: “When exactly does God reassemble your brain in heaven and with what parts? Since she doesn’t do that right away, it’s pretty much guaranteed to be the case that some of those atoms from your brain will be inside a different person’s brain when he or she dies. But if you were both using that atom when you died, who gets it in heaven? This will be true of millions and millions of your atoms. At least in the tower case, you really did use the same blocks to remake the tower. It doesn’t look like God is going to be able to do that. She’s going to have to remake your brain with some new atoms. But then… why think that’s you? At that point, where she’s using new atoms, she could create thousands of copies of your brain. They wouldn’t all be literally you.

[Slide 56] Objection 2 : Body-swap

[Slide 57] Pop culture contains quite a few body swap stories. Freaky Friday involves a mother and daughter swapping bodies and LEARNING IMPORTANT LIFE LESSONS. Body swaps are weird but they seem basically coherent. (That is to say, we understand the premise! It’s Lindsay Lohan walking around in a Jamie Lee Curtis suit, and vice versa.)

[Slide 58] There’s also Kafka’s Metamorphosis, in which a man wakes up in the body of an insect.

[Slide 57] Pop culture contains quite a few body swap stories. Freaky Friday involves a mother and daughter swapping bodies and LEARNING IMPORTANT LIFE LESSONS. Body swaps are weird but they seem basically coherent. (That is to say, we understand the premise! It’s Lindsay Lohan walking around in a Jamie Lee Curtis suit, and vice versa.)

[Slide 58] There’s also Kafka’s Metamorphosis, in which a man wakes up in the body of an insect.

[Slide 59] So body swaps make sense. But what about personality swaps? Imagine trying to pitch a personality swap movie to a studio executive, You: “James is just an everyday American guy until ONE DAY… he wakes up as a bear! ” Studio exec: “Oh, so the movie is like a comedy where a man tries to live his old life while stuck in a bear’s body? That could be fun.” You: “Nonono. That’s basically been done before. The twist is it’s still the same old James body… but now James is a bear!” Exec: “... I’m not getting it. So it’s actually a story about a bear trapped in a human body?” “No! It’s about James! The human person from the beginning of the movie! But now he’s got the mind of bear – a bear personality, memories, and intelligence. There is no trace of his old psychology remaining. But it’s still James!”

[Slide 60] That seems incoherent. The thing with the James-body and bear-mind isn’t James . Since the thing with the James body isn’t James, we are forced to conclude that bodily persistence is not what matters. God can’t merely bring your body up into heaven. (Else, She could then stuff it with a bear-mind and we’d be forced to say that that was really you up there in heaven)

[Slide 1] The Ethics of Belief This marks our entry into Unit 2 of this course. In one way, today’s lecture will be very different from what’s come before while in another way, it really won’t. We’re no longer going to be concerned with whether God exists – i.e. we’re not doing metaphysics. We’re going to talk about what we should believe about the existence of God. So the topic will stay the same, while the focus will shift. Later in this unit, we’ll turn away from questions about God and look at a variety of other topics in Epistemology – questions about belief and evidence and justification and knowledge.

[Slide 2] Clifford’s wrongful belief principle A case An objection A reply The Anti-Faith Argument 4 Objections “Faith” means “knowledge” Junk facts as counterexamples Irrational beliefs can be morally good Doesn’t this apply to atheism?

[Slide 3] A shipowner is preparing one of his ships for a trans-Atlantic voyage, its cargo a host of emigrants seeking better economic opportunities in a new country. He knows the ship is old and was never well-built. Reliable employees have suggested she needs repairs before she sails again. Doubt gnaws at the shipowner, and he wonders if he should cancel the voyage and have her overhauled and refit. Is time infinite or did God create the universe at some point?

[Slide 4] With some effort he manages to put these concerns aside. He tells himself, “She’s already seen many safe journeys, weathered many storms, and surely God would not allow these poor emigrants to die at sea.” He fosters in himself a sincere conviction that the vessel is safe and seaworthy, and he watches her departure with a light heart. Is time infinite or did God create the universe at some point?

[Slide 31] Some objections

[Slide 32] Objection – By ‘faith’ religious people just mean belief in a higher power. It’s possible to have faith AND to know that one is correct. One common worry about the argument is this: that it is employing the word ‘faith’ in a sneaky and unfair way. Maybe “faith” just means, like, “religious belief”. If that’s right, then you can’t infer that someone lacks evidence merely from the fact that they have faith.

[Slide 8] Evidence

[Slide 9] First, a distinction: Some evidence is public evidence , which can be shared among people. Some evidence is private evidence , which cannot directly be shared.

[Slide 10] Your evidence that Barack Obama has hands VS My evidence that Barack Obama has hands I’ve seen photos and video – in all of them he has hands. That’s public evidence! I can share those videos/photos with you! We can look at the same evidence.

[Slide 9] First, a distinction: Some evidence is public evidence , which can be shared among people. Some evidence is private evidence , which cannot directly be shared.

[Slide 10] Your evidence that Barack Obama has hands VS My evidence that Barack Obama has hands I’ve seen photos and video – in all of them he has hands. That’s public evidence! I can share those videos/photos with you! We can look at the same evidence.

[Slide 11] Your evidence that you have a headache VS My evidence that you have a headache Suppose you have a headache. How can you tell? You feel your headaching . That’s very good evidence! But notice, I can’t feel your headaching. I don’t have access to that evidence. At best, you can tell me that your headaches – but, notice, that evidence is different from (and less persuasive than ) your evidence, the feeling of your head aching .

[Slide 12] Your evidence that 94 × 7 = 658 VS My evidence that 94 × 7 = 658 Is this both? In one sense, it’s private since I can’t share my internal mental calculations with you. But we can share a written accout of the calculation. You could write out the problem on a piece of paper and I could watch you complete it step by step, agreeing at each point along the way. So the paper is public evidence, even if the actual internal calculations are private

[Slide 20] Evidence and God

[Slide 21] Is there public evidence for God? Yes, but it’s mixed. We have, Religious texts (but they disagree – different religions say different things) Believer testimony (but they disagree – different believers insist that different religions are true) Arguments (but they disagree – some like the Ontological Arg seem to show that God exists, others like the Problem of Evil seem to show that She doesnt) This was the point of the Anti-Faith argument: The public evidence for God is not very good.

[Slide 22] Is there private evidence for God? Maybe! What might that look like? Quiz Probably that would look like religious experiences .

[Slide 23] Why think that religious experiences are private evidence ?

[Slide 24] Let’s start with a non-relgious case. Suppose you have a friend with a balloon fetish. One day, you’re both confronted with a balloon. She might say, “I’m having all the same perceptual experiences as you: I smell latex; I see a red, round thing. I hear it squeak when you rub it. BUT my experience also has an additional sexual component. I perceive the balloon as sexy. I can’t point to any particular part as the sexy part; I can’t show you how the balloon is sexy. I simply perceive it that way, and the best I can do is describe to you verbally what it’s like for me. Should you doubt that your friend is experiencing the balloon as sexy? Surely not. Why would they lie about that? You should believe, My friend is experiencing this balloon as sexy.

[Slide 23] Why think that religious experiences are private evidence ?

[Slide 24] Let’s start with a non-relgious case. Suppose you have a friend with a balloon fetish. One day, you’re both confronted with a balloon. [Slide 25] Now compare that to this case: Suppose you and I go hiking. We arrive a magnificent natural vista and spend a few minutes in quiet contemplation. At one point, you turn to me and say, “I think I’m having a religious experience. It’s like… it’s like I can feel God’s presence her with us, surrounding us. I feel her love and comfort and peace.” “I can’t point to any particular part of this vista as the religious part; I can’t show you how this scene is religious. I simply perceive it that way, and the best I can do is describe to you verbally what it’s like for me.” Should you doubt that your friend’s experience includes a component that seems religious? Again, as in the balloon case, it doesnt’ really make a lot of sense to doubt them. Why would they lie about that? You should believe, My friend is experiencing this scene as religious.

[Slide 26] Why think that religious experiences are private evidence ? Because they seem like genuinely experiences for the experiencer just ones that can’t be shared with others. So here’s what Hick wants to say.

[Slide 38] Maybe the lesson we should draw is this, Theism seems like a rational response to profound religious experiences. Because such experiences seem to count as private evidence . BUT… It must equally be true that agnostics (and atheists!) without religious experiences are responding rationally to the public evidence . Why atheists? Why does this story change what we say about atheists? The atheist might rationally reason this way, “If God exists, She would want me to know that She exists and she would have the power to let me know! Thus, The fact that I haven’t had any religious experiences seems itself to be some evidence against the existence of god. (because no religious experiences is more compatible with the the 3-O God does not exist hypothesis than with the the 3-o God does exist hypothesis.)

[Slide 3] René Descartes (1596-1650), while not himself a skeptic, famously introduced some powerful skeptical arguments in his Meditations . These arguments support a strong form of External World Skepticism . Here’s our man. Descartes made important contributions to mathematics, science, and metaphysics. It’s worth noting: he was not himself a skeptic, and skepticism itself predates Descartes by more than a thousand years.
