[Slide 4] Skepticism

[Slide 5] Skepticism is a family of philosophical views, according to which, We think have knowledge that __, but we don’t. We get different forms or varieties of skepticism, depending on what we fill into the blank.

[Slide 6] Other Minds Skepticism says that, We think we know that other people have minds – i.e. rich inner lives like ours – but we don’t. We get different forms or varieties of skepticism, depending on what we fill into the blank.

Before we look at those argument though – before we consider whether Descartes is right that we lack knowledge of the external world – we need to get clear on what knowledge is. (How can we tell if we lack it, if we don’t know what it is?)

[Slide 19] Does Elma know that Napoleon’s favorite color was blue? Does Raul know ? Elma believes in her gut – has always believed – that Napoleon’s favorite color was blue. When asked why she believes this, Elma has no reasons to give, no evidence to cite – she didnt hear that it was from anyone trustworthy or read about it somewhere. She just believes it. Now consider another person, Raul. Raul is a historian. He has read innumerable books and documents about Napoleon – both from primary and secondary sources. He has come to believe quite firmly that Napoleon’s favorite color was blue. This is supported by many texts. Let us suppose that Elma’s favorite color really was blue. Seemingly, Elma’s belief doesn’t count as knowledge because she doesn’t have any evidence or justification. She’s just making shit up about history and, in this case, she happened to get lucky. Raul on the other hand, really has evidence. He knows his stuff.

[Slide 20] Historically, most philosophers have accepted, the J ustified, T rue, B elief ( JTB ) Account having knowledge = having a belief that is justified and true . That suggests that knowledge finally requires some justification.

[Slide 22] What is justification ? Basically, justification amounts to the possession of evidence . Your belief that ___ is justified just in case you have evidence for ___.

[Slide 23] Recall the “ Evidence Principle ” used in the Fine-tuning Argument . With small modification, we can turn it into a definition of evidence . A definition of ‘ evidence ’ An observation, O, is evidence for P over not-P (and is justification for a belief that P) when O is more likely or probable under P than under not-P.

[Slide 24] They don’t smell like bananas. They don’t smush when you hit stuff. They haven’t gone rotten yet. They don’t peel very good. They aren’t yellow, waxy, and stupid. What’s your evidence that your hands aren’t bananas? Each of these observation is more likely in a world in which your hands are NOT bananas than in a world where your hands are bananas. Thus, each of these is a bit of evidence that your hands are not bananas. Each of these facts justifies your belief that your hands aren’t bananas.

[Slide 29] The Argument for External World Skepticism

[Slide 30] P1) By definition, knowledge requires justification . ( K = JTB ) P2) By definition, to have justification for a belief that P, you must have evidence that supports P over not-P. P3) We have no evidence that supports E over not-E . (E = there is an external world, and it’s basically the way we think it is. ) The Argument for External World Skepticism

[Slide 29] The Argument for External World Skepticism

[Slide 30] P1) By definition, knowledge requires justification . ( K = JTB ) P2) By definition, to have justification for a belief that P, you must have evidence that supports P over not-P. P3) We have no evidence that supports E over not-E . (E = there is an external world, and it’s basically the way we think it is. ) The Argument for External World Skepticism

[Slide 31] Why P3? Why think there’s no evidence?

[Slide 32] The Dream Hypothesis My senses seem to give me information about the world outside my body. That info is inaccurate when I’m asleep. Thus, if I’m not sure that I’m awake, I can’t be sure that my sensory info is accurate. I can’t ever be sure that I’m awake. Thus, I can’t ever be sure that my senses are providing me with accurate info. When I’m dreaming, I am prone to believe my senses – i.e. I confuse my dream experiences with experiences of reality. But of course, dream experiences do not represent an external reality . The things I dream aren’t the way I dream them to be; the events I dream do not actually happen. There is no sure way to tell whether I am dreaming and, if I am, for how long I’ve been dreaming.

[Slide 31] Why P3? Why think there’s no evidence?

Maybe we’re in one of those bad cases right now!

[Slide 34] The Evil Demon Hypothesis My senses seem to give me information about the world outside my body. That info would be inaccurate if I were being mind-controlled by an evil demon. Thus, if I can’t rule out the demon possibility, I can’t be sure my senses are accurate. I can’t ever rule out the demon possibility. Thus, I can’t be sure my senses are accurate.

[Slide 35] The Brain-in-a-vat Hypothesis My senses seem to give me information about the world outside my body. That info would be inaccurate if I were a brain in a vat, controlled by a supercomputer. Thus, if I can’t rule out the vat scenario, I can’t be sure my senses are accurate. I can’t ever rule out the vat scenario. Thus, I can’t be sure my senses are accurate. Here’s an updated version of the same scenario: Maybe when you were just 5 days old, some evil scientists removed your brain from your body. They placed your brain into a sophisticated Brain-Sustaining Vat and hooked you up to a very, very powerful supercomputer. Since then, the computer has been interacting with your brain constantly. It has been perfectly simulating for you all the experiences and brain states you would have had had you remained embodied. You’ve lived your whole life like this: interacting with simulated people, working at simulated jobs, perceiving a simulated world. The simulation tech is so good that there is no way to tell that this is what’s going on – no consistent and obvious signs that the things you’re experiencing aren’t real. This might seem surprising, given what you know about computers, but the world being simulated for you has less-sophisticated technology than the actual world. (i.e. the supercomputers in your version of the world aren’t powerful enough to do what the supercomputers in the actual world are actually doing to you .)

[Slide 36] You have two live hypotheses, H1 – John Green is the killer. H2 – John Red is the killer. You find a note in the victim’s hand, “I’m being stalked by a man named John.” This is equally compatible with H1 & H2. So the note provides no new evidence . Imagine you’re investigating a murder and you’ve narrowed it down to suspects: John Brown and John Green. A new clue is discovered. A note turns up in the victim’s handwriting claiming a man named ‘John’ is trying to kill her. In this case, the clue provides you with NO NEW EVIDENCE because it’s equally compatible with both available options.

[Slide 37] Consider these two hypotheses, H1 – My experiences are caused by normal, external world things. H2 – My experiences are caused by a vast supercomputer. Everything I’ve ever experienced is equally compatible with both scenarios. Thus, I have no evidence for H1 over H2. This is how ALL our evidence looks! ALL OF OUR EVIDENCE is compatible with our living in the real world and with our being plugged into the Matrix. All evidence is like finding a note that says “John is trying to kill me” – which is to say, no new evidence at all.

[Slide 21] David Hume (1711 - 1776) is famous for his skepticism about inductive reasoning . Hume argues, while we use inductive reasoning all the time (and can’t help it!), this method cannot give us knowledge because the only way to justify induction is circular .

[Slide 22] Circular Reasoning

[Slide 23] Suppose your friend gets sucked into a religious cult. The cult-leader (Bob) says the Book of Bob is the inerrant word of God. The Book of Bob states that Bob is the infallible messenger of God. Are your friend’s new religious beliefs justified ? You ask your friend, “But why do you trust Bob? How do you know that Bob is infallible?” Your friend says, “Because our holy scripture – the Book of Bob – says that Bob is always right. The scripture is the inerrant word of God.” You ask, “Wait… how do you know that the scripture is infallible, though? Couldn’t the scripture be wrong about Bob?” Your friend says, “Well the scripture has to be infallible because Bob says it’s infallible.” “Wait… so Bob is infallible because the scripture says he is and the scripture is always right? But we know the scripture is always right because… Bob says so? You don’t see the problem with that?”

[Slide 24] The Book says Bob is infallible. Bob says the Book is inerrant. Hopefully, you can see the mistake here. If Bob justified scripture and the scripture justifies Bob, we never actually get any justification. We don’t have any good reason to trust Bob or the scripture.

[Slide 25] Everyone at your new school is so excited about Zane. You ask some people, “Why do you like Zane so much?” They each reply, “I guess I love how, like, popular he is!” Do you now have any good justification for liking Zane? You ask your friend, “But why do you trust Bob? How do you know that Bob is infallible?” Your friend says, “Because our holy scripture – the Book of Bob – says that Bob is always right. The scripture is the inerrant word of God.” You ask, “Wait… how do you know that the scripture is infallible, though? Couldn’t the scripture be wrong about Bob?” Your friend says, “Well the scripture has to be infallible because Bob says it’s infallible.” “Wait… so Bob is infallible because the scripture says he is and the scripture is always right? But we know the scripture is always right because… Bob says so? You don’t see the problem with that?”

[Slide 26] Zane is popular. Lots of people like Zane. Hopefully, you can see the mistake here. To say that Zane is popular just is to say that lots of people like him. But if the only reason why people like Zane in the first place is that he’s popular, then it’s not clear that anyone has a good reason for liking Zane. Is Zane likable? Is he a good guy? Should you like Zane? You have no justification for thinking so. His popularity isn’t justified, not really. It’s just self-perpetuatingly circular.

[Slide 45] Inductive Skepticism We think we know things on the basis of inductive reasoning , but we don’t. Thus, the Problem of Induction gets us Inductive Skepticism. Let me note in closing (as I noted last time) None of you are going to change the way you live because of this argument. (And actually, I don’t really expect any of you to be persuaded .) Hume, himself, acknowledged that we can’t help but use induction all the time and think ourselves justified. That’s not the point! The point isn’t to change your behavior. The point is get us to think more carefully about the foundations of our knowledge. We blithely assume that we have lots of knowledge and knowledge is easy to come by and easy to justify. The Problem of Induction (and the Skeptical arguments about the External World) seem to show us that it’s not so easy. Maybe we do have knowledge and maybe it is easy to come by. But it’s not easy to justify that knowledge. Our knowledge rests on a surprisingly shaky foundation.

[Slide 8] Obviously, lots of people disagree about lots of stuff. Sometimes that’s because they’ve seen different evidence . Sometimes that’s because they’ve evaluated the evidence differently .

[Slide 9] Recall the conversation about private evidence vs. public evidence. You may believe in God because you had a visitation from an angel. That’s real evidence that you have access to. But if I wasn’t visited by an angel, we have different evidence, you and i. And the fact that we have differnt evidence may lead us to disagree about whether God exists

[Slide 10] Alternatively, sometimes people have the same evidence, but assess it very differently,. Imagine two friends who sit down to watch football together, but are rooting for opposing teams. They probably disagree about which team is better and about which team is likely to win. Let’s suppose, too, that they’re both football buffs, who have roughly the same evidence – they’ve seen the same stats, they’ve considered the same expert analysis, they’ve watched the same past games. What they disagree about is how to evaluate that evidence. One of them thinks the evidence supports the conclusion that Team A is the better team; the other thinks the evidence supports the conclusion that Team B is the better team.

[Slide 11] Quiz Here’s an interesting case. The University of Chicago periodically polls Economics professors about various economics issues. The economists’ answers very often diverge substantially from the opinions of non-economists. In this example, 81% of economists think that rent control laws have no positive impact on the amount and quality of affordable housing. (with only 2% of economists thinking they do have a positive impact) Lots of people, I suspect, disagree with the economists about that. Lots of people probably think rent control laws do cause there to be more and better affordable housing. QUIZ: What’s the source of disagreement in this case? Diff evidence? Diff evaluation of the evidence? Both? That disagreement surely involves a difference in both what evidence people have seen and in how that evidence is assessed. People with a PhD in economics have seen lots more evidence about this sort of thing – they actually read published economics papers, after all! – and their experience in the field surely leads them to assess that evidence differently than would a randomly selected American.

[Slide 12] What do we mean by ‘ rational ’ ? How should we respond to disagreement ? (or, what’s the rational response to disagreement ?) Here’s our question for today. What should you DO when you discover that someone disagrees with you about something? What’s the rational response to disagreement? Of course, before we can answer that question, we should probably say something about what we mean by ‘rational’.

[Slide 13] Rational You believe that your arm is broken. Irrational You believe that your leg is broken. Evidence Your doctor tells you your arm is broken. It helps to look at some cases. Here’s an easy case. Clearly, if you believe on the basis of the doctor’s testimony that your leg is broken, you’re doing something irrational. You are making a serious error in judgment.

[Slide 14] Rational I must be walking along her route. Irrational She must be stalking me. Evidence The mailman is still walking behind me. You go for a walk out in your neighborhood. Every few minutes, you turn around and see the same damn mailman walking slowly along behind you, apparently stopping at each house. What should you conclude abotu what’s going on? It’s irrational to conclude that the mailman is stalking you and that’s because we have a lot of background information about how mailmen work: it’s their job to walk slowly around the neighborhood. Thus, when you see a mailman doing just that, the reasonable thing to conclude is that she’s doing her job, and not that she’s stalking you. In this casem, it seems like you made a serious error in judgment .

[Slide 15] Rational The meteor hit before the center was built. Irrational The meteor just barely missed the center. Evidence The visitors’ center is next to the crater. This is a joke obviously. But it’s funny precisely because of how irrational it is – it’s a completely stupid confusion of cause and effect. This is irrational because of things we know about how craters and visitor’s centers work. Visitors centers are not set up in the middle of nowhere in the hopes that something interesting will happen nearby that will be worth visiting. FIRST something intersting happens – like an asteroid impact – then the visitors center is built nearby. Again, if you conclude that the meteor missed the center, you’ve clearly made a very serious error in judgment

[Slide 16] A rational (or reasonable ) response is one that doesn’t involve any serious errors in judgment or reasoning, given the evidence. How should we respond to disagreement ? (or, what’s the rational response to disagreement ?) So the question is, When someone disagrees with you, how should you respond – what kind of responses can you make without committing any serious errors in judgment or reasoning?

So the question is, When someone disagrees with you, how should you respond – what kind of responses can you make without committing any serious errors in judgment or reasoning?

[Slide 17] How should we respond to disagreement ? (or, what’s the rational response to disagreement ?) Answer It depends on whether they’re an epistemic inferior , superior , or peer. I think this is the correct answer: but, as we’ll see, it’s incomplete. To make sense of this answer, we need to introduce a few new ideas/concepts

[Slide 18] On Epistemic Peers

[Slide 17] How should we respond to disagreement ? (or, what’s the rational response to disagreement ?) Answer It depends on whether they’re an epistemic inferior , superior , or peer. I think this is the correct answer: but, as we’ll see, it’s incomplete. To make sense of this answer, we need to introduce a few new ideas/concepts

[Slide 18] On Epistemic Peers

[Slide 19] Not everyone is on the same playing field about every topic.

[Slide 20] On different topics, you and I may have different expertise / cognitive abilities evidence / background knowledge available time / attention biases intellectual virtues (e.g. diligence, humility, creativity, discernment, wisdom) N.B. Not only do these things differ topic by topic, they may also differ moment by moment. It depends on the context! I am usually your superior about philosophy – having PhD and all – but if you and I try to talk philosophy while I’m drunk and distracted, you’re probably my superior.

[Slide 19] Not everyone is on the same playing field about every topic.

[Slide 20] On different topics, you and I may have different expertise / cognitive abilities evidence / background knowledge available time / attention biases intellectual virtues (e.g. diligence, humility, creativity, discernment, wisdom) N.B. Not only do these things differ topic by topic, they may also differ moment by moment. It depends on the context! I am usually your superior about philosophy – having PhD and all – but if you and I try to talk philosophy while I’m drunk and distracted, you’re probably my superior.

[Slide 21] Not everyone is on the same playing field about every topic. The rational response to disagreement , depends on who’s disagreeing.

[Slide 22] Sometimes our disagreement is with an epistemic inferior (i.e. someone less competent / less likely to get the right answer in this case .) Here’s an example: You find out that your 5-year-old cousin disagrees with you about the name of the cheesy, crunchy, corn puff snack made by Frito-Lay. You think they’re called ‘Cheetos’. The 5 year old is convinced they are called ‘Cheebos’ - with a B. He’s being really shitty about it – like, super confident and sneering at you for disagreeing with him. (“Nuh-UHHHH. They’re called CHEE-BOHS) (you’re out camping at the moment, else you’d just Google it.) In this case, I think it’s clear, the 5 year old is your epistemic inferior. You are better equipped than him to know the truth about this.

[Slide 21] Not everyone is on the same playing field about every topic. The rational response to disagreement , depends on who’s disagreeing.

[Slide 22] Sometimes our disagreement is with an epistemic inferior (i.e. someone less competent / less likely to get the right answer in this case .) Here’s an example: You find out that your 5-year-old cousin disagrees with you about the name of the cheesy, crunchy, corn puff snack made by Frito-Lay. You think they’re called ‘Cheetos’. The 5 year old is convinced they are called ‘Cheebos’ - with a B. He’s being really shitty about it – like, super confident and sneering at you for disagreeing with him. (“Nuh-UHHHH. They’re called CHEE-BOHS) (you’re out camping at the moment, else you’d just Google it.) In this case, I think it’s clear, the 5 year old is your epistemic inferior. You are better equipped than him to know the truth about this.

[Slide 23] Sometimes our disagreement is with an epistemic superior (i.e. someone more competent / more likely to get the right answer in this case.) You find out that your next door neighbor, a PhD in History who specializes in Roman culture, disagrees with you about whether vomiting was a regular part of Roman dining customs. You believe that Romans had a special room called a “vomitorium” where they would go to hurl. Your neighbor disagrees. She insists that a “vomitorium” was an entranceway through which crowds entered and exited a stadium. She seems sober and competent. She seems to be making an earnest attempt to inform you about a thing she’s passionate about. (Also you’re camping again? Don’t worry about it!) In this case, I think it’s clear that your neighbor is your epistemic superior . She is better equipped than you to know the truth about this. (It’s literally her job .)

[Slide 24] Sometimes our disagreement is with an epistemic peer (i.e. someone about as competent / likely as you to get the right answer in this case.) You and your friend discover that you disagree about what it means to say that there’s a “north” or “northerly” wind blowing. You think that a north wind blows to the north. She thinks that a north wind comes from the north (and, thus, blows to the south.) Neither of you has any particular expertise in weather. You are roughly the same age and of roughly equal intelligence. (YEP! You’re camping.) In this case, I think it’s clear that neither of you is the inferior or superior of the other. You seem to be roughly equally likely, equally well-equipped to have the right answer about this. Thus, we say that you two are epistemic peers.

[Slide 25] On different topics, you and I may have different expertise / cognitive abilities evidence / background knowledge available time / attention biases intellectual virtues (e.g. diligence, humility, creativity, discernment, wisdom) Some of you may be wondering… How do we tell whether someone is an epistemic inferiot/superior or peer? Well, that’s the point of this list. These are what we assess when trying to determine whether someone is an epistemic peer or superior or inferior, we’re largely looking at these factors. Insofar as you and I are rougly equal in these factors, we’re peers. If you score better, you’re my superior. If I score better, you’re my inferior.

[Slide 26] Not everyone is on the same playing field about every topic. The rational response to disagreement , depends on who’s disagreeing and their relative epistemic standing. Again, what matters when it comes to disagreement is who’s disagreeing.

[Slide 27] Sometimes our disagreement is with an epistemic inferior (i.e. someone less competent / less likely to get the right answer in this case .) The rational response in such a case is to hold onto your original belief . In this case, about this matter, your cousin is your epistemic inferior . You are clearly more equipped than him to know the truth about this matter. It would be irrational to respond to this disagreement by believing your cousin. By accepting that that Frito-Lay makes Cheebos.

[Slide 28] Sometimes our disagreement is with an epistemic superior (i.e. someone more competent / more likely to get the right answer in this case.) The rational response in such a case is to change your original belief . In this case, about this matter, your neighbor is your epistemic superior . She is clearly more equipped than you to know the truth on these matters. It would be irrational to respond to the disagreement by continuing to believe that vomitoriums were for vomiting.

[Slide 31] There are basically two views, The Steadfast View The rational response to peer disagreement is to retain full confidence (as you would with an epistemic inferior ) Conciliationism The rational response to peer disagreement is to lower your confidence (as you would with an epistemic superior )

[Slide 33] The Argument from Self-trust (for the Steadfast View) P1) If Conciliationism is true, it’s irrational to trust in myself over others. P2) Surely it’s not irrational to trust myself over others. C) So, surely Conciliationism is false. (P1) According to Conciliationism, the only rational thing to do in a case of peer disagreement is to place roughly as much trust in our own faculties as we do in the faculties of others – it tells us to conciliate between the equally-trustworthy positions. That is to say, Conciliationism tells us that our own views carry no extra weight. (P2) But you might think that’s wrong. You might think Conciliationism demands an objectionable kind of self-denial or self-abnegation. It treats my own views, my own beliefs as fragile things that must be tossed away at the first sign of contention. It asks that we be servile to the views of others, to be spineless in the face of disagreement. On the contrary, we might thin, when it comes to disagreement, we should stick to our guns because they’re our guns . The fact that it was me who reasoned to this conclusion – that fact that it’s my belief in question – surely does give me a reason to hold on to it, to trust myself to get it right.)

[Slide 34] Objection #1 There must be limits to self-trust! A person who self-trusts all the time is basically an epistemic monster. So the Steadfaster owes us a story about how much self-trust is rational . It is clearly not rational for me to refuse to accept my neighbor’s testimony in the vomitorium case merely out of self-trust. Nor, in general, is it rational to self-trust when disagreeing with an epistemic superior. Picture someone who you think is wrong about everything. Now imagine that person trumpeting the virtue of self-trust the next time you get into an argument with them – as though that were a reason to dismiss you. Imagine that person being wholly self-confident and self-righteous, unmoved by argument or reasons, completely dismissive of alternative viewpoints. Now imagine a world full of such people. Clearly that world would be a dogmatic, partisan nightmare. Most people are wrong about lots of stuff – there are, after all, more ways to be wrong than to be right. So it can’t be right that self-trust is the final word about disagreement. At the very least, the self-trust argument proponent owes us a story about how much extra weight we should be giving our own conclusions – not too much or we become monsters, not too little or this is just the Conciliationist position.

[Slide 35] Objection #2 It’s confused to call Conciliationism an enemy of self-trust. The question isn’t whether to trust my own judgments but which to trust : Judgment #1: P is true. Judgment #2: You are my epistemic peer. Judgment #1 leads me to believe that P. Judgment #2 - when coupled to the fact that you think P is false - leads me not to believe that P. So… which judgment is more important? Should I trust myself and go with judgment #1 or trust myself and go with judgment #2? Of course, if I trust Judgment #1, I am thereby mistrusting my second judgment that you are an epistemic peer. In keeping my original degree of confidence, I am treating you like an epistemic inferior! On the other hand, if i trust Judgment #2 and I change my credence in light of our disagreement, then clearly I am mis- trusting my first judgment about the truth of P. Either way, I am both trusting myself and mis-trusting myself. THus, it is simply false to say the Conciliationism means a rejection of self-trust.

[Slide 36] The Thermometer Analogy (an argument for Conciliationism)

[Slide 37] We have 2 equally reliable thermometers. You check one; I check one. Mine reads 71.1 degrees Yours reads 74.1 degrees What should I believe about the temp? Surely, I should conciliate . I should think the two temps equally likely. It would be irrational for me to believe the temperature is 70. We agree that the thermometers are equally reliable! So why would I trust one more than the other? (Think about how weird it would be for me to insist that the temperature is 70 – as though it mattered that that’s the one I checked. I’m not better at reading digital thermometers than you. And it’s not as though I made a special choice when deciding which thermometer to go check. I could equally well have decided to check the other one.)

[Slide 38] Our two friends are equally good at converting from C to F ° in their heads. Starting with the same temp in Celsius, you ask one to convert; I ask the other. Mine calculates 71.1° F Yours calculates 74.1° F What should I believe about the temp? Again, surely I should conciliate . I should think the two temps equally likely. It would be irrational for me to believe the temperature is 71.1. We agree that the friends are equally reliable! So why would I trust one more than the other? (Think about how weird it would be for me to insist that the temperature is 71.1 – as though it mattered that that’s the friend I asked. I’m not better at asking friends; the people I ask aren’t thereby more accurate for my being the one who asks. And it’s not as though I made a special choice when deciding which friend to ask. I could equally well have decided to ask the other one.)

[Slide 37] We have 2 equally reliable thermometers. You check one; I check one. Mine reads 71.1 degrees Yours reads 74.1 degrees What should I believe about the temp? Surely, I should conciliate . I should think the two temps equally likely. It would be irrational for me to believe the temperature is 70. We agree that the thermometers are equally reliable! So why would I trust one more than the other? (Think about how weird it would be for me to insist that the temperature is 70 – as though it mattered that that’s the one I checked. I’m not better at reading digital thermometers than you. And it’s not as though I made a special choice when deciding which thermometer to go check. I could equally well have decided to check the other one.)

[Slide 38] Our two friends are equally good at converting from C to F ° in their heads. Starting with the same temp in Celsius, you ask one to convert; I ask the other. Mine calculates 71.1° F Yours calculates 74.1° F What should I believe about the temp? Again, surely I should conciliate . I should think the two temps equally likely. It would be irrational for me to believe the temperature is 71.1. We agree that the friends are equally reliable! So why would I trust one more than the other? (Think about how weird it would be for me to insist that the temperature is 71.1 – as though it mattered that that’s the friend I asked. I’m not better at asking friends; the people I ask aren’t thereby more accurate for my being the one who asks. And it’s not as though I made a special choice when deciding which friend to ask. I could equally well have decided to ask the other one.)

[Slide 39] You and I are equally good at converting from C to F ° in their heads. Starting with the same temp in Celsius, we both do the maths. I calculate 71.1° F You calculate 74.1° F What should I believe about the temp? Again, surely I should conciliate . I should think the two temps equally likely. Again, it seems like it would be irrational for me to believe the temperature is 71.1. We agree that we two are equally good at math! So why would I trust one of us more than the other? It’s like the thermometer case again, but the instrumental is in my head rather than outside it. But why should that matter? And it’s not obvious why I should privilege the instrument in my head merely because it’s mine. That seems weird! That seems as weird as trusting the thermometer that I checked merely because I’m the one who checked (and not because I have any special reason to think that it’s more reliable.) Or as weird as trusting the friend I asked merely because I asked her. At the point where you and I agree that we are both equally reliable at mental math, it’s really bizarre - seemingly irrational! – to then treat the other person’s calculator as worse than our own.

[Slide 40] The Thermometer Analogy (an argument for Conciliationism) P1) If the Steadfast View is true, it’s rational in the mental math case to trust myself more than you. P2) It’s not rational in the mental math case to trust myself more than you. (that’s like trusting my thermometer more than yours.) C) So, the Steadfast View is false.

[Slide 23] To what extent did epistemic bubbles exist before the internet? Quiz

[Slide 24] I should think: Epistemic bubbles have been the norm for most of human history. If you lived in 13th centure France, or whatever, probably your community was a few hundred or a few thousand people, and, given how poor communication technology was back then, you mostly didn’t have access to important information – about anything. You didn’t know about current events. You didn’t know about scientific research. You didn’t know about politics or other religions.

[Slide 25] In this respect, I think, the 20th century was really the exception. For the first time in human history, most people had access to the same high quality information sources. It’s only in the last decade or so that we’ve regressed back into a period of epistemic bubbles. (And even still, our current bubbles seem way smaller and way less concerning, than the bubbles that existed in 13th century france or whatever.

[Slide 27] Nguyen lists two reasons, Ignorance Overconfidence (1) is obvious. If you aren’t getting all the informaiton, you don’t know all the relevant facts. Almost by definition, if you’re trapped in an epistemic bubble, you’re ignorant of important information. (2) is a bit more subtle. The basic thought is: if I’m trapped in an epistemic bubble, there’s a very serious risk that I won’t realize it. That’s not good. I may false conclude that I do have access to all the relevant information, and so I may have a false degree of confidence in my beliefs. (I’ve seen all there is to see, so my beliefs couldn’t possibly be false.)

[Slide 33] Your mother has pushed you into an echo chamber . As long as you can remember, your mom’s been telling you that everyone in the world – everyone but you and her – is a robot. You two are the only real people in a sea of malicious automatons. She’s warned you, “Don’t let your guard down and don’t let yourself get fooled. They’re gonna act like people. They might tell you that they are people. They might get mad if you let on that you know. (“Hey! Fuck you! I’m not robot!”) But that’s how they get you. It’s just you and me, kiddo.”

[Slide 34] Your mother has pushed you into an echo chamber . How will you react when other people try to tell you you’re wrong? Presumably, you’ll entirely dismiss them. You’ll think ‘Nice try, you robot bastard’ You might even increase your level of confidence that everyone else is a robot. (Which is pretty messed up, when you think about it. It’s weird to respond to someone telling you “Hey I’m not a robot!” by becoming more confident that they are, in fact, a robot.)

[Slide 41] Case #1 : Your mom spends 18 years telling you that everyone is a robot, starting from when you’re 2 . Other people all insist that they’re not robots. Case #2 : Your mom spends 18 years telling you that everyone is a robot, starting from when you’re 20 . Other people all insist that they’re not robots. Recall the robot case from earlier. You have the same evidence in each case. But only in one of them do you get stuck in an echo chamber. (This is largely because children are very susceptible to this mistake: treating earlier evidence as better evidence.)

(and, recalling the point Clifford made earlier this semester, you might be more likely therefore to do bad thing)

[Slide 49] “ Listen to what it actually sounds like when people reject the plain facts – it doesn’t sound like brute irrationality. One side points out a piece of economic data; the other side rejects that data by rejecting its source. They think that newspaper is biased, or the academic elites generating the data are corrupt… In many ways, echo-chamber members are following reasonable and rational procedures of enquiry. They are engaging in critical reasoning... It’s simply that their basis for evaluation – their background beliefs about whom to trust – are radically different. They are not irrational, but systematically misinformed about where to place their trust.” – C. Thi Nguyen

[Slide 50] P1) If the Post-Truth Thesis is true, we should observe brute irrationality – i.e. a complete disregard for evidence, reasoning, and critical thinking. P2) We do not observe brute irrationality. C) So the Post-Truth Thesis is false. Nguyen’s argument against the PTT

[Slide 4] What is fake news ? More than just false news ! Sometimes well-intentioned journalists get things wrong.

[Slide 5] Here’s an example of false news. One time a couple years ago the NYT had to correct a mistake where it falsely claimed that Elon Musk missed his brother’s wedding. He didn’t! Oops. The NYT regrets the error.

[Slide 6] Here’s another example. One time the NYT printed a sentence about how America’s trade deficit shrank during “the Time of Shedding and Cold Rocks.” They meant to say “during the Great Recession” but one of their copy-editors had a browser extension installed that automatically replaces all instances of the latter with the former. But I really don’t think that’s fake news. That’s not what people are worrying about when they worry about fake news. This is just false news - just an honest mistake made by a well-intentioned journalist, which was eventually noticed and corrected.

[Slide 19] A standard case of testimony among social contacts You’re talking to your friend, Tessie (the Testifier.) She would really like you to believe that a certain proposition, P , is true. So she’s trying to decide whether and how strongly to assert P to you. (e.g. “ P .” “ Did you know that P ? ” “ I’ve heard that P, but I’m not sure what to think about that.” ) <Ask someone for sample Ps – something you imagine someone wanting to convince you is true – could be, e.g, a political claim, a scientific claim, a bit of gossip, etc. >

[Slide 20] Basic Facts You want to avoid false beliefs (and to a lesser extent, to acquire true beliefs.) You trust Tessie’s testimony more than you would a stranger’s You will trust Tessie less if you discover her giving bad testimony Tessie wants you to believe (or seriously consider) that P (otherwise she wouldn’t be giving testimony) Tessie wants to preserve (or even increase) her credibility

[Slide 30] “ [On social media] the epistemic relationship between testifier and testimony is ambiguous, as we haven’t yet settled on a norm whereby sharing entails assertion ... Many of us treat social media sharing as if it were ordinary testimony, at least until something goes wrong. This is why the “a retweet is not an endorsement” mantra causes so much argument; many of us implicitly assume that our social media interlocutors do believe what they share, even though we are vaguely aware they may later disclaim it. This is part of what makes social media testimony aberrant.” – Regina Rini

[Slide 31] Offline Testimony Assert Strongly + Risk Credibility OR Assert Weakly + Preserve Credibility Social Media Testimony Assert Strongly + Preserve Credibility On social media, your friends don’t face the same trade-off between strong assertion and risking trust. That’s why fake news spreads. There is no strong trust incentive to STOP from sharing.

[Slide 32] Regina Rini Content sharing is an ambiguous form of assertion. This makes it less risky for the testifier (and, thus, more appealing ) to share otherwise suspicious content. This is the view. This is why Rini thinks it’s individually sensible for people to share fake news

[Slide 41] P1) If a person shares your political views, then it’s rational to trust them about political matters. C) If a person shares your political views, then it’s rational to regard them as a reliable source of political testimony. On the rationality of believing fake news P2 is, simply, a claim about what it means to trust someone. (P2 is not supposed to be controversial or suprising.)

[Slide 1] Righteous Incivility In this unit, we’re going to shift gears and start doing ethics. Where Epistemology is concerned with knowledge, justification, evidence, and rational belief, Ethics is concerned with the nature of the right and the good: the rules of right action, the components of virtuous and vicious character, the details of what we owe to each other.

[Slide 2] Background: civility and disagreement Righteous incivility on issues of social consensus Righteous incivility on issues on ongoing social disagreement A case of righteous incivility Two arguments against righteous incivility The Asymmetry Argument The Argument for Self-Doubt

[Slide 3] Previously, we considered disagreement as an interesting epistemic problem. “What’s the rational thing to believe when confronted by disagreement?”

[Slide 4] Disagreement also gives rise to an interesting ethical problem. “What’s the correct way to behave when confronted by disagreement?” OR “ What rules or norms must we follow in communicating our disagreement?” Notice, these questions are about how we should treat each other, what we owe each other, what counts as ethically acceptable behavior. It’s not about how we should reason, what we should believe.

[Slide 11] “ It takes a hardy moral character to receive a slap as a summons to be good. But why would I think that one so low in my opinion will not just rise but soar to heights of circumspection, receiving disrespect as a provocation to be better? If she’s bad enough to need a punch, she’s not likely good enough to take it well and change. ” – Amy Olberding This is how Olberding puts it. *chefs kiss* This paragraph is so good, it belongs in a museum. Basically, the thought is: If your goal is to convince someone else that they’re wrong, it simply doesn’t make much sense to be uncivil. Basically no one responds to rudeness positively. A punch in the face is not typically understood as an invitation to be a better person.

[Slide 17] It’s OK to be uncivil with a slavery supporter. That’s righteous incivility. Similarly if you meet someone wants to defend slavery as a just institution, very plausibly do not need to show them respect, toleration, and consideration. If anything, it would seem to be positively bad to show them consideration, to treat their view as somehow “on the table,” as somehow worthy of discussion and consideration.

[Slide 18] It’s righteous incivility to be uncivil with a defender of pedophilia. Why? Again, (a) Pretty much everyone agrees that pedophilia morally awful. (b) it’s really important for us to protect that common consensus. It’s really important that everyone continue to believe that pedophilia is really awful. Thus, we’re justified in being disrepectful to people who try to defend the practice. We are justified in not giving their views fair consideration.

[Slide 26] Here’s a plausible case. In the early 1910’s the women’s suffrage movement in the UK was getting desperate as years of political activism had yet to secure them the vote. They resorted to incivility: to verbal aggression, heckling, rudeness, and physical violence (like physical confrontations with police and window smashing.) Their worry was that precisely because women had historically been well-behaved – had been polite, well-mannered, and civil – men had no trouble dismissing their demands. Women were meek; their complaints were easily dismissed.

[Slide 31] The asymmetry argument Recall, earlier I pointed out that one reason we should in general be civil is that we ourselves expect others to be civil toward us. By symmetry, if I can demand respect from you, then surely you can demand respect from me. However, I think most people who behave uncivilly think that they are justified in doing so precisely because there is no real symmetry between themselves and their political opponents.

[Slide 32] Observation Most people think there’s an asymmetry between themselves & their opponents. “My opponents sometimes need to be called out, roughed up, treated with contempt. As for me and mine, we’re doing our best. We are owed respect.” We could put the point like this.

[Slide 31] The asymmetry argument Recall, earlier I pointed out that one reason we should in general be civil is that we ourselves expect others to be civil toward us. By symmetry, if I can demand respect from you, then surely you can demand respect from me. However, I think most people who behave uncivilly think that they are justified in doing so precisely because there is no real symmetry between themselves and their political opponents.

[Slide 32] Observation Most people think there’s an asymmetry between themselves & their opponents. “My opponents sometimes need to be called out, roughed up, treated with contempt. As for me and mine, we’re doing our best. We are owed respect.” We could put the point like this.

[Slide 33] There are basically two possible principles that might justify this asymmetry and, thus, righteous incivility in cases of ongoing social disagreement : Option 1 – Righteous-in-Fact Incivility is justified when I actually am fighting for a righteous cause and when the people I would be uncivil toward actually do have false beliefs. Option 2 – Righteous-in-Feeling Incivility is morally justified when I feel very confident both that I am fighting for a righteous cause and that those I would be uncivil toward are in the wrong. The basic thought is, What could possibly ground the asymmetry? Why would it be true that YOU owe ME respect, but I don’t owe YOU respect? There are only really two options. Option #1 YOU owe ME respect because i am actually right. I don’t owe YOU respect because you’re are actually wrong Option #2 YOU owe ME respect because your view is weak and you don’t believe it very strongly. I don’t owe YOU respect because I am very confident in my view, because I am sure that I am right.

Quiz Our question is this: When should I think myself justified in being uncivil to my political opponents? [Slide 35] Imagine a man who disagrees with you about, basically, everything – about all matters of ongoing social dispute. (e.g. abortion, policing, the existence of God, the death penalty, universal healthcare, etc.) And, let’s suppose, on each issue, he is exactly as confident as you are. (e.g. if you are 98% sure that you have the correct view about abortion, he is 98% sure that he has the correct view.) Now imagine that he’s persistently really shitty to you online – mocking you, harrassing you, telling you he hopes you die, threatening you. You message him like,...

[Slide 36] The trouble with Righteous-in-Fact is that we can’t tell merely by inspection which of our beliefs are the actually true ones. In cases of ongoing social disagreement , many people on both sides will feel sure that their side is the actually right one. But, of course, one side is actually wrong ! Our false beliefs feel exactly the same as our true beliefs! My true beliefs don’t, like, sparkle with some inner worthiness that is clearly absent from my false beliefs. No. My true beliefs and my false beliefs FEEL THE SAME TO ME. (Notice, if false beliefs felt different from true beliefs, You would have already rejected all your false beliefs on the grounds that they have that distinctive false belief feel to them. (But there is no such feeling, is my point.)) Thus, Righteous in Fact is unhelpful. I can’t tell which causes are the actually righteous ones! So I cannot use this principle to make decisions. It’s unhelpful.

[Slide 36] The trouble with Righteous-in-Fact is that we can’t tell merely by inspection which of our beliefs are the actually true ones. In cases of ongoing social disagreement , many people on both sides will feel sure that their side is the actually right one. But, of course, one side is actually wrong ! Our false beliefs feel exactly the same as our true beliefs! My true beliefs don’t, like, sparkle with some inner worthiness that is clearly absent from my false beliefs. No. My true beliefs and my false beliefs FEEL THE SAME TO ME. (Notice, if false beliefs felt different from true beliefs, You would have already rejected all your false beliefs on the grounds that they have that distinctive false belief feel to them. (But there is no such feeling, is my point.)) Thus, Righteous in Fact is unhelpful. I can’t tell which causes are the actually righteous ones! So I cannot use this principle to make decisions. It’s unhelpful.

[Slide 37] And that means, Righteous-in-Fact either, (a) tells us we’re never justified in incivility (b/c we can never be sure we’re actually right ) (b) or collapses into Righteous-in-Feeling. (b/c being confident is all you need.) We can put the point this way, Righteous-in-Fact tells us that we are righteously uncivil when we our beliefs are actually true. Since I can’t tell which of my beliefs are the actually true ones, this means we really only have two options in how we interpret the Righteous-in-Fact principle: Either it tells us we’re never justified Or, it tells us we’re justified whenever we feel confident. If we’re never justified, then Righteous-in-Fact cannot explain the asymmetry. If you and me are both never justified in being righteously uncivil, then you and me are symmetric when it comes to owing each other respect. On the other hand, if Righteous-in-Fact is interpreted as saying that we are justified whenever we think we are actually correct, then it just collapses into the other option: Righteous-in-Feeling.

[Slide 38] There are basically two possible principles that might justify this asymmetry and, thus, righteous incivility in cases of ongoing social disagreement : Option 1 – Righteous-in-Fact Incivility is justified when I actually am fighting for a righteous cause and when the people I would be uncivil toward actually do have false beliefs. Option 2 – Righteous-in-Feeling Incivility is morally justified when I feel very confident both that I am fighting for a righteous cause and that those I would be uncivil toward are in the wrong. So, Not Option 1, apparently. What about Option 2?

Quiz QUIZ Q: As before, hopefully you can already sort of make out why Righteous-in-Feeling is also an unsatisfying answer to that question. Take a shot at it: Why doesn’t this work? The basic idea is: This principle also cannot explain the asymmetry. And that’s because my political opponents and I are equally confident about our opposing positions. Since we are symmetric in our confidence, we are, according to this principle, symmetric in our permissions to be righteously uncivil.

[Slide 40] Again, imagine a confrontation the man who’s harrassing you online… THIS IS NOT HOW THIS CONVERSATION WOULD END But there’s another very serious problem with Righteous-in-Feeling, and that is that almost no one actually likes this view. Almost no one thinks “I feel very confident” is a legitimate reason for being rude and uncivil. When someone is being uncivil toward me, I do not care whether or not they feel confident that I deserve to be treated rudely, without respect. That’s irrelevant. I think they owe me respect. Period. Since I clearly don’t think that feeling confident is a sufficient reason for others to be uncivil, I should not think it a sufficient reason for me to be uncivil.

[Slide 43] It’s righteous incivility to be uncivil with a defender of pedophilia. To push the point home, Notice, we don’t have the same problem when it comes to defending righteous incivility against, say, a defender of pedophilia. There really is an asymmetry here! Almost everyone agrees with me that pedophilia is morally wicked; almost no one agrees with my opponent that pedophilia is morally unobjectionable. That’s a real difference! And it’s an important difference!

[Slide 44] The argument for self-doubt

[Slide 45] Observation Our motivations for (and feelings about) being uncivil often don’t look like the motivations and feelings of a truly virtuous moral agent. We could put the point like this.

[Slide 46] “ Truly righteous incivility would issue from a deeply moral wish against its own necessity. It would come about as forced, a sorry step one feels reluctantly obliged to take. Morally good people want to respect others – they want a world in which we can, in all good conscience and effect, treat each other humanely and kindly. They do not want to signal disrespect even when they see they must. They are people who perceive a moral need to be rough and inconsiderate as distressing.” – Amy Olberding

[Slide 47] P1) If my motivation for incivility were truly righteous , I would feel distressed about the prospect of being uncivil toward my opponents. P2) I do not feel distressed about being uncivil . (if anything, I rather enjoy being uncivil !) C) Thus, my motivations for incivility are not truly righteous (and, so, I’m lying to myself if I think I’m being a noble warrior for the good.) The Argument for Self-doubt

[Slide 1] On Legalizing Prostitution A rather different ethics topic today. If you’re wondering how I’m deciding topics, I’m mostly following my nose for arguments. The argument we’re going to look today is, I think, so cool and clever.

[Slide 2] Background An argument for legalization Anderson’s reply 7 features of / regulations on personal services A world where sex work is no different Sexual Autonomy An argument for criminalization

[Slide 3] Background

[Slide 4] Some Clarifications ‘ sex work ’ and ‘ sex worker ’ have begun to replace ‘ prostitution ’ and ‘ prostitute ’ I’ll mostly use the former – in line with the WHO – but I don’t thereby mean to imply that I reject Anderson’s arguments. The aim of this change in language has been to reframe the debate as being primarily about labor relations. As we will see, whether or not such a reframing is good or desirable is precisely what is in question in Anderson’s paper. I’m going to adopt the ‘sex worker’ linguistic paradigm for the purposes of this lecture just because it’s the one used by the WHO, but I want to make it clear that this is not to imply that I favor the associated philosophical position on decriminalization.

[Slide 3] Background

[Slide 4] Some Clarifications ‘ sex work ’ and ‘ sex worker ’ have begun to replace ‘ prostitution ’ and ‘ prostitute ’ I’ll mostly use the former – in line with the WHO – but I don’t thereby mean to imply that I reject Anderson’s arguments. [Slide 5] We’re only talking about sex sales among adults with some ongoing, basic freedoms. The question is, Should those transactions be legal? or (better) What’s the best way to protect the interests of those sex workers ? It’s important that it be adults because children can’t consent to sex And it’s important that we restrict ourselves to cases in which the sex workers have ongoing, basic freedoms because otherwise, their decisions to do sex work non-autonomous and likely coercive (and so, in that sense, also not consensual.) We set aside cases involving children and cases involving coercion because those are just cases of sexual assault. No reasonable person can argue that those practices ought to be legal.

[Slide 6] Everyone agrees that sex work in its current form is truly awful for sex workers Even when they’re not literally enslaved or otherwise coerced, they’re still - Stigmatized by society - Exploited / abused by clients & pimps - Denied legal recourse for mistreatment - Denied basic occupational protections (OSHA, etc.)

[Slide 9] Compare Everyone agrees that child enslavement is truly awful for the children . What should we do??? Option 1 Legalize and normalize Option 2 Criminalize and aggressively prosecute (the slavers, not the children) We might note: Things are really bad for enslaved children. That is not in dispute. But what policies should we adopt to improve things for those enslaved children? We seem to have the same 2 options. But in this case, it’s not really an open question which option is best.

[Slide 10] Compare Everyone agrees that medicine import laws are very bad for U.S. residents who cannot afford their essential medicines. What should we do??? Option 1 Legalize and normalize Option 2 Criminalize and aggressively prosecute (medicine importers, not patients) It is currently illegal to import prescription medicine from Canada, or other countries where the very same medicines are on sale for, often, 10% of the U.S. prince. You can get in trouble for buying that stuff. But of course, many of the people who buy imported medicine in the US are just trying to stay alive. That’s why they’re buying these illegal import drugs. So, NOT IN DISPUTE: it’s bad to be a low income person in the US with expensive medical needs. And again we seem to face two options... But in this case, as before, I don’t think it’s really an open question which option is best. (What does the opposing case even look like? How do you argue for Option 2? “It is morally better to protect drug industry profits – even though it costs the lives and livelihoods of tens of thousands of Americans per year.”)

[Slide 11] P eople disagree about how best to promote the interests of sex workers . Option 1 Legalize and normalize Option 2 Criminalize and aggressively prosecute (i.e. prosecute clients / pimps, not sex workers) Quiz Unlike the last two cases, it really does seem to be an open question which of these two options is best. At least, among philosophers and ethicists, among the people whose literal job it is to think about these things, there is lively debate between these two options. Which option we should adopt depends on whether we think we can best reduce the harms suffered by sex workers by trying to eliminate prostitution or by creating a less-stigmatized, more-protected version of the profession. [Slide 23] #1 – People can make contracts to engage in personal services, and the state can enforce contracts to perform those services. Think about, e.g., a tutor. If you hire a physics tutor to help you for 20 hours of work, and they only work for 10 hours, you can sue them and the state will force to either complete their contract or pay damages

[Slide 24] #2 – Employers can make performance of personal services a condition of employment. (even if you were hired to do something else) If you work for a finance company or a marketing company or a non-profit or whatever, employers are allowed to demand that you complete personal service tasks as a condition of your employment. Your boss can say, “Hey Bob from accounting is threatening to quit if we don’t shoulder some of his transportation costs. We really need to retain Bob this year. So we promised him that you’d drive him to-and-from meetings, as needed. Refusing will, of course, result in us firing you for cause.”

[Slide 41] Sexual Autonomy

[Slide 42] If sex work isn’t just like other personal services , what’s the difference? Quiz QUIZ: What do you think the difference is? Why do we recoil from a world where sex work is treated just like other personal services?

[Slide 43] Anderson thinks, The difference is that we, as a society, place a very high value on sexual autonomy – i.e. the freedom to determine the frequency, timing, and manner of one’s sexual encounters. Relative to other personal services, sex work sacrifices something of great value. If sex work isn’t just like other personal services , what’s the difference? Obviously, if you are a sex worker, you no longer have full freedom to determine the frequency, timing, and manner of one’s sexual encounters – especailly not if you work for someone else. Sex workers surrender their sexual autonomy. No other workers have to do that. (A driver may give up his driving autonomy – his freedom to decide when and where to drive – but we dont’ really care about our own personal driving autonomy. That’s not a valuable thing we want to protect.)

[Slide 7] Kidney transplants have been going on since the 50’s. A person with end stage liver disease is cut open and a healthy, donor kidney is attached to the circulatory system below the original damaged ones.

[Slide 8] We source kidneys from both the living and the dead . This makes kidneys relatively unique. Most kidney donors can expect to live a normal, healthy life post-operation. (which is not true of, say, heart donors!) On average, about ⅓ of transplanted kidneys are from living donors . Humans have two kidneys and can survive with just one The avg kidney donor has undiminished quality-of-life & lifespan

[Slide 9] U.S. kidney shortages are large, persistent, and fatal. About 100,000 people are on the waitlist at any given time. Average wait time is about 5 years Roughly 30,000 kidney transplants are performed annually About 8,000 people die each year in the U.S. while waiting for a kidney The supply of kidneys from dead patients is limited b/c Only ~45% of the U.S. opt-in to organ donation A person must die in a particular way for their kidney to be usable

[Slide 11] Two challenges for opponents of kidney selling Here I follow Radcliffe-Richards. She suggests there are two easy arguments we might give in support of legalizing kidney sales. The challenge for the opponent of kidney selling is to overcome these arguments, to show why kidney selling really is objectionable despite these two arguments in favor.

[Slide 12] For those who think kidneys sales should remain illegal , there are essentially two challenges, (1) Allowing kidney sales would almost certainly save lives (2) Kidney donations are widely regarded as permissible (and the same justification seems to apply to kidney sales .)

[Slide 11] Two challenges for opponents of kidney selling Here I follow Radcliffe-Richards. She suggests there are two easy arguments we might give in support of legalizing kidney sales. [Slide 12] For those who think kidneys sales should remain illegal , there are essentially two challenges, (1) Allowing kidney sales would almost certainly save lives (2) Kidney donations are widely regarded as permissible (and the same justification seems to apply to kidney sales .)

[Slide 13] Challenge #1 Kidney sales would save lives Were kidney sales legal, there’d very likely be fewer people dying for lack of a kidney. In general, societies should adopt policies that save citizens’ lives... (e.g. crosswalks, speed limits, suicide hotlines) unless the policy’s bad effects would outweigh the good of saving lives. I say “very likely” b/c there is some economic research that suggests in some cases and in some markets you actually get less of a good when you offer to pay people for it. The people who would have donated for free decide they don’t want to, and only a smaller number decide to sell. So less of the good is available than before. BUT the only country where kidney sales are legal, Iran, is telling on the point: there is no kidney waitlist nor kidney shortage in Iran. Thus, almost certainly: the fact that kidney sales are legal in Iran is saving Iranian lives.

[Slide 14] Challenge #1 Kidney sales would save lives Thus, the kidney selling opponent will need to identify some bad effects of legalizing kidney sales . More specifically, she must claim, “ These effects are so bad that it’s honestly better to let people die. ”

[Slide 22] Challenge #2 Kidney donations are clearly fine Kidney donations are fine because we each have a right to bodily integrity : While a doctor may not extract my kidney without my valid consent , my valid consent is all she needs. But then… Doesn’t that also justify kidney selling ? Hopefully, you can see now why this represents a challenge for those who would oppose kidney selling: If my consent is enough to justify this explanation would seem to imply that kidney selling is permissible too. If my consent is sufficient for letting people take my kidney, then, seemingly, it is sufficient for letting them take my kidney and then give me money in return.

[Slide 23] For those who think kidneys sales should remain illegal , there are essentially two challenges, (1) Allowing kidney sales would almost certainly save lives (2) Kidney donations are widely regarded as permissible (and the same justification seems to apply to kidney sales .) So these are the two challenges facing anyone who would want to oppose kidney sales. To defend the ban on kidney sales, we need to identify some bad outcome of legalizing kidney sales that (a) is so bad, that it outweighs the goodness of saving thousands of lives but (b) doesn’t imply that kidney donation is impermissible. E.g. “It’s immoral to allow kidney sales because some people may regret having sold their kidney” implies that kidney donation is immoral (AND it’s implausible that some regret is worse than letting thousands die.) “We should ban kidney sales because some people may experience kidney problems or other adverse health effects years after the surgery” implies that kidney donation is immoral, too.

[Slide 24] Possible Objections to Kidney Sales Here’s Radcliff-Richard’s strategy: She’s going to try to canvas the possible reasons one might give for objecting to kidney sales and, she’ll argue, all of them seem to fail.

[Slide 33] Coercion is a wrongful narrowing of another person’s choices, to pressure them to make a decision they might not otherwise make. To illustrate what Radcliffe-Richards has in mind, lets look at a case: Suppose some kidnappers grab your dog and threaten to harm it if you don’t give them your entire stamp collection. If you give them the stamps, we would surely say that your consent was illegitimate because it was coerced . What makes this a case of coersion? Radcliffe-Richards suggests that it’s coercion because you’re options have been limited in an inappropriate way. The kidnappers artificially and wrongfully narrowing your options: your stamps or your dog. Before you could keep both; now you only get one. That’s coercion.

[Slide 34] Proposal The background conditions of poverty make kidney sales coercive . (poverty doesn’t really influence kidney donors ) Reply Coercion is about being wrongfully denied an option that you used to have. (but kidney sellers now have a new option!) Quiz So this is a second reply to the suggestion that poverty is coercive: when it comes to allowing kidney sales, we’re giving people an extra option, not denying them an option. And, Radcliffe-Richards thinks, this actually suggests a plausible argument is support of allowing kidney sales.

[Slide 35] Think about the dognapper case again. Now imagine that the police show up on the scene and they prevent you from giving up your stamps – perhaps out of some misguided policy that no one should negotiate with terrorists . This is not helpful. You would be pissed at the police and rightly so. By intervening, the police force you to give up your pet where you would have preferred to give up your stamp collection. This is analogous to banning kidney sales. The kidnappers are like poverty. OF COURSE you shouldn’t have to deal with that bullshit – the dognappers or the povery. Of course it’s unjust that you’ve been put in this situation. But given that YOU ARE in this situation, you’d prefer to sell your kidney (or give up your stamps) than remain in poverty (or let your pet be hurt.) A ban on kidney sales is like the police preventing you from giving up your stamps; it’s blocking you from doing what you’d most like to do, given your non-ideal, unjust circumstances.

[Slide 36] “ It is really pretty obvious, as soon as the matter is addressed directly, that increasing someone’s range of options – which is what an offer of payment always does – could not in itself constitute any kind of coercion or restriction of freedom. ” - Janet Radcliffe-Richards

[Slide 37] Harmless Options Principle In general, giving X one more option does not limit X’s freedom nor make X worse off than she was before. (even if the option is deeply unattractive!) Let’s give this idea a name. Radcliffe-Richards is proposing a general moral principle. It’s a pretty plausible one!

[Slide 47] Your eldery patient has weak agency . He doesn’t have the control he ought to have over his own body and choices . Let me give you an example. Imagine you’re a primary care physician. You have this one eldery patient who always comes in with his son. For the duration of every visit, the son runs the show: he asks you questions about his dad, he answers your questions about his dad’s health, he takes charge of scheduling appointments, picking up medications, and even making medical decisions. As far as you can tell, your elderly patient is perfectly mentally capable – he doesn’t have dementia or anything; he’s just under his son’s thumb.

[Slide 48] Quiz We already see this happening in black markets in some parts of the world. Satz Under conditions of weak agency , we should be worried that decisions to sell a kidney will be coerced . (not by poverty, but by other people.) A person with weak agency is not fully in control of her own body or her life decisions.

[Slide 49] “ [Certain] facts stand out in the Indian cases [involving kidney sales ]: first, an overwhelming majority of those interviewed (79%) regretted their decision and would not recommend that others sell a kidney; and second, a majority of sellers found and interviewed (71%) were married women. Given the weak position of women in Indian society, the voluntary nature of the sales is questionable . The most common explanation offered by wives as to why they and not their husbands sold their organs were that the husbands were the family’s income source (30%) or were ill (28%). Of course, as the authors of the study point out, most of the interviews of women were conducted in the presence of their husbands or other family members , so they may have been reluctant to admit to being forced. ” – Debra Satz

[Slide 50] Satz Under conditions of weak agency , we should be worried that decisions to sell a kidney will be coerced . (not by poverty, but by other people.) Quiz We already see this happening in black markets in some parts of the world. SO, Her argument is this: If we legalize kidney sales in countries where lots of people have weak agency, there is good reason to worry that many of those selling kidneys are coerced. Thus, in those countries, it may be true that kidney selling is much worse than kidney donation and, thus, we may be able to justify a ban. ON THE OTHER HAND, she notes, keeping kidney sales illegal does not eliminate black markets. And insofar as there’s coercion going on in the black markets already, we may ultimately decide we can protect people better by legalizing kidney sales and implementing robust legal regulations and protections.

[Slide 51] Objection #2 Against the Harmless Option Principle

[Slide 52] Satz The H.O.P. is false. Sometimes an extra option really does make people worse off or restrict their freedom. ( but it’s indirect, so it’s easy to overlook.) Recall, the Harmless Options Principle says In general, giving X one more option does not limit X’s freedom nor make X worse off than she was before. (even if the option is deeply unattractive!) Radcliffe-Richards used the principle to argue that allowing kidney sales is just giving people an extra option – and, thus, doesn’t make anyone worse off than they already were.

[Slide 53] The CU PHIL Dept starts offering a new course, “The Metaphysics of Manuscripts in Neo-Platonic Thought through the Early Middle Ages” Recall this case. You think that sounds SO FUCKING BORING. But it’s just another extra option – no worries! Just don’t take it! But now suppose you’re trying to get into one of my courses one semester (Let’s pretend you like me enough to take two of my courses!) You’re stuck on the waitlist, and you email me, Hey, Caleb! I really need to take one more Philosophy course next semester, but I’m stuck on the waitlist for your course. Can you request a manual override? While I might normally agree, in this case perhaps I know that the Metaphysics of Manuscripts class meets at the same time and has a bunch of openings. So I reply, “It’s department policy to direct students to low-enrollment classes before piling them into full courses. Since the Metaphysics of Manuscripts class is still open and has lots of slots, you should take that one instead of my course.” Thus, here’s a case in which giving you one more option actually does, in an indirect way, end up being bad for you and ends up limiting your freedom. Whereas you would have had the option to join my class from the waitlist, now you don’t. I remove that option, one you otherwise would have had, because of this other option that I know that you have.

[Slide 54] The state can cut off benefits to individuals who refuse to take personal service jobs, when available. Quiz Or, recall this point from the Anderson article about Prostitution. How does this case undermine the Harmless Option Principle? Can piece together that argument? Here’s the idea: Suppose sex work is legalized. You now have one more occupational option than you had before – a pretty unattractive option but no matter. You can just ignore it! But the government knows that you have this new option, and they may restrict some of your other choices accordingly. When you lose your job and go to collect some temporary unemployment, the government may say, We noticed you turned down that job offer for sex work. Your call, of course, but that does mean we won’t be giving you unemployment. Unemployment is for people who can’t find a job, not for people who can’t find the perfect job. Here, again, is a case in which giving you one more option actually does, in an indirect way, end up being quite bad for you and limiting your freedom. Whereas you would have been able to both collect unemployment and not take up sex work, now you can’t. The govt removes that option because they are aware that sex work was added to your list of options.

[Slide 55] Satz The H.O.P. is false. A new option can often indirectly undermine or disvalue certain other options, available to you elsewhere. IN the cases I just gave you, other people were aware that you had an extra option and as a result, they offered you fewer options in some other domain or choice situation

[Slide 56] Satz In kidney markets we could see, e.g., (i) Bank agents demanding that you put up a kidney as collateral on a loan (ii) Means-tested assistance programs counting your kidney as an asset (iii) Bankruptcy courts treating kidneys as saleable parts of one’s estate (i) So whereas before you could get a loan without needing to risk your kidney, the addition of this new option undermines your ability to get a loan – thus, you are worse off than you were before (ii) Whereas before kidney sales were legalized you would qualify for a particular assistance program (say, assistance with your monthly energy bill) now that your kidney is counted as a part of your personal wealth, you no longer qualify for the program – thus, this new option to sell a kidney has made you worse off than you were before (iii) Whereas before kidney sales were legalized you would not have to count your kidney as among your estate assets, things to be divided up among your creditors, now it is – thus, this new option to sell a kidney has made you worse off than you were before. None of these are predictions about what the future would necessarily be like! Sats isn’t saying we’d be forced allow this stuff to happen. The point is that these possibiilties undermine the Harmless Option Principle.

[Slide 57] “ Anthropologist Lawrence Cohen (2003) found that in areas of India where kidney selling was widespread, creditors placed additional pressures on those who owed them money. Cohen notes, ‘In the Tamil countryside with its kidney belts, debt is primary ... Operable women are vehicles for debt collateral . ” – Debra Satz But, nevertheless, Satz thinks these possibilty are very real. She cites some evidence from India and Sri Lanka that some of this is already going on.

[Slide 58] P1) Harmless Options Principle – Giving X one more option does not limit X’s freedom, coerce X, or make X worse off than she was before P2) Permitting kidney sales amounts to giving people one more option C1) Thus, permitting kidney sales does not make people worse off (or limit their freedom or coerce them) P3) If permitting kidney sales doesn’t make anyone worse off, kidney sales should be legal . (b/c no one is worse off, and some lives would be saved!) C2) Thus, kidney sales should be legal . The Harmless Option Argument Thus, this is Satz reply to the Radcliffe-Richards Argument. She thinks we should reject P1. (Notice, Satz position is not that we should keep kidney sales illegal. Her position is that Radcliffe-Richard’s argument fails.)

[Slide 13] Technological development is accelerating Reason #1: We know what’s happened *previously* but… is that an accurate reflection of the future? The temptation to say “We’ve always been able to find other work in the past” is dampened by the fact that the future may not much resemble the past.

[Slide 14] Technological development is accelerating Relatedly: it takes time to retrain workers to do new jobs So if tech keeps changing and at an accelerating rate, the losses due to technical change will happen too quickly for workers to find replacement work

[Slide 19] The ETU

[Slide 20] Imagine a plausible future where human workers have mostly been replaced by non-humans – such that most of us are unable to find alternative work . work =df the performance of some act/skill in return for economic reward (or hope of receiving same) Call this the, Era of Technological Unemployment or ( ETU ) Let’s suppose that’s how things actually work out.

[Slide 19] The ETU

[Slide 20] Imagine a plausible future where human workers have mostly been replaced by non-humans – such that most of us are unable to find alternative work . work =df the performance of some act/skill in return for economic reward (or hope of receiving same) Call this the, Era of Technological Unemployment or ( ETU ) Let’s suppose that’s how things actually work out.

[Slide 21] Question Would it be bad for us to live in an ETU ?

[Slide 22] An Obvious Concern That would be terrible. Everyone would be desperately poor. As things now stand, it looks pretty likely that the future will be one with lots of income inequality. All those unemployed people will wind up desperately poor – because they don’t have any income! – while the Jeff Bezos’ who displaced them wind up unimaginably rich. That would be a terrible outcome.

(i) By stipulation, the ETU is a place where everyone has enough to live, regardless of the work they do (ii) In a world where NO ONE WORKS, surely social status would be determined by and acquired through other means than one’s job. (iii) In a world where no one works, people would still be able to develop excellence, to acquire skills and hone them to perfection. They would just have more freedom to decide what skills to pursue (they won’t be locked into developing skills that are necessary for their job. I don’t think I would have bothered trying to develop that skill, if I weren’t required to for my job teaching philosophy.) (iv)
