[Slide 36] Danaher’s Argument

[Slide 37] Dahaner’s Argument (pt. 1) P1) In our era, the primary downside for people who’d prefer to not-work is that one needs to work to obtaining a number of critical human goods. P2) In an ETU , one won’t need to work to obtain those critical human goods [ P3) If the ETU eliminates the primary downside of not working, the ETU will be better for people who’d prefer to not-work] C) So in an ETU , people who’d prefer to not-work will be better off So those observations give rise to the first part of Dahaner’s argument: Think about those people who would, at the end of the day, really prefer not to work. For them, he argues, the ETU is probably better and at least no worse

[Slide 38] Dahaner’s Argument (pt. 2) P1) In an ETU , people will be able to choose to do work-like activities but won’t be forced to; in our era, people are effectively forced to. P2) All else equal, it’s better to choose to be in a circumstance, C, than to be forced into C. (even for those who’d ultimately prefer C anyway) But what about the people who DO want to work? Is the ETU bad for them? Again, Dahaner argues: No.

Combining those two sub-arguments, we get this:

This new invention, he argues, will ruin men’s minds and diminish their culture.

[Slide 5] “ The parent or inventor of an art is not always the best judge of the utility or disutility of his own inventions... This discovery of yours will create forgetfulness in the learners' souls, because they will not use their memories; they will trust to the external written characters and not remember for themselves. The technology you have discovered is an aid not to memory, but to reminiscence, and you give your disciples not truth, but only the semblance of truth; they will be hearers of many things and learners of nothing; they will be omniscient in appearance and will know nothing. ” – Plato, Phaedrus

[Slide 6] I tell this story because (A) it’s funny! And (B) it’s contains an important kernel of truth – and a nice jumping off point for what I want to talk about today.

[Slide 7] The Golden Age of Illiteracy It’s funny because Plato apparently wants us to go back to some Golden Age of Illiteracy. You know – THE GOOD TIMES: when no one could read, no one could write, nothing was written down, the only records were those we could carry around in our heads

[Slide 8] But Plato was right that the invention of writing came at a price. Writing really did replace the old oral traditions by which humans had passed down information for the past tens of thousands of years.

[Slide 23] One consequence of this unpredictability is that new tech is inherently risky . We can’t know in advance which new inventions will be good and which bad. [Slide 24] The Urn of Technologies

[Slide 25] “ One way of looking at human creativity is as a process of pulling balls out of a giant urn. The balls represent possible ideas, discoveries, technological inventions. Over the course of history, we have extracted a great many balls—mostly white (beneficial) but also various shades of grey (moderately harmful ones and mixed blessings). The cumulative effect on the human condition has so far been overwhelmingly positive, and may be much better still in the future… What we haven’t extracted, so far, is a black ball: a technology that invariably or by default destroys the civilization that invents it. The reason is not that we have been particularly careful or wise in our technology policy. We have just been lucky. ” – Nick Bostrom Bostrum offers us a helpful metaphor for thinking about this process – i.e. the process of discovering and developing and employing new technologies.

[Slide 26] White Balls Net good techs Grey Balls Net neutral to moderately bad techs Black Balls Catastrophically bad techs Bostrom – We’ve been lucky not to have pulled out a black ball. Since we cannot know what the net value of a new technology will be until after it’s invented, we won’t know whether a technology has devastating downsides until after it already exists. That we haven’t already screwed ourselves seems to be a matter of luck – that is, the result of processes outside of our control.

[Slide 27] “ ... to make the metaphor more realistic [we might] imagine that there are strings or elastic bands between some of the balls, so that when we pull on one of them we drag along several others to which it is linked. [Also] presumably the urn is highly tubular, since certain technologies must emerge before others can be reached (we are not likely to find a society that uses [both] jet planes and flint axes). The metaphor would also become more realistic if we imagine that there is not just one hand daintily exploring the urn: instead, picture a crowd of scuffling prospectors shoving in their arms in hopes of gold and glory and citations. ” – Nick Bostrom It’s not a perfect metaphor, obviously.

[Slide 28] What makes a technology a “ black ball ”?

[Slide 29] Quiz Imagine a possible world where nuclear weapons turn out to be way easier and cheaper to make than they are in our world. There, the process of engineering a nuclear bomb requires no more than a Physics Minor’s worth of education, costs about $10 in materials, and takes a couple hours to construct. This technology is discovered by accident and multiple times, by different people in different countries. Information about these weapons can be found with a determined bit of Google searching. This is an example of black ball technology. QUIZ QUESTION: Why?

[Slide 30] This world is going to have a hard time avoiding catastrophe In OUR world, making a nuke multiple Physics PhDs, many many millions of dollars, and multiple years to make. It’s so hard to make a nuke in our world that only a handful of countries have managed it – and NOT for lack of trying on the part of other countries and groups. But in the Easy Nukes world, literally hundreds of thousands – maybe millions – of individuals are in a position to build their own nukes. Think about how hard it was to stop the world from ending when, like, 6 countries had access to nukes. Now imagine how hard it would be if 6 million people had access to nukes. They’re screwed, probably. It’s hard to see a way out for Easy Nukes world that doesn’t end VERY BADLY

[Slide 32] How might the world in Easy Nukes prevent catastrophe? Quiz Think about this for a second. Suppose the cat is already out of the bag: information about how to make easy nukes is widely available and it’s basically impossible at this point to scrub it from the internet. (When has anyone ever successfully scrubbed information from the internet?) What sorts of desperate measures might societies adopt to prevent the world from going down in flames?

[Slide 31] What makes a technology a “ black ball ”? It’s a function of 2 factors, (1) the tech’s inherent dangerousness (2) the social conditions of the societies that have access to it

[Slide 32] How might the world in Easy Nukes prevent catastrophe? Quiz Think about this for a second. Suppose the cat is already out of the bag: information about how to make easy nukes is widely available and it’s basically impossible at this point to scrub it from the internet. (When has anyone ever successfully scrubbed information from the internet?) What sorts of desperate measures might societies adopt to prevent the world from going down in flames?

[Slide 33] We might prevent catastrophe … with preventative policing. Here are Bostrom’s suggestions: If we can anticipate people who want to misuse easy nukes, then we can stop worldwide destruction. (So in the movie Minority Report, there are these like magic beings who have visions of crimes before they happen. Then the state arrests the perp before the crime is ever committed. That’s not how predictive policing would likely work in our world – we don’t have any floaty magic 8-Ball people. We’d probably have to create some really extreme sort of surveillance state.)

[Slide 34] We might prevent catastrophe … with preventative policing. with a powerful global government. Bostrum also suggests that we’d need some sort of international organization with sufficient power to actually enforce policing decision in all countries around the world. After all, it’s not enough for the UNITED STATES to stamp out all use of Easy Nukes. That won’t save the world. We need to be able to prevent all people in all nations from misusing easy nukes. The easiest way to do that is with a global govt.

After all, it’s not enough for the UNITED STATES to stamp out all use of Easy Nukes. That won’t save the world. We need to be able to prevent all people in all nations from misusing easy nukes. [Slide 35] We might prevent catastrophe … with preventative policing. with a powerful global government. with a homogenized populace ( no evil actors ). A final way Bostrom suggests would be to eliminate the possibility of bad actors. Part of what’s so worrying about the easy nukes world is that there are lots of religious and political extremists in the world, people with really strange world views who actually would want to destroy the world with nukes. How do you stop those people? How do you prevent those thousands of evil actors from ruining it for everyone? Well, we might try some sort of dystopian social conditioning. If we eliminate religious and political extremism, we could close that hole in our vulnerability. But how do you eliminate extremism? Idk. Some sort of overbearing social policies, genetic tampering, or brainwashing.

[Slide 36] What makes a technology a “ black ball ”? It’s a function of 2 factors, (1) the tech’s inherent dangerousness (2) the social conditions of the societies that have access to it semi-anarchic default condition ( SADC ) =df a global political situation in which (a) we aren’t good at preventing crimes (b) there’s no global government, and (c) there are some extremists. The ABSENCE of these 3 social features (i.e. preventative policies, strong global govt, homogeneous population) is what Bostrum refers to as the semi-anarchic default condition This is the way the world is now. This is the way the world has always been so far. We’ve never been good at preventative policing. We’ve never had a strong global government. We’ve never been free of extremism.

[Slide 37] “ Let us introduce the hypothesis that the urn of creativity contains at least one black ball. We can refer to this as the ​ Vulnerable World Hypothesis ​ . Intuitively, the hypothesis is that there is some level of technology at which civilization almost certainly gets destroyed unless quite extraordinary and historically unprecedented degrees of preventive policing and/or global governance are implemented... It is ​not​ a primary purpose of this paper to argue VWH is true… I regard that as an open question, though it would seem to me unreasonable, given the available evidence, to be at all confident that VWH is ​false​ .) ” – Nick Bostrom

[Slide 38] Bostrom’s Thesis VWH – If we continue to develop new techn ologies and remain in the semi-anarchic default condition , we’ll eventually pull out a black ball . We should take seriously the Vulnerable World Hypothesis . black ball = a new technology that (a) kills off >15% of the global pop., o r (b) causes global GDP to drop by >50% SADC =df a global political situation in which (a) we aren’t good at preventing crimes (b) there’s no global government, and (c) there are some extremists. I should note: Obviously that definition of Black Ball is arbitrarily precise. There’s no particular reason to draw the line at 15% of the global population. But that’s no problem. Bostrum just wants us to think about black balls as technologies that devastate civilization – not necessary destroy them permanently. That’s why he’s drawn those lines where he has. Losing 15% of the global pop would be devastating. That’s like 1 out of every 7 people you know dying Likewise, a global economy cut in half would be devastating.

[Slide 39] A suggested taxonomy of black balls Bostrum suggests

[Slide 40] Type 1 | Ammo for Extremists A technology so destructive and easy to acquire that bad actors are likely to use it to devastate civilization. EX| Easy, devastating weapon Deadly biological agent Vulnerable system/economic lynchpin ??? (i) the easy nukes case is an example of a Type 1 black ball. (ii) if we discovered or invented some super-virus – a disease that spreads quickly and can kill hundreds of millions – that would be a Type 1 black ball. The concern would be that just one instance in which that virus fell into the wrong hands would prob be enough for catastrophe (iii) another kind of Type 1 black ball could involve some system that is critical to the global economy and also really vulnerable to sabotage. If, say, there were some easy way to bring down the global banking industry. Or some easy way to destroy all the world’s wheat and rice crops. Or some easy way to bring down the internet. Any of those cases would be Type 1 vulnerabilities.

[Slide 39] A suggested taxonomy of black balls Bostrum suggests

[Slide 40] Type 1 | Ammo for Extremists A technology so destructive and easy to acquire that bad actors are likely to use it to devastate civilization. EX| Easy, devastating weapon Deadly biological agent Vulnerable system/economic lynchpin ??? (i) the easy nukes case is an example of a Type 1 black ball. (ii) if we discovered or invented some super-virus – a disease that spreads quickly and can kill hundreds of millions – that would be a Type 1 black ball. The concern would be that just one instance in which that virus fell into the wrong hands would prob be enough for catastrophe (iii) another kind of Type 1 black ball could involve some system that is critical to the global economy and also really vulnerable to sabotage. If, say, there were some easy way to bring down the global banking industry. Or some easy way to destroy all the world’s wheat and rice crops. Or some easy way to bring down the internet. Any of those cases would be Type 1 vulnerabilities.

[Slide 41] Type 2a | The Threatened State Powerful actors have access to a devastating tech and have strong incentives to use it EX| A safe, first strike between super-powers A defensive, desperate strike by a small state A protectionist strike by an economic coalition ??? Type 1 black balls are technologies that extremists might use to destroy the world. Type 2a black balls don’t require extremists. These are devastative technologies that could be used by basically rational agents – i.e. people who aren’t crazy and don’t have bizarre world views. In particular, Type 2a black balls involve superpowers, threatened states, or threatened super-groups. Basically, there could be cases where one of these groups has strong incentives to employ a technology defensively, but where doing so would lead to global devastation. At the beg. of the Cold War, the U.S and the Soviets had little ability to counter-strike if hit by a nuclear missile. Basically, whoever had nuked the other first would have won the Cold War AND would have emerged relatively unscathed. That gave both countries an incentive to strike first. (since that was the best way to guarantee safety) Suppose the U.S. is militarily dominating Ecuador. If E. has access to a deadly pathogen – and if they can innoculate their own population – releasing that might be their best bet to survive the war Suppose, like, OPEC is worried about Nuclear Fusion coming online.They might have good reason to cause nuclear meltdowns in all the fusion reactors to protect their economic dominance of the energy market.

[Slide 42] Type 2b | Supersize Tragedy of the Commons A technology that lots of individuals have incentives to adopt but that, in the aggregate, causes devastation. Type 2b black balls are also about incentives. Type 2a black balls involve technologies that non-crazy big states might have incentives to use. Type 2b techs are ones that lots of normal, non-extremist individuals might have incentives to use. There could be some technologies that are only devastating when millions or billions of individuals use them. That’s a classic Tragedy of the Commons scenario – just one where the stakes are absurdly high. So let us take a detour and talk about Tragedies of the Commons.

[Slide 43] SIDEBAR – Tragedies of the Commons

[Slide 44] Tragedy of the Commons Members of a pop. face some choice ( to X or not to X ) such that, (a) a very bad collective outcome (O) results from lots of people X-ing , (b) each individual does better for herself by X-ing , (c) no individual thinks her decision determines whether O occurs. Less formally, a tragedy of the commons is a situation in which each individual has self-interested incentives to X , but bad things happen when everyone X’s. [Slide 44] Tragedy of the Commons Members of a pop. face some choice ( to X or not to X ) such that, (a) a very bad collective outcome (O) results from lots of people X-ing , (b) each individual does better for herself by X-ing , (c) no individual thinks her decision determines whether O occurs. Less formally, a tragedy of the commons is a situation in which each individual has self-interested incentives to X , but bad things happen when everyone X’s. In the abstract, a Tragedy of the Commons ar

[Slide 45] X = Precautionary Antibiotic Use O = Antibiotic-resistant bacteria Members of a pop. face some choice (to use Antibiotics every time they feel sick) but a very bad collective outcome will result if lots of people do that. (namely, antibiotic resistant bacteria emerge and lots of people die from bacterial infections that would previously have been preventable). What makes this a tragedy of the commons is that: (a) each individual does better for herself by using antibiotics frivolously (they may occasionally prevent an illness and they get peace of mind.) (b) no individual thinks that her decision determines whether antibiotic resistant bacteria emerge and kill lots of people. (antibiotic resistance only emerges as the macro-level when millions of people are doing this.)

[Slide 46] Others mostly X Others mostly abstain from X-ing You X You abstain from X-ing Tragedy of the Commons This is how a decision theorist might represent this situation. There are four different outcomes for you, the individual. RANK THESE OUTCOMES

(b) no individual thinks that her decision determines whether antibiotic resistant bacteria emerge and kill lots of people. (antibiotic resistance only emerges as the macro-level when millions of people are doing this.)

[Slide 55] A technology that lots of individuals have incentives to adopt but that in the aggregate causes devastation. EX| Super global warming Anti-vaxxing + super-pathogen Robot servant uprising ??? Type 2b | Supersize Tragedy of the Commons (i) Imagine a new technology that causes a climate problem like global warming but worse. Everyone’s individual contributions to the problem from using the tech are small but the net result is VERY BAD – much worse than the net results predicted for global warming (ii) Or imagine there’s some terrible disease ravaging the world (like, at least 10X worse than Covid), and there’s a vaccine but that the vaccine is a little dangerous. That might turn into a tragedy of the commons. If each individual would prefer not to get the vaccine (because its a little dangerous) that may be the rational thing for them to do – the one individual doesn’t contribute much to herd immunity – but the net result would be very bad. (iii) Or suppose we invent servant robots. Maybe there’s a small chance that they turn on us in a massive global uprising. Each individual may reason, I do best when I have a robot servant, even though my servant will add to the horde of evil robots in the case of an uprising. But what’s one more evil robot among millions? My choice doesn’t affect the outcome much.

[Slide 56] Type 0 | Some Big Oops A technology that carries a hidden risk and unexpectedly causes civilizational devastation EX| Unexpectedly dangerous physics experiment Malign (or accidentally zealous) A.I. Biology lab accident ??? Finally, the last category is just black holes that we don’t see coming. Technologies that seem innocent but then accidentally destroy the world. (i) Back when the Large Hadron Collider was being built, people speculated that it might create a mini-black hole and swallow up the earth. That’s the kind of outcome Bostrum has in mind for Type O black balls. (ii) Some people have worried that this could happen if we ever developed a truly intelligent AI. If we messed up the programing, we might end up with a Superintelligence run amok, who seizes control of the economy b/c it’s been told to make paperclips – and damn it, it needs more resources to make paperclips. (iii) Other people have worried about biological researchers accidentally creating a super-pathogen in the lab. It gets out, kils millions. (iv) And, look, it’s hard to predict Type 0 black balls BY DEFINITION. This is a category of suprises.

[Slide 57] Responses to the VWH

[Slide 58] VWH – If we continue to develop new tech and remain in the SADC , we’ll eventually pull out a black ball . We seem to have 4 options, (1) Stop developing new technologies (2) Exit the SADC (w/ dystopian social policies!) (3) Show that the VWH is clearly false (4) Do nothing

[Slide 59] VWH – If we continue to develop new tech and remain in the SADC , we’ll eventually pull out a black ball . We seem to have 4 options, (1) Stop developing new technologies (2) Exit the SADC (w/ dystopian social policies!) (3) Show that the VWH is clearly false (4) Do nothing Quiz BUT if you think about it, Option 1 actally just collapses into – or requires – Option 2. Hopefully you can already sorta make out why that is. Why?

[Slide 60] VWH – If we continue to develop new tech and remain in the SADC , we’ll eventually pull out a black ball . We seem to have 4 options, (1) Stop developing new technologies (2) Exit the SADC (w/ dystopian social policies!) (3) Show that the VWH is clearly false (4) Do nothing But of course WE CANT SIMPLY STOP PEOPLE FROM DEVELOPING NEW TECH! The only way to literally and permanently stop technological progress would be by force: either by preventative policing, or with a strong global government, or maybe with some sort of social conditioning (to prevent people from being interested in science and progress) Thus, Option 1 actually just collapses into Option 2. Also, we should ask: how do we prevent all 4 varieties of black ball? It’s not enough only to prevent Type 1 or Type 0 black balls. We need to prevent them all!

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 1] (Bad) Responses to the Problem of Evil

[Slide 2] Recap Three (bad) responses God has a plan Without the bad, we couldn’t appreciate the good The original sin doctrine

[Slide 3] The shorthand version of the problem of evil is simply this, If the 3-O God exists, then why is the world so full of suffering?

[Slide 4] Some kinds of suffering are necessary . The problem of evil is about unnecessary suffering . Suffering is unnecessary when it, (a) doesn’t prevent some greater bad AND (b) doesn’t secure a greater good. Recall this VERY IMPORTANT distinction between necessary suffering and unnecessary suffering. The Problem of Evil is only concerned with Unnecessary Suffering. (Thus, the existence of vaccines or dentists or exercise is not being used as evidence against the existence of god.)

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 3] The shorthand version of the problem of evil is simply this, If the 3-O God exists, then why is the world so full of suffering?

[Slide 4] Some kinds of suffering are necessary . The problem of evil is about unnecessary suffering . Suffering is unnecessary when it, (a) doesn’t prevent some greater bad AND (b) doesn’t secure a greater good. Recall this VERY IMPORTANT distinction between necessary suffering and unnecessary suffering. The Problem of Evil is only concerned with Unnecessary Suffering. (Thus, the existence of vaccines or dentists or exercise is not being used as evidence against the existence of god.)

[Slide 5] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil And here’s the full argument.

[Slide 6] Responding to the Problem of Evil

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 5] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil And here’s the full argument.

[Slide 6] Responding to the Problem of Evil

[Slide 7] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C The argument is valid, so we really only have 3 options. We can Reject P1, arguing that a 3-O God actually would allow unnecessary suffering We can Reject P2, arguing that there actually isn’t any unnecessary suffering Or We can Accept C, acknowledging that the 3-O God does not exist.

[Slide 8] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option To my knowledge, almost no one has tried to argue that P1 is false. It just… looks very obviously correct, to both theists and atheists. How could an all-powerful, all-knowing, perfectly good being possibly allow unnecessary suffering? And of course, most theists aren’t going to like the third option. They want to continue believing in a 3-O God. Thus, we’re left with Option 2: we have to find a way to reject P2. We must argue, There has never been – not even once – an instance of unnecessary suffering.

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 7] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C The argument is valid, so we really only have 3 options. We can Reject P1, arguing that a 3-O God actually would allow unnecessary suffering We can Reject P2, arguing that there actually isn’t any unnecessary suffering Or We can Accept C, acknowledging that the 3-O God does not exist.

[Slide 8] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option To my knowledge, almost no one has tried to argue that P1 is false. It just… looks very obviously correct, to both theists and atheists. How could an all-powerful, all-knowing, perfectly good being possibly allow unnecessary suffering? And of course, most theists aren’t going to like the third option. They want to continue believing in a 3-O God. Thus, we’re left with Option 2: we have to find a way to reject P2. We must argue, There has never been – not even once – an instance of unnecessary suffering.

[Slide 9] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture And here, let me offer a shortlist of candidates for instances of unnecessary evil. Again, in order to succeed, the argument only need for there to have been ONE SINGLE TIME IN THE HISTORY OF THE WORLD WHERE THERE WAS UNNECESSARY SUFFERING So even if it turns out that most of the items on this list are instances of Necessary suffering, the argument will still go through. The theists tasks is to tackle the whole list. (Tay-sachs is a genetic nerve disorder that manifests at around 6-months. The child experiences really offer nerve problems for a few years and then dies. Every time – – often by around age 4. A really awful disease.)

[Slide 10] The conversation goes like this… Atheist: “What about X? Doesn’t X count as unnecessary suffering?” Theist: “No. Plausibly, we need X in order to prevent <greater evil> or to secure <greater good.>” Atheist: “But what about Y? Doesn’t Y count as unnecessary suffering?” Theist: “No. Plausibly, we need Y…” This is a theodicy. In English, we have a special word for this kind of story. It’s called a “theodicy”. A theodicy is an explanation that a certain variety of suffering is actually necessary (and, thus, isn’t evidence against the existence of God.)

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 9] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture And here, let me offer a shortlist of candidates for instances of unnecessary evil. Again, in order to succeed, the argument only need for there to have been ONE SINGLE TIME IN THE HISTORY OF THE WORLD WHERE THERE WAS UNNECESSARY SUFFERING So even if it turns out that most of the items on this list are instances of Necessary suffering, the argument will still go through. The theists tasks is to tackle the whole list. (Tay-sachs is a genetic nerve disorder that manifests at around 6-months. The child experiences really offer nerve problems for a few years and then dies. Every time – – often by around age 4. A really awful disease.)

[Slide 10] The conversation goes like this… Atheist: “What about X? Doesn’t X count as unnecessary suffering?” Theist: “No. Plausibly, we need X in order to prevent <greater evil> or to secure <greater good.>” Atheist: “But what about Y? Doesn’t Y count as unnecessary suffering?” Theist: “No. Plausibly, we need Y…” This is a theodicy. In English, we have a special word for this kind of story. It’s called a “theodicy”. A theodicy is an explanation that a certain variety of suffering is actually necessary (and, thus, isn’t evidence against the existence of God.)

[Slide 11] What about ass hair ? Is ass hair an example of unnecessary suffering? Theodicy: No. Body hair is useful in areas that are hard to keep dry. We need ass hair (as we need armpit hair) to wick sweat away from spots that doesn’t get much air exposure. We did a little practice with this last lecture, but it’s worth trying our hand at it again. So, here’s an example. Maybe the atheist says, “Look at ass hair! It’s kinda gross. It’s kinda messy. It’s an extra place to shed from. No one really likes it. Is ass hair an instance of unnecessary suffering.” I don’t know if that’s true of course, but the point is that it’s plausible. This is a reasonably satisfying story about the necessity of ass hair. I’m content to let this one go – to not insist that ass hair is evidence that God doesn’t exist.

[Slide 12] What about nausea ? Is nausea an example of unnecessary suffering? Theodicy: No. We need the bad-feeling of nausea to (a) strongly disincentivize the ingestion of poisons and (b) to warn us about an eminent vomiting episode. Quiz Nausea feels terrible. We might wonder, “Wouldn’t the world be better w/o it? Why does God allow nausea?” Think about that for a second. The thought is: nausea is needed to prevent a greater evil – accidental poisoning. I don’t know if that’s true of course, but the point is that it’s plausible. This is a reasonably satisfying story about the necessity of nausea. I’m content to let this one go – to not insist that nausea is evidence that God doesn’t exist.

content:
[Slide 11] What about ass hair ? Is ass hair an example of unnecessary suffering? Theodicy: No. Body hair is useful in areas that are hard to keep dry. We need ass hair (as we need armpit hair) to wick sweat away from spots that doesn’t get much air exposure. We did a little practice with this last lecture, but it’s worth trying our hand at it again. So, here’s an example. Maybe the atheist says, “Look at ass hair! It’s kinda gross. It’s kinda messy. It’s an extra place to shed from. No one really likes it. Is ass hair an instance of unnecessary suffering.” I don’t know if that’s true of course, but the point is that it’s plausible. This is a reasonably satisfying story about the necessity of ass hair. I’m content to let this one go – to not insist that ass hair is evidence that God doesn’t exist.

[Slide 12] What about nausea ? Is nausea an example of unnecessary suffering? Theodicy: No. We need the bad-feeling of nausea to (a) strongly disincentivize the ingestion of poisons and (b) to warn us about an eminent vomiting episode. Quiz Nausea feels terrible. We might wonder, “Wouldn’t the world be better w/o it? Why does God allow nausea?” Think about that for a second. The thought is: nausea is needed to prevent a greater evil – accidental poisoning. I don’t know if that’s true of course, but the point is that it’s plausible. This is a reasonably satisfying story about the necessity of nausea. I’m content to let this one go – to not insist that nausea is evidence that God doesn’t exist.

[Slide 13] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture I should make one important note before we look at some theodicies: The challenge for the theist isn’t merely to show that SOME war or SOME disease is necessary. That’s not enough. The Problem of Evil says that “there has been at least one instance of necessary suffering.” That’s it. At least one. So consider torture. Sometimes people will respond to this case by arguing that sometimes torture is the right thing to do to prevent a greater evil. But… so what? Even if that’s true, that’s not nearly enough. To remove torture from this list, the theist has to show that every act of torture that’s ever been committed by anyone anywhere was a necessary evil. Which… yeah. That’s gonna be hard. There’s been a lot of torture-for-fun in the history of mankind. Gonna be hard to explain those cases away as necessary evil. And of course, similar remarks apply to our other examples. The theists needs to explain ALL wars, ALL genocides, ALL natural disasters.

[Slide 14] Three bad theodicies I want to address some very common (and pretty terrible) replies.

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 13] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture I should make one important note before we look at some theodicies: The challenge for the theist isn’t merely to show that SOME war or SOME disease is necessary. That’s not enough. The Problem of Evil says that “there has been at least one instance of necessary suffering.” That’s it. At least one. So consider torture. Sometimes people will respond to this case by arguing that sometimes torture is the right thing to do to prevent a greater evil. But… so what? Even if that’s true, that’s not nearly enough. To remove torture from this list, the theist has to show that every act of torture that’s ever been committed by anyone anywhere was a necessary evil. Which… yeah. That’s gonna be hard. There’s been a lot of torture-for-fun in the history of mankind. Gonna be hard to explain those cases away as necessary evil. And of course, similar remarks apply to our other examples. The theists needs to explain ALL wars, ALL genocides, ALL natural disasters.

[Slide 14] Three bad theodicies I want to address some very common (and pretty terrible) replies.

[Slide 15] All suffering is a necessary part of God’s plan This is often the first theodicy you hear from people. It’s also distinctively bad.

[Slide 16] Atheist: “What about genocide? Isn’t that an example of unnecessary evil?” Theist: “Even genocides are a part of God’s plan.” Atheist: “I wasn’t asking whether God has a plan; I was asking whether it’s any good . Cuz it looks kinda shitty!” Theist: “Well it’s God’s Plan. So it’s morally perfect.” Atheist: “I don’t think ‘ morally perfect ’ aptly describes any plan that signs off on genocide.” Theist: “Maybe we simply can’t understand the plan.” The God’s Plan Theodicy Quiz One way to see what’s defective about this theodicy is by imagining what it would sound like to defend it. Let me have you try this one first: Why is this an unsatisfying response to the Problem of Evil?

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 15] All suffering is a necessary part of God’s plan This is often the first theodicy you hear from people. It’s also distinctively bad.

[Slide 16] Atheist: “What about genocide? Isn’t that an example of unnecessary evil?” Theist: “Even genocides are a part of God’s plan.” Atheist: “I wasn’t asking whether God has a plan; I was asking whether it’s any good . Cuz it looks kinda shitty!” Theist: “Well it’s God’s Plan. So it’s morally perfect.” Atheist: “I don’t think ‘ morally perfect ’ aptly describes any plan that signs off on genocide.” Theist: “Maybe we simply can’t understand the plan.” The God’s Plan Theodicy Quiz One way to see what’s defective about this theodicy is by imagining what it would sound like to defend it. Let me have you try this one first: Why is this an unsatisfying response to the Problem of Evil?

[Slide 17] The God’s Plan Theodicy Objection #1 This isn’t a solution. It’s a restatement of the problem. Objection #2 This theodicy commits some circular reasoning . The problem is 2-fold This theodicy isn’t an explanation; it’s just a restatement of the question but posed as an answer. It’s true that if the 3-O God exists, then her plan is perfect and all suffering is necessary. But that’s not a reason to think that the 3-O God does exist. The question is Can we make sense of all the suffering in the world as necessary? And this theodicy amounts to the answer, No. As best as we can tell, God does not exist. We are too stupid to understand his plan, which is why the world clearly appears to include lots of unnecessary suffeirng. That’s not solving the problem! That’s quitting! (also, isn’t our stupidity then ANOTHER example of unnecessary evil? SUrely a 3-O God would have made us smart enough to understand her morally perfect plan?) This theodicy is circular. “Is God’s plan a good plan?” Yes it must be because God is morally perfect. “Why should we think God is morally perfect?” Just look at the evidence! Everything we see is in accordance with God’s plan. “But what we see is suffering. So our evidence suggests it’s a bad plan.” No it’s a good plan! It’s God’s plan and She’s morally perfect! And so on.

[Slide 18] Without suffering, we couldn’t appreciate the good stuff Here’s a different theodicy. In fact, this is a lot of people’s second reaction to hearing the problem of evil. “But don’t you need the bad with the good? We need a rich variety of experiences to have a full and happy life.”

The question is Can we make sense of all the suffering in the world as necessary? And this theodicy amounts to the answer, No. As best as we can tell, God does not exist. We are too stupid to understand his plan, which is why the world clearly appears to include lots of unnecessary suffeirng. That’s not solving the problem! That’s quitting! (also, isn’t our stupidity then ANOTHER example of unnecessary evil? SUrely a 3-O God would have made us smart enough to understand her morally perfect plan?) This theodicy is circular. “Is God’s plan a good plan?” Yes it must be because God is morally perfect. “Why should we think God is morally perfect?” Just look at the evidence! Everything we see is in accordance with God’s plan. “But what we see is suffering. So our evidence suggests it’s a bad plan.” No it’s a good plan! It’s God’s plan and She’s morally perfect! And so on.

[Slide 18] Without suffering, we couldn’t appreciate the good stuff Here’s a different theodicy. In fact, this is a lot of people’s second reaction to hearing the problem of evil. “But don’t you need the bad with the good? We need a rich variety of experiences to have a full and happy life.”

[Slide 19] Objection #1 There’s an important difference between some evil is necessary to enjoy the good & all this evil is necessary to enjoy the good

[Slide 20] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture Look again at our list of unnecessary evil candidates. Think about how stupid it sounds to say something like, “Without Alzheimer’s, we wouldn’t be able to value the good in life!” OR “If baby deer didn’t sometimes burn to death in the forest, we couldn’t appreciate the finer things!” OR “How can we truly hold dear our friends and family if they don’t sometimes get tortured in prison or murdered in race-based pogroms?”

[Slide 22] Imagine that you have been presented with a lovely cake. It is your favorite variety of cake. You adore this cake. You love it so much you should marry it. Close your eyes, and I want you to imagine, in detail, how very good this cake is. It is moist, and rich, and sweet but not too sweet, and just so utterly without equal, as far as cakes go. Got it? Now: Did you know that, between 1983 and1985, approx 150,00 to 200,000 East Timorese were killed by Indonesian forces in what is now generally regarded as a campaign of genocide? Ok! Back to cake! Are you imagining it again? Is it any better now that you have a more complete grasp of the depth of the world’s suffering?

content:

[Slide 24] All suffering is the result of Original Sin One final theodicy you commonly hear goes like this. “Here’s why all suffering is necessary: it’s not really God’s doing at all…”

[Slide 25] Objection #1 How does this even help us understand the moral perfection of God’s plan? Objection #2 If anything, this response makes human suffering sound positively unjust . The doctrine of Original Sin isn’t the right sort of story. It tells us what caused there to be suffering. It doesn’t tell us why all the suffering is justified. It doesn’t make sense to say “All suffering is necessary because of Adam and Eve and the apple.” If we try to force the doctrine of Original Sin into the right sort of story (i.e. into a justification of suffering) the result is genuinely repulsive. It says, it is morally perfect for all humanity to suffer and suffer and suffer because one time, thousands of years ago, a couple people did a bad thing with some fruit. Since that bad thing, is has been morally perfect for God to stand back and watch the genocides.”

[Slide 26] This makes Original Sin sounds like North Korea’s 3 Generations Policy , which says: If one person is found guilty of a crime and sent to a prison camp, so is (a) their entire family and (b) all future descendants for 3 generations. I don’t think we make God look morally perfect by describing Her as very similar in governing style to North Korea.

[Slide 1] Objections to the Fine-tuning Argument

[Slide 2] Review of the Fine tuning Argument Three Objections The God-of-the-gaps Objection + A Reply The Anthropic Principle Objection + A Reply The Multiverse Objection + A Reply

[Slide 3] Recall: the universe was compared to a pocketwatch – something intricate and delicate that almost certainly wouldn’t come to be by natural accident.

[Slide 4] P1) Fine tuning : The laws of the universe could have been a little different, and if they had been, the universe would be uninhabitable P2) Evidence Principle : Take two competing hypotheses, H 1 and H 2 . An observation, O, is strong evidence for H 1 when O is far more likely under H 1 than under H 2 (i.e. when O is far more likely in an H 1 -world than an H 2 -world) P3) Fine-tuning is far more likely under H 1 (H 1 = God exists) than under H 2 (H 2 = God does not exist) C) So, fine-tuning is strong evidence for the existence of God. The Fine-tuning Argument And here’s the full argument. P1 is an observation from physics. P2 tells us how we should assess observations as evidence P3 tells us how this observation compares, given two different hypotheses – that God exists or doesn’t.

[Slide 3] Recall: the universe was compared to a pocketwatch – something intricate and delicate that almost certainly wouldn’t come to be by natural accident.

[Slide 4] P1) Fine tuning : The laws of the universe could have been a little different, and if they had been, the universe would be uninhabitable P2) Evidence Principle : Take two competing hypotheses, H 1 and H 2 . An observation, O, is strong evidence for H 1 when O is far more likely under H 1 than under H 2 (i.e. when O is far more likely in an H 1 -world than an H 2 -world) P3) Fine-tuning is far more likely under H 1 (H 1 = God exists) than under H 2 (H 2 = God does not exist) C) So, fine-tuning is strong evidence for the existence of God. The Fine-tuning Argument And here’s the full argument. P1 is an observation from physics. P2 tells us how we should assess observations as evidence P3 tells us how this observation compares, given two different hypotheses – that God exists or doesn’t.

[Slide 18] The Anthropic Principle Objection

[Slide 19] It’s THE FUTURE. 100 years ago, the Earth underwent a nuclear annihilation event, and now Cleveland is the only place fit for human habitation. [Slide 20] You wake up from a coma. Whoa, how are you still alive? H1: God protected you ! H2: You got lucky. Framing the case as we did last time, we can state our two hypotheses like this:

[Slide 19] It’s THE FUTURE. 100 years ago, the Earth underwent a nuclear annihilation event, and now Cleveland is the only place fit for human habitation. (Literally, leaving Cleveland will kill you in seconds.) This is all common knowledge You wake up one day with no recollection of your past or present situation except that there was a bad accident. Whoa! How are you still alive? You reason, Either I got lucky or God is personally protecting me!

[Slide 20] You wake up from a coma. Whoa, how are you still alive? H1: God protected you ! H2: You got lucky. Framing the case as we did last time, we can state our two hypotheses like this:

[Slide 21] Observation: You’re in Cleveland! The only safe place. H2: You got lucky. Quiz H1: God protected you ! You ask someone where you are, and they tell you that you’re in Cleveland. You think: “Wow! 99.99…% of the earth is uninhabitable. Basically, nowhere is safe. But here I am! In Cleveland! The only safe place. What are the chances? Unimaginably small. That demands explanation. The only plausible explanation is that God exists and is personally protecting me.” This is a example of bad reasoning. Why?

[Slide 22] Given that you’re alive, what are the chances you’d find alive yourself in Cleveland? If H1 is true? If H2 is true? If H1 is true: 100% – God can’t set you down anywhere but cleveland. So if God’s protected you, you’re basically guaranteed to find yourself in Cleveland If H2 is true: 100% – If God’s not protecting you, then again, there’s only one place where you can be alive: Cleveland. All living people are in Cleveland, so given that you’re alive, you must be too. So the observation that you’re in Cleveland isn’t evidence for either hypothesis. All living beings are in Cleveland; you exist, therefore you are in Cleveland. So you don’t learn anything new when you learn that you are in Cleveland, and you certainly don’t get important evidence that God is watching out for you.

[Slide 21] Observation: You’re in Cleveland! The only safe place. H2: You got lucky. Quiz H1: God protected you ! You ask someone where you are, and they tell you that you’re in Cleveland. [Slide 23] Being in a fine-tuned universe is like discovering that you’re in Cleveland. Since only Cleveland supports life, all observers are in Cleveland. Since only fine-tuned universes support life, all observers are in fine-tuned universes. The Anthropic Principle Objection This is known as the anthropic principle: Since all possible observers exist in life-supporting universes, all observers will find that they live in a life-supporting universe.

[Slide 24] What are the chances that we’d find ourselves in a finely-tuned universe? 100%! The fine-tuning of the universe is not evidence that God exists It has to be fine-tuned. (since we’re alive in it!) The Anthropic Principle Objection The Fine-tuner asks:... This objection replies:...

[Slide 26] What’s our evidence? The universe we’re in supports life. OR There is a universe that supports life. Quiz The Anthropic Principle Objection The problem with the Anthropic Principle Objection is that it’s done a sneaky little bait-n-switch. We have to very careful about restating what exactly is being claimed by the original Fine-tuning Argument. [Slide 25] Reply – This Objection misstates our evidence. The Anthropic Principle Objection Does this objection succeed? Well in this case, the proponent has a very plausible rejoinder. And that is that the Anthropic Princ Obj mistates our evidence

[Slide 26] What’s our evidence? The universe we’re in supports life. OR There is a universe that supports life. Quiz The Anthropic Principle Objection The problem with the Anthropic Principle Objection is that it’s done a sneaky little bait-n-switch. We have to very careful about restating what exactly is being claimed by the original Fine-tuning Argument. QUIZ: Which of these is the evidence that the proponent of the Fine-tuning argument is trying to point to?

[Slide 27] What’s our evidence? The universe we’re in supports life. OR There is a universe that supports life. This isn’t surprising. This is. The Anthropic Principle Objection The first claim isnt surprising. OF COURSE, the universe that we’re in supports life. We’re in it! We are life! Thus, our universe must support us. But that’s not the claim at issue. The second claim is the one that’s at issue in the Fine-tuning argument – and that seems genuinely surprising in a universe that happened by accident. What’s crazy is that there is any life at all – when there might very easily have been none. Given that there’s a universe that supports life, we would of course find ourselves there. But it’s still surprinsg that there’s a universe like that.

[Slide 28] The Multiverse Objection

What’s crazy is that there is any life at all – when there might very easily have been none. Given that there’s a universe that supports life, we would of course find ourselves there. But it’s still surprinsg that there’s a universe like that.

[Slide 28] The Multiverse Objection

[Slide 29] We live in a universe. Could there be others? With different laws?

[Slide 32] To get at the objection, consider an analogy case: Imagine you have the ability to teleport. It works like this: you snap your fingers, and you disappear and reappear somewhere else. But, like, you’re not very good at it. You basically reappear at a random location in a 500 mile radius. If you snap your fingers and reappear at home, ON THE VERY FIRST TRY, that would be pretty weird, right? But what if you appeared at home after a solid year of trying? (everyday, *snap* nope *snap* nope *snap* nope.)

[Slide 31] We live in a universe. Could there be others? With different laws? Maybe there’s a multiverse like this… ANd maybe each individual universe in the multiverse has its own set of physical laws and constants

[Slide 32] To get at the objection, consider an analogy case: Imagine you have the ability to teleport. It works like this: you snap your fingers, and you disappear and reappear somewhere else. But, like, you’re not very good at it. You basically reappear at a random location in a 500 mile radius. If you snap your fingers and reappear at home, ON THE VERY FIRST TRY, that would be pretty weird, right? But what if you appeared at home after a solid year of trying? (everyday, *snap* nope *snap* nope *snap* nope.)

[Slide 33] Suppose we live in a multiverse where each universe has different constants. Is it surprising that one supports life? That depends on how many there are! The Multiverse Objection With a bajillion universes, there’s a bajillion chances to get things just right. SO if there are lots of universes each with different physical law and constants, then its not surprising that one of them supports life. And it’s not surprising that we live in that universe. Thta’s like finding yourself teleported back home after a year of failed attempts.

[Slide 34] The Fine-tuning Argument only works if we assume there’s one universe. But there might be bajillions. So, the Fine-tuning Argument fails. The Multiverse Objection

SO if there are lots of universes each with different physical law and constants, then its not surprising that one of them supports life. And it’s not surprising that we live in that universe. Thta’s like finding yourself teleported back home after a year of failed attempts.

[Slide 34] The Fine-tuning Argument only works if we assume there’s one universe. But there might be bajillions. So, the Fine-tuning Argument fails. The Multiverse Objection

[Slide 1] The Free Will Theodicy

[Slide 2] Recap The Free Will Theodicy Motivations Replies Last time we looked at 3 bad responses to the problem of evil – solutions that don’t even look plausible on their face. Today I want to talk about a much better solution: the free will theodicy. This theodicy has been historically very popular and influential.

[Slide 3] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option To my knowledge, almost no one has tried to argue that P1 is false. It just… looks very obviously correct, to both theists and atheists. How could an all-powerful, all-knowing, perfectly good being possibly allow unnecessary suffering? And of course, most theists aren’t going to like the third option. They want to continue believing in a 3-O God. Thus, we’re left with Option 2: we have to find a way to reject P2. We must argue, There has never been – not even once – an instance of unnecessary suffering.

[Slide 4] The Free Will Theodicy

[Slide 3] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option To my knowledge, almost no one has tried to argue that P1 is false. It just… looks very obviously correct, to both theists and atheists. How could an all-powerful, all-knowing, perfectly good being possibly allow unnecessary suffering? And of course, most theists aren’t going to like the third option. They want to continue believing in a 3-O God. Thus, we’re left with Option 2: we have to find a way to reject P2. We must argue, There has never been – not even once – an instance of unnecessary suffering.

[Slide 4] The Free Will Theodicy

[Slide 5] Imagine God’s position in the moment you decide to do something wrong. She can either, Let you act OR Step in and stop you What should She do? One good way to get at the free will theodicy is to imagine God’s position as she watches you choose to do something bad. (PICTURED: God watching over your shoulder as you send an unsolicited dick pic or something)

[Slide 6] It might seem like no-big-deal for God to step in. But consider for a moment what the world would look like if god always did that. Imagine a world in which God makes all of our choices for us. She picks out our outfits in the morning, decides what we’ll eat, where we’ll work, who we’ll live with, how much Netflix we’ll watch, how often we’ll sing God’s praises, etc. God decides whom we’ll love and with whom we’ll procreate – and then, of course, once our children are born, God takes full control of their lives too. Each life is, of course, morally perfect: You always do the right thing (b/c you have no choice.) You love your neighbor as yourself (b/c you have no choice.) You love God with all your heart, mind, soul, and strength (b/c you have no choice.) (think of this as the Puppetmaster world – where God is pulling all the strings)

[Slide 5] Imagine God’s position in the moment you decide to do something wrong. She can either, Let you act OR Step in and stop you What should She do? One good way to get at the free will theodicy is to imagine God’s position as she watches you choose to do something bad. (PICTURED: God watching over your shoulder as you send an unsolicited dick pic or something)

[Slide 6] It might seem like no-big-deal for God to step in. But consider for a moment what the world would look like if god always did that. Imagine a world in which God makes all of our choices for us. She picks out our outfits in the morning, decides what we’ll eat, where we’ll work, who we’ll live with, how much Netflix we’ll watch, how often we’ll sing God’s praises, etc. God decides whom we’ll love and with whom we’ll procreate – and then, of course, once our children are born, God takes full control of their lives too. Each life is, of course, morally perfect: You always do the right thing (b/c you have no choice.) You love your neighbor as yourself (b/c you have no choice.) You love God with all your heart, mind, soul, and strength (b/c you have no choice.) (think of this as the Puppetmaster world – where God is pulling all the strings)

[Slide 7] This is a defective world – both from God’s perspective and ours . Here’s the punchline: a lot of people think that this world sucks. There’s a good reason why God doesn’t make all of our decisions for us – why she doesn’t consistently intervene to make us act in ways she’d prefer. There’s little value in loving God and one another if we don’t freely choose it There’s little value in being good unless we have to struggle with evil In our puppet world, no one has the power to do make or change anything – and that’s part of why the world seems defective God should ask herself, Why even bother with humans if I’m just going to puppetmaster them?

[Slide 8] Observation There’s something really important about human freedom – about our ability to be the authors of our own decisions. This has important religious implications. It’s positively good – from God’s perspective – that humans be allowed to make decisions for themselves. It’s positively bad for God to make all our decisions for us. Hopefully you can: this Good Thing – human freedom – may give us a way to make sense of evils as necessary.

[Slide 9] What’s so great about freedom ? Free choices have weight . The s truggle to choose and the ability to do otherwise give our choices meaning. The freedom to make and re-make our world is a valuable gift. Before we look at the details of that theodicy, though, let’s spend a little more time thinking about freedom and why we might think it’s really good and importna.t Here are 3 different suggestions.

[Slide 10] Imagine a bratty child who apologizes to you only after her parents demands it. Or a movie where the hero gets saved at the last minute by God, rather than by the villain. Or a surprise Valentine’s Day card gushing about how great you are. Signed by your mom. Here are some cases that seem to support these claims about the value of free choice through struggle The point is this, these gestures aren’t very meaningful. And the reason in each case seems to be roughly the same, These actions do not represent free choices through struggle. They aren’t powerful because they aren’t backed up by the right sort of surprising, impressive freedom.

[Slide 11] The Free Will Theodicy Freedom is good because it gives our choices weight, meaning, & power. It forces us to struggle and grow. Freedom is bad because we may misuse it to do evil. Thus, God faces a choice, preserve our freedom and allow evil OR prevent evil and revoke our freedom In short, then, And we can see this puts God in a bit of pickle… The Free Will Theodicy consists of the view that the first choice is allows better. It’s always better for God to preserve our freedom and allow evil than to prevent evil and revoke our freedom

[Slide 12] The Free Will Theodicy It is consistently worse to violate a human’s free will than to allow evil. Thus, the suffering that stems from human choices is necessary suffering. More precisely, this is the view.

[Slide 13] Why doesn’t God force parents to love their children? Free Will Theodicy : She knows that chosen love is a greater good, one that can only be secured by respecting parents’ freedom. Recall a theodicy is a story that a theist tells to make sense of why some particular instances of suffering are necessary – needed for some greater good or to prevent some greater evil. So here’s an example of the free will theodicy in action.

People used to burn cats for fun.

[Slide 17] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture Look again at our list of unnecessary evil candidates. A lot of them seem to stem from human choices – and so lots of them can be explained by the free will theodicy. (Worth noting, however, the free will theodicy doesn’t obviously explain all instances of suffering – though, the Swinburne reading for this week is an attempt to show that the Free Will theodicy covers more cases that one might initially suppose. It’s not obvious that we can explain Rowe’s fawn who burns to death in the forest or the badness of Alzheimer’s by pointing to the value of human freedom. When God is deciding whether to let someone get Alzheimer’s, it’s not clear that She is deciding whether to violate someone’s freedom. Surely God could prevent disease without literally STOPPING someone from making a choice – she could just wave her hand, and all the mis-folded proteins in the person’s brain would disappear, leaving that person none the wiser.)

As we push, we’ll find that the defender of this theodicy ends up having to say some (relatively) implausible things to maintain the argument. I’ll keep track of those.

[Slide 27] Commitments of the Free Will Theodicy It’s always better to preserve a person’s freedom than to prevent harm. Again, we’re going to make a list of the troubling implications of the Free Will Theodicy, to get a sense for its costs . This our first troubling implication of the Free Will Theodicy. We seem to be committed to this claim but is it plausible?

[Slide 28] Back to Stalin. If the value of freedom lays in the struggle, Stalin’s freedom doesn’t seem very valuable. He was habitually choosing evil, making the same bad decisions day after day. Here’s a second worry about the Free Will Theodicy. That’s…. Weird. If I’m choosing something out of habit, I’m not struggling with it. Thus, we can’t point to the good of struggle when trying to explain why God allows habitual evil. But since God does allow people to be habitually evil, the Free Will Theodicy is going to have to affirm that those free choices are more valuable than the interests of the people who are harmed.

There’s no obvious trade-off here between freedom and evil prevention. It looks like a win-win for God to just make Bundy a normal person with normal moral sentiments.

He doesn’t feel the force of ethics, of basic human decency. So what value then has his free choice? THis is like the habitual choice problem but even worse. Bundy didn’t even struggle the first time. There was never a conflict! Why is that valuable? Why is that freedom worth more than 30 human lives?

[Slide 35] Back to Bundy. And couldn’t God prevent his bad choices from playing out w/o violating his freedom ? But it gets worse. First, it’s not obvious that God couldn’t make the world a better place by simply altering Bundy’s circumstances. God could have placed obstacles in such a way that Bundy simply never came across any potential victims. If he never has a convenient victim, he never chooses evil – and yet all his choices are still freely made.

[Slide 36] X freely chooses to do evil Moment 1 X does evil Moment 2 DIVINE INTERVENTION Second, it’s not even obvious (in this case or in other cases) that God really does face a trade-off between preserving our freedom and allowing evil. What’s wrong with this? Maybe God could step in at the very last instant, and say “Well, Ted, I see you’ve made your choice, you’ve exercised the freedom I’ve given you. Fine. That’s great! I love freedom. But I’m not going to stand by while you murder this person.” and then God would whisk the victim away. Freedom preserved! Evil prevented! The point being, it’s not at all obvious that God must violate our freedom in order to prevent wrongdoing. It seems like God could, in principle, step in between the free choice and the bad consequences.

[Slide 39] Suppose you had the opportunity to stop an atrocity, to step in and block the choice. You should, right? That’s a good thing to do. Quiz Imagine that you have the opportunity to stop Stalin. Or that you have the chance to tie Ted Bundy up in your basement. If so, surely you’re required to do that? If you can stop mass murders from mass murderin’, you should! You can’t simply stand by and let people commit terrible atrocities. So, quiz question: Why is this observation a serious problem for the Free Will Theodicy ?

[Slide 40] Suppose you had the opportunity to stop an atrocity, to step in and block the choice. You should, right? That’s a good thing to do. But, then… why can’t God do the same? The trouble is that we don’t seem to value Bundy’s free will when it’s us humans deciding between preventing evil and preserving freedom. We think the value of Bundy’s freedom is tiny compared to the value of human lives. ( Should we put Bundy in jail and violate his freedom or let him roam free and commit murders – keep in mind his freedom is very valuable! Is not a serious question that people entertain. Jail him everyone agrees without hesitation.) So… uh… that’s weird. Which is it? Is it better to stop evil or is it better to preserve freedom? The Free Will Theodicy seems to tell us: Both! One standard applies to God (preserve freedom!) and another standard applies to humans (prevent evil) That’s very very strange.

[Slide 3] So far we’ve looked at arguments for the existence of God.

[Slide 4] The Ontological Argument : Consider the properties that define God. Surely we must conclude that a non-existent God is impossible. Thus, God exists. The Fine-tuning Argument : Look at the universe. See how elegant and fragile it is? It seems very, very unlikely that such a thing could exist by accident. The best explanation is that the universe was carefully designed by God.

[Slide 3] So far we’ve looked at arguments for the existence of God.

[Slide 4] The Ontological Argument : Consider the properties that define God. Surely we must conclude that a non-existent God is impossible. Thus, God exists. The Fine-tuning Argument : Look at the universe. See how elegant and fragile it is? It seems very, very unlikely that such a thing could exist by accident. The best explanation is that the universe was carefully designed by God.

[Slide 5] Today we’ll look at an argument against the existence of God. (the best and most important one) You might wonder: Not fair! Two arguments for but only one against? I’m not stacking the deck, I promise. The argument we’ll look at today is A BIG DEAL. I dare say, far far more philosophers have been persuaded by today’s argument than by either of the pro-God arguments we’ve looked at.

[Slide 7] The Greatest Being God God is the greatest possible being – the being than which none greater can be conceived . This was Anselm’s definition.

[Slide 8] The Creator God God is that being who was responsible for fine-tuning the universe to support life . This was the definition at issue when we talked about the Fine-tuning argument. (notice how sparse it is! We don’t learn much about God from the Fine-tuning argument.)

ANd this is definition we’ll be using today.

[Slide 32] The shorthand version of the problem of evil is simply this, If the 3-O God exists, then why is the world so full of suffering? This observation is at the heart of the Problem of Evil: We would not expect the 3-O to create a world with lots of suffering in it. But… our world has lots of suffering in it. So… what’s up with that?

[Slide 33] P1) If the 3-O God exists, we won’t find suffering in the world. P2) We do find suffering in the world C) So, the 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil (an imperfect first pass) This isn’t quite right. Here’s a first pass at the problem of evil argument. As it happens, though, that first premise isn’t quite right. It’s too strong.

[Slide 34] Necessary vs. Unnecessary Suffering So let’s draw a distinction. It turns out there are two different kinds of suffering that we see in the world, and only one of those kinds seems incompatible with the existence of the 3-O God.

[Slide 35] There are some kinds of suffering that we wouldn’t expect the 3-O God to stop. If you think about it for a second, it quickly becomes obvious that a 3-O God probably would allow some suffering to occur.

[Slide 34] Necessary vs. Unnecessary Suffering So let’s draw a distinction. It turns out there are two different kinds of suffering that we see in the world, and only one of those kinds seems incompatible with the existence of the 3-O God.

[Slide 35] There are some kinds of suffering that we wouldn’t expect the 3-O God to stop. If you think about it for a second, it quickly becomes obvious that a 3-O God probably would allow some suffering to occur.

[Slide 36] There are some kinds of suffering that we wouldn’t expect the 3-O God to stop. Consider the pain involved in receiving a vaccination shot. Clearly, it would be silly for us to argue, “Vaccines exist. Therefore, God isn’t real” It’s silly because vaccines are not a bad thing. The world would not be better without vaccines. Yes they hurt a little but God would be doing us no favors by plucking vaccines out of the world.

[Slide 37] There are some kinds of suffering that we wouldn’t expect the 3-O God to stop. Likewise, the fact that there are root canals is not evidence against the existence of the 3-O God. It would be confused to ask “If God is so great, then how come there are dentists?” Dentistry hurts but we need dentistry in order to secure mouth-health – which, I’m told, is important. Again, God would be doing us no favors by plucking all the dentists out of the world.

[Slide 40] Some kinds of suffering are necessary . The problem of evil is about unnecessary suffering . Suffering is unnecessary when it, (a) doesn’t prevent some greater bad AND (b) doesn’t secure a greater good. To sum up, we are only concerned with certain kinds of suffering. Think back on all the examples we just looked at. Why don’t vaccines count as unnecessary suffering? Because we need them to prevent some greater bad (namely, disease) Why doesn’t exercise count as unnecessary suffering ? Because we need to exercise to secure a greater good (namely, physical health)

[Slide 41] Quiz Why is thirst an instance of necessary suffering?

[Slide 50] “Suppose in some distant forest lightning strikes a dead tree, resulting in a forest fire. In the fire a fawn is trapped, horribly burned, and lies in terrible agony for several days before death relieves its suffering… There does not appear to be any greater good… nor does there seem to be any equally bad or worse evil so connected to the fawn's suffering that it would have had to occur had the fawn's suffering been prevented.” – William Rowe Here’s a case from the Rowe reading for this week. This will turn out to be one of hardest cases for us to make sense of. (It’s worth noting, although Rowe’s case is fictional – he’s not reporting on some event he witnessed – the case is very very plausible. It is overwhelmingly likely that something like this has happened in the world. In fact, it is overwhelmingly like that this has happened thousands upon thousands of times.)

[Slide 52] What exactly does the Problem of Evil purport to show us? Quiz Quiz Question: Does the Problem of Evil argument conflict with the Fine-tuning argument?

[Slide 53] What exactly does the Problem of Evil purport to show us? The conclusion is rather limited: the 3-O God does not exist. There could still be a 2-O God ! Or a Creator God ! Turns out: No. You can accept both the Fine-tuning Argument and the Problem of Evil without contradiction. … Of course, we’d have to think carefully about which O we jettison. Maybe God is not all-knowing – is ignorant or oblivious? Maybe God is not all-powerful – has real limitation in what She can accomplish? Maybe God is not perfectly good – is wicked or indifferent to suffering? Maybe God is simply not here. Maybe She made the universe and then left or died. That’s perfectly compatible with the God of the Fine-tuning argument. Maybe She fine tuned the universe and then peaced out. And that’s why there’s so much unnecessary suffering.

[Slide 4] This course is titled, Philosophy of Religion What’s philosophy exactly? What do we mean by ‘ religion ’ ? So I want to say a bit, first, about what ‘religion’ means in context for this course and a bit about philosophy as a discipline (and what it means to do philosophy OF something)

[Slide 5] A religion is, roughly, a collection of beliefs, practices, symbols, texts, or objects pertaining to the relationship between humans and the transcendental, supernatural, or spiritual . We’re going to focus on just the beliefs . This is a really crude definition! The notion is notoriously hard to pin down and this is hardly the most sophisticated definition – But it’s close enough for our purposes today. My point is simply that religions are really complicated and have lots of different kinds of parts. Religions may include particular rituals or specific buildings or special hats or sacred animals. Religions are really complex. THIS class is not about all that complexity. This class is very specifically about religious beliefs. That’s the part of religion that philosophers are best equipped to deal with. (The other bits – the rituals, the symbols, the sacred hats, etc – are also important! They’re just not philosophically important. I don’t have anything philosophically substantive to say about religious hats. But fortunately, there’s this entire other academic discipline called Religious Studies. Those guys study all the parts of religion, not just the beliefs.) So all THAT is to say: here is what is meant by ‘religion’ in the course title: Specially, religious beliefs.

[Slide 6] Crudely, we might say that philosophers are interested in discovering the truth . But that’s obviously too broad… Chemists are interested in discovering the truth (about chemicals .) Linguistic anthropologists are interested in discovering the truth (about human languages .) Let me say a bit about philosophy too, while we’re defining basic concepts... It’s kinda controversial among philosophers how best to characterize philosophy. (Which, if you’ve taken any PHIL classes before will be zero surprising. Pretty much anytime you ask, “What do philosophers think about x?” the answer is “Well, i’s a controversy!)

[Slide 7] More specifically, we might say, philosophers seek to discover the truth about matters that are too general/fundamental for the sciences. Which, obviously, seems to include the truth about religion.

[Slide 8] Does God exist? If so, what is God like? What happens to us when we die? Why does our universe exist at all – what caused it? What spiritual reality is there beyond/behind the physical reality we see around us? Consider these questions,... Most modern religions give us answers to these questions. Each religion tells us what God is like, why we exist, and what sort of spiritual reality there is to the universe. Hopefully, you can see why these religious questions also count as philosophical questions . These are important issues and ones that seem too fundamental for the sciences to gain a lot of traction. (It doesn’t seem like, for instance, we could simply do experiments on God to see what She’s like.)

[Slide 9] The other distinctive feature of philosophy is its methodology . The way that philosophers try to discover the truth is through careful, critical, & systematic thinking and argumentation . This is the philosophical method precisely because philosophers are concerned with questions that aren’t answerable via scientific inquiry. Like, if there’s some question you’re trying to answer and you could either (a) try to answer it by doing an experiment or (b) try to answer it by thinking really hard, You should do the experiment. Scientific methods tend to be faster, better, and more reliable than mere critical thinking all by itself. Philosophers simply work on questions that science isn’t well-equipped to answer.

[Slide 10] The history of philosophy supports this way of thinking about the discipline – i.e. the contrast with science. If you go back and look at ancient Greek philosophy, you’ll quickly notice that a lot of it is actually proto-physics. People like aristotle and Epicurus (who was one of the first to believe in atoms) were all trying to do physics by just, like, thinking really hard. Then, eventually, we realized that you can learn about physics better by doing science about it, by conducting experiments. Thus, physics splintered off from philosophy and became its own scientific discipline. (And, for the most part…) Philosophers no longer do physics from the armchair, and physicists are no longer doing philosophy.

[Slide 11] We see this same pattern in the history of Psychology. Until around the turn of the 20th century, most of the best psychologists were just philosophers: they were trying to figure out how the human mind works by just thinking about it really hard. But, again, we eventually realized that psychology can be studied scientifically – with experimentation and carefully manipulated real-world observation. Thus, psychology splintered off from philosophy. It became its own discipline. (And for the most part…) philosophers are no longer doing psychology, and psychologists are no longer considered philosophers.
