[Slide 13] Some stray remarks about the course

[Slide 14] The suggested prerequisite for PHIL 3600 is 6 hours of prior philosophy coursework. (i.e. 2 classes) I want to mention this upfront because and say a bit about what that means for the course. In the CU PHIL dept, the expectation for a 3000-level philosophy classes like this one is that students will be asked to write original, argumentative philosophy papers – and, the expectation is, the quality of those papers will be reasonably high. Since it’s unreasonable to expect good and original papers from people who’ve never taken a philosophy class before, this class has a suggested prerequisite of 2 prior philosophy classes. I don’t mention this to scare you or even to tell you to drop the class if you haven’t taken any philosophy before! I just want to be clear and upfront about what it means that this is the 3000-level Philosophy of Religion course (and not a 1000-level.)

[Slide 15] Our course (PHIL 3600) has a lot of overlap with PHIL 1600. Ours isn’t a continuation of that course; it’s more like a demanding alternative . The other thing I want to point out: This course does not assume any prior experience with Philosophy of Religion. There’s actually a lower-level course, PHIL 1600, that serves the same function as this course and covers most of the same content, just pitched at a slightly lower level and with less rigorous assignments. And in fact, if you have already taken PHIL 1600, you might want to take another look at the syllabus for this course and decide whether this is a good use of your time. We’ll be talking about a lot of the same stuff that you would have already talked about in PHIL 1600.

[Slide 16] Another thing that I want to mention up front concerns the limitations of this course. The content of this course is largely fixed by the history of Philosophy, as a discipline in the English-speaking parts of the world. As you might expect, though, English-speaking philosophers have mostly been in conversation with the dominant religious traditions of the English-speaking world – Christianity is the big one, but also atheism, Judaism, and Islam. So that means: We’ll be mostly focused on religions that are Monotheistic (i.e. believe in one single divine entity rather than a divine pantheon) and ones where God is depicted as present in our lives and morally good. That describes all of the major Abrahamic religions – Christianity, Islam, and Judaism. But it excludes religions like Zoroastrianism, Hinduism, and Buddhism. We simply won’t have a chance to talk about those religions as much. That said, there are rich philosophical traditions rooted in Hinduism, Buddhism, Zoroastrianism! I certainly don’t want to imply otherwise. Only: we won’t be spending much time with those texts. (Most of which, unfortunately, aren’t terribly available in English)

[Slide 17] Our course focus is thinking carefully and systematically about religious beliefs . But that doesn’t really indicate what I want students to get out of the course. Lastly, I want to say a bit right now about what my goals are for this course. ... The first thing I want you to get from the course isn’t actually specific to this course at all. The point of taking philosophy courses isn’t to learn about philosophy – having a lot of philosophy facts in your brain isn’t very helpful. I don’t care about that. You shoudn’t care about that. The point of this course is to hone your critical faculties – your abilities to think, read, listen, speak, and write with clarity, subtlety, and compassion. In philosophy courses, you hone your critical faculties by practicing doing philosophy. In this course, we’ll be practicing doing philosophy by talking about Philosophy of Religion in particular. So that’s the first thing I want you to get out of the course. But there’s a second thing that I want students to get from this course that is specific to PHIL of Religion. To explain what that is, it might be helpful to reflect a bit about religious beliefs.

[Slide 18] Religious beliefs can be difficult to talk about b/c many people feel very strongly about religion – both in the confidence sense and in the personal identity sense. ... Thus, when you challenge someone’s religious beliefs, you meet two kinds of resistance: First, they are resistant because they are really quite sure that their own beliefs are true. (i.e. your challenge hits them with the same force as a impassioned defense of Flat Eartherism. Which is to say, they don’t actually take it very seriously, because they already think they know what’s up) Second, when you challenge a person’s religious beliefs, they are often resistant because they identify with their religious beliefs. And, thus, challenging their beliefs means challenging who they are and how they see themselves in a pretty fundamental way. As you might expect, that sort of challenging is threatening or, at best, uncomfortable.

[Slide 21] People are willing to expend absurd amounts of money, time, and effort on their religions. … Again, all of this is to say: people feel very strongly about their religious beliefs. So strongly that they do pretty radical things like go to war, build palaces, and refuse to use cars.

[Slide 22] Not only to people feel strongly about their religious beliefs , they also vehemently disagree with one another It would be one thing if everyone felt strongly about religion but ALSO everyone belonged to the same religion. In that world, where everyone is on the same page, religious conversations probably wouldn’t be that challenging. But that’s not the world we live in! Different people belong to different religions and those different religions genuinely contradict each other. Many of the things that a Christian believes to be true about religion are incompatible with the things that a Buddhist believes to be true. And, of course, to make matters worse, everyone is VERY CONVINCED that they are right and everyone else is wrong.

[Slide 25] One of the goals for the course is to get you to take other people’s religious beliefs more seriously. All of that is pre-amble to my second goal for this course: … Not to change your minds, but to encourage you to be less confident that everyone else is wrong about religion and that you’re right. Or, to be less confident that everyone else is so incredibly stupid for believing what they believe. Put differently, lots of people have the following concerning intellectual trait: when they find out that another person disagrees with them about religion, they take that as some evidence that that other person is stupid. That’s obviously not great, for a lot reason. It’s not charitable, of course, but I’m not sure that inference is even well-founded. One of my goals this semester is to move some distance toward demonstrating that that inference is not very well-founded.

[Slide 26] Some humbling thoughts for atheists … 45% of Americans (and 98% of Pakistanis!) think it’s impossible to be an atheist and have good values . To that end, let me quickly point to a couple facts that, I think, should give both sides some pause… That’s kinda kinda concerning. Lots of religious people think not only that atheists tend to be bad people – they think that atheism is incompatible with being a good person . That belief (that atheists are necessarily bad people) would have to be abandoned, of course, if they ever met a morally decent atheist. So… it’s maybe telling that so many religious people haven’t abandoned that belief. (it means they haven’t met any decent atheists!) Yikes!

[Slide 27] Some humbling thoughts for atheists … the most atheist-leaning demographic group, and by a wide margin, is young, white men. (which, no offense none taken, isn’t widely known for being The Most Enlightened Social Group .) Another concerning fact... If we made a list of all the beliefs that Young, White Men hold in greater numbers than other demographics (i.e. a list of beliefs that are most distinctive of young white men) that list would include a lot of… not-flattering stuff. Looking at that list would be like, “Gross Belief, Weird Belief about Sex, Atheism, Kinda Scary Belief, Another Gross Belief.” etc

[Slide 28] Some humbling thoughts for theists … In the U.S., 72% of people keep the same faith they grew up with. (and that’s low compared to other countries!) That is to say: pretty much everyone is every country grows up to hold the same religious beliefs as their parents. Basically no one converts between religions. (Relative to other countries, the U.S. actually has a lot of religious conversion going on. 28% of people convert. In other countries it’s more like 5%.) This is concerning for at least two reasons: First, it means for our Christian friends: if they’d been born in Iran, they’d be Muslim. That’s not a great sign that your religions is true! While most religions claim to be the one, true religion, it looks like no religion has much success at convincing outsiders of that. That’s not promising! We normally expect for the truth to be more successful at spreading than falsehoods. Second, it’s not a good sign that most people simply hold onto whatever views they were taught when they were 5. The fact that everyone still believes whatever they did in Kindergarten does not suggest that people are being particularly clear-headed and rational about these things.

That’s not to say you should just believe whatever the philosophers believe! [Slide 30] Arguing about God’s existence

[Slide 30] Arguing about God’s existence

[Slide 31] Does God exist? If so, what is God like? What happens to us when we die? Why does our universe exist at all – what caused it? What spiritual reality is there beyond/behind the physical reality we see around us? Look at these religious questions again. These questions are hard because we can’t simply inspect God the way that we might inspect a moose or a boulder. God’s properties and nature seem hidden from us. We can’t obviously do experiments on God or test her with our instruments. and yet – All major modern religions claim to have answers to these questions. Which is interesting! And it raises a philosophically substantive question: Where are those answers come from? What is the source of this religious knowledge? Where’s the evidence coming from?

[Slide 32] Option #1 – Divine revelation Most religions claim to have access to divine revelation. They claim that, at some point, God or one of her agents revealed the truth to us. On this view, we know what God is like because She told us! (Obviously, God didn’t literally tell you and me. We’ve never spoken to God. But supposedly She DID talk to some other people awhile ago, and they told the rest of us.) But this view raises a number of tough philosophical questions. For instance, Why did God reveal the truth to some people directly but not to everyone? How do we tell whether a person who claims to have received divine revelation actually received some or is just lying or hallucinating? Is it ever rational to believe someone who claims to have received divine revelation? (like, what if your good and trustworthy friend Mike claims to have spoken to God. Is it rational to believe him? If not, why should you believe someone like the Prophet Elijah whom you don’t know AT ALL? And if it IS rational to believe people who claim to have received divine revelation, which people should we believe? (For instance, Mormons think that Joseph Smith received divine revelation. Muslims don’t think that. Who’s right? How do we tell?)

QUIZ Question: (brief description of how this is going to work for the rest of the semester) This option also raises a number of tough, philosophical questions. Think about it for a second: what philosophical questions does this answer give rise to?

[Slide 34] Option #2 – Direct access to religious information Some philosophical questions I came up with: Does everyone have this religious sense? If so, why do millions of people earnestly claim to have no religious experiences or spiritual sensations? If not, why not – why don’t we all have this ability? Is it something you have train? Is it something that a rare handful are born with? If so, why is that? ALSO, if we can directly inspect God with our spiritual senses, then why is there so much disagreement about what God is like? (c.f. There isn’t much disagreement about what camels are like. That’s because we can directly inspect them with our senses) AND why should we think that the people who claim to be directly sensing God are actually sensing God – and not simply delusional? (c.f. some people claim to be able to directly sense ghosts. We mostly don’t believe those people.)

[Slide 35] Option #3 – Reasoning Other religious people also claim that we can answer these questions about God (e.g. What is God like ?) by using our reasoning ability. That is, they claim that we can answer religious questions simply by thinking really hard and really carefully. (Or, sometimes, religious people say we don’t even need to do that much! They claim that the truth about God is obvious . You don’t need to think hard about it, just think for like a second and you’ll immediately see that God is exists.) This view is even more philosophically rich than the other two! One great thing about reasoning and argumentation is that they are really easy to share. So, on this view, the evidence for God and religion is publically available . That’s interesting - we should look at that evidence. Are those arguments any good?

[Slide 36] That’s what we’ll be talking about for the first part of the course. What arguments can be offered for the existence of God? (and are those arguments any good?)

[Slide 35] Option #3 – Reasoning Other religious people also claim that we can answer these questions about God (e.g. What is God like ?) by using our reasoning ability. That is, they claim that we can answer religious questions simply by thinking really hard and really carefully. (Or, sometimes, religious people say we don’t even need to do that much! They claim that the truth about God is obvious . You don’t need to think hard about it, just think for like a second and you’ll immediately see that God is exists.) This view is even more philosophically rich than the other two! One great thing about reasoning and argumentation is that they are really easy to share. So, on this view, the evidence for God and religion is publically available . That’s interesting - we should look at that evidence. Are those arguments any good?

[Slide 36] That’s what we’ll be talking about for the first part of the course. What arguments can be offered for the existence of God? (and are those arguments any good?)

[Slide 37] We should be thoughtful about what we’re really looking for from theistic arguments . Like, what is it fair to demand of these arguments / what does success look like? The obvious answer is, We’re trying to figure out whether God exists – and, if these theistic arguments any good, success looks like is Arguments that successfully persuade non-theists to believe in God. But I don’t think that’s actually true. As we’ve seen, most people feel super confident about their religious beliefs, most people deeply identify with their religious beliefs, and very few people ever actually end up changing religions. So: I don’t think it’s particularly fair OR realistic to think that a few abstract, philosophical arguments are going to change already-settled minds. We should not, I submit, approach these arguments with that attitude – i.e. expecting theistic arguments to be either wholly persuasive or stupid failures. (I’ve had lot of atheist students approach them that way in the past! I think it’s a mistake. It also tends to makes course conversations feel really hostile. Which sucks.)

[Slide 38] We might usefully distinguish 4 groups of people, (1) Theists , who firmly believe that God exists (2) Atheists , who firmly believe that God does not exist. (3) Settled Agnostics , who firmly believe that God’s existence is unknowable (4) Searching Agnostics , who’re unsure what to believe about God’s existence Let me suggest instead: What you should expect from these arguments depends on your own pre-existing religious commitments … I distinguish groups (3) and (4) because there’s an important ambiguity in how people use the agnostic in everyday English. Some people claim to be agnostic in the sense that they aren’t sure whether God exists. They are on the fence or conflicted about their religious beliefs. But other people claim to be agnostic in the sense that they are sure that questions about God are unanswerable. That’s an important difference! The Settled Agnostic is confident in her religious beliefs (or confident in the lack thereof) – in much the same way that a theist or an atheist is confident. Obviously, of these groups, only the people in (4) are realistic targets for persuasion – the other groups are already religiously committed. A theist isn’t trying to figure out whether God exists. He’s already convinced She does – and, thus, won’t be persuaded to a position he already accepts. Likewise, an atheist and a settled agnostic aren’t trying to figure out whether God exists. The atheist is already convinced She doesn’t – and, thus, isn’t open to persuasion – the settled agnostic is already convinced that it’s unknowable – and, thus isn’t open to persuasion. I submit: how you approach this unit should depend on what your existing religious commitments are.

[Slide 14] Defining God

[Slide 15] Does God exist? This is our question. And I want to make a few preliminary remarks about what we’re really asking. The word ‘God’ picks out a variety of different ideas. (which is to say, different people use this same word ‘God’ to pick out a number of very different ideas.

[Slide 16] Does God exist? Some people have in mind some sort of impersonal creative force – a “first-mover” being who made or designed the universe

[Slide 15] Does God exist? This is our question. And I want to make a few preliminary remarks about what we’re really asking. The word ‘God’ picks out a variety of different ideas. (which is to say, different people use this same word ‘God’ to pick out a number of very different ideas.

[Slide 16] Does God exist? Some people have in mind some sort of impersonal creative force – a “first-mover” being who made or designed the universe

[Slide 17] Does God exist? others imagine some sort of spiritual essence that pervades everything in the universe

[Slide 18] Does God exist? others have in mind a personal God like the one depicted in the Bible – a God who loves us, cares about us, has plans for us, and wants a relationship with us.

[Slide 17] Does God exist? others imagine some sort of spiritual essence that pervades everything in the universe

[Slide 18] Does God exist? others have in mind a personal God like the one depicted in the Bible – a God who loves us, cares about us, has plans for us, and wants a relationship with us.

[Slide 19] Does the universe contain anything that can aptly described as God ? When we ask “Does God exist?”, we are trying to simultaneously ask about all of those things. Our question is something like, Does the universe contain anything that may aptly be called ‘God’? Theists think the answer to that question is Yes; atheists think the answer is No.

[Slide 20] If it turns out that there was a powerful divine creator, who made the universe and then peaced out, then the answer is yes. Yes, there is a being in existence who can aptly be described as god. If it turns out that the Christian God is real, then the answer is, obvs, Yes. God exists If we find out that Zeus exists or that the ancient Egyptian Ra exists, then the answer is Yes. Again, that would mean there something in the universe that can aptly be described as God. (That particular ‘Yes’ Is of course going to be disappointing to pretty much everyone, of course. Neither theists nor atheists would be excited to find out that Zeus is real. But the point is: If Zeus is real, then it will be true to say, “God exists.”)

[Slide 21] The Ontological Argument is intended to demonstrate that a very particular sort of God exists. The conclusion of the Ontological Arg is that ‘God exists’ and that means something very specific. There’s a particular definition of God at play in this argument.

[Slide 22] The Greatest Being God God is the greatest possible being – the being than which none greater can be conceived . Here’s the definition that we’ll be working with today. This is how Anselm is understanding ‘God’. When Anselm says “God exists” what he means is, There is in our actual universe a being who meets this description. He’s calling that being ‘god’. (But, we could call the being ‘George’ instead and the argument would still work just the same. In that case, The Ontological Argument would demonstrate that George exists, where ‘George’ is the name of the greatest possible being) 3 points need to made here. By “great”, we mean “better” or “more admirable” or “more perfect” – God is the perfectest being. Also bear in mind that what’s relevant here is possibility, rather than actuality. So the class of things than which God is greater is all the possible things; all the things not in category 3, in other words. I cannot stress this enough: This is NOT THE ONLY POSSIBLE WAY OF UNDERSTANDING GOD. I am not claiming that this is THE definition of God. I am not claiming this is the best definition of God. Rather, this is how we’ll define God for the purposes of the Ontological Argument . This is what St. Anselm has in mind when he says ‘God’. When Anselm says ‘God exists’, he means ‘the greatest possible being exists.’ A s we’ll see in future lectrues, there are other good ways of defining God that are genuinely distinct from this definition. Other people might say ‘God exists’ and mean something like, ‘there was a powerful being who created our universe – that being exists.’

[Slide 24] Great-making Properties

[Slide 25] Let’s define a great-making property as follows, a great-making property is such that when we consider adding this property to a being which lacks it, we invariably make that being greater. By “great” here, we mean something very generic like “more admirable” or “more valuable” or “improved” Thus, we could also describe a great-making property is a property that is always an improvement, always an upgrade when you add that property to beings which lack it. I know that’s a bit telescopic, so we’ll look at some examples.

[Slide 26] Is greenness a great-making property ? Greenness is a property. Some objects are green; they ahve this property. Other objects are not green; they lack this property.

[Slide 33] Is kindness a great-making property ? You might be wondering, “What about ketchup again? Surely kind ketchup is no better than regular ketchup? And if that’s right, doesn’t that mean kindness isn’t great-making? Because it doesn’t always make things greater?” That’s an interesting case. Here’s what I think we should say. When we imagine adding the property of kindness to ketchup… we fail. The thing that we imagine is just plain old ketchup. It doesn’t do anything on it’s own, so it doesn’t do any kind or mean things. Since we aren’t actually imagining kind ketchup, it’s not the case that we’re imagining kind ketchup as no greater than plain ketchup. (COMPARE: Imagine there was a walkin, talking bottle of ketchup who’s kind of of douche. Now it seems like we CAN imagine a version of that ketchup who’s kind. But, then, doesn’t it also seem like the kind ketchup-man is better than the unkind one?)

[Slide 34] Is kindness a great-making property ? Almost certainly. (I haven’t proven this. There could be some counter-example cases that I’m simply overlooking. Nevertheless it seems v plausible to me.)

[Slide 48] P1) God is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) God is not logically impossible C1) God is not a Category 3 entity (b/c we can imagine God!) P3) By definition, Category 2 beings don’t have the property of existence P4) Existence is a great-making property C2) Thus, all Category 2 beings lack at least one great-making property P5) By definition, God is the greatest possible being C3) God is not a Category 2 entity (else he would lack a great-making property ) C4) So God must be a Category 1 entity (i.e. God actually exists. ) The Ontological Argument What happens next is exactly what you expect: Anselm argues that God is not in Category 2 either. On P3) to be in Cat 2 is to be a possible thing that could exist but doesn’t actually exist. Centaurs are Cat 2. They could exist but don’t actually. That’s true by definition of all Cat 2 beings. On P5): You can think of this as the claim the God is unimprovable : he’s the greatest possible being so he must already be at peak performance, perfect in every way, completely and utterly unimprovable.

[Slide 3] The Ontological Argument

[Slide 3] The Ontological Argument

[Slide 4] P1) God is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) God is not logically impossible C1) God is not a Category 3 entity (b/c we can imagine God!) P3) By definition, Category 2 beings don’t have the property of existence P4) Existence is a great-making property C2) Thus, all Category 2 beings lack at least one great-making property P5) By definition, God is the greatest possible being C3) God is not a Category 2 entity (else he would lack a great-making property ) C4) So God must be a Category 1 entity (i.e. God actually exists. ) The Ontological Argument

[Slide 5] As written, the Ontological Argument is valid, so we have 6 options (1) Reject P1 (2) Reject P2 (3) Reject P3 (4) Reject P4 (5) Reject P5 (6) Accept the conclusion

[Slide 6] P1) God is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) God is not logically impossible ? C1) God is not a Category 3 entity (b/c we can imagine God!) P3) By definition, Category 2 beings don’t have the property of existence P4) Existence is a great-making property ? C2) Thus, all Category 2 beings lack at least one great-making property P5) By definition, God is the greatest possible being C3) God is not a Category 2 entity (else he would lack a great-making property ) C4) So God must be a Category 1 entity (i.e. God actually exists. ) ? The Ontological Argument Of those 6 options, only 3 of them seem actively viable. P1 seems like a logical truth. (Assuming we’ve done our possible-being metaphysics correctly.) Surely our three categories of being really do constitute a legitimate partition. Those 3 options really do seem to be both exhaustive and mutually exclusive. P3 & P5 are both definitions. It typically doesn’t make sense to say that a definition is false, unless the definition is reporting on the world – unless the definition has an empirical truth value. The def in P3 is a logical entailment. An analytic truth that falls out of the way we’ve described Category 2. Nothing to argue with there. What about P5? Maybe we can argue with Anselm’s def of God? Probably not. In general I think, here and in other cases, it just doesn’t make a ton of sense to argue with a definition.

[Slide 10] Let’s stipulatively define ‘God’ as the greatest possible being . This is what Anselm is doing. It just doesn’t make a ton of sense to argue with him about that. Thus, EVEN IF, you’re inclined to reject Anselm’s definition of God, he can just make up a new word and stick it into argument. If it bothers you that he’s co-opted the word ‘God’, you can simply replace all instances of the word ‘God’ in your head with the word ‘blozon’. Anselm is arguing that the greatest possible being, Blozon, actually exists. (And if you say, “NO. You can’t just do that!” Anselm will just shrug and say, “Fine. Instead of giving this concept a name, I’ll just repeatedly say, “The greatest possible being”. My argument still shows that the greatest possible being exists.)

[Slide 14] The Greatest Possible Island Obj

[Slide 15] Gaunilo of Marmoutiers (1100’s C.E.) famously argued that we can use the Ontological Argument to prove that the Greatest-Possible-Island exists – thus showing the Argument to be bad.

[Slide 16] The Greatest Possible Island The Greatest Possible Island (GPI) is that island than which none greater can be conceived . Here’s the definition that we’ll be working with in the Greatest Possible Island version of the Ontological Argument. 2 points need to made here. By “great”, we mean “better” or “more admirable” or “more perfect” – God is the perfectest being. Also bear in mind that what’s relevant here is possibility, rather than actuality.

[Slide 21] The Greatest Possible Island Reply – Is the GPI really a coherent, imaginable being? … The GPI, unlike Anselm’s God, is fundamentally a finite being. It is, at the end of the day, just an island! We might wonder, Does it even make sense to talk about an island as being perfect ? It seems coherent on its face, but can we actually imagine such a place? How many squares miles is it? It has to have a size, and that size has to be perfect! So… what’s the size? How many different kinds of fruit does it have? It has to have some number of fruit-bearing species, and that number has to be the perfect one! So… what’s the number? Likewise: what is the sand like? What is the weather like? What is the animal life like? What kinds of natural ores are there? The point is this: As soon as one ties to imagine the specific perfections of the GPI, one is confronted with the fact that it’s not-at-all clear how to make sense of the perfect island size or the perfect number of fruit-bearing species . So this is one thing Anselm might say in response to Gaunilo: God, unlike the island, can actually be coherently imagined, in detail. We can say with some specificity what properties God has. Thus, while the GPI is probably in Cat 3 (an impossible being), God is not.

[Slide 22] The Existence-Isn’t-A-Property Obj

[Slide 21] The Greatest Possible Island Reply – Is the GPI really a coherent, imaginable being? … The GPI, unlike Anselm’s God, is fundamentally a finite being. It is, at the end of the day, just an island! We might wonder, Does it even make sense to talk about an island as being perfect ? It seems coherent on its face, but can we actually imagine such a place? How many squares miles is it? It has to have a size, and that size has to be perfect! So… what’s the size? How many different kinds of fruit does it have? It has to have some number of fruit-bearing species, and that number has to be the perfect one! So… what’s the number? Likewise: what is the sand like? What is the weather like? What is the animal life like? What kinds of natural ores are there? The point is this: As soon as one ties to imagine the specific perfections of the GPI, one is confronted with the fact that it’s not-at-all clear how to make sense of the perfect island size or the perfect number of fruit-bearing species . So this is one thing Anselm might say in response to Gaunilo: God, unlike the island, can actually be coherently imagined, in detail. We can say with some specificity what properties God has. Thus, while the GPI is probably in Cat 3 (an impossible being), God is not.

[Slide 22] The Existence-Isn’t-A-Property Obj

[Slide 23] Immanuel Kant (1724 - 1804) famously argued that the Ontological Argument fails because existence is not a property. (And thus, cannot be a great-making property )

[Slide 24] P1) God is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) God is not logically impossible C1) God is not a Category 3 entity (b/c we can imagine God!) P3) By definition, Category 2 beings don’t have the property of existence P4) Existence is a great-making property C2) Thus, all Category 2 beings lack at least one great-making property P5) By definition, God is the greatest possible being C3) God is not a Category 2 entity (else he would lack a great-making property ) C4) So God must be a Category 1 entity (i.e. God actually exists. ) The Ontological Argument Visually, here’s what Kant’s doing – he’s telling us to reject P4.

[Slide 30] Kant thinks this is incoherent. Existence cannot be conceptually added or subtracted from an entity. Existence is rather a precondition for an entity’s having properties .

[Slide 31] “ ‘Being’ is obviously not a real property ; that is, it is not a concept of something which could be added to the concept of a thing. It is merely the positing of a thing… By whatever and by however many properties we may think a thing – even if we completely determine it – we do not make the least addition to the thing when we further declare that this thing is. Otherwise, it would not be exactly the same thing that exists, but something more than we had thought in the concept; and we could not, therefore, say that the exact object of my concept exists … ” – Immanuel Kant, Critique of Pure Reason What does this mean, exactly? Here’s my best attempt at glossing the argument

[Slide 32] A horse is not the same thing as a unicorn . A unicorn = a horse + a new property ( one-horned ) What’s the difference? … The addition of that extra property means horse and unicorn are different things; they pick out different concepts.

[Slide 31] “ ‘Being’ is obviously not a real property ; that is, it is not a concept of something which could be added to the concept of a thing. It is merely the positing of a thing… By whatever and by however many properties we may think a thing – even if we completely determine it – we do not make the least addition to the thing when we further declare that this thing is. Otherwise, it would not be exactly the same thing that exists, but something more than we had thought in the concept; and we could not, therefore, say that the exact object of my concept exists … ” – Immanuel Kant, Critique of Pure Reason What does this mean, exactly? Here’s my best attempt at glossing the argument

[Slide 32] A horse is not the same thing as a unicorn . A unicorn = a horse + a new property ( one-horned ) What’s the difference? … The addition of that extra property means horse and unicorn are different things; they pick out different concepts.

[Slide 33] Principle – adding a new property to a concept results in a new concept This example is meant to illustrate a general principle that Kant seems to accept: Namely, when you add a new property to an existing concept, the result is a new concept. (thus, when you add the property one-horned to the concept horse, the result is the new concept unicorn .)

[Slide 34] Kant reasons, If existence is a property , then an existing unicorn is not a unicorn at all. ( Unicorn + new property = new concept ) Obvs, an existing unicorn is a unicorn . Thus, existence is NOT a property . Why isn’t an existing unicorn a unicorn? Remember our general principal: WHen you take a concept and add a new property, the result is a new concept. So IF existence is a property, adding the property to the concept unicorn results in a brand-new concept. (C.F. adding the new property one-horned to the concept, horse, results in a new concept.) But surely an existing unicorn really is a unicorn. (that is to say, surely we don’t think that the concept of a unicorn that exists is genuinely distinct from our concept of a unicorn – not in the way that our concept horse is genuinely distinct from our concept, unicorn. Obviously, Kant doesn’t actually mention unicorns. But this seems to be the essential pattern of his objection. We must deny that existence is a property because were it a property, our concepts of existing things (like existing horses or unicorns or whatever) would be clearly distinct from our concepts of those same things, sans existence. But that’s stupid. An existing horse just is a horse. That’s the same concept. Thus, existence is not a property.

[Slide 35] The Sneaky Definition Obj

[Slide 36] Consider the leprechaun. A clear Category 2 entity.

[Slide 37] Let’s define a new kind of leprechaun. A real-leprechaun , we’ll say, is a leprechaun that isn’t just merely possible but that actually exists. (an R.L. for short)

[Slide 38] P1) An R.L. is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) An R.L. is not logically impossible (we can imagine an R.L.!) C1) An R.L. is not a Category 3 entity P3) By definition, Category 2 beings don’t have the property of existence P4) By definition, an R.L. is a leprechaun that actually exists. C2) An R.L. is not a Category 2 entity (b/c no Category 2 entity actually exists) C4) So an R.L. must be a Category 1 entity – i.e. real-leprechauns exist . The Ontological Argument – Leprechaun Edition So…. obviously that’s bullshit. Something’s gone wrong. This argument is defective. But WHY? What’s the defect? Where did we screw up?

[Slide 39] The mistake seems to be this. We also know they aren’t in Category 1 . (SPOILER: Leprechauns don’t exist) Thus, they’re impossible. R.L.s are in Category 3 . By definition, real-leprechauns don’t belong in Category 2 . It helps to try to assemble our facts about Real Leprechauns in a different order. Fact #1: Real Leprechauns are by definition not in Category 2 Fact #2: There are no leprechauns in reality. So they aren’t in Category 1 Conclusion: Real Leprechauns are in Category 3: the are impossible beings.

[Slide 43] So too with the Ontological Arg. By Anselm’s def, God isn’t merely possible . (at least, if we accept that existence is great-making .) That is, by definition God is either Category 1 or Category 3 . (either real or impossible) But then, we should not accept P2 . It looks innocent, but it’s not. (b/c Anselm’s definition is sneaky .) Anselm is saying, “Let’s define God as real or impossible. Since we can imagine God, God is not impossible. So, She’s real.”

[Slide 44] P1) God is either a Category 1 , a Category 2 , or a Category 3 entity. P2) God is not logically impossible (is not Category 3 ) P3) By definition, God is not Category 2 C) So, God is in Category 1 , is real The Ontological Argument (un-sneaky’d) Not a good argument! No one should accept P2, given the definition in P3. What the R.L. case teaches us is that just because we can imagine something doesn’t mean that thing is possible. We seem to be able to imagine Real Leprechauns, even though they are impossible. (b/c they don’t actually exist and by definition aren’t merely possible.) Likewise, just because we seem to be able to imagine God as the greatest possible being, that doesn’t mean God – as Anselm defines Her – isn’t impossible.

[Slide 1] The Kalām Cosmological Argument

[Slide 2] Background for the Cosmological Argument The Cosmological Argument In support of P1 On Infinity In support of P2

[Slide 3] Why does the universe exist at all – why isn’t there nothing? Option 1 – the universe has always existed (nothing was never a possibility) Option 2 – the universe just started existing one day (and for no reason) Option 3 – the universe started to exist for a reason (call it ‘God’) The genetic question for the Cosmological Argument – you will not be surprised to learn – concerns the existence of the cosmos, the universe. The question is… (… surely it was possible that nothing existed rather than the universe. So why didn’t that happen? What’s the explanation?) The proponent of the Cosmological Argument distinguishes 3 possible answers to this question: First, we might think that the question doesn’t make sense b/c the universe is infinitely old. If so, if the universe has always existed, then the question doesn’t arise. (Why isn’t there nothing? There has never been nothing – nothing has never been an option. There has always been the universe.) That’s Option 1. But maybe you don’t think that’s right. Maybe you think the universe did begin to exist at some point. If so, our question does make sense: Why did that happen?! Why did the universe suddenly start existing when before it didn’t? There seem to be, basically two answers. Either: Literally no reason. The universe just started existing one day, uncaused, unintended, uncreated. That’s Option 2. Or: For a reason. The universe started existing one day because something caused it to exist – a something that we might as well call ‘God’. That’s Option 3.

[Slide 4] Cosmological Arguments have a rich history in the West, going back at least to Plato. The basic idea is, Given the way the universe is, we can infer that there must have been a first cause. These arguments aim to rule out Option 2. (“the universe just started one day for no reason”)

[Slide 4] Cosmological Arguments have a rich history in the West, going back at least to Plato. The basic idea is, Given the way the universe is, we can infer that there must have been a first cause. These arguments aim to rule out Option 2. (“the universe just started one day for no reason”)

[Slide 5] The Kalām version of the Cosmological Argument was developed by the Medieval Islamic scholar Al-Ghazali. This version tries to show that the universe began to exist – thus, ruling out Option 1 (“the universe has always existed”) Whereas as most Cosmological Arguments are concerned with ruling out Option 2 – where the universe just exists for no reason – Al-Ghazali acknowledged that Option 1 was also on the table and so he developed a more sophisticated version of the Cosmological Argument which seeks to demonstrate that the universe cannot be infinitely old. His argument works, in effect, by trying to show that it’s impossible for anything to be infinitely old – or infinite in any respect.

[Slide 7] Why does the universe exist at all – why isn’t there nothing? Option 1 – the universe has always existed (nothing was never a possibility) Option 2 – the universe just started existing one day (and for no reason) Option 3 – the universe started to exist for a reason (call it ‘God’) Visually, this is what the Kalam Cosmological Argument is doing. P1 tells us that the universe hasn’t always existed. P2 tells us that nothing ever “just starts existing for no reason” – not even universes. Leaving us with Option 3 – the universe was created or kicked off at some point and for some reason.

Look at the conclusion! “The universe has a cause”. That’s not very informative. [next slide]

Look at the conclusion! “The universe has a cause”. That’s not very informative. [next slide]

[Slide 9] Supposing the argument succeeds, we don’t learn (i) whether God is singular or plural, (ii) whether God has a mind or is some sort of impersonal force, (iii) Whether God is still around (maybe god made it then peaced out), (iv) whether God is still powerful (maybe God used up all her power in creating the universe), (v) what God’s character is like, if God has a character (is She kind? cruel? indiffferent?) We do not learn much at all about God. To drive the point home, we might point out that the Cosmological Argument is compatible with the view that: ‘God’ is the name for a committee or supernatural goats who were very wicked but who died violently when they made the universe. That’s a pretty crazy cosmology but it’s one that’s perfectly compatible with our Argument! (not very likely maybe, but certainly not ruled-out!)

[Slide 10] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument While the basic argument is really simple, there’s a lot going on in the background to support each premise. (Here, I’ll be leaning pretty heavily on William Lane Craig’s articulation of the Kalam Cosmological argument – which I assigned for this module) So let’s start by looking at the support for P1, the claim that the universe began to exist. Craig offers us two sorts of reasons for this claim: scientific reasons and theoretical reasons.

[Slide 9] Supposing the argument succeeds, we don’t learn (i) whether God is singular or plural, (ii) whether God has a mind or is some sort of impersonal force, (iii) Whether God is still around (maybe god made it then peaced out), (iv) whether God is still powerful (maybe God used up all her power in creating the universe), (v) what God’s character is like, if God has a character (is She kind? cruel? indiffferent?) We do not learn much at all about God. To drive the point home, we might point out that the Cosmological Argument is compatible with the view that: ‘God’ is the name for a committee or supernatural goats who were very wicked but who died violently when they made the universe. That’s a pretty crazy cosmology but it’s one that’s perfectly compatible with our Argument! (not very likely maybe, but certainly not ruled-out!)

[Slide 10] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument While the basic argument is really simple, there’s a lot going on in the background to support each premise. (Here, I’ll be leaning pretty heavily on William Lane Craig’s articulation of the Kalam Cosmological argument – which I assigned for this module) So let’s start by looking at the support for P1, the claim that the universe began to exist. Craig offers us two sorts of reasons for this claim: scientific reasons and theoretical reasons.

[Slide 11] Here’s scientific reason #1: It turns out, Every stellar object outside of our galaxy is moving away from us – and, in fact, things that are further from us are moving away faster. When you retrodict the positions of those objects to figure out where they originated, it looks like they all occupied the same point in space around 13.8 Billion years ago. That’s the Big Bang. (There’s ALSO a bunch of other and better evidence for the Big Bang, too, but it’s harder to wrap one’s head around.) Suffice it to say that the Big Bang Theory is the consensus position among cosmologists. The experts are all pretty sure it happened. But – hey! – if there was a Big Bang that kicked off the universe, then the universe isn’t infinitely old!

[Slide 12] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago

[Slide 12] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago

[Slide 13] ANother bit of scientific evidence for P1 is this: The second law of thermodynamics states that the total entropy of a closed system always increases over time. The universe is a closed system, so the entropy of the universe is ever-increasing. ( entropy can be a tough concept to wrap one’s head around – maybe think of it as a measure of the uniformity of temperature in a volume. That’s not right, but it’s close enough for our purposes.) This is significant for 2 reasons: First, Since entropy is currently non-zero, we should expect it to approach zero as we look further back in time. (remember, entropy is always increasing – so running time backwards should cause it to decrease.) Since negative entropy is impossible, we’ll eventually hit a point in time when the entropy of the universe is zero. That’s the Big Bang – that’s when the universe started.

[Slide 14] Quiz Second, the fact that entropy is increasing in the universe means that end state for our universe is something called ‘heat death’ – a situation in which all matter and energy is smeared out uniformly across the universe. (i.e. a universe in heat death doesn’t have any matter clumps like planets or humans or any energy clumps like stars.) If it helps, you can think of the Big Bang as a bit like permanently throwing open the doors of a refrigerator. At first there’s a big temp diff between the inside of the fridge and the surrounding room, but after enough time passes, that difference is gone. It’s all the same temp. (The Big Bang is actually the opposite – it was a concentrated HOT thing that cooled as it expanded out, rather than a concentrated COLD place that was invaded by warm air. But – still – it’s a workable for our purposes.) Quiz Question: Can you see why these two facts imply that the universe had a beginning? (Fact #1: entropy in the universe is always increasing. Fact #2: the universe will eventually enter heat death)

[Slide 15] Since we’ll reach heat death in a finite amount of time (that is, since it won’t take infinitely long for our universe to reach heat death), if the universe were infinitely old, we’d be in heat death already. (enough time would have already passed.) It’s like walking into a room and finding the fridge door wide open. If the stuff in the fridge is still cold, you can infer that the fridge wasn’t always open but was-opened relatively recently.

Since we haven’t reached the end of the universe’s life yet, the universe must not be infinitely old.

[Slide 19] On infinity So obviously at this point we’re going to have to talk about infinity. What does it mean for something to be actually infinite and why is such a thing impossible?

[Slide 20] To be infinite is to be unbounded . (or, alternatively, to have a magnitude that’s larger than any particular number .)

[Slide 19] On infinity So obviously at this point we’re going to have to talk about infinity. What does it mean for something to be actually infinite and why is such a thing impossible?

[Slide 20] To be infinite is to be unbounded . (or, alternatively, to have a magnitude that’s larger than any particular number .)

[Slide 21] A hot dog that’s infinitely long might start right here, but there’s no other end. No last bite. And there is no number that accurately expresses the length of the infinite hotdog.

[Slide 22] A musical that’s infinitely long may start right now, but there’s no end. There’s no final song, no last line of dialogue.

[Slide 21] A hot dog that’s infinitely long might start right here, but there’s no other end. No last bite. And there is no number that accurately expresses the length of the infinite hotdog.

[Slide 22] A musical that’s infinitely long may start right now, but there’s no end. There’s no final song, no last line of dialogue.

[Slide 23] The Natural Numbers 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, ... The Integers …, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ... You’re probably most familiar with infinity, as a property of sets of numbers. We know that the Natural Numbers are infinite in one direction: they start at 1, but there’s no final number. They go on forever. We also know: the Integers are infinite in two directions: there is no smallest integer, no starting point NOR is there a largest integer.

[Slide 24] There are different sizes of infinity . (called ‘cardinalities’) The Natural Numbers and The Integers are both the smallest size . The Real Numbers are a larger size of infinity . There is no maximum size. The number of cardinalities is itself infinite . Here’s an important fact that you probably didn’t learn in high school. There are different sizes of infinity (called cardinalities ). And those two sets we were just looking at are both the smallest size. (And, hence, the same size! Which is surprising!) Also, the real numbers are of a larger size. [though, recent proofs suggest, not ONE size larger.] The real numbers, you may recall, are all the numbers that express a position on a continuous number line. (Which includes fractions and irrational numbers like PI.) T his fact is a relatively new discovery - it wasn’t proven until ~140 years ago by Georg Cantor. It’s quite a bit beyond our scope to look at Cantor’s proof, but it’s actually not all that complicated, as mathematical proofs go. You can find some good Youtube videos explaining it, if you’re curious. And there’s a Mathematical Logic course here in the CU Philosophy Dept that covers it, too, if you want a full philosophical treatment. (Quickly, though, the gist of the proof is this: two infinities count as being the same size if and only if there’s a bijection function relating them – where a bijection is a one-to-one mapping of items in one set to items in the other). Cantor demonstrates that the real number cannot be bijected with any countably infinite set, like the natural numbers. Thus, the real numbers have a greater cardinality than the natural numbers.) But again – that’s beyond our scope, so no worries if that went over your head.) As a final important fact, it turns out that there is no maximum size of infinity. We’ll see in a sec why it matters that infinities come in different sizes.

[Slide 25] ‘ Infinite ’ is sometimes used to describe things that are extreme . ( “I’m infinitely tired of this boring ass lecture.”) But, for our purposes, that’s imprecise. Often, what we actually mean is that the thing is maximal , rather than infinite . This isn’t super important right now, but it’s going to be important later in the course, and since we’re talking about infinity now, we might as well touch on it.

[Slide 26] A thing is maximally X when it couldn’t be any X-er . e.g. this glass is maximally full => … The glass IS NOT infinitely full. If you said that, you would be wrong. If it were infinitely full then there would be no number to express how much water is in the glass. There would be no way for you to drink this glass of water during your limited life span. But… yeah. Neither of those things is true. Here’s a number: This glass holds ~8 oz of water. And you could drink it in about 5 seconds. It’s not infinitely full. It’s maximally full – it could not be any fuller.

[Slide 28] Which is more plausible, God is infinitely great or God is maximally great ? Quiz Here’s why this matters. It’s really common to hear people describe God as having infinite properties. (God is infinitely powerful, infinitely wise, infinitely good, infinitely great.) We’ll be looking at just those sorts of views in a few weeks. But… is that plausible? Is that what we want to say about God? Or do we want to say, instead, that God has maximal properties. So think about that for a second.

[Slide 28] Which is more plausible, God is infinitely great or God is maximally great ? Quiz Here’s why this matters. It’s really common to hear people describe God as having infinite properties. (God is infinitely powerful, infinitely wise, infinitely good, infinitely great.) We’ll be looking at just those sorts of views in a few weeks. But… is that plausible? Is that what we want to say about God? Or do we want to say, instead, that God has maximal properties. So think about that for a second.

[Slide 29] God is infinitely great or God is maximally great Presumably, we want to say the second one, that God is maximally great. What theists mean, I think, is that is nothing greater than God. More greatness isn’t possible. (think about Anselm: God is the greatest possible being. That’s maximal greatness, not infinite greatness.) Why not infinite greatness? Well, here we have to appeal to one of those funny facts we just learned about infinity. Since infinities come in different sizes and since there is no largest size of infinity, to say that God is infinitely great is to imply that God is not the greatest possible being. No matter what size of infinity God’s greatness sits at, it will always be possible for there be being that are even greater – i.e. beings whose greatness cardinality is one higher than God’s.

What we still need to talk about is this claim that actually infinite things are impossible.

[Slide 31] Potentially infinite things are finite right now . (but have the potential to be infinite, if given infinite time to grow) Actually infinite things are infinite right now . The proponents of the Cosmological Argument are appealing to the following distinction… Their point is that lots of things are potentially infinite – lots of processes have the potential to go on forever

[Slide 32] For instance, simply counting the natural numbers up from 1 is potentially infinite. At each point in time, you’ve only gotten through a finite amount of the list. But the counting process has the potential to be infinite, since there’s no last number of stopping point that will ever be reaching. (Mostly unrelatedly, this is one of my favorite bits of stock footage in the world. I don’t understand why this was filmed. There’s another version where the woman gets stuck at 8 and just gives up on counting.)

[Slide 33] Potentially infinite things are finite right now . (but have the potential to be infinite, if given infinite time to grow) Actually infinite things are infinite right now . possible impossible The claim is that while potentially infinite things are possible, actually infinite things are impossible. No existing thing can be actually infinite. Why is that? Well, the William Lane Craig article offered us an argument that points to the absurdities that would result from an actually infinite thing really existing.

[Slide 34] Consider Hilbert’s Hotel – a hotel with infinitely many rooms and no vacancies. A new guest arrives. The desk clerk says, “ No problem, we can squeeze you in. ” To illustrate the point, Craig points to a famous thought experiment – one developed by the mathematician David Hilbert to illustrate the properties of infinity.

[Slide 34] Consider Hilbert’s Hotel – a hotel with infinitely many rooms and no vacancies. A new guest arrives. The desk clerk says, “ No problem, we can squeeze you in. ” To illustrate the point, Craig points to a famous thought experiment – one developed by the mathematician David Hilbert to illustrate the properties of infinity.

[Slide 35] THIS IS THE WRONG PHOTO! (it’s not one-down, it’s double-the-room-number) How? The clerk tells the people in Room 1 to move to Room 2; tells those in Room 2 to move to Room 3; those in 3 to move to 4, etc. Because there are infinitely many rooms there is no person who needs to be kicked out. For any room you name, I can tell you where that person ends up.

[Slide 36] An infinitely long bus arrives. The driver asks, “D’ya have room for my infinite passengers ?” Again, the desk clerk says, “ Yep – no problem, we can squeeze you in. ”

[Slide 36] An infinitely long bus arrives. The driver asks, “D’ya have room for my infinite passengers ?” Again, the desk clerk says, “ Yep – no problem, we can squeeze you in. ”

[Slide 37] Here’s how they do it: The person in Room 1 goes to Room 2, the person in room 2 goes to room 4, the person in 3 goes to 6, etc. That leaves open all of the odd numbered rooms. Since there are infinitely many odd numbers, there are infinitely many vacancies. The hotel can easily squeeze in the whole bus load.

[Slide 38] The infinitely long bus departs, taking everyone except the people in Room 1,2,3. Quiz QUIZ: Why is that problem? … It seems like we’re committed to say, The bus departed with the same number of people that it arrives with (namely, infinitely many), but the hotel has fewer guests than before (before: infinitely many guests. Now: 3 guests.) How is that possible?

[Slide 39] Craig, The absurdity of Hilbert’s Hotel shows that actually infinite things cannot exist. What’s the point of all this hotel silliness? Craig thinks that we learn something important from contemplating what a truly infinite hotel would be like: The hotel seems absurd and impossible – with its infinite comings and goings and ever-available rooms – because it IS absurd and impossible. No such thing could ever exist. No actually infinite things are possible.

[Slide 40] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible { { 1. Actually infinite things do not (and cannot) exist. 2. If the universe is infinitely old, it’s an actually infinite thing. C. So, the universe is not infinitely old. So that, at least, is the argument for ‘1’. The rest of the argument is straightforward: Obviously, were the universe infinitely old, it would be an actually infinite thing. Since there cannot be any actually infinite things, the universe is not infinitely old. Thus – remember the point of all this – thus the universe must have had a beginning. That’s the third reason we’re given in support of P1 of the Cosmological Argument.

[Slide 41] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause The Cosmological Argument has one other premise, which we haven’t talked about yet. That’s the claim, Whatever begins to exist has a cause. Or, in other words, nothing ever just starts existing for no reason at all. As with P1, we’re given a couple different reasons for accepting P2.

[Slide 42] Everything we’ve ever observed obeys this principle. The first is, simply, that we’ve never seen it violated. We’ve never seen anything simply pop into existence out of nothing and for no reason. All of nature affirms that things do not simply come into being out nothing and for no reason.

[Slide 41] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause The Cosmological Argument has one other premise, which we haven’t talked about yet. That’s the claim, Whatever begins to exist has a cause. Or, in other words, nothing ever just starts existing for no reason at all. As with P1, we’re given a couple different reasons for accepting P2.

[Slide 42] Everything we’ve ever observed obeys this principle. The first is, simply, that we’ve never seen it violated. We’ve never seen anything simply pop into existence out of nothing and for no reason. All of nature affirms that things do not simply come into being out nothing and for no reason.

[Slide 43] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this That’s the first reason. Of course, you might be wondering, What about universes? We’ve never actually seen a universe come into being. So how do we know they universes have causes? Maybe they do pop into existence out of nothing and for no reason!

[Slide 44] Surely universes obey the same laws as everything else. That’s brings us to the second consideration cited in favor of P2. Craig – and other proponents of the Cosmological Argument – note: There’s no reason to think that the universe is some special exception to this rule. If universes can pop into existence out of nothing, why not tigers or opera singers? Unless we can point to some special reason for thinking universes are different from, like, literally everything else, we should assume that they are, in fact, the same and abide by the same rules. Thus: no popping into existence out of nothing. That’s against the rules.

[Slide 43] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this That’s the first reason. Of course, you might be wondering, What about universes? We’ve never actually seen a universe come into being. So how do we know they universes have causes? Maybe they do pop into existence out of nothing and for no reason!

[Slide 44] Surely universes obey the same laws as everything else. That’s brings us to the second consideration cited in favor of P2. Craig – and other proponents of the Cosmological Argument – note: There’s no reason to think that the universe is some special exception to this rule. If universes can pop into existence out of nothing, why not tigers or opera singers? Unless we can point to some special reason for thinking universes are different from, like, literally everything else, we should assume that they are, in fact, the same and abide by the same rules. Thus: no popping into existence out of nothing. That’s against the rules.

[Slide 45] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this … if universes could pop into being, so could other things – but they don’t C) So the universe has a cause (God!) From P1 and P2 together, we get our conclusion: The universe must have had a cause!

[Slide 46] Why does the universe exist at all – why isn’t there nothing? Option 1 – the universe has always existed (nothing was never a possibility) Option 2 – the universe just started existing one day (and for no reason) Option 3 – the universe started to exist for a reason (call it ‘God’) Again, recall the set-up:

[Slide 45] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this … if universes could pop into being, so could other things – but they don’t C) So the universe has a cause (God!) From P1 and P2 together, we get our conclusion: The universe must have had a cause!

[Slide 46] Why does the universe exist at all – why isn’t there nothing? Option 1 – the universe has always existed (nothing was never a possibility) Option 2 – the universe just started existing one day (and for no reason) Option 3 – the universe started to exist for a reason (call it ‘God’) Again, recall the set-up:

[Slide 47] P1) The universe began to exist (not Option 1) P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause (not Option 2) C) So, the universe has a cause (which we’ll call ‘God’) The Kalām Cosmological Argument And, at heart, the Kalam Cosmological ARgument works by rejecting those first two options. Leaving us with only the 3rd: the universe began to exist at some point b/c someone or something made that happen. Call that thing ‘God’ In the next video, we’ll look at some objections to this argumen.t

[Slide 47] P1) The universe began to exist (not Option 1) P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause (not Option 2) C) So, the universe has a cause (which we’ll call ‘God’) The Kalām Cosmological Argument And, at heart, the Kalam Cosmological ARgument works by rejecting those first two options. Leaving us with only the 3rd: the universe began to exist at some point b/c someone or something made that happen. lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
[Slide 1] Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument

[Slide 2] Recap Objections to arguments for P1 An objection to the scientific support Objections to the theoretical support Objections to arguments for P2

[Slide 3] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this … if universes could pop into being, so could other things – but they don’t C) So the universe has a cause (God!) Here’s our argument. Two simple premises, but each of those premises has multiple lines of support. Today I want to talk through some potential objections to those lines of support for P1 and P2 of the Cosmological Arg.

[Slide 4] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this … if universes could pop into being, so could other things – but they don’t C) So the universe has a cause (God!) Let’s start here, with the scientific support for P1. Why should we think the Universe began to exist? The Proponents of the Cosmological Arg tell us, Because (a) our best science tells us the Big Bang happened (~13.8 billion yrs ago) and (b) entropy is going to put the universe in heat death in a finite amount of time, so the universe must still be pretty young or it’d be dead already.

lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
[Slide 3] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this … if universes could pop into being, so could other things – but they don’t C) So the universe has a cause (God!) Here’s our argument. Two simple premises, but each of those premises has multiple lines of support. Today I want to talk through some potential objections to those lines of support for P1 and P2 of the Cosmological Arg.

[Slide 4] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this … if universes could pop into being, so could other things – but they don’t C) So the universe has a cause (God!) Let’s start here, with the scientific support for P1. Why should we think the Universe began to exist? The Proponents of the Cosmological Arg tell us, Because (a) our best science tells us the Big Bang happened (~13.8 billion yrs ago) and (b) entropy is going to put the universe in heat death in a finite amount of time, so the universe must still be pretty young or it’d be dead already.

[Slide 5] An analogy You buy a used USB drive off Craigslist. The person who had it before you didn’t wipe it, so you can still see all their files. Browsing through gives you a pretty good sense for what they’ve been up to – at least, right up to July 1st, 2016, when the first file was added. Can you infer that July 1st, 2016 was the first time they used the USB drive? Quiz One powerful objection to this scientific support goes like this. We start with an analogy… So, quiz question:... (and if you can’t infer that, what can you infer?)

[Slide 6] No. That is the date when new files started accumulating , but we cannot infer why . Maybe that’s when they bought it, or maybe that’s when they last reformatted it. One powerful objection to this scientific support goes like this. We start with an analogy… So, quiz question:...

lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
[Slide 5] An analogy You buy a used USB drive off Craigslist. The person who had it before you didn’t wipe it, so you can still see all their files. Browsing through gives you a pretty good sense for what they’ve been up to – at least, right up to July 1st, 2016, when the first file was added. Can you infer that July 1st, 2016 was the first time they used the USB drive? Quiz One powerful objection to this scientific support goes like this. We start with an analogy… So, quiz question:... (and if you can’t infer that, what can you infer?)

[Slide 6] No. That is the date when new files started accumulating , but we cannot infer why . Maybe that’s when they bought it, or maybe that’s when they last reformatted it. One powerful objection to this scientific support goes like this. We start with an analogy… So, quiz question:...

[Slide 7] The Big Bang is when physical evidence started accumulating Hopefully, you can see the relevance for the Big Bang. Our best physics tells us that all of our data points back to the Big Bang. That’s the point in time when our current body of evidence started accumulating. But the fact that our evidence allows us to peer back to a time, T-nought, just shows that evidence started accumulating at T-0. It doesn’t tell us why that’s the date when evidence started accumulating. Maybe the Big Bang really is the start of the universe! That’s very possible! But it’s not the only possibility. For all we know, the Big Bang was just the point at which the old-version of the universe ended and ours began – perhaps because the universe regularly expands and contracts like a lung as some physicists have suggested. This seems like very serious error in the Cosmological Argument. On it’s face, at least, it simply seems false to claim that our best physics tells us that our universe began with the Big Bang. That’s only one possible explanation. (All of this also applies, mutatis mutandis, to the entropy-argument. It’s true that our current closed system began to increase in entropy at the Big Bang. But that’s not decisive evidence that the Big Bang was the first moment of the universe. Again, maybe the universe expands and contracts like a lung. Every contraction is followed by a Big Bang that starts the process over again, restarting the clock on entropy)

[Slide 8] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible { { 1. Actually infinite things do not (and cannot) exist. 2. If the universe is infinitely old, it’s an actually infinite thing. C. So, the universe is not infinitely old. What about the other argument in support of P1? Recall, this last consideration was supported by the thought that it’s impossible for any actually infinite being to exist in the universe.
