content:
[Slide 7] The Big Bang is when physical evidence started accumulating Hopefully, you can see the relevance for the Big Bang. Our best physics tells us that all of our data points back to the Big Bang. That’s the point in time when our current body of evidence started accumulating. But the fact that our evidence allows us to peer back to a time, T-nought, just shows that evidence started accumulating at T-0. It doesn’t tell us why that’s the date when evidence started accumulating. Maybe the Big Bang really is the start of the universe! That’s very possible! But it’s not the only possibility. For all we know, the Big Bang was just the point at which the old-version of the universe ended and ours began – perhaps because the universe regularly expands and contracts like a lung as some physicists have suggested. This seems like very serious error in the Cosmological Argument. On it’s face, at least, it simply seems false to claim that our best physics tells us that our universe began with the Big Bang. That’s only one possible explanation. (All of this also applies, mutatis mutandis, to the entropy-argument. It’s true that our current closed system began to increase in entropy at the Big Bang. But that’s not decisive evidence that the Big Bang was the first moment of the universe. Again, maybe the universe expands and contracts like a lung. Every contraction is followed by a Big Bang that starts the process over again, restarting the clock on entropy)

[Slide 8] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible { { 1. Actually infinite things do not (and cannot) exist. 2. If the universe is infinitely old, it’s an actually infinite thing. C. So, the universe is not infinitely old. What about the other argument in support of P1? Recall, this last consideration was supported by the thought that it’s impossible for any actually infinite being to exist in the universe.

[Slide 9] Craig, The absurdity of Hilbert’s Hotel shows that actually infinite things cannot exist. Recall how that sub-argument went. We’re supposed to reason, The hotel seems absurd and impossible – with its infinite comings and goings and ever-available rooms – because it IS absurd and impossible. No such thing could ever exist. Because no actually infinite things are possible. The trouble is… it’s not at all clear how this argument is supposed to go. And when we attempt to work out the details, the argument appears to disintegrate like cotton candy in water

[Slide 10] 1) Hilbert’s Hotel is impossible . ... C) Actual infinities are impossible . Clearly, the argument starts with the claim that Hilbert’s Hotel is impossible, and it concludes with the claim that all actual infinities are impossible. But what goes on in the middle? What sort of linking principle or intermediate premise is getting us from that first claim to that final claim?

lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
[Slide 9] Craig, The absurdity of Hilbert’s Hotel shows that actually infinite things cannot exist. Recall how that sub-argument went. We’re supposed to reason, The hotel seems absurd and impossible – with its infinite comings and goings and ever-available rooms – because it IS absurd and impossible. No such thing could ever exist. Because no actually infinite things are possible. The trouble is… it’s not at all clear how this argument is supposed to go. And when we attempt to work out the details, the argument appears to disintegrate like cotton candy in water

[Slide 10] 1) Hilbert’s Hotel is impossible . ... C) Actual infinities are impossible . Clearly, the argument starts with the claim that Hilbert’s Hotel is impossible, and it concludes with the claim that all actual infinities are impossible. But what goes on in the middle? What sort of linking principle or intermediate premise is getting us from that first claim to that final claim?

[Slide 11] 1) Hilbert’s Hotel is impossible . 2) Hilbert’s Hotel is actually infinite C) Actual infinities are impossible . 1) A r ound square is impossible . 2) A r ound squares is a shape C) Shapes are impossible . Here’s a first stab. Maybe something like this?... But that won’t work. Compare with this argument… (Or, if you don’t like that one: A dog that’s taller than itself is impossible. A dog that’s taller than itself is a dog. So dogs are impossible) In general, the mere fact that something is impossible doesn’t, by itself, indicate WHY it’s impossible. So simply pointing out that Hilbert’s Hotel can’t exist does not, by itself, tell us that it can’t exist BECAUSE it is infinite. It could be for some other reason!

[Slide 12] The thought has to be that the Hotel is impossible because it is actually infinite . But… is that right? That suggests another way to fill in the hole in Craig’s argument.… But we haven’t actually been given any reasons for thinking that’s true. And, if you think about it, there do seem to be other explanations that might be offered. Maybe the Hotel is impossible b/c the Hotel staff completes an infinite task in a finite amount of time. That could potentially explain all the absurdities! Of course you can’t complete an infinite task in hour – but that’s what happens in Hotel! So maybe what we learn from Hilbert’s hotel is that there cannot be any completed infinite tasks. (But, if so, that doesn’t prove that the universe has a beginning.) Or Maybe it’s that the Hotel is materially infinite. That is, the Hotel is infinitely in it’s material size, requiring an infinite amount of matter. (Indeed, simply filling the hotel full of people also requires an infinite amount of matter.) So maybe what we learn from the Hotel is that there cannot be any that is actual infinite in its material size. (Which – is plausible! The universe contains only finite materials.) But, again, if that’s the lesson, then we haven’t proven that the universe had a beginning.

lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
[Slide 11] 1) Hilbert’s Hotel is impossible . 2) Hilbert’s Hotel is actually infinite C) Actual infinities are impossible . 1) A r ound square is impossible . 2) A r ound squares is a shape C) Shapes are impossible . Here’s a first stab. Maybe something like this?... But that won’t work. Compare with this argument… (Or, if you don’t like that one: A dog that’s taller than itself is impossible. A dog that’s taller than itself is a dog. So dogs are impossible) In general, the mere fact that something is impossible doesn’t, by itself, indicate WHY it’s impossible. So simply pointing out that Hilbert’s Hotel can’t exist does not, by itself, tell us that it can’t exist BECAUSE it is infinite. It could be for some other reason!

[Slide 12] The thought has to be that the Hotel is impossible because it is actually infinite . But… is that right? That suggests another way to fill in the hole in Craig’s argument.… But we haven’t actually been given any reasons for thinking that’s true. And, if you think about it, there do seem to be other explanations that might be offered. Maybe the Hotel is impossible b/c the Hotel staff completes an infinite task in a finite amount of time. That could potentially explain all the absurdities! Of course you can’t complete an infinite task in hour – but that’s what happens in Hotel! So maybe what we learn from Hilbert’s hotel is that there cannot be any completed infinite tasks. (But, if so, that doesn’t prove that the universe has a beginning.) Or Maybe it’s that the Hotel is materially infinite. That is, the Hotel is infinitely in it’s material size, requiring an infinite amount of matter. (Indeed, simply filling the hotel full of people also requires an infinite amount of matter.) So maybe what we learn from the Hotel is that there cannot be any that is actual infinite in its material size. (Which – is plausible! The universe contains only finite materials.) But, again, if that’s the lesson, then we haven’t proven that the universe had a beginning.

[Slide 13] 1) Hilbert’s Hotel is impossible because it is actually infinite . 2) If X is impossible because it is Y, then Y-things are impossible C) Actual infinities are impossible . 1) A rounded square is impossible because it is round 2) If X is impossible because it is Y, then Y-things are impossible C) Round things are impossible But set that aside . Let’s suppose it’s true that the Hotel is impossible because it is actually infinite. What follows from that? Well, to make the argument work, we’re going to need to claim that every instance of an actual infinity is going to run into the same problem – in other words, we need to claim that being actually infinitely creates the same problems everywhere and every time it shows up. The argument would then go like this... Unfortunately, that new principle is, simply, false! Compare with this argument… It’s clear what’s gone wrong with the round-square argument. What we should say is this, You cannot add the property of roundness to a square. But not because roundness is impossible – rather, because squares aren’t the kinds of things that can be round. But then… maybe the same thing is going on in the Hotel case? Maybe what we should actually say in response to the absurdity of the hotel is this, You cannot add the property of being infinite to a hotel. But not because actual infinities are impossible – rather, because hotels aren’t the kinds of things that can be actually infinite. All of that’s to say: We still need to know what, exactly, makes the Hotel impossible. That has not been clearly established. Only when we have that explanation in hand can we assess whether an infinitely old universe is also impossible for the same reason.

[Slide 14] Lastly, take another look at the claim, Actual infinities are impossible . If this is required for the Cosmological Argument, the C.A. seems to undermine the doctrine that God grants eternal life . We all must eventually perish because the alternative – a life with no ending – is, we’re told, quite impossible. ... Lots of theists believe that God is going to grant us eternal life after we die. But if actual infinities are impossible, then no life can be actually infinite. That is, if the Cosmological Argument works, it seems that the best God can do for us is give us a very long life that is ultimately finite. ... Thus, if the theist really want to insist on this point, on the impossibility of actually infinite things, then they must also be prepared to give up on parts of their faith. (Of course, that won’t be a problem for those who believe in a mere Creator God, but that’s… not very many people. Most theists believe in a personal, loving God and also believe in the promise of eternal life with that God in heaven.) That doctrine apparently cannot be sustained if one rests one’s faith on the Cosmological Arg

[Slide 15] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible { { 1. Actually infinite things do not (and cannot) exist. 2. If the universe is infinitely old, it’s an actually infinite thing. C. So, the universe is not infinitely old. So everything we’ve said to this point has been directed at the support for P1, the claim that the universe began to exist. Now, a word of caution: even if you found some of those objections moving, I don’t think we should conclude with certainty that the universe is actually infinitely old. The upshot is, at most, that the proponents of the Cosmological Argument have failed to rule out an infinitely old universe as a live possibility.

[Slide 16] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this … if universes could pop into being, so could other things – but they don’t C) So the universe has a cause (God!) Let’s finally make a couple remarks about the arguments we’ve been given in support of P2. Recall, we’re supposed to accept that whatever began to exist has a cause b/c that principle is corroborated by all of our experiences to date; plus, all of nature affirms that things do not simply come into being out nothing and for no reason.

lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
[Slide 17] This reasoning mostly tells against the kind of God that theists want to support. The theist says, “Look at this exceptionless natural rule ! It sure does seem to apply to everything . ”

[Slide 18] Exceptionless natural rule – we’ve never seen a being pop into existence Thus, surely the universe didn’t either. In particular, they reason...

[Slide 19] Exceptionless natural rule – material things are only caused by material things Thus, God is made of matter But that’s not the only exceptionless natural rule out there. Here’s one:... That is, since God supposedly created the universe, and since the universe is material, God must also be material

[Slide 20] Exceptionless natural rule – all causes of material things exist in time Thus, God exists in time. Here’s another natural rule that we’ve never seen violated:... That is, since God supposedly created the universe, and since the universe is material, God must also exist in time. (And isn’t outside of time as many theists like to believe.)

lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
[Slide 19] Exceptionless natural rule – material things are only caused by material things Thus, God is made of matter But that’s not the only exceptionless natural rule out there. Here’s one:... That is, since God supposedly created the universe, and since the universe is material, God must also be material

[Slide 20] Exceptionless natural rule – all causes of material things exist in time Thus, God exists in time. Here’s another natural rule that we’ve never seen violated:... That is, since God supposedly created the universe, and since the universe is material, God must also exist in time. (And isn’t outside of time as many theists like to believe.)

[Slide 21] Exceptionless natural rule – all minded things have mortal, fleshy bodies Thus, if God has a mind, God has a mortal fleshy body Here’s another natural rule that we’ve never seen violated:... That is, insofar as the theist believes that God has a mind (and isn’t some sort of impersonal force), the theist is apparently committed to God have a mortal, fleshy body. After all, that is an exceptionless natural rule – we’ve never seen that violated – and the theist takes those very seriously.

[Slide 22] This reasoning mostly tells against the kind of God that theists want to support. The theist says, “Look at this exceptionless natural rule ! It sure does seem to apply to everything . ” Again the point is this: IF the theist wants to insist that the universe has a cause because it’s an exceptionless natural rule that everything has a cause, then it seems like the theist must also be prepared to apply exceptionless natural rules to God. Insofar as most theists are presumably not willing to do that, most theists do not have access to this line of reasoning.

content:
[Slide 21] Exceptionless natural rule – all minded things have mortal, fleshy bodies Thus, if God has a mind, God has a mortal fleshy body Here’s another natural rule that we’ve never seen violated:... That is, insofar as the theist believes that God has a mind (and isn’t some sort of impersonal force), the theist is apparently committed to God have a mortal, fleshy body. After all, that is an exceptionless natural rule – we’ve never seen that violated – and the theist takes those very seriously.

[Slide 22] This reasoning mostly tells against the kind of God that theists want to support. The theist says, “Look at this exceptionless natural rule ! It sure does seem to apply to everything . ” Again the point is this: IF the theist wants to insist that the universe has a cause because it’s an exceptionless natural rule that everything has a cause, then it seems like the theist must also be prepared to apply exceptionless natural rules to God. Insofar as most theists are presumably not willing to do that, most theists do not have access to this line of reasoning.

[Slide 23] Lastly, we might worry that the Cosmological Argument commits the informal Fallacy of Composition . The Fallacy of Composition involves an inference of the form, Since all of X’s parts have the property P, the whole of X must also have P. This is an informal fallacy because it is often not the case that wholes have the same properties as their parts. Sometimes the whole’s properties are summations of their parts properties. Othertimes there are properties that emerge as the level of the whole but that are absent from the parts.

[Slide 24] “ Since no neuron is conscious, nothing made of neurons is conscious.”

[Slide 23] Lastly, we might worry that the Cosmological Argument commits the informal Fallacy of Composition . The Fallacy of Composition involves an inference of the form, Since all of X’s parts have the property P, the whole of X must also have P. This is an informal fallacy because it is often not the case that wholes have the same properties as their parts. Sometimes the whole’s properties are summations of their parts properties. Othertimes there are properties that emerge as the level of the whole but that are absent from the parts.

[Slide 24] “ Since no neuron is conscious, nothing made of neurons is conscious.”

[Slide 25] “ Since every U.S. state has 2 senators, the U.S. itself has 2 senators.”

[Slide 26] Why might we think the Cosmological Argument commits this Fallacy ? Quiz

lecture_title: Objections to the Kalām Cosmological Argument
content:
[Slide 25] “ Since every U.S. state has 2 senators, the U.S. itself has 2 senators.”

[Slide 26] Why might we think the Cosmological Argument commits this Fallacy ? Quiz

[Slide 27] “ Since everything in the universe has a cause, the universe itself has a cause.” This is effectively the reason we’re given for P2 – the claim that everything that begins to exist has a cause.

[Slide 28] A word of caution The Fallacy of Composition is – like all informal fallacies – a helpful rule-of-thumb . Not every argument that employs this pattern of reasoning is defective. (e.g. “All of X’s parts are made of wood. So X is made of wood”) Though, it’s worth noting, as with any informal fallacy it’s simply FALSE that every instance of this argument pattern is a mistake. For some properties and some wholes, wholes necessarily DO have the same properties as their parts. (e.g. “All of X’s parts are made of wood. So X is made of wood”) That’s a good inference. Don’t let some intro logic students talk you out of that argument merely because it has the same form as the Fallacy of Composition. Rookie mistake : Lots of intro logic students get so caught up pointing out informal fallacies that they forget that informal fallacies are crude rules-of-thumb. There’s even a name for this mistake: it’s called the Fallacy Fallacy. It’s the error of dismissing a good argument merely b/c it has the same form as an informal fallacy. (All of this is worth keeping in mind if you ever find yourself arguing on the internet. Internet people LOVE pointing out informal fallacies, but they’re really dumb about it.) ANYWAY: merely pointing out that the Cosmological Argument exhibits this same pattern of reasoning isn’t by itself sufficient for showing that there’s a reasoning error. What’s needed is some reason for thinking that the property in question (being caused) is something that the parts might have without guaranteeing that the whole will have it. I’m not sure what to say about that. At the very least, though, this line of reasoning may succeed at passing the burden to proof to the proponent of the Cosmological Argument: they need to explain why their argument doesn’t count as fallacious.

[Slide 29] P1) The universe began to exist The Cosmological Argument … according to our best physics, it Big Banged about 13.8 billion yrs ago … according to our best physics, the universe has a finite lifespan … the alternative (an infinitely old universe) is impossible P2) Whatever began to exist has a cause … all observations we’ve ever made confirm this … if universes could pop into being, so could other things – but they don’t C) So the universe has a cause (God!) So that sums up our discussion of the Cosmological Arg. There’s much much more to be said – as always. Hopefully some of those things will come up in this week’s discussion.

[Slide 1] The Fine-tuning Argument

[Slide 2] Ingredients for the Fine-tuning Argument An analogy The evidence for fine-tuning An easy principle about evidence The Fine-tuning Argument

[Slide 3] The Ontological Argument : Let’s examine the properties that define God. Surely we must conclude that a non-existent God is impossible. Thus, God exists. The Kalām Cosmological Argument: Consider the universe – a thing that is but might not have been. Surely the universe began to exist at some point, and that event requires an explanation. Why did that happen? Call the explanation, ‘God’ . The Fine-tuning Argument : Look at the universe. See how elegant and fragile it is? It seems very, very unlikely that such a thing could exist by accident. The best explanation is that the universe was carefully designed by God.

[Slide 4] You’re walking along the beach when you stumble across this really exquisite bit of driftwood. It’s a beautifully gnarled thing, all sharp curves and jagged spirals. Its surface is mottled in streaks of browns and blacks and greys, worn down to a smooth sheen. You think, WOW - What are the chances that this was created by natural processes? Well, pretty high, you realize. It IS a beautiful piece of wood, but nothing about it strikes you as obviously unlikely.

[Slide 3] The Ontological Argument : Let’s examine the properties that define God. Surely we must conclude that a non-existent God is impossible. Thus, God exists. The Kalām Cosmological Argument: Consider the universe – a thing that is but might not have been. Surely the universe began to exist at some point, and that event requires an explanation. Why did that happen? Call the explanation, ‘God’ . The Fine-tuning Argument : Look at the universe. See how elegant and fragile it is? It seems very, very unlikely that such a thing could exist by accident. The best explanation is that the universe was carefully designed by God.

[Slide 4] You’re walking along the beach when you stumble across this really exquisite bit of driftwood. It’s a beautifully gnarled thing, all sharp curves and jagged spirals. Its surface is mottled in streaks of browns and blacks and greys, worn down to a smooth sheen. You think, WOW - What are the chances that this was created by natural processes? Well, pretty high, you realize. It IS a beautiful piece of wood, but nothing about it strikes you as obviously unlikely.

[Slide 5] You continue walking. Further along the beach you spy a small pocket watch glinting in the sun. You pick it up. It has a long chain comprising hundreds of tiny gold links and, inside, you can see a number of finely meshed gears, all turning in perfect rhythm. On its outer cover has been etched with a picture of wild vines. You wonder again, What are the chances that this was created by natural processes? Very very low, you judge. It’s not literally impossible, of course. Yet thousands of things would need to go just perfectly, exactly right for this watch to look and function as it does. It’s just overwhelmingly more likely that this watch is the product of a watchmaker and not natural chance.

[Slide 6] Our best physics suggests, this is what the universe is like. If we randomly selected values for the universe’s laws/constants, the chance of the universe supporting life would be very, very, tiny. Our best physics suggests that the physical universe needed to be just perfectly, exactly right for it to support life. While it’s possible that it happened by chance, it’s very very unlikely. If the physical constants had been selected at random, the chance that the universe would support life is rather like the chances of trying to hit a 1-inch dartboard with a dart while standing on the opposite side of the universe.

It’s just overwhelmingly more likely that this watch is the product of a watchmaker and not natural chance.

[Slide 7] P1) Fine tuning : The laws of the universe could have been a little different, and if they had been, the universe would be uninhabitable The Fine-tuning Argument Here’s how the fine-tuning argument starts.

[Slide 8] In defense of P1 (i.e. Fine-tuning ) Here I’m just going to cite some physics facts offered up in the Collins article. I am not a physicist, so am not in a position to critically assess these facts. But our best physicists seem to agree that these things are true.

[Slide 15] P1) Fine tuning : The laws of the universe could have been a little different, and if they had been, the universe would be uninhabitable P2) Evidence Principle: Take two competing hypotheses, H 1 and H 2 . An observation, O, is strong evidence for H 1 when O is far more likely under H 1 than under H 2 (i.e. when O is far less surprising in an H 1 -world) The Fine-tuning Argument

[Slide 17] We all already intuitively understand of how evidence works. The Evidence Principle is supposed to formalize those intuitions.

[Slide 18] You find an object on the beach. Where did it come from? H2: Natural processes made this object H1: A person made this object

[Slide 17] We all already intuitively understand of how evidence works. The Evidence Principle is supposed to formalize those intuitions.

[Slide 18] You find an object on the beach. Where did it come from? H2: Natural processes made this object H1: A person made this object

[Slide 19] Observation : This little object has tiny, interlocking gears H1: A person made this object H2: Natural processes made this object We ask, Which is more surprising? Tiny gears when H1 is true or tiny gears when H2 is true? Obviously, the latter. Thus, our observation is strong evidence for H1. It’s not impossible that the gears were made by nature, but damn would that be surprising.

[Slide 20] You get an email from a strange account. Who sent this??? H1: It’s from someone I know! H2: It’s spam.

Tiny gears when H1 is true or tiny gears when H2 is true? Obviously, the latter. Thus, our observation is strong evidence for H1. It’s not impossible that the gears were made by nature, but damn would that be surprising.

[Slide 21] Observation: The message includes the words ‘pen1s’ and ‘hot singels’ H1: It’s from someone I know! H2: It’s spam. We ask, Which is more surprising? Hot singels when H1 is true or hot singels when H2 is true? Obviously, the former. Thus, our observation is strong evidence for H2 It’s not impossible that the email is from, like, your mom, but damn would that be surprising.

[Slide 22] You wake to noises downstairs. Who’s there??? H1: It’s your roommate. H2: It’s a no-good burglar.

[Slide 23] H1: It’s your roommate. H2: It’s a no-good burglar. Observation: Your apartment is starting to smell like burned popcorn. Quiz We ask, Which is more surprising? Burned popcorn when H1 is true or burned popcorn when H2 is true? Obviously, the latter. Thus, our observation is strong evidence for H1 It’s not impossible that someone broke into your apt and used your microwave to burn popcorn… but it would be very surprising.

[Slide 24] This is what the Evidence Principle is meant to capture. An observation is evidence for one hypothesis over another when there’s in difference in how surprising that observation would be under each hypothesis. The evidence principle says that the observation is evidence for whichever hypothesis makes the observation less surprising (if you like, you can think of the evidence principle as telling us to minimize the degree of mismatch between our evidence and our beliefs, or telling us to minimize surprise – we should adopt whatever beliefs are least surprising given our evidence

An observation, O, is strong evidence for H 1 when O is far more likely under H 1 than under H 2 (i.e. when O is far more likely in an H 1 -world than an H 2 -world) P3) Fine-tuning is far more likely under H 1 (H 1 = God exists) than under H 2 (H 2 = God does not exist) The Fine-tuning Argument P3 is just supposed to be intuitively correct. That was the whole point of the pocketwatch analogy, after all. Delicate things are more likely to result from intentional acts of creation than from random processes.

[Slide 29] What exactly does the Fine-tuning Argument purport to show us? Quiz QUIZ (select all that apply): God is all powerful God is at least powerful enough to create a universe God at least existed for awhile – long enough to create the universe God exists today God is morally good and cares about us God has a deep understanding of physics God is singular – i.e. there is a single being God, who made the universe.

[Slide 30] The Designer God God is the being that tailored our universe to support life What do we mean by “God” in the Fine-tuning Arg? Something like this: God is the being who fine-tuned the universe. It’s is absolutely imperative to note that this is not equivalent to the Greatest Possible Being God we talked about in conj w/ the ontological arg. The fine-tuning arg does not (and does not try to) prove that God is the greatest possible being. The fact the universe was fine-tuned does not show that God is unimprovable. It only shows that God has enough power to finely-tune a universe. Thus, the God we get from the Fine-Tuning ARgument is very similar to the God we get from the Cosmological Argument – it’s a Creator God. This being is responsible for causing the universe to be (and causing it to support life ) but beyond that, we cannot say much.

This being is responsible for causing the universe to be (and causing it to support life ) but beyond that, we cannot say much.

[Slide 31] What exactly does the Fine-tuning Argument purport to show us? The conclusion is rather limited: there was a fine-tuner . We don’t know much about God’s whereabouts, intentions, or powers. For all that the Fine-tuning Argument shows us, God might have up and left. Or died. Or maybe She’s still around but she hates humans and is trying to destroy us. Or maybe “God” wasn’t a single being but a committee of powerful beings who together made the universe. We don’t know! All we’ve learned is that some powerful thing at at some point fine-tuned the universe!

[Slide 1] Objections to the Fine-tuning Argument

[Slide 2] Review of the Fine tuning Argument Three Objections The God-of-the-gaps Objection + A Reply The Anthropic Principle Objection + A Reply The Multiverse Objection + A Reply

[Slide 3] Recall: the universe was compared to a pocketwatch – something intricate and delicate that almost certainly wouldn’t come to be by natural accident.

[Slide 4] P1) Fine tuning : The laws of the universe could have been a little different, and if they had been, the universe would be uninhabitable P2) Evidence Principle : Take two competing hypotheses, H 1 and H 2 . An observation, O, is strong evidence for H 1 when O is far more likely under H 1 than under H 2 (i.e. when O is far more likely in an H 1 -world than an H 2 -world) P3) Fine-tuning is far more likely under H 1 (H 1 = God exists) than under H 2 (H 2 = God does not exist) C) So, fine-tuning is strong evidence for the existence of God. The Fine-tuning Argument And here’s the full argument. P1 is an observation from physics. P2 tells us how we should assess observations as evidence P3 tells us how this observation compares, given two different hypotheses – that God exists or doesn’t.

[Slide 3] Recall: the universe was compared to a pocketwatch – something intricate and delicate that almost certainly wouldn’t come to be by natural accident.

[Slide 4] P1) Fine tuning : The laws of the universe could have been a little different, and if they had been, the universe would be uninhabitable P2) Evidence Principle : Take two competing hypotheses, H 1 and H 2 . An observation, O, is strong evidence for H 1 when O is far more likely under H 1 than under H 2 (i.e. when O is far more likely in an H 1 -world than an H 2 -world) P3) Fine-tuning is far more likely under H 1 (H 1 = God exists) than under H 2 (H 2 = God does not exist) C) So, fine-tuning is strong evidence for the existence of God. The Fine-tuning Argument And here’s the full argument. P1 is an observation from physics. P2 tells us how we should assess observations as evidence P3 tells us how this observation compares, given two different hypotheses – that God exists or doesn’t.

[Slide 17] The God of the Gaps Objection We don’t need to take the Fine-tuning Argument seriously b/c it’s just another God of the Gaps argument. Reply – Science probably won’t fill in this gap. … But, in this case, the theist has a ready reply.

[Slide 18] ATHEIST: “While we can’t yet explain the universe’s fine tuning, I’m willing to bet we’ll eventually find a General Law of Universal Structure that explains why the universe had to be this way. THEIST: “Yeah, ok, but can’t we just ask the same question about the General Law ? Why that law? Couldn’t it have been different? Is it not finely tuned?” ATHEIST: “Well – and I’m speculating here – maybe there’s some General, General Law that forced the General Law to be like that? THEIST: “But that just raises the very same questions: Why that law? Couldn’t it have been different? Is it not finely tuned? And don’t try to posit another law; I’ll just ask the same questions.” Maybe the best way to get the idea across is by imagining the dialectic as a conversation between a theist and an atheist. In short, the theist isn’t worried that the Fine-tuning Argument is a god of the gaps objection b/c it seems like there’s a permanent explanatory hole here. Even if the fine-tuning can be explained by some General Law, the hole will remain. And if that General Law can be explained by yet another law, the hole will remain. The hole is the need to explain why the laws of universe (whether the Finely tuned ones or the higher-order laws that explain the fine-tuning) are the way that they are.

[Slide 19] The Anthropic Principle Objection

[Slide 20] It’s THE FUTURE. 100 years ago, the Earth underwent a nuclear annihilation event, and now Cleveland is the only place fit for human habitation. (Literally, leaving Cleveland will kill you in seconds.) This is all common knowledge You wake up one day with no recollection of your past or present situation except that there was a bad accident. Whoa! How are you still alive? You reason, Either I got lucky or God is personally protecting me!

[Slide 21] You wake up from a coma. Whoa, how are you still alive? H1: God protected you ! H2: You got lucky. Framing the case as we did last time, we can state our two hypotheses like this:

[Slide 22] Observation: You’re in Cleveland! The only safe place. H2: You got lucky. Quiz H1: God protected you ! You ask someone where you are, and they tell you that you’re in Cleveland. You think: “Wow! 99.99…% of the earth is uninhabitable. Basically, nowhere is safe. But here I am! In Cleveland! The only safe place. What are the chances?! Surely they’re unimaginably small. And that demands explanation. The only plausible explanation is that God exists and is personally protecting me.” This is a example of bad reasoning. Why?

[Slide 21] You wake up from a coma. Whoa, how are you still alive? H1: God protected you ! H2: You got lucky. Framing the case as we did last time, we can state our two hypotheses like this:

[Slide 22] Observation: You’re in Cleveland! The only safe place. H2: You got lucky. Quiz H1: God protected you ! You ask someone where you are, and they tell you that you’re in Cleveland. You think: “Wow! 99.99…% of the earth is uninhabitable. Basically, nowhere is safe. But here I am! In Cleveland! The only safe place. What are the chances?! Surely they’re unimaginably small. And that demands explanation. The only plausible explanation is that God exists and is personally protecting me.” This is a example of bad reasoning. Why?

[Slide 23] Given that you’re alive, what are the chances you’d find alive yourself in Cleveland? If H1 is true? If H2 is true? If H1 is true, if God scooped you up to save you, where can She put you down? Only in Cleveland. So if God’s protected you, you’re basically guaranteed to find yourself in Cleveland. That’s the only place God can put you. Thus, If God is protecting you, the chances you’d find yourself in Cleveland, given that you’re alive, is basically 100%. If H2 is true, though, if God is not protecting you, then again, there’s only one place where you can be alive: Cleveland. All living people are in Cleveland. Thus, the chances that you’d find yourself in Cleveland, given that you’re alive, is also basically 100% if God isn’t protecting you. So the observation that you’re in Cleveland isn’t evidence for either hypothesis. Since all living beings are in Cleveland and since you exist, you are in Cleveland. So you don’t learn anything new when you learn that you are in Cleveland, and you certainly don’t get important evidence that God is watching out for you.

[Slide 24] Being in a fine-tuned universe is like discovering that you’re in Cleveland. Since only Cleveland supports life, all observers are in Cleveland. Since only fine-tuned universes support life, all observers are in fine-tuned universes. The Anthropic Principle Objection This is known as the anthropic principle: Since all possible observers exist in life-supporting universes, all possible observers will observe that they live in a life-supporting universe. [Slide 27] What’s our evidence? The universe we’re in supports life. OR There is a universe that supports life. Quiz The Anthropic Principle Objection The problem with the Anthropic Principle Objection is that it’s done a sneaky little bait-n-switch. We have to very careful about restating what exactly is being claimed by the original Fine-tuning Argument. QUIZ: Which of these is the evidence that the proponent of the Fine-tuning argument is trying to point to?

[Slide 28] This isn’t surprising. This is. The Anthropic Principle Objection What’s our evidence? The universe we’re in supports life. OR There is a universe that supports life. The first claim isnt surprising. OF COURSE, the universe that we’re in supports life. We’re in it! We are life! Thus, our universe must support us. But that’s not the claim at issue. The second claim is the one that’s at issue in the Fine-tuning argument – and that seems genuinely surprising in a universe that happened by accident. What’s crazy is that there is any life at all – when there might very easily have been none. Given that there’s a universe that supports life, we would of course find ourselves there. But it’s still surprinsg that there’s a universe like that.

[Slide 29] The Multiverse Objection

[Slide 29] The Multiverse Objection

[Slide 30] We live in a universe. Could there be others? With different laws?

[Slide 31] We live in a universe. Could there be others? With different laws? Maybe the universe works like this… ANd maybe each time the universe is reborn with a big bang, there are different physical laws and constants.

[Slide 32] We live in a universe. Could there be others? With different laws? Maybe there’s a multiverse like this… ANd maybe each individual universe in the multiverse has its own set of physical laws and constants. If so, each there are a bajillion diff universes, each with its own set of physical laws and constants, then it is really not surprising that one of them supports life. (and, then, of course, not surprising that we’d find ourselves in the life-supporting one.) That’s the Multiverse Objection: if we live in a multiverse, the fine-tuning of our own universe is not evidence for God.

That’s the Multiverse Objection: if we live in a multiverse, the fine-tuning of our own universe is not evidence for God.

[Slide 33] To get at the objection, it may help to consider an analogy case: Imagine you have the ability to teleport. It works like this: you snap your fingers and disappear, instantly reappearing somewhere else. But, suppose, you’re not very good at it. You basically reappear at a random location in a 500 mile radius. It’s not a reliable travel method. Now suppose you snap your fingers and reappear at home, ON THE VERY FIRST TRY, that would be pretty weird, right? You could have wound up in any random location in a 500 mile radius, but you ended up at home? Crazy. But what if you appeared at home after a solid year of trying? (everyday, *snap* nope *snap* nope *snap* nope.) That surely wouldn’t be weird. When you finally arrived in your bedroom, you would think Finally! And not WHOA, how did that happen?

[Slide 34] Suppose we live in a multiverse where each universe has different constants. Is it surprising that one supports life? That depends on how many there are! The Multiverse Objection With a bajillion universes, there’s a bajillion chances to get things just right. SO if there are lots of universes each with different physical law and constants, then its not surprising that one of them supports life. And it’s not surprising that we live in that universe. That’s like finding yourself teleported back home after a year of failed attempts.

[Slide 3] So far we’ve looked at arguments for the existence of God.

[Slide 4] The Ontological Argument : Let’s examine the properties that define God. Surely we must conclude that a non-existent God is impossible. Thus, God exists. The Kalām Cosmological Argument: Consider the universe – a thing that is but might not have been. Surely the universe began to exist at some point, and that event requires an explanation. Why did that happen? Call the explanation, ‘God’ . The Fine-tuning Argument : Look at the universe. See how elegant and fragile it is? It seems very, very unlikely that such a thing could exist by accident. The best explanation is that the universe was carefully designed by God.

[Slide 23] We’d not expect a world with lots of brutal war.

[Slide 24] Or a world with weapons of mass destruction.

[Slide 31] Why these expectations? If God is Omnipotent ... She’s never powerless to stop suffering. If God is Omniscient. ... She’s never oblivious of suffering. If God is Omnibenevolence ... She's always motivated to reduce suffering. What unites all of the examples of things we wouldn’t expect to find in the world is that all of them involve suffering of some sort of other. [Slide 32] The shorthand version of the problem of evil is simply this, If the 3-O God exists, then why is the world so full of suffering? This observation is at the heart of the Problem of Evil: We would not expect the 3-O to create a world with lots of suffering in it. But… our world has lots of suffering in it. So… what’s up with that?

[Slide 34] Necessary vs. Unnecessary Suffering So let’s draw a distinction. It turns out there are two different kinds of suffering that we see in the world, and only one of those kinds seems incompatible with the existence of the 3-O God.

[Slide 35] There are some kinds of suffering that we wouldn’t expect the 3-O God to stop. If you think about it for a second, it quickly becomes obvious that a 3-O God probably would allow some suffering to occur.

[Slide 34] Necessary vs. Unnecessary Suffering So let’s draw a distinction. It turns out there are two different kinds of suffering that we see in the world, and only one of those kinds seems incompatible with the existence of the 3-O God.

[Slide 35] There are some kinds of suffering that we wouldn’t expect the 3-O God to stop. If you think about it for a second, it quickly becomes obvious that a 3-O God probably would allow some suffering to occur.

[Slide 36] There are some kinds of suffering that we wouldn’t expect the 3-O God to stop. Consider the pain involved in receiving a vaccination shot. Clearly, it would be silly for us to argue, “Vaccines exist. Therefore, God isn’t real” It’s silly because vaccines are not a bad thing. The world would not be better without vaccines. Yes they hurt a little but God would be doing us no favors by plucking vaccines out of the world.

[Slide 37] There are some kinds of suffering that we wouldn’t expect the 3-O God to stop. Likewise, the fact that there are root canals is not evidence against the existence of the 3-O God. It would be confused to ask “If God is so great, then how come there are dentists?” Dentistry hurts but we need dentistry in order to secure mouth-health – which, I’m told, is important. Again, God would be doing us no favors by plucking all the dentists out of the world.

[Slide 40] Some kinds of suffering are necessary . The problem of evil is about unnecessary suffering . Suffering is unnecessary when it doesn’t, (a) prevent some greater bad NOR (b) secure some greater good. To sum up, we are only concerned with certain kinds of suffering. Think back on all the examples we just looked at. Why don’t vaccines count as unnecessary suffering? Because we need them to prevent some greater bad (namely, disease) Why doesn’t exercise count as unnecessary suffering ? Because we need to exercise to secure a greater good (namely, physical health)

[Slide 46] Is torture necessary suffering? (I don’t mean the kind of torture where there’s like a ticking bomb and we need information so we torture someone to get it. I mean the kind of torture that people do for pleasure or out of cruelty and spite. Like in this photo from Abu Ghraib. Torture-for-pleasure does not seem to prevent a greater suffering nor achieve a greater benefit. It seems like unnecessary evil.)

[Slide 47] Likewise genocide. It’s REALLY NOT OBVIOUS that every genocide in the history of mankind was necessary for achieving some greater good or preventing some greater evil. (WHAT GREATER EVIL? Genocide is like the greatest evil. What evil could possibly be so bad that we have to Commit Genocide to prevent it?)

[Slide 48] What about things like floods, mudslides, tornadoes, hurricanes, volcano eruptions, and droughts? What greater benefit or greater evil do these things get us?

[Slide 49] What about diseases like Alzheimer’s? What is the greater good that Alzheimer’s gets us? What is the greater evil it prevents?

[Slide 50] “Suppose in some distant forest lightning strikes a dead tree, resulting in a forest fire. In the fire a fawn is trapped, horribly burned, and lies in terrible agony for several days before death relieves its suffering… There does not appear to be any greater good… nor does there seem to be any equally bad or worse evil so connected to the fawn's suffering that it would have had to occur had the fawn's suffering been prevented.” – William Rowe Here’s a case from the Rowe reading for this week. This will turn out to be one of hardest cases for us to make sense of. (It’s worth noting, although Rowe’s case is fictional – he’s not reporting on some event he witnessed – the case is very very plausible. It is overwhelmingly likely that something like this has happened in the world. In fact, it is overwhelmingly like that this has happened thousands upon thousands of times.)

[Slide 52] What exactly does the Problem of Evil purport to show us? Quiz Quiz Question: Does the Problem of Evil argument conflict with the Fine-tuning argument?

[Slide 53] What exactly does the Problem of Evil purport to show us? The conclusion is rather limited: the 3-O God does not exist. There could still be a 2-O God ! Or a Creator God ! Turns out: No. You can accept both the Fine-tuning Argument and the Problem of Evil without contradiction. … Of course, we’d have to think carefully about which O we jettison. Maybe God is not all-knowing – is ignorant or oblivious? Maybe God is not all-powerful – has real limitation in what She can accomplish? Maybe God is not perfectly good – is wicked or indifferent to suffering? Maybe God is simply not here. Maybe She made the universe and then left or died. That’s perfectly compatible with the God of the Fine-tuning argument. Maybe She fine tuned the universe and then peaced out. And that’s why there’s so much unnecessary suffering.

Maybe God is not all-knowing – is ignorant or oblivious? Maybe God is not all-powerful – has real limitation in what She can accomplish? Maybe God is not perfectly good – is wicked or indifferent to suffering? Maybe God is simply not here. Maybe She made the universe and then left or died. That’s perfectly compatible with the God of the Fine-tuning argument. Maybe She fine tuned the universe and then peaced out. And that’s why there’s so much unnecessary suffering.

[Slide 1] (Bad) Responses to the Problem of Evil

[Slide 2] Recap Three (bad) responses God has a plan Without the bad, we couldn’t appreciate the good The original sin doctrine

[Slide 3] The shorthand version of the problem of evil is simply this, If the 3-O God exists, then why is the world so full of suffering?

[Slide 4] Some kinds of suffering are necessary . The problem of evil is about unnecessary suffering . Suffering is unnecessary when it doesn’t, (a) prevent some greater bad NOR (b) secure some greater good. Recall this VERY IMPORTANT distinction between necessary suffering and unnecessary suffering. The Problem of Evil is only concerned with Unnecessary Suffering. (Thus, the existence of vaccines or dentists or exercise is not being used as evidence against the existence of god.)

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 5] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil And here’s the full argument.

[Slide 6] Responding to the Problem of Evil

[Slide 7] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C The argument is valid, so we really only have 3 options. We can Reject P1, arguing that a 3-O God actually would allow unnecessary suffering We can Reject P2, arguing that there actually isn’t any unnecessary suffering Or We can Accept C, acknowledging that the 3-O God does not exist.

[Slide 8] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option To my knowledge, almost no one has tried to argue that P1 is false. It just… looks very obviously correct, to both theists and atheists. How could an all-powerful, all-knowing, perfectly good being possibly allow unnecessary suffering? And of course, most theists aren’t going to like the third option. They want to continue believing in a 3-O God. Thus, we’re left with Option 2: we have to find a way to reject P2. We must argue, There has never been – not even once – an instance of unnecessary suffering.

C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C The argument is valid, so we really only have 3 options. We can Reject P1, arguing that a 3-O God actually would allow unnecessary suffering We can Reject P2, arguing that there actually isn’t any unnecessary suffering Or We can Accept C, acknowledging that the 3-O God does not exist.

[Slide 8] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option To my knowledge, almost no one has tried to argue that P1 is false. It just… looks very obviously correct, to both theists and atheists. How could an all-powerful, all-knowing, perfectly good being possibly allow unnecessary suffering? And of course, most theists aren’t going to like the third option. They want to continue believing in a 3-O God. Thus, we’re left with Option 2: we have to find a way to reject P2. We must argue, There has never been – not even once – an instance of unnecessary suffering.

[Slide 9] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture And here, let me offer a shortlist of candidates for instances of unnecessary evil… (Tay-sachs is a genetic nerve disorder that manifests at around 6-months. The child experiences really offer nerve problems for a few years and then dies. Every time – A really awful disease.)... Again, in order to succeed, the argument only need for there to have been ONE SINGLE TIME IN THE HISTORY OF THE WORLD WHERE THERE WAS UNNECESSARY SUFFERING So even if it turns out that most of the items on this list are instances of Necessary suffering, the argument will still go through. The theists tasks is to tackle the whole list. (And not just this list, of course, This is just a short version of a much longer list of candidates.)

[Slide 10] The conversation goes like this… Atheist: “What about X? Doesn’t X count as unnecessary suffering?” Theist: “No. Plausibly, we need X in order to prevent <some greater evil> or to secure <some greater good.>” Atheist: “But what about Y? Doesn’t Y count as unnecessary suffering?” Theist: “No. Plausibly, we need Y…” This is a theodicy. In English, we have a special word for this kind of story. It’s called a “theodicy”. A theodicy is an explanation that a certain variety of suffering is actually necessary (and, thus, isn’t evidence against the existence of God.)

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 9] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture And here, let me offer a shortlist of candidates for instances of unnecessary evil… (Tay-sachs is a genetic nerve disorder that manifests at around 6-months. The child experiences really offer nerve problems for a few years and then dies. Every time – A really awful disease.)... Again, in order to succeed, the argument only need for there to have been ONE SINGLE TIME IN THE HISTORY OF THE WORLD WHERE THERE WAS UNNECESSARY SUFFERING So even if it turns out that most of the items on this list are instances of Necessary suffering, the argument will still go through. The theists tasks is to tackle the whole list. (And not just this list, of course, This is just a short version of a much longer list of candidates.)

[Slide 10] The conversation goes like this… Atheist: “What about X? Doesn’t X count as unnecessary suffering?” Theist: “No. Plausibly, we need X in order to prevent <some greater evil> or to secure <some greater good.>” Atheist: “But what about Y? Doesn’t Y count as unnecessary suffering?” Theist: “No. Plausibly, we need Y…” This is a theodicy. In English, we have a special word for this kind of story. It’s called a “theodicy”. A theodicy is an explanation that a certain variety of suffering is actually necessary (and, thus, isn’t evidence against the existence of God.)

[Slide 11] What about ass hair ? Is ass hair an example of unnecessary suffering? Theodicy: No. Body hair is useful in areas that are hard to keep dry. We need ass hair (as we need armpit hair) to wick sweat away from spots that doesn’t get much air exposure. We did a little practice with this last lecture, but it’s worth trying our hand at it again. So, here’s an example. Maybe the atheist says, “Look at ass hair! It’s kinda gross. It’s kinda messy. It’s an extra place to shed from. No one really likes it. Surely ass hair is an instance of unnecessary suffering.” The theist might then give a theodicy , a plausible story the can explain why ass hair might be a necessary evil ... I don’t know if that’s true of course, but the point is that it’s plausible. This is a reasonably satisfying story about the necessity of ass hair. I’m content to let this one go – to not insist that ass hair is evidence that God doesn’t exist.

[Slide 12] What about nausea ? Is nausea an example of unnecessary suffering? Theodicy: No. We need the bad-feeling of nausea to (a) strongly disincentivize the ingestion of poisons and (b) to warn us about an eminent vomiting episode. Quiz Nausea feels terrible. We might wonder, “Wouldn’t the world be better w/o it? Why does God allow nausea?” Think about that for a second. The thought is: nausea is needed to prevent a greater evil – accidental poisoning. I don’t know if that’s true of course, but the point is that it’s plausible. This is a reasonably satisfying story about the necessity of nausea. I’m content to let this one go – to not insist that nausea is evidence that God doesn’t exist.

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 11] What about ass hair ? Is ass hair an example of unnecessary suffering? Theodicy: No. Body hair is useful in areas that are hard to keep dry. We need ass hair (as we need armpit hair) to wick sweat away from spots that doesn’t get much air exposure. We did a little practice with this last lecture, but it’s worth trying our hand at it again. So, here’s an example. Maybe the atheist says, “Look at ass hair! It’s kinda gross. It’s kinda messy. It’s an extra place to shed from. No one really likes it. Surely ass hair is an instance of unnecessary suffering.” The theist might then give a theodicy , a plausible story the can explain why ass hair might be a necessary evil ... I don’t know if that’s true of course, but the point is that it’s plausible. This is a reasonably satisfying story about the necessity of ass hair. I’m content to let this one go – to not insist that ass hair is evidence that God doesn’t exist.

[Slide 12] What about nausea ? Is nausea an example of unnecessary suffering? Theodicy: No. We need the bad-feeling of nausea to (a) strongly disincentivize the ingestion of poisons and (b) to warn us about an eminent vomiting episode. Quiz Nausea feels terrible. We might wonder, “Wouldn’t the world be better w/o it? Why does God allow nausea?” Think about that for a second. The thought is: nausea is needed to prevent a greater evil – accidental poisoning. I don’t know if that’s true of course, but the point is that it’s plausible. This is a reasonably satisfying story about the necessity of nausea. I’m content to let this one go – to not insist that nausea is evidence that God doesn’t exist.

[Slide 13] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture I should make one important note before we look at some theodicies: The challenge for the theist isn’t merely to show that SOME war or SOME disease is necessary. That’s not enough. The Problem of Evil says that “there has been at least one instance of necessary suffering.” That’s it. At least one. So consider torture. Sometimes people will respond to this case by arguing that sometimes torture is the right thing to do to prevent a greater evil – like in a ticking bomb scenario or whatever. But… so what? Even if that’s true, that’s not nearly enough. To remove torture from this list, the theist has to show that every act of torture that’s ever been committed by anyone anywhere was a necessary evil. Which… yeah. That’s gonna be hard. There’s been a lot of torture-for-fun in the history of mankind. Gonna be hard to explain those cases away as necessary evil. And of course, similar remarks apply to our other examples. The theists needs to explain ALL wars, ALL genocides, ALL natural disasters – not just some wars, genocides, or natural disasters.

[Slide 14] Three bad theodicies I want to address some very common (and pretty terrible) replies.

And of course, similar remarks apply to our other examples. The theists needs to explain ALL wars, ALL genocides, ALL natural disasters – not just some wars, genocides, or natural disasters.

[Slide 14] Three bad theodicies I want to address some very common (and pretty terrible) replies.

[Slide 15] All suffering is a necessary part of God’s plan This is often the first theodicy you hear from people. It’s also distinctively bad.

[Slide 16] Atheist: “What about genocide? Isn’t that an example of unnecessary evil?” Theist: “Even genocides are a part of God’s plan.” Atheist: “I wasn’t asking whether God has a plan; I was asking whether it’s any good . Cuz it looks kinda shitty!” Theist: “Well it’s God’s Plan. So it’s morally perfect.” Atheist: “I don’t think ‘ morally perfect ’ is ever an apt description for any plan that signs off on genocide.” Theist: “Maybe we simply can’t understand the plan. It’s perfect but also too complex for us.” The God’s Plan Theodicy Quiz One way to see what’s defective about this theodicy is by imagining what it would sound like to defend it. Let me have you try this one first: Why is this an unsatisfying response to the Problem of Evil?

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 15] All suffering is a necessary part of God’s plan This is often the first theodicy you hear from people. It’s also distinctively bad.

[Slide 16] Atheist: “What about genocide? Isn’t that an example of unnecessary evil?” Theist: “Even genocides are a part of God’s plan.” Atheist: “I wasn’t asking whether God has a plan; I was asking whether it’s any good . Cuz it looks kinda shitty!” Theist: “Well it’s God’s Plan. So it’s morally perfect.” Atheist: “I don’t think ‘ morally perfect ’ is ever an apt description for any plan that signs off on genocide.” Theist: “Maybe we simply can’t understand the plan. It’s perfect but also too complex for us.” The God’s Plan Theodicy Quiz One way to see what’s defective about this theodicy is by imagining what it would sound like to defend it. Let me have you try this one first: Why is this an unsatisfying response to the Problem of Evil?

[Slide 17] The God’s Plan Theodicy Objection #1 This isn’t a solution. It’s a restatement of the problem. Objection #2 This theodicy commits some circular reasoning . The problem is 2-fold This theodicy isn’t an explanation; it’s just a restatement of the question but posed as an answer. It’s true that if the 3-O God exists, then her plan is perfect and all suffering is necessary. But that’s not a reason to think that the 3-O God does exist nor a reason to believe that there exists a perfect plan. The question is Can we make sense of all the suffering in the world as necessary? And this theodicy amounts to the answer, No. We cannot make sense of suffering. We are too stupid to understand his plan, which is why the world clearly appears to include lots of unnecessary suffering. But, hopefully you can see, that’s not a solution! That’s quitting! (also, the atheist at this point might simply reply, “Isn’t our stupidity ANOTHER example of unnecessary evil? Surely a 3-O God could have made us smart enough to understand her morally perfect plan? The fact that we’re too stupid to understand Her perfect plan is another instance of unnecessary evil.”) The second problem with this theodicy is that it’s flagrantly circular. We ask, “Is God’s plan a good plan?” The theist answers, Yes it must be perfect because God is morally perfect. We ask, “Well, why should we think God is morally perfect?” The theist replies, Just look at the evidence! Everything around us is perfect! There is nothing out of place; everything in accordance with God’s plan. We reply, “Uhhh… mostly what I see is suffering. So... our evidence actually suggests it’s a bad plan.” The theist replies, No it’s a good plan! It’s God’s plan and She’s morally perfect! And so on. The theist never actually manages to explain anything at all.

[Slide 18] Without suffering, we couldn’t appreciate the good stuff Here’s a different theodicy. In fact, this is a lot of people’s second reaction to hearing the problem of evil. “But don’t you need the bad with the good? We need a rich variety of experiences to have a full and happy life.”

[Slide 20] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture Look again at our list of unnecessary evil candidates. Think about how stupid it sounds to say something like, “Without Alzheimer’s, we wouldn’t be able to value the good in life!” OR “If baby deer didn’t sometimes burn to death in the forest, we couldn’t appreciate the finer things!” OR “How can we truly hold dear our friends and family if they don’t sometimes get tortured in prison or murdered in race-based pogroms?”

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 19] Objection #1 There’s an important difference between, some evil is necessary to enjoy the good & all this evil is necessary to enjoy the good

[Slide 20] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture Look again at our list of unnecessary evil candidates. Think about how stupid it sounds to say something like, “Without Alzheimer’s, we wouldn’t be able to value the good in life!” OR “If baby deer didn’t sometimes burn to death in the forest, we couldn’t appreciate the finer things!” OR “How can we truly hold dear our friends and family if they don’t sometimes get tortured in prison or murdered in race-based pogroms?”

[Slide 21] Objection #1 There’s an important difference between, some evil is necessary to enjoy the good & all this evil is necessary to enjoy the good So, the first claim might be true – maybe we can’t enjoy the good things in life with a little bit of suffering. But the second claim seems very very false. And it’s the second claim that we’ll need if we want to rescue the 3-O God from the Problem of Evil.

[Slide 22] Objection #2 It seems doubtful that suffering consistently enhances our experiences of the good. Objection #1 There’s an important difference between, some evil is necessary to enjoy the good & all this evil is necessary to enjoy the good

content:
[Slide 21] Objection #1 There’s an important difference between, some evil is necessary to enjoy the good & all this evil is necessary to enjoy the good So, the first claim might be true – maybe we can’t enjoy the good things in life with a little bit of suffering. But the second claim seems very very false. And it’s the second claim that we’ll need if we want to rescue the 3-O God from the Problem of Evil.

[Slide 22] Objection #2 It seems doubtful that suffering consistently enhances our experiences of the good. Objection #1 There’s an important difference between, some evil is necessary to enjoy the good & all this evil is necessary to enjoy the good

[Slide 23] Imagine that you have been presented with a lovely cake. It is your favorite variety of cake. You love it so much you should marry it. Close your eyes, and I want you to imagine, in detail, how very good this cake is. It is moist, and rich, and sweet but not too sweet, and just so utterly without equal, as far as cakes go. Got it? Now: Did you know that, between 1983 and1985, approx 150,00 to 200,000 East Timorese were killed by Indonesian forces in what is now generally regarded as a campaign of genocide? Ok! Back to cake! Are you imagining it again? Is it any better now that you have a more complete grasp of the depth of the world’s suffering?

[Slide 24] Objection #2 It seems doubtful that suffering consistently enhances our experiences of the good. Objection #1 There’s an important difference between, some evil is necessary to enjoy the good & all this evil is necessary to enjoy the good Probably not. It’s doubtful that understanding or experiencing suffering consistently makes the good parts of life better.

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 23] Imagine that you have been presented with a lovely cake. It is your favorite variety of cake. You love it so much you should marry it. Close your eyes, and I want you to imagine, in detail, how very good this cake is. It is moist, and rich, and sweet but not too sweet, and just so utterly without equal, as far as cakes go. Got it? Now: Did you know that, between 1983 and1985, approx 150,00 to 200,000 East Timorese were killed by Indonesian forces in what is now generally regarded as a campaign of genocide? Ok! Back to cake! Are you imagining it again? Is it any better now that you have a more complete grasp of the depth of the world’s suffering?

[Slide 24] Objection #2 It seems doubtful that suffering consistently enhances our experiences of the good. Objection #1 There’s an important difference between, some evil is necessary to enjoy the good & all this evil is necessary to enjoy the good Probably not. It’s doubtful that understanding or experiencing suffering consistently makes the good parts of life better.

[Slide 25] All suffering is the result of Original Sin One final theodicy you commonly hear goes like this. “Here’s why all suffering is necessary: it’s not really God’s doing at all…”

[Slide 26] Objection #1 How does this even help us understand the moral perfection of God’s plan? Objection #2 If anything, this response makes human suffering sound positively unjust . The doctrine of Original Sin isn’t the right sort of story. It tells us what caused there to be suffering. It doesn’t tell us why all the suffering is justified. It doesn’t make sense to say “All suffering is necessary because of Adam and Eve and the apple.” That’s like saying “Yes, I burned down your house, but it’s not my fault b/c fire was invented thousands of years ago.” If we try to force the doctrine of Original Sin into the right sort of story (i.e. into a justification of suffering) the result is genuinely repulsive. That theodicy says, it is morally perfect for all humanity to suffer and suffer and suffer because one time, thousands of years ago, a couple people did a bad thing with some fruit. Since that bad thing, is has been morally perfect for God to stand back and watch the genocides.”

lecture_title: Bad Responses to the Problem of Evil
content:
[Slide 25] All suffering is the result of Original Sin One final theodicy you commonly hear goes like this. “Here’s why all suffering is necessary: it’s not really God’s doing at all…”

[Slide 26] Objection #1 How does this even help us understand the moral perfection of God’s plan? Objection #2 If anything, this response makes human suffering sound positively unjust . The doctrine of Original Sin isn’t the right sort of story. It tells us what caused there to be suffering. It doesn’t tell us why all the suffering is justified. It doesn’t make sense to say “All suffering is necessary because of Adam and Eve and the apple.” That’s like saying “Yes, I burned down your house, but it’s not my fault b/c fire was invented thousands of years ago.” If we try to force the doctrine of Original Sin into the right sort of story (i.e. into a justification of suffering) the result is genuinely repulsive. That theodicy says, it is morally perfect for all humanity to suffer and suffer and suffer because one time, thousands of years ago, a couple people did a bad thing with some fruit. Since that bad thing, is has been morally perfect for God to stand back and watch the genocides.”

[Slide 27] This makes Original Sin sounds like North Korea’s 3 Generations Policy , which says: If one person is found guilty of a crime and sent to a prison camp, so is (a) their entire family and (b) all future descendants for 3 generations. I don’t think we make God look morally perfect by describing Her as very similar in governing style to North Korea.

[Slide 28] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil So those are 3 bad theodicies. Next time, we’ll look at some more promising theodicies, ones that do actually help the theist defend her faith in the 3-O God.

content:
