(b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil So those are 3 bad theodicies. Next time, we’ll look at some more promising theodicies, ones that do actually help the theist defend her faith in the 3-O God.

[Slide 1] The Free Will Theodicy

[Slide 2] Recap The Free Will Theodicy Motivations Replies Last time we looked at 3 bad responses to the problem of evil – solutions that don’t even look plausible on their face. Today I want to talk about a much better solution: the free will theodicy. This theodicy has been historically very popular and influential.

[Slide 3] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option To my knowledge, almost no one has tried to argue that P1 is false. It just… looks very obviously correct, to both theists and atheists. How could an all-powerful, all-knowing, perfectly good being possibly allow unnecessary suffering? And of course, most theists aren’t going to like the third option. They want to continue believing in a 3-O God. Thus, we’re left with Option 2: we have to find a way to reject P2. We must argue, There has never been – not even once – an instance of unnecessary suffering.

[Slide 4] The Free Will Theodicy

[Slide 3] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option To my knowledge, almost no one has tried to argue that P1 is false. It just… looks very obviously correct, to both theists and atheists. How could an all-powerful, all-knowing, perfectly good being possibly allow unnecessary suffering? And of course, most theists aren’t going to like the third option. They want to continue believing in a 3-O God. Thus, we’re left with Option 2: we have to find a way to reject P2. We must argue, There has never been – not even once – an instance of unnecessary suffering.

[Slide 4] The Free Will Theodicy

[Slide 5] Imagine God’s position in the moment you decide to do something wrong. She can either, Let you act OR Step in and stop you What should She do? One good way to get at the free will theodicy is to imagine God’s position as she watches you choose to do something bad. (PICTURED: God watching over your shoulder as you send an unsolicited dick pic or something)

[Slide 6] It might seem like no-big-deal for God to step in. But consider for a moment what the world would look like if god always did that. Imagine a world in which God makes all of our choices for us. She picks out our outfits in the morning, decides what we’ll eat, where we’ll work, who we’ll live with, how much Netflix we’ll watch, how often we’ll sing God’s praises, etc. God decides whom we’ll love and with whom we’ll procreate – and then, of course, once our children are born, God takes full control of their lives too. Each life is, of course, morally perfect: You always do the right thing (b/c you have no choice.) You love your neighbor as yourself (b/c you have no choice.) You love God with all your heart, mind, soul, and strength (b/c you have no choice.) (think of this as the Puppetmaster world – where God is pulling all the strings)

[Slide 5] Imagine God’s position in the moment you decide to do something wrong. She can either, Let you act OR Step in and stop you What should She do? One good way to get at the free will theodicy is to imagine God’s position as she watches you choose to do something bad. (PICTURED: God watching over your shoulder as you send an unsolicited dick pic or something)

[Slide 6] It might seem like no-big-deal for God to step in. But consider for a moment what the world would look like if god always did that. Imagine a world in which God makes all of our choices for us. She picks out our outfits in the morning, decides what we’ll eat, where we’ll work, who we’ll live with, how much Netflix we’ll watch, how often we’ll sing God’s praises, etc. God decides whom we’ll love and with whom we’ll procreate – and then, of course, once our children are born, God takes full control of their lives too. Each life is, of course, morally perfect: You always do the right thing (b/c you have no choice.) You love your neighbor as yourself (b/c you have no choice.) You love God with all your heart, mind, soul, and strength (b/c you have no choice.) (think of this as the Puppetmaster world – where God is pulling all the strings)

[Slide 7] This is a defective world – both from God’s perspective and ours . Here’s the punchline: a lot of people think that this world sucks. There’s a good reason why God doesn’t make all of our decisions for us – why she doesn’t consistently intervene to make us act in ways she’d prefer. There’s little value in loving God and one another if we don’t freely choose it There’s little value in being good unless we have to struggle with evil In our puppet world, no one has the power to do make or change anything – and that’s part of why the world seems defective God should ask herself, Why even bother with humans if I’m just going to puppetmaster them?

[Slide 8] Observation There’s something really important about human freedom – about our ability to be the authors of our own decisions. This has important religious implications. It’s positively good – from God’s perspective – that humans be allowed to make decisions for themselves. It’s positively bad for God to make all our decisions for us. Hopefully you can: this Good Thing – human freedom – may give us a way to make sense of evils as necessary.

[Slide 7] This is a defective world – both from God’s perspective and ours . Here’s the punchline: a lot of people think that this world sucks. There’s a good reason why God doesn’t make all of our decisions for us – why she doesn’t consistently intervene to make us act in ways she’d prefer. There’s little value in loving God and one another if we don’t freely choose it There’s little value in being good unless we have to struggle with evil In our puppet world, no one has the power to do make or change anything – and that’s part of why the world seems defective God should ask herself, Why even bother with humans if I’m just going to puppetmaster them?

[Slide 8] Observation There’s something really important about human freedom – about our ability to be the authors of our own decisions. This has important religious implications. It’s positively good – from God’s perspective – that humans be allowed to make decisions for themselves. It’s positively bad for God to make all our decisions for us. Hopefully you can: this Good Thing – human freedom – may give us a way to make sense of evils as necessary.

[Slide 9] What’s so great about freedom ? Free choices have weight . The s truggle to choose and the ability to do otherwise give our choices meaning. The freedom to make and re-make our world is a valuable gift. Before we look at the details of that theodicy, though, let’s spend a little more time thinking about freedom and why we might think it’s really good and importna.t Here are 3 different suggestions.

[Slide 10] Imagine a bratty child who apologizes to you only after her parents demands it. Or a movie where the hero gets saved at the last minute by God, rather than by the villain. Or a surprise Valentine’s Day card gushing about how great you are. Signed by your mom. Here are some cases that seem to support these claims about the value of free choice through struggle ... The point is this, these gestures aren’t very meaningful. And the reason in each case seems to be roughly the same, These actions do not represent free choices through struggle. They aren’t powerful because they aren’t backed up by the right sort of surprising, impressive freedom.

[Slide 9] What’s so great about freedom ? Free choices have weight . The s truggle to choose and the ability to do otherwise give our choices meaning. The freedom to make and re-make our world is a valuable gift. Before we look at the details of that theodicy, though, let’s spend a little more time thinking about freedom and why we might think it’s really good and importna.t Here are 3 different suggestions.

[Slide 10] Imagine a bratty child who apologizes to you only after her parents demands it. Or a movie where the hero gets saved at the last minute by God, rather than by the villain. Or a surprise Valentine’s Day card gushing about how great you are. Signed by your mom. Here are some cases that seem to support these claims about the value of free choice through struggle ... The point is this, these gestures aren’t very meaningful. And the reason in each case seems to be roughly the same, These actions do not represent free choices through struggle. They aren’t powerful because they aren’t backed up by the right sort of surprising, impressive freedom.

[Slide 11] The Free Will Theodicy Freedom is good because it gives our choices weight, meaning, & power. It forces us to struggle and grow. Freedom is bad because we may misuse it to do evil. Thus, God faces a choice, preserve our freedom and allow evil OR prevent evil and revoke our freedom Clearly, then, human freedom has both pros and cons... And we can see this puts God in a bit of pickle… The Free Will Theodicy consists of the view that the first choice is allows better. It’s always better for God to preserve our freedom and allow evil than to prevent evil and revoke our freedom

[Slide 12] The Free Will Theodicy It is consistently worse to violate a human’s free will than to allow evil. Thus, the suffering that stems from human choices is necessary suffering. More precisely, this is the view.

[Slide 11] The Free Will Theodicy Freedom is good because it gives our choices weight, meaning, & power. It forces us to struggle and grow. Freedom is bad because we may misuse it to do evil. Thus, God faces a choice, preserve our freedom and allow evil OR prevent evil and revoke our freedom Clearly, then, human freedom has both pros and cons... And we can see this puts God in a bit of pickle… The Free Will Theodicy consists of the view that the first choice is allows better. It’s always better for God to preserve our freedom and allow evil than to prevent evil and revoke our freedom

[Slide 12] The Free Will Theodicy It is consistently worse to violate a human’s free will than to allow evil. Thus, the suffering that stems from human choices is necessary suffering. More precisely, this is the view.

[Slide 13] Why doesn’t God force parents to love their children? Free Will Theodicy : She knows that chosen love is a greater good, one that can only be secured by respecting parents’ freedom. Recall a theodicy is a story that a theist tells to make sense of why some particular instances of suffering are necessary – needed for some greater good or to prevent some greater evil. So here’s an example of the free will theodicy in action.

[Slide 14] “ Cat burning was a form of zoosadistic entertainment in 17th century Paris, France. In this form of entertainment, people would gather dozens of cats in a net and hoist them high into the air from a special bundle onto a bonfire… The assembled people “shrieked with laughter as the animals, howling with pain, were singed, roasted, and finally carbonized. ” – Wikipedia , “Cat Burning” Here’s another example, using a strange historical evil. People used to burn cats for fun.

[Slide 13] Why doesn’t God force parents to love their children? Free Will Theodicy : She knows that chosen love is a greater good, one that can only be secured by respecting parents’ freedom. Recall a theodicy is a story that a theist tells to make sense of why some particular instances of suffering are necessary – needed for some greater good or to prevent some greater evil. So here’s an example of the free will theodicy in action.

[Slide 14] “ Cat burning was a form of zoosadistic entertainment in 17th century Paris, France. In this form of entertainment, people would gather dozens of cats in a net and hoist them high into the air from a special bundle onto a bonfire… The assembled people “shrieked with laughter as the animals, howling with pain, were singed, roasted, and finally carbonized. ” – Wikipedia , “Cat Burning” Here’s another example, using a strange historical evil. People used to burn cats for fun.

[Slide 15] Why did God allow people to burn cats for fun? Free Will Theodicy – She knew that it would be worse to violate the people’s free will than to prevent the cats’ suffering. Here’s a bit of historical evil. People used to burn cats for fun.

[Slide 16] Why, according to the Free Will Theodicy, doesn’t God stop famines? Free Will Theodicy – She knows that it would be worse to violate our free will (by, say, forcing us to plant more food / charge fairer prices / donate more to charity) than to prevent the famine Herself. Quiz

[Slide 15] Why did God allow people to burn cats for fun? Free Will Theodicy – She knew that it would be worse to violate the people’s free will than to prevent the cats’ suffering. Here’s a bit of historical evil. People used to burn cats for fun.

[Slide 16] Why, according to the Free Will Theodicy, doesn’t God stop famines? Free Will Theodicy – She knows that it would be worse to violate our free will (by, say, forcing us to plant more food / charge fairer prices / donate more to charity) than to prevent the famine Herself. Quiz

[Slide 17] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture Look again at our list of unnecessary evil candidates. A lot of them seem to stem from human choices – and so lots of them can be explained by the free will theodicy. BUT the free will theodicy doesn’t obviously explain all instances of suffering. It’s not obvious that we can explain Rowe’s fawn who burns to death in the forest or the badness of Alzheimer’s by pointing to the value of human freedom. When God is deciding whether to let someone get Alzheimer’s, it’s not clear that She is deciding whether to violate someone’s freedom. Surely God could prevent disease without literally STOPPING someone from making a choice – she could just wave her hand, and all the mis-folded proteins in the person’s brain would disappear, leaving that person none the wiser and also Alzheimer’s free. That sounds like a win-win.

[Slide 18] Can the Free Will Theodicy explain the necessity of natural evils? Swinburne: Yes . God allows, e.g., rabies and tornadoes so that we can learn of those things – and that learning itself enhances our freedom . That said, many proponents of the Free Will Theodicy insist that it does and can explain the necessity of natural evil. The Swinburne reading for this module, for instance, offers us a story about why natural evils are necessary parts of human freedom. His story is that God allows us to experience natural evils – things like tornadoes and rabies – so that we can acquire important knowledge about the natural world. By watching people die of rabies, we both acquire information about the disease itself AND acquire extra options in the future. In the future, we’ll be able to choose whether to intentionally infect people with rabies, knowing what that will do to them, or can choose to research a cure. Those extra options mean extra freedom. And since extra freedom is super super valuable, natural evils are necessary suffering.

[Slide 17] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture Look again at our list of unnecessary evil candidates. A lot of them seem to stem from human choices – and so lots of them can be explained by the free will theodicy. BUT the free will theodicy doesn’t obviously explain all instances of suffering. It’s not obvious that we can explain Rowe’s fawn who burns to death in the forest or the badness of Alzheimer’s by pointing to the value of human freedom. When God is deciding whether to let someone get Alzheimer’s, it’s not clear that She is deciding whether to violate someone’s freedom. Surely God could prevent disease without literally STOPPING someone from making a choice – she could just wave her hand, and all the mis-folded proteins in the person’s brain would disappear, leaving that person none the wiser and also Alzheimer’s free. That sounds like a win-win.

[Slide 18] Can the Free Will Theodicy explain the necessity of natural evils? Swinburne: Yes . God allows, e.g., rabies and tornadoes so that we can learn of those things – and that learning itself enhances our freedom . That said, many proponents of the Free Will Theodicy insist that it does and can explain the necessity of natural evil. The Swinburne reading for this module, for instance, offers us a story about why natural evils are necessary parts of human freedom. His story is that God allows us to experience natural evils – things like tornadoes and rabies – so that we can acquire important knowledge about the natural world. By watching people die of rabies, we both acquire information about the disease itself AND acquire extra options in the future. In the future, we’ll be able to choose whether to intentionally infect people with rabies, knowing what that will do to them, or can choose to research a cure. Those extra options mean extra freedom. And since extra freedom is super super valuable, natural evils are necessary suffering.

[Slide 19] Can the Free Will Theodicy explain the necessity of natural evils? Swinburne: Yes . God allows, e.g., rabies and tornadoes so that we can learn of those things – and that learning itself enhances our freedom . Stump : If God hadn’t created rabies in the first place , we wouldn’t need to learn about it. So this doesn’t explain why there is rabies . ( c.f. if God unleashed Super Death Pox on the world tomorrow, we wouldn’t say, “ Thank you God, for this wonderful learning opportunity! ”) As Stump points out, though, that story seems flagrantly circular… As for Swinburne’s the claim that extra knowledge and extra choices enhances our freedom, i think it’s ultimately easier to assess that part of the view when looking at cases of moral evil – which is where the Free Will Theodicy is most plausible.

[Slide 20] If you’re still feeling shaky about that reply from Stump, try this: Imagine you have a friend who keeps throwing punches at your head. He hits you right in the jaw. You say, “OW. STOP. Why are you doing that?” And he says, “I’m trying to train you to dodge punches.” To which you reply, “Why? I don’t need to learn how to do that. I don’t ever get into fights.” Your friend says, “Yes, but in the future, I’m going to continue trying to punch you in the head, so I’m teaching you how to dodge NOW so that you can avoid my punches in the future.” Hopefully, you can see why that reply is unsatisfying and circular. Your friend has absolutely failed to justify his decision to punch you in the face. If he would just stop being an asshole and throwing punches at your face, your “bad at dodging” problem would resolve itself. Stump’s thought is: that’s what God sounds like, on Swinburne’s story. Why does God keep punching us in the head with rabies? How is that justified? How is that necessary? Swinburne says, WELL, in the future, God is going to continue trying to punch us in the head with rabies. In her perfect Omniscience, she’s knows that to be true. So she’s trying to teach us how to dodge those punches now so we can avoid those punches in the future. As before, that story is profoundly unsatisfying. If God would just stop being an asshole and giving us rabies, the “we don’t understand rabies” problem would resolve itself.

[Slide 21] Unnecessary Evil Candidates War Disease (e.g. Tay-Sachs, Alzheimer's) Natural Disasters Animal suffering Genocide Sociopathy Torture So all of that is to say: I don’t think the Free Will Theodicy is all that helpful for natural evils. So let’s just focus on those cases where is does seem helpful: cases of moral evil, stemming from human choices. (That said, if any of you think the Free Will Theodicy can explain the necessity of natural evil, you should write that up for your discussion post this week! I’m interested to hear new arguments about it!)

[Slide 22] Putting Pressure on the Free Will Theodicy In what follows, I will try to push on the Free Will Theodicy to find out where it might be weak. As we push, we’ll find that the defender of this theodicy ends up having to say some (relatively) implausible things to maintain the argument. I’ll keep track of those.

[Slide 31] Commitments of the Free Will Theodicy It’s always better to preserve a person’s freedom than to prevent harm. Again, we’re going to make a list of the troubling implications of the Free Will Theodicy, to get a sense for its costs . This our first troubling implication of the Free Will Theodicy. We seem to be committed to this claim but is it plausible?

[Slide 32] Back to Stalin. If the value of freedom lays in the struggle, Stalin’s freedom doesn’t seem very valuable. He was habitually choosing evil, making the same bad decisions day after day. Here’s a second worry about the Free Will Theodicy. ... That’s…. Weird. If I’m choosing something out of habit, I’m not struggling with it. Thus, we can’t point to the good of the struggle when trying to explain why God allows habitual evil. But since God does allow people to be habitually evil, the Free Will Theodicy is going to have to affirm that those free choices are more valuable than the interests of the people who are harmed by habitual, unthinking acts of evil.

So here’s our next commitment. We really do need to say this is we’re going to endorse the Free WIll Theodicy as our reply to the Problem of Evil

[Slide 34] Consider Ted Bundy. Bundy killed at least 30 people between 1974 and 1978. He killed them because he enjoyed it. He enjoyed feeling in control of people, and causing them pain. This drive for domination led him to kill. That’s just how he was. On the other hand, I (and I assume you) have never killed anybody. Is this because my superior moral knowledge and self-control enable me to resist the impulses that Bundy could not? No. I don’t kill people mostly because I don’t have any impulse to. Hurting people is not pleasant for me. I don’t want to do the things Bundy did. That’s just how I am.

[Slide 35] Consider Ted Bundy. Bundy didn’t choose to be a sociopath You & I didn’t choose to be non-sociopaths So couldn’t God have given Bundy a better moral compass w/o violating his free will ? But then, if his character wasn’t something he chose, then surely God could fix his character without violating any free choices. He didn’t choose to be a sociopath! So there’s no conflict, no obvious trade-off here between freedom of choice and evil prevention. It looks like a win-win for God to just make Bundy a normal person with normal moral sentiments.

[Slide 36] Commitments of the Free Will Theodicy It’s always better to preserve a person’s freedom than to prevent harm. Freedom is of the utmost value even when a person habitually chooses evil. God can’t improve people’s moral compasses w/o making the world worse. So again, we have a troubling implicatoin.

[Slide 37] Back to Bundy. If Bundy lacks a moral compass, then why think his free choice of evil has value ? Recall the original justification for the Free Will Theodicy. The thought was that our free moral choices are only really valuable when we have grapple with evil. But Bundy doesn’t grapple with evil. That’s what it means to say he lacks a moral compass. He doesn’t feel the force of ethics, of basic human decency. So what value then has his free choice? This is like the habitual choice problem but even worse. Bundy didn’t even struggle the first time. There was never a conflict! Why is that valuable? Why is that freedom worth more than 30 human lives?

God can’t improve people’s moral compasses w/o making the world worse. Free will is valuable even if a person never feels the pull of choosing good … even if there’s no struggle.

[Slide 39] Back to Bundy. And couldn’t God prevent his bad choices from playing out w/o violating his freedom ? But it gets worse. First, it’s not obvious that God couldn’t make the world a better place by simply altering Bundy’s circumstances. God could have placed obstacles in such a way that Bundy simply never came across any potential victims – God could have made is so that Bundy only ever came across helpful people in public places with police nearby. If he never had a convenient victim, he never would have chosen evil – and yet all his choices would still have been freely made.

[Slide 40] X freely chooses to do evil Moment 1 X does evil Moment 2 DIVINE INTERVENTION Second, it’s not even obvious (in this case or in other cases) that God really does face a trade-off between preserving our freedom and allowing evil. What’s wrong with this? Maybe God could step in at the very last instant, and say “Well, Ted, I see you’ve made your choice, you’ve exercised the freedom I’ve given you. Fine. That’s great! I love freedom. But I’m not going to stand by while you murder this person.” and then God would whisk the victim away. Freedom preserved! Evil prevented! The point being, it’s not at all obvious that God must violate our freedom in order to prevent wrongdoing. It seems like God could, in principle, step in between the free choice and the bad consequences.

[Slide 43] Suppose you had the opportunity to stop an atrocity, to step in and block the choice. You should, right? That’s a good thing to do. Quiz Imagine that you have the opportunity to stop Stalin. Or that you have the chance to tie Ted Bundy up in your basement. If so, surely you’re required to do that? If you can stop mass murders from mass murderin’, you should! You can’t simply stand by and let people commit terrible atrocities. So, quiz question: Why is this observation a serious problem for the Free Will Theodicy ?

[Slide 44] Suppose you had the opportunity to stop an atrocity, to step in and block the choice. You should, right? That’s a good thing to do. But, then… why can’t God do the same ? The trouble is that we don’t seem to value Bundy’s free will when it’s us humans deciding between preventing evil and preserving freedom. We think the value of Bundy’s freedom is tiny compared to the value of human lives. ( Should we put Bundy in jail and violate his freedom ORrrrrr should we let him roam free and commit murders?) Is not a serious question that serious people entertain. Jail him everyone agrees without hesitation.) So… uh… that’s weird. Which is it? Is it better to stop evil or is it better to preserve freedom? The Free Will Theodicy seems to tell us: Both! One standard applies to God (preserve freedom!) and another standard applies to humans (prevent evil) That’s very very strange.

[Slide 3] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option Recall once more, the shape of the discussion. We have this argument, The Problem of Evil, which observes an incompatibility between the world as it actually is and the world as it should be if the 3-O God exists. Most theists want to respond to this problem by arguing that all of the suffering in the world is necessary – all of it is needed to prevent some greater evil or to secure some greater good.

[Slide 4] The Free Will Theodicy It is consistently worse to violate a human’s free will than to allow evil. Thus, the suffering that stems from human choices is necessary suffering. Last time we looked at the FreeWill Theodicy. It says, roughly, that God knows that human freedom is more valuable than stopping evil. So She lets evils occur whenever stopping evil would require violating someone’s free will. We also looked at some objections to this Theodicy and tried to keep track of all the commitments the theist must make to hold onto her view.

We also looked at some objections to this Theodicy and tried to keep track of all the commitments the theist must make to hold onto her view.

[Slide 5] The Soul-making Theodicy Today we’ll talk about a different theodicy. This one attempts to explain the necessity of suffering not by pointing out what we’d lose through God’s intervention but by emphasizing what we gain by dealing with evil ourselves.

[Slide 6] Motivation #1: God prefers human intervention to divine intervention. I want to offer two different motivations for the soul-making theodicy. The first one picks up on a thread from our conversation last time.

[Slide 5] The Soul-making Theodicy Today we’ll talk about a different theodicy. This one attempts to explain the necessity of suffering not by pointing out what we’d lose through God’s intervention but by emphasizing what we gain by dealing with evil ourselves.

[Slide 6] Motivation #1: God prefers human intervention to divine intervention. I want to offer two different motivations for the soul-making theodicy. The first one picks up on a thread from our conversation last time.

[Slide 7] God could step in to help. But in Her moral perfection, She knows She shouldn’t. You could step in, too. Although far from morally perfect, you know that you should. (and God presumably wants you to!) How do we explain this strange asymmetry? Suppose you live next to a young couple with a baby. As best as you can tell, the couple is gone for 2-3 days at a time, leaving the baby alone at home. You hear it crying through your shared walls. You worry that it’s being severely underfed and under-nurtured. You think about calling child protective services, but you ultimately decide not to.

[Slide 8] The Free Will Theodicy cannot explain this. For both you and God, helping means violating the free choice to ignore the baby. And if freedom is really more important than infant suffering, you must not help. This is what we talked about last time. This asymmetry is a very big problem for the Free Will Theodicy. To accept the Free Will Theodicy is to accept that human freedom is, like, the most valuable thing. But if that’s right, it’s hard to understand why we’d ever be morally required to violate the freedom of others in the name of stoppping evil.

[Slide 7] God could step in to help. But in Her moral perfection, She knows She shouldn’t. You could step in, too. Although far from morally perfect, you know that you should. (and God presumably wants you to!) How do we explain this strange asymmetry? Suppose you live next to a young couple with a baby. But if that’s right, it’s hard to understand why we’d ever be morally required to violate the freedom of others in the name of stoppping evil.

[Slide 9] Maybe God wants to and does help? She uses you as her instrument of assistance. Maybe. But that would imply that God Herself is ready to step in if you refuse. That’s visibly not how these things go IRL. (If no human helps, the baby goes unhelped.) Here’s a different possible suggestion. The problem here is the implication that God is intent on helping. She is definitely going to make sure that the baby is helped. Which,simply, isnt’ true. That is quite clearly not the world we live in.

[Slide 10] Maybe it’s not really about the baby. Maybe it’s about you. Maybe God wants you to grow as a person . (And, frankly, you really need the practice.) So, yes, God could help the baby. But then what would you learn from that? But that suggests yet another possible story. We might think: God doesn’t want to be our safety net. We need to think of our world as our responsibility. If there’s some evil to be stopped, we need to see ourselves as the only ones to stop it. There’re no training wheels in this world. If’ we’re to grow and mature, we have to struggle on our own.

[Slide 11] Soul-making Theodicy Suffering and struggle are necessary in that we need to endure them to secure the greater good of our own maturation, development, & communion with God . So this brings us to the Soul-making Theodicy... Here’s one way to motivate it We say: “All of the evils in the world are actually crises/opportunities. God is inviting us to step in and stop evil for ourselves. She is inviting us to become like her, to work together to make our world and ourselves better.” After all, the 3-O God is already morally perfect. But for you and I to become more like God, we need to practice. It’s like helping your child work on a math problem. It’s simply not very helpful if you do the problems for him. You could do them – you’re pretty good at math, look at you! – but you don’t need the practice. Your kid is the one who needs to practice; he needs to be the one who struggles.

[Slide 13] All suffering is the result of Original Sin Recall this (bad) theodicy from a few lectures ago. I didn’t mention this at the time, but one very good reason for rejecting this theodicy is that the story it relies on, when taken quite literally, appears to conflict with our best science. The earth and human beings appear to be far more than 6,000 years old. The Garden of Eden story cannot really explain the necessity of suffering if it’s not true So here’s another path to the Soul Making Theodicy. Think about God as making humans through the process of evolution, as described by our best science. Turns out, when we do that, the Soul-making theodicy starts to look pretty good.

[Slide 24] Soul-making Theodicy Suffering and struggle are necessary in that we need to endure them to secure the greater good of our own maturation, development, & communion with God . [Slide 32] Examples & Applications

[Slide 33] How does the soul-making theodicy make sense of the evils of war? Well, war is a bad thing that humans do to each other. But, like all bad things, it presents opportunities for maturation and development. Politicians have a chance to develop into wiser, more restrained leaders; soldiers and civilians into more thoughtful, cautious, compassionate people. Through suffering and struggling, we have a chance to grow.

[Slide 34] Disease, likewise, is a chance for growth. Disease and poor health are hard. They suck. But through that struggle we can learn important things about ourselves. About courage and determination. About humility and compassion.

It can teach humility and kindness and courage.

[Slide 36] Objections and Implications

[Slide 36] Objections and Implications

[Slide 37] Objection 1 : Many religions claim that people can be damned – i.e. sentenced to eternal posthumous punishment . Why would the 3-O God do that to her friends? Well… She wouldn’t. I think that’s what we have to say. Hell makes exactly zero sense, if you buy the soul-making theodicy. Two problems: (1) The soul-making God would never make a person, then spend a lifetime trying to improve them, and then throw up her hands when the going gets hard and the person resists getting better. She wouldn’t simply quit on someone in the midst of soul-making. (Nor, given her Omniscience and Omnipotence, is it possible for there to even BE a human who She can’t perfect with enough time and effort. God would know in advance exactly what to do to guide this person toward moral perfection and would have the power to make that happen.) (2) The soul-making God would never make a person, try to improve them, and then torture them forever for failing. That’s nonsense. God would know in advance that the person would fail. She created that person after all – and could have made them a little different if she was worried about failure. In her perfect moral character, it’s hard to imagine Her setting someone up for failure like that.

[Slide 38] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. As we did last time, I want to make a list of all the commitments we’re forced to make in defending the Soul-making THeodicy. This is the first one. I personally don’t think this one is that big of a deal, but your mileage may vary. Some people may feel VERY CONFIDENT that there is a hell, and that individuals who go there are tortured forever. The soul-making theodicy is probably not for those people. I actually think, at that point, that the 3-O God is not for those people. If one believes that some humans are tortured infinitely by God for their finite Earthly crimes, then one believes in an afterlife that is plainly and fundamentally unjust. Thus, God is not omnibenevolent. =(

[Slide 39] Objection 2 : Some people mature more than others. What does the 3-O God do with those who die without developing any significant virtue or spiritual maturity? Suppose the world ends tomorrow in a giant extinction event – a big meteor or something. Does God say, “Well, time’s up. Fuck it. Everyone gets an A”? Presumably not. The whole point is to perfect each human. If a person fails to be perfected during her time on earth, then God must continue her perfecting efforts after the person’s death. If you don’t get it done in time, you’re going to have to stay after class.

[Slide 40] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. For many people, soul-making must continue after they die . ANother implication. Again, I don’t think this one is all that tough to swallow. But it’s still worth noting.

[Slide 41] Objection 3 : What about people who die in childhood? Do they finish soul-making in heaven? If that’s an option, then why do some of us have to soul-make on the objectively shittier Earth? Before the advent of modern medicine, about ⅓ people died before the age of 5. Obviously, no 5 year old is a morally perfected being. Thus, we need some sort of story about how those individuals finish soul-making. We have two choices: They finish in heaven They finish elsewhere It’s hard to believe that they finish in heaven. If ⅓ of people die young and get to soul-make in heaven, then the world starts to look patently unfair. ( i want to soul-make in heaven, too! Why didn’t I get to die in childhood?!) It’s hard to imagine the 3-O having such an unfair plan. Which means, those who die in childhood must soul-make elsewhere. There’s only one place we know of that God has carefully designed for soul-making: the earth. Thus, those who die early prolly continue soul-making on earth… meaning, what exactly? One obvious option is reincarnation. Those who die early are sent-back into new bodies and allowed to continue soul-making.

[Slide 42] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. For many people, soul-making must continue after they die . Children who die prematurely continue soul-making on Earth . (reincarnation??) A third implication. Now things are starting to look a little weird. But, still, the Soul-making Theodicy still hasn’t committed itself to anything, like, patently silly or stupid. These first three commitments are mostly just surprising, I think.

[Slide 43] Objection 4 : Doesn’t the cost of soul-making seem… pretty high? Like, my moral development is important, sure, but couldn’t it proceed in a world with just a little less suffering? Do we really need all of these genocides, hurricanes, and diseases?

[Slide 44] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. For many people, soul-making must continue after they die . Children who die prematurely continue soul-making on Earth . (reincarnation??) Every bad thing is absolutely, 100% necessary for soul-making. This commitment is the most troubling so far. It’s not easy in some cases to see how a particular instance of evil could plausibly contribute to soul-making. But it really does seem like the SMT is committed to this. (alternatively, we could combine the SMT with another theodicy to help take up the slack. Unfortunately, the only other promising one we’ve looked at (the Free Will Theodicy) struggles in exactly the same cases)

[Slide 45] Objection 5 : The average life today is far less painful than the average life ~300 years ago. Plus, fewer people die of violence, malnutrition, and infectious disease. Quiz Take a second and think about why this might be troubling. (There are a few good answers here.)

[Slide 46] If suffering is the path to improvement, are we now soul-making slower? Is it bad to, say, give my child Advil? Maybe it’s better to let her suffer/grow? Doesn’t species-improvement slow individual-improvement? Like, if we as a species stop fighting wars, doesn’t that mean that future people will be deprived of all the great soul-making benefits of war? Here’re two of the more troubling implications of this observation. The soul-making theodicy seems to treat all suffering as an important growth opportunity. If a morally-perfect God sees suffering that way, then a morally-perfect human should too. But that means that a genuinely morally good person may sometimes correctly refuse to alleviate the suffering of others . She will say, “Yes your tooth aches, and I could give you a root canal right now, but you’ll grow more as a person if we wait a week, so let’s do that. I don’t want to prolong the amount of time you spend on Earth after all!” Likewise, the improvement of the species seems at odds with the improvement of the individual. Individuals need to suffer to get better while societies need to prevent suffering to get better. The soul-making theodicy makes it hard to understand the elimination of starvation, of war, and of disease as a good thing. Like, obviously it’s a good thing that most people aren’t starving anymore. But the soul-making theodicy seems to suggest that’s… a bad thing? We’ve deprived all those people of a real Growth Opportunity.

[Slide 48] Objection 6 : Sociopathy? Objection 7 : Animal suffering? What do we say about Sociopaths? Almost by definition, it seems, they are unable to soul-make because they are insensitive to or unmotivated by moral reasons. Maybe those people just require lots of reincarnations? But how it that fair? It seems shitty for God to make certain people lack moral compasses and thereby force them to spend even MORE time on the shitty earth. (2) Can we really justify millions of years of animal suffering by saying “Yes, but God really wanted friends”? Think again about Rowe’s fawn, who dies over the course of 3 days all alone trapped under a tree. There aren’t any humans around, so there aren’t any souls to be made better by this suffering. Or, alternatively, maybe we now need to attribute perfectable souls to animals? “God lets the deer die of exposure trapped under the burning tree b/c the deer needs to be morally perfected” That’s a weird thing to say. But that honestly strikes me as more plausible than simply “God kills these animals for US! ”

So these are two final, troubling implications of the Soul-making theodicy And our the final question is just this, Considering all these claims together – all of these commitments we’d be making by endorsing the Soul-making theodicy – how attractive is this view? This concludes our discussion of the problem of evil. As always in this course, there’s a lot that’s been left unsaid. There’s more to say about our two main theodicies – and other theodicies to be considered. [next slide]

[Slide 50] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary suffering . (b/c She would know about it, want to prevent it, and be able to) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary suffering . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Evil I want to close by making some cursory remarks about the Problem of Evil and what happens from here. You’ll notice, both of our Two Big Theodicies tell the same basic story: They make sense of all the suffering in the world by explaining how that suffering is GOOD FOR US. The Free Will Theodicy says it’s better for us to have freedom of choice than to have freedom from suffering. The Soul-making theodicy says that suffering is a growth opportunity. Any other theodicies we might want to develop will, I suspect, have to follow this same pattern. They will need to explain why all of the suffering we see in the world is good for us. After all, we are the ones who are suffering. So it better in our best interests that we do so. (otherwise, we have a very serious fairness complaint against God. It would be unfair for God to allow us to suffer so that some other beings can benefit. That is a textbook description of injustice. And surely the 3-O God has not set up the world that way.) Thus, future theodicies should focus on this same question: How else might all this suffering be good for us ?

[Slide 49] Commitments of the Soul-making Theodicy There’s no damnation, and everyone ends up in heaven in the fullness of time. For many people, soul-making must continue after they die . Children who die prematurely continue soul-making on Earth . (reincarnation??) Every bad thing is absolutely, 100% necessary for soul-making. Sometimes, the morally perfect thing is to let people suffer for their own good . Sociopaths are soul-making too? Animal suffering is justified ? (b/c it’s good for us? good for them?) So these are two final, troubling implications of the Soul-making theodicy And our the final question is just this, Considering all these claims together – all of these commitments we’d be making by endorsing the Soul-making theodicy – how attractive is this view? This concludes our discussion of the problem of evil. As always in this course, there’s a lot that’s been left unsaid. There’s more to say about our two main theodicies – and other theodicies to be considered. [next slide]

[Slide 1] Divine Hiddenness

[Slide 2] On divine absences and love An analogy to maternal hiddenness Evidence for divine absences Formalizing the Argument Parallels to the Problem of Evil Some bad responses

[Slide 3] It is not uncommon for theists, especially in times of distress, to report feeling a sense of divine absence . This experience can be acutely painful. So well known is this phenomenon that it explicitly described and explained in the Catechism of the Catholic Church – they call it desolation or spiritual dryness. (Catholics are hardly alone in experiencing this phenomenon! But they have been very active in describing and discussing it)

[Slide 4] “ In the darkness . . . Lord, my God, who am I that you should forsake me? The child of your love — and now become as the most hated one. The one — you have thrown away as unwanted — unloved. I call, I cling, I want, and there is no one to answer . . . Where I try to raise my thoughts to heaven, there is such convicting emptiness that those very thoughts return like sharp knives and hurt my very soul. Love — the word — it brings nothing. I am told God lives in me — and yet the reality of darkness and coldness and emptiness is so great that nothing touches my soul. ” – Mother Teresa Mother Teresa famously spent large portions of her life feeling this way.

I want to love him as he has not been loved, and yet there is that separation, that terrible emptiness, that feeling of absence of God. They say people in hell suffer eternal pain because of the loss of God . . . In my soul I feel just this terrible pain of loss, of God not wanting me, of God not being God, of God not really existing. ” – John of the Cross The 16th Century Spanish priest John of the Cross is also famous for writing about this wrenching feeling of divine absence.

[Slide 6] This phenomenon – the anguishing sense of divine absence – is surprising . Specifically, it’s surprising for a God that loves us to inflict anguish thru absence . … Of course, a surprising thing is just an unexpected thing. So to make sense of why this is surprising, we need to talk through our expectations. What should we expect from God, given that She loves us? And, first, what do we even mean when we say that God loves us?

[Slide 7] ‘ Love ’ picks out a particular bundle of attitudes & emotions that humans are capable of feeling for other beings. (not just any bundle – the BEST bundle!) Love is the often thought to be the highest aspiration of our emotional lives.

[Slide 8] When we say, “God loves us” We mean , God feels about us the same way we feel about our most cherished companions . (though, God’s love is maybe stronger than ours.) ... That’s important. God’s love is supposed to be, essentially, the same feeling as OUR love. We don’t use scare-quotes when we say that God “loves” us. It’s not supposed to be a metaphor or a gross oversimplification. It’s not as though God is feeling something completely alien and un-human towards us, a feeling we can only crudely approximate with the word ‘love’. No. God loves like we love – though, probably even better. Indeed, the story we get (at least from Abrahamic religions) is that god made us in Her image. Love was her feeling first, and she made us in such a way that we’re capable of that feeling too.

[Slide 17] Imagine that you come across a child in a dark forest. He’s alone and clearly terrified, calling out repeatedly for his mother. You ask him what the matter is and if you can help. “I need to find my mother, so we can leave the forest before dark,” he says. “She’s here in the forest with you?” you ask. “Yeah. She’s really close. I’m sure she can hear me.” he says. You ask the boy why his mother hasn't already come for him, if she's close by. “Do you think she’s hurt or detained somehow? Or lost, like you?" You ask. “No,” he says. “She’s fine. She could come get me if she wanted.” The boy’s assurance strikes you as odd and unsettling. You ask cautiously, “Do you… think your mother has left you here intentionally?... Does your mother ever mistreat you?" "No!” The boy is angry. “She’s loves me very very very much. She loves me more than anything.” You think you might have missed something. “So… your mother can hear you… and she could come save you if she wanted… and she loves you dearly?” The boy nods. “I don’t understand." you say. "If all that’s true, then why hasn’t she come for you?” The boy just shrugs. “Dunno.” He turns away and begins calling out for his mother again.

[Slide 21] Evidence for absence We’ll formalize that argument in a bit, but for now I want to point to some cases of divine absence, to give you a better sense for what the concern is supposed to be.

[Slide 22] The faithful often seek out God for comfort, companionship, or understanding – but come up empty. As I pointed out a few minutes ago, this is a very well-documented phenomenon. Lots of theists report experiencing this at some point in their religious lives. They earnestly reach for some connection to God, some shred of her presence and love, and they find nothing. If God is perfectly loving, that is very puzzling. That absence cries out for explanation. Why is God not more present?

[Slide 21] Evidence for absence We’ll formalize that argument in a bit, but for now I want to point to some cases of divine absence, to give you a better sense for what the concern is supposed to be.

[Slide 22] The faithful often seek out God for comfort, companionship, or understanding – but come up empty. As I pointed out a few minutes ago, this is a very well-documented phenomenon. Lots of theists report experiencing this at some point in their religious lives. They earnestly reach for some connection to God, some shred of her presence and love, and they find nothing. If God is perfectly loving, that is very puzzling. That absence cries out for explanation. Why is God not more present?

[Slide 23] Some people die have never even conceived of God – never having the opportunity to develop a relationship. Whatever religion you believe in, it’s inevitably going to turn out that some isolated societies (whether today or in history) simply never have contact with divine revelation. They didn’t themselve receive any prophets from God, nor did any apostles from other societies ever visit them. Of course, almost all societies have some sort of religion, but lots of those religions are quite radically dissimilar to the modern Abrahamic ones. A society who believes in a mean-spirited and vengeful pantheon does really have a chance to know the 3-O God. These people apparently, never had the chance to know God, to enter into a relationship with her. Almost certainly, this is true of hundreds of thousands if not millions of people, throughout history. If God is perfectly loving, that is very puzzling. That absence cries out for explanation. Why is God not more present?

[Slide 24] Some people die have never even conceived of God – never having the opportunity to develop a relationship. Or imagine a 5 year old who dies of leukemia but whose parents are atheists and so never took her to church or introduced her to religious teachings about God. That child dies having never had the opportunity to know God, to know that she is loved by God. (Compare: the child does know that her human parents love her! Her parents do a good job expressing and communicating their love. But God doesn’t. The child dies never even realizing that God exists.) Again, If God is perfectly loving, that is very puzzling. That is to say: surely something like this has happened at least once in our world – and probably thousands upon thousands upon thousands of times. Those divine absences demand anexplanation. Why is God not more present?

[Slide 25] Some people are non-resistant non-believers. They’re open to believing in God, but never find her. Imagine a student who showed up to this class just completely unsure whether God exists or not. First we talk about arguments for the existence of God. Maybe she finds the Fine-Tuning Argument kinda moving. She’s leaning toward theism. But then we hit the Problem of Evil, and she decides, the 3-O God doesn’t exist. Surely this happened thousands upon thousands of times. (Not in my class in particular. I haven’t had that many students and I’m probably not all that persuasive. But still: among all the college students who’ve ever taken a Philosophy of Religion course like this one: surely thousands have been turned away from God. That has to happen , sometimes. Again, If God is perfectly loving, that is very puzzling. That absence cries out for explanation. Why is God not more present? Why does God allow people like that to be pushed away, for lack of evidence?

[Slide 26] Some theists ultimately convert to atheism , in the absence of a sense of God’s presentness in their lives. This, in particular, is so weird. These people were already in a relationship with God; many of them must have been very willing to continuing that relationship . But God allows them to drift away from the faith. Many of these people are simply so struck by the utter lack of evidence for God in their lives, that they turn away from religion. Again, If God is perfectly loving, that is very puzzling. That absence cries out for explanation. Why is God not more present? Why does God allow people like that to be pushed away, for lack of evidence?

[Slide 27] Formalizing the Argument

[Slide 28] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary absence . (b/c God loves us, knows about our needs, wants what’s best for us, and has the power to be with us.) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary divine absence . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Divine Hiddenness As it happens, the easiest way to formalize the argument from divine hiddenness is to mirror our original reasoning from the problem of evil. We point out in P1) that we expect certain things to be true of our world, if there is a loving 3-O God: Given God’s nature, it seems impossible for the 3-O God to exist and for the world to contain unnecessary absences . We then point out in P2) that it sure does look like God has been unnecessarily absent at least once.

[Slide 28] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary absence . (b/c God loves us, knows about our needs, wants what’s best for us, and has the power to be with us.) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary divine absence . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Divine Hiddenness Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C From this point forward, the dialectic is pretty much perfectly isomorphic to the problem of evil. As before, the theist has 3 options… As before, basically no one tries to argue that unnecessary divine absences are to be expected from a 3-O God. Nor do any theists want to accept the conclusion.

[Slide 31] Some kinds of absences are necessary . The Problem of Divine Hiddenness is about unnecessary absences . Absences are unnecessary when they, (a) don’t prevent some greater bad AND ALSO (b) don’t secure some greater good. As before, we’re drawing a distinction here between necessary absences and unnecessary absences...

[Slide 32] An evil dictator is building a new extermination camp for his political enemies. He believes he’s doing God’s work, and he prays to God for guidance on where to locate the extermination camp. God gives no response. This is an instance of hiddenness. There is an earnest attempt by the dictator to find God, and God is nowhere to be found. This is a case of divine absence – b ut, hopefully you can see, this case is easy for the theist to deal with. This case is presumably not good evidence against the 3-O God b/c the theist can tell a plausible story according to which God’s absence is necessary, is needed to prevent some greater evil. Maybe, e.g., we can say: God doesn’t respond to the dictator b/c God doesn’t want to endorse the extermination camp. And, perhaps, when the dictator doesn’t hear anything from God, he will be able to infer that God doesn’t want him to build an extermination camp at all. Thus: God is not just hiding; She is withholding her approval. (Just as a loving mother might in the same situation!)

[Slide 31] Some kinds of absences are necessary . The Problem of Divine Hiddenness is about unnecessary absences . Absences are unnecessary when they, (a) don’t prevent some greater bad AND ALSO (b) don’t secure some greater good. As before, we’re drawing a distinction here between necessary absences and unnecessary absences...

[Slide 32] An evil dictator is building a new extermination camp for his political enemies. He believes he’s doing God’s work, and he prays to God for guidance on where to locate the extermination camp. God gives no response. This is an instance of hiddenness. There is an earnest attempt by the dictator to find God, and God is nowhere to be found. This is a case of divine absence – b ut, hopefully you can see, this case is easy for the theist to deal with. This case is presumably not good evidence against the 3-O God b/c the theist can tell a plausible story according to which God’s absence is necessary, is needed to prevent some greater evil. Maybe, e.g., we can say: God doesn’t respond to the dictator b/c God doesn’t want to endorse the extermination camp. And, perhaps, when the dictator doesn’t hear anything from God, he will be able to infer that God doesn’t want him to build an extermination camp at all. Thus: God is not just hiding; She is withholding her approval. (Just as a loving mother might in the same situation!)

[Slide 33] A young woman has procrastinated writing a paper for months. It’s due tomorrow, and she hasn’t even read the book she’s supposed to write about. She prays to God for aid and comfort and finds none. Here’s another case of divine hiddenness. There is an earnest attempt by the young woman to find God, and God is nowhere to be found. But this case too is pretty easy for the theist to deal with: it’s not hard to tell a plausible story according to which God’s absence is needed to prevent some greater evil. Maybe we say, God wants this young woman to learn from her mistake and to be more assiduous in future. But in order for her to learn from her mistake, the mistake needs to hurt at least a little bit. That’s why God is withholding her comfort – just as a loving mother might in the same situation.

[Slide 34] A theist and an atheist are arguing about God’s existence. The atheist says, “ God, if you’re out there, come down here and make yourself manifest to us. ” Nothing happens. Quiz Here’s another case of divine hiddenness. The question is, Does this seem like a necessary absence or like an unnecessary absence? You take a shot at this one: how might we explain this instance of divine hiddenness as necessary ? … We might say something like this: There are lots of good reasons for God to refuse this request. First, God might not want the atheist to think of Her as some sort of display piece or servant to be commanded about. God might not want people to believe in Her merely because She appears when commanded to. God might think that the atheist isn’t arguing in good faith and that it would be better for this person to find God at another time, when they’re feeling less combative.

[Slide 35] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary absence . (b/c God loves us, knows about our needs, wants what’s best for us, and has the power to be with us.) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary divine absence . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Divine Hiddenness Possible Responses: Reject P1 Reject P2 Accept C Preferred theist option But again those are easy cases. As before, the real challenge is for the theist to tell some sort of story according to which ALL divine absences might be understood as necessary, as needing for stopping some greater evil or preventing some greater good.

[Slide 36] Unnecessary Absence Candidates Theists feeling anguish w/o God Societies that never imagine a 3-O God Children who die w/o knowing God Agnostics convinced by atheistic args Theists who convert to atheism Here’s our short list of really tough cases. We have:... The theists task is, once again, to try to tell some story (or a collection of stories) according to which all of those absences might count as necessary – as important decisions that God is making to prevent greater evils or provide greater goods. As we did with the problem of evil, I want to quickly canvas a few of the bad responses that you most often hear from people

[Slide 37] Most people are closed off to the possibility of finding God. A lot of people’s first reaction is this:... And yeah. Sure. That’s true of some people. But notice, we didn’t even include people who are militant atheists on our list of divine absences that need to be explained. Those are easy for the theist to explain! They didn’t even make the short list. But this explanation seems pretty worthless for the items we actually did include on our shortlist. (theists who feel desolate without God, entire cultures who never receive divine revelation, children who die never even thinking there might be a God, etc.) It’s obviously not going to work to simply say, “Oh that five year old with terminal cancer? Yeah, she was just too closed off to have a relationship with God. So close minded, that 5 year old. And anyway she’s dead now! Nothing to be done!”

[Slide 38] God is never absent . It’s just necessary for Her to hide. … But this reply simply pushes the question back a step. Instead of ‘why so absent,’ now it’s ‘why so hard to find.’ It’s not obvious that question is any better. Put differently, this response doesn’t seem to actually help us at all. This merely asserts that God’s absences are necessary. But what’s needed is an explanation for why those absences are necessary.

[Slide 39] All hiddenness is a necessary part of God’s plan Finally... Well, yes. Any solution to the problem will take this form. But notice the analogy with the problem of evil. The trick is to explain, in terms we can understand, why a good plan would involve hiddenness. Because from where we’re standing, it sure does look like a garbage plan – what with all the apparently unnecessary absences. Again, T=this merely asserts that God’s absences are necessary when what’s needed is an explanation for why those absences are necessary. The Atheist challenge is: Can you explain why God is so often absent? And this reply amounts to: No I cannot. But there must be an explanation. Which isn’t victory; it’s quitting.

[Slide 40] P1) If the 3-O God exists, there could never be even one instance of unnecessary absence . (b/c God loves us, knows about our needs, wants what’s best for us, and has the power to be with us.) P2) There has been at least one instance of unnecessary divine absence . C) The 3-O God does not exist The Problem of Divine Hiddenness There’s obviously a lot more that can be said here, but at this point, I think you can see that most of it will follow the same paths as the problem of evil. The theist needs some sort of theodicy sufficient for rejecting P2. Maybe they can point to Free Will. (“Surely we wouldn’t really be free if God were overbearingly present in our lives.”) Maybe they can point to soul-making. (“Surely we can only grow as people if we feel ourselves alone and required to be self-sufficient, without God’s comfort and aid.”) So that’s an open question that I’ll leave for this week’s discussions. What’s the best way for the theist to respond to the Problem of Divine Hiddenness? The other open question concerns the relationship between this Problem and the Problem of Evil. As we’ve seen, the two problems share a lot of the same essential features and are largely isomorphic. But that raises the question, What’s the relationship between the two? Is divine hiddenness just a specific sort of evil (meaning, therefore, that this problem is just a special case of the broader problem of evil)? Or maybe it’s better to reverse that: maybe all evil is best understood as a kind of divine absence? Or alternatively, maybe the two problems are more like brothers – related but not identical in whole or in part?

[Slide 2] Religious diversity and disagreement Can we be inclusivists about truth? A look at the evidence

[Slide 3] Here’s a recent list of religions and religious adherents from Wikipedia, sorted by size. You’ll notice, This excludes all of the branches, sects, cults within the larger religions. The categories “traditional religions” and “ethnic religions” are themselves staggeringly diverse. There are lots of religions, is my point. LOTS. Each of them makes some claim to being true. And most of them substantively disagree with the rest.

[Slide 4] On this much, the major world religions agree, There is a supernatural/transcendent reality behind or beyond the physical world of everyday experience. It is only by correctly orienting our lives toward that transcendent reality that we humans can attain salvation, can be saved from the depravities of the human condition. … but try to get more specific than that, and you hit real discord . At one level of description, of course, the major world religions can be aptly said to agree with one another. … As soon as you try to say anything more specific than that, you get rather radically different answers from diff religious traditions. If you ask, for instance, “What is the nature of this transcendent reality, and how do you know it exists?” You’re going to get some very divergent answers. If you ask, “What exactly do I need to do to orient my life toward this transcendent reality you’re talking about?” Again, you’re going to get some very divergent answers.

[Slide 21] There is religious disagreement. The major world religions disagree about all sorts of stuff. This raises different questions, depending on your interests, If your interest is in truth : Which religion(s) are true? If it’s in being rational : Which religion(s) are rational? If it’s being saved : Which religion(s) can save me? Here’s the point… I’m obviously not in a position to answer any of these questions definitively! I don’t know how you get saved or which religions are true! But each question is, nevertheless, philosophically interesting. And I want to talk about those first two questions today.

[Slide 22] A lot of people are attracted to inclusivism about religions. If you ask them, “Who’s right about God?” They’ll say, “Isn’t everyone kinda right?” If you ask, “Which religions are rational?” They’ll say, “Aren’t they all?” And if you ask, “Which religion is a path to salvation?” They’ll say, “Surely we all get there eventually.”

[Slide 23] There is religious disagreement. The major world religions disagree about all sorts of stuff. This raises different questions, depending on your interests, If your interest is in truth : Which religion(s) are true? If it’s in being rational : Which religion(s) are rational? If it’s being saved : Which religion(s) can save me? The point is: You can be inclusive about any, all, or none of these questions: You can be inclusive about truth – if so, you think that lots of religions are true. You can be inclusive about rationality – if so, you think that lots of religious people, from lots of different religions, have rational beliefs And you can be inclusive about salvation – if so, you think that lots of people, for lots of different religions, will achieve salvation . (And the opposite views are also possible: you can exclusive about truth or about rationality or about salvation. If so, you believe that only one religion is true, or only one religion is rational, or only one religion is a path to salvation.)

Why is probably not the case that we can endorse the simultaneously truth of multiple religions?

[Slide 33] How are we to determine which religion is correct? Let’s follow this line of thought a little further. The natural next question is this:... That is, insofar as it’s clear that at most one religion can be right about those matters of substantive disagreement, how should we work out which religion that is?

[Slide 34] Imagine you’re an alien coming down to earth in your fancy spaceship. You notice all these religions – a totally unique phenomenon in the universe, as far as you know. You want what these religions have to offer! But… they clearly can’t all be true . And of course, you want to pick the right one . How will you make your decision? Let me suggest a helpful framing device / thought experiment for answering this question… The point is, obviously, this: try to abstract away from your own religious upbringing and your own cultural embeddedness as best you can. Try to simply consider the question: which religion seems most appealing from the outside ?

[Slide 37] What evidence do we have access to? Religious texts Testimony Consistency w/ other knowledge Here’s my shortlist – I’m not sure if this list is complete , but I think it’s close; I think it’s pretty good … By ‘testimony’, I mean you can talk to other people and hear what they have to say about their own religious experiences and convictions. That’s a potential source of evidence. By ‘consistency’, what I have in mind is that different religions will make different claims about the world, and those claims may conflict with what our best science tells us is true. Insofar as a religion disagrees with science, that may count as some evidence against it.

[Slide 38] So, e.g., a religion that says, “God used evolution and natural selection to bring humans into existence over the course of many millions of years” is more compatible with our best science than a religion that says, “God created the world 6,000 years ago.” You can think about INconsistency as imposing a cost on a religion. Our imagined alien will think, “If I accept this religion, I’m going to have totally revise my understanding of history. That’s unattractive! That makes this religion more costly since i will have to sacrifice a bunch of well-justified beliefs about the world if I want to join.”

[Slide 37] What evidence do we have access to? Religious texts Testimony Consistency w/ other knowledge Here’s my shortlist – I’m not sure if this list is complete , but I think it’s close; I think it’s pretty good … By ‘testimony’, I mean you can talk to other people and hear what they have to say about their own religious experiences and convictions. That’s a potential source of evidence. By ‘consistency’, what I have in mind is that different religions will make different claims about the world, and those claims may conflict with what our best science tells us is true. Insofar as a religion disagrees with science, that may count as some evidence against it.

[Slide 38] So, e.g., a religion that says, “God used evolution and natural selection to bring humans into existence over the course of many millions of years” is more compatible with our best science than a religion that says, “God created the world 6,000 years ago.” You can think about INconsistency as imposing a cost on a religion. Our imagined alien will think, “If I accept this religion, I’m going to have totally revise my understanding of history. That’s unattractive! That makes this religion more costly since i will have to sacrifice a bunch of well-justified beliefs about the world if I want to join.”

[Slide 39] Alternatively, we might put the point this way: To a knowledgeable outsider, everytime a religion asserts a claim that contradicts our best science, that’s going to throw up a red flag that the religion is false. And, obviously, the more red flags get thrown, the less likely our neutral observer is to accept the religion in question. (So for instance, maybe our alien will think, “I guess I can scratch Scientology off the list b/c it claims that the ancient city of Atlantis fought a space war with disintegration rays AND ALSO claims the universe is governed by a Galactic Confederacy. I know both of those claims are false. So, I can safely ignore this religion.”)

[Slide 40] What evidence do we have access to? Religious texts Testimony Consistency w/ other knowledge Unambiguous religious experiences Verified, testable predictions To finish off the list… An “unambiguous religious experience” is an experience that clearly indicates that some particular religion is correct. So, e.g., suppose our the alien has a clear and distinct vision of an angel coming down and saying, “You should believe in Islam. It’s the one true religion.” That would seem to count as some pretty persuasive evidence! (On the other hand, an ambiguous religious experience presumably wouldn’t help, wouldn’t count as evidence for one religion over another. If our alien experiences a sublime sense of peace while watching the sun set, that experience won’t help it decide which religion is true.) Lastly, testable predictions are any claims that the religions makes about the world or about the future that can checked. This is basically the same thing that what we do with scientific theories. A theory that makes testable predictions and whose predictions consistently come true, is thereby better supported. The ability to make accurate predictions is a powerful form of evidence. The theory of general relativity, for instance, makes a bunch testable predictions about the universe. And it turns out, all of those predictions come true, are verified. Those verified predictions count in favor of the theory! Surely the same applies to any religion that makes testable predictions.

[Slide 41] How do we assess evidential support? For simplicity, let’s focus on evidential quality and evidential quantity . Recall, this was our second question: How do we measure the evidence for the different religions, so that we can actually tell which one is the best supported? … For each of our sources of religions evidence, we can simply ask: Does any religion have higher quality evidence ? And Does any religion have more evidence ? (i.e. insofar as more is better, which as we’ll see, isn’t always the case. More evidence isn’t always better.)

[Slide 42] Religious texts Quality : Everybody has some, but there’s no great way to assess their merits. We could ask adherents which ones are best, but we already know what they’ll say. Quantity : Seems irrelevant. The Mormons have an extra book compared to the Christians, but that doesn’t make their view more likely to be true. Let’s start with religions texts. From the outside, does it look like any particular religion is better supported by its religions texts? ...

Quantity : Seems irrelevant. The Mormons have an extra book compared to the Christians, but that doesn’t make their view more likely to be true. Let’s start with religions texts. [Slide 43] Testimony Quality : Each religion’s adherents insist that theirs is the correct view. Everyone is sincere, and there’s no obvious way to tell who’s right. Quantity : Some religions are larger than others – maybe that’s a little helpful? But hardly anyone willingly converts to a new religion. Thus, size differences may largely be explainable by differential birth rates / historical conquest / colonialism. What about testimony? Suppose we go around and talk to everyone on earth and ask them which religion they think we should join. Will that help us out? … Which is to say, it’s true that there are more Christians telling us that Christianity is true than there are Buddhists telling us that Buddhism is true. Thus, there’s a larger quantity of testimony supporting Christianity. But it’s not at all obvious that Christianity’s size is due to its truth or even plausibility. It seems quite possible that christianity is the largest religion because it’s adherents have historically been more successful at having lots of kids and at forcing other peoples to be Christians, at gunpoint. Which, if that’s the explanation, means we shouldn’t take its size as evidence that it’s more likely to be true.

[Slide 44] Unambiguous Religious Experiences Quality / Quantity : Unfortunately, such experiences are quite rare and cannot simply be had at will. Certainly, if I myself have some , that can help me decide. But if I’m only listening to other people tell me about theirs, that’s just testimony again. … and everyone will just insist that their religious experiences clearly favor their own religion. The Christians will insist that their religious experiences prove that the Bible is true; the Mormons will insist that their support the Book of Mormon; etc.
