[Slide 45] Consistency Quality : All religions seem to have some consistency issues. Most try to mitigate them. At best we might rule out some religions on the fringe. Quantity : Not helpful. Christians could print out Wikipedia and staple it to the back the Bible (thus making their religion maximally consistent with our present knowledge) but that wouldn’t make Christianity any more likely to be true. How about consistency ? Are there any religions that are super-consistent with our best science? Are there any there super-inconsistent? Start with quality: does any religion fastidiously avoid contradicting out best science? … What about quantity? Well, by “quantity” here, presumably we mean something like, “How many claims does this religion make that are consistent with our best science?”

[Slide 46] Testable Predictions Quality : This would help, if we had some. But most religions only issue posthumous, eschatological, and moral predictions. We can’t observe the afterlife or the End of Days, and no religion consistently seems to produce better people. Quantity : See Above. Finally, what about testable predictions? The short answer is: We don’t really get any. Most religions only issue predictions about (a) what happens after we die, (b) What will happen at the end of days, or (c) what kind of wonderful people we’ll become if we join them. Unfortunately, the first two aren’t testable, and the last one doesn’t really support any particular religion.

[Slide 47] Unless we made a mistake in reasoning just now, the upshot seems to be – no religion stands-out as best supported . It’s not obvious that any religion has a leg-up over the others. At least from the perspective of an outsider – someone who was not raised in any particular religion, nor has any strong cultural attachment to any particular religion, nor has any unambiguous religious experiences – it doesn’t really look like there IS a BEST SUPPORTED religion.

[Slide 48] There is religious disagreement. The major world religions disagree about all sorts of stuff. This raises different questions, depending on your interests, If your interest is in truth : Which religion(s) are true? If it’s in being rational : Which religion(s) are rational? If it’s being saved : Which religion(s) can save me? Let’s zoom out. Recall the set-up: There are dozens of religions out there any they disagree about all sorts of stuff. In most of those cases, they literally contradict each other. Thus, at most, one religion can be true. The obvious question is: Which one? I don’t know! I’m not here to tell you which one. But we have just apparently learned something helpful: namely, that it’s not at all obvious from the outside than any particular religion has a better claim than the others to being the true one.

This raises different questions, depending on your interests, If your interest is in truth : Which religion(s) are true? If it’s in being rational : Which religion(s) are rational? If it’s being saved : Which religion(s) can save me? Before we finish for today, I want to quickly look at that second question. While only one religion can be TRUE, it may still be the case that it’s rational to believe in other religions. (If so, we’d say: the adherents of Religion X are wrong, but, given their epistemic situation, it’s perfectly rational for them to continue believing in Religion X. ) So the question is: Which religions is it rational to believe in?

[Slide 50] There are, basically, 3 possible answers, None – there is no religion that it’s rational to believe in One – there is one particular religion that it’s rational to believe in Many – there are many religions that it can be rational to believe in As Zagzebski points out in the reading for this module…

(If so, we’d say: the adherents of Religion X are wrong, but, given their epistemic situation, it’s perfectly rational for them to continue believing in Religion X. ) So the question is: Which religions is it rational to believe in?

[Slide 50] There are, basically, 3 possible answers, None – there is no religion that it’s rational to believe in One – there is one particular religion that it’s rational to believe in Many – there are many religions that it can be rational to believe in As Zagzebski points out in the reading for this module…

[Slide 51] There are, basically, 3 possible answers, None – there is no religion that it’s rational to believe in One – there is one particular religion that it’s rational to believe in Many – there are many religions that it can be rational to believe in Given our previous discussion, it seem we can rule out the second option. If there WERE one particular religion that stands out as the most and only rational option, it would have to be the one that is best supported by the evidence. But s ince there doesn’t seem to be a particular religion that is better supported than the others, there is no one religion that can uniquely claim to be the only rational one. So we’re left with two options: We can either say that it’s always irrational to believe in a religion. Or, we can say that lots of religions can count as rational. That first option is, obviously, pretty unappealing to all theists, of every religion. So let’s set that aside for now. But what about the 3rd option? Can’t we just say that lots of religions are rational? Maybe! But it turns out, that view has some problems of its own.

[Slide 52] “So religious [pluralism] poses a problem… If we retain our [own religious] beliefs because we judge that they are rational, and also judge that the conflicting beliefs of the atheist and of other religions are rational, we cannot maintain that the rationality of each belief makes it likely to be true…. On the other hand, if we give up our beliefs on the grounds that their rationality is not closely connected to getting the truth,... we are forced to admit that we have given up any chance of getting the truth about a very important matter.” – Linda Trinkaus Zagzebski Here’s how Zagzebski puts it. Let me try to gloss the problem she has in mind here.

[Slide 52] “So religious [pluralism] poses a problem… If we retain our [own religious] beliefs because we judge that they are rational, and also judge that the conflicting beliefs of the atheist and of other religions are rational, we cannot maintain that the rationality of each belief makes it likely to be true…. On the other hand, if we give up our beliefs on the grounds that their rationality is not closely connected to getting the truth,... we are forced to admit that we have given up any chance of getting the truth about a very important matter.” – Linda Trinkaus Zagzebski Here’s how Zagzebski puts it. Let me try to gloss the problem she has in mind here.

[Slide 53] There’s supposed to be a tight connection between rationality and truth . To call a belief ‘rational’ is to approve of it as likely to be true , given the evidence . To see the concern, we need to say a bit about rationality claims. What does it mean to say that a belief is rational ? One obvious thing we’re doing when we call a belief ‘rational’ is approving of it and complimenting it – more particularly, we are complimenting it as truth-apt, as a good belief to have, given one’s interest in believing true things. That is to say... … Put differently, it’s a good thing to have rational beliefs! A person with all and only rational beliefs is doing better than a person with lots of irrational beliefs. And, more precisely, they are doing better truth-wise. The person with lots of irrational beliefs is, thereby, a person who surely has lots of false beliefs.

[Slide 54] Here’s an easy example: Suppose you believe that it’s going to rain on Tues, Wed, or Thurs (and quite possibly all of them) after looking at this forecast. I’d call that a rational belief, given the evidence. Good job! Good beliefs. Now, of course, it certainly could turn out that it rains none of those days. Your rational beliefs could turn out to be false. But they’re still good beliefs. The alternative beliefs – that it won’t rain on Tues nor on Wed nor on Thurs – are clearly worse beliefs. Indeed, unless you have some special insight into the weather, those seem like positively irrational beliefs. They’re way more likely be false! (Again, they could turn out to be true beliefs! Irrational beliefs can sometimes be true. But they are still bad beliefs because they are unlikely to be true. If you looked at this forecast and concluded that it’s gonna be nonstop sunshine all week, you’d be doing a bad job at believing. In sum, the point is this: There is supposed to be a tight connection between rationality and truth. To call a belief rational is, usually, just is to say that it’s the belief that’s most likely to be true, given the evidence.

[Slide 55] An analogy You and 9 friends can’t agree on where to go to dinner, so you decide to roll a d10. One number per person; winner chooses. Aaron (2) and Rakim (4) each say, “ I honestly believe that I’m going to win. And I think that that belief is rational – but so is his belief. ” (* points across the table *) They insist on this, even when you point out that there’s only a 10% chance of winning and that, odds are, both beliefs are false. So. This gives rise to a problem for the person who wants to say, most religions are raitonal. To get at the problem, let me offer an analogy. … The point being, this is a weird thing to insist on. Aaron thinks his belief is rational, even though there’s only a 10% chance that it’s true. And aaron also thinks that Rakim’s belief is rational, even though there’s only a 10% chance that his belief is true. Those two beliefs beliefs are incompatible and are probably false – and yet, he insists, are rational to have. In this case, it seems, it’s really not much of a compliment to call those beliefs rational. They aren’t, actually, likely to be true. They are very, very likely to be false.

[Slide 56] There are, basically, 3 possible answers, None – there is no religion that it’s rational to believe in One – there is one particular religion that it’s rational to believe in Many – there are many religions that it can be rational to believe in Zagzebski points out: That’s what it sounds like when we endorse the 3rd option, and insist that lots of religions can count as rational. The details, obviously, depend on how many religions you accept as being genuinely on-the-table and rational – but let’s say it’s 10. That means, when you say that another person’s religious beliefs are rational, you are saying more-or-less the same thing as Aaron and Rakim: While there’s only about a 10% chance that that person’s beliefs are true (assuming again, that there’s no particular reason to favor one religion over another), their religious beliefs are nevertheless rational. Very very likely to be false, but rational. Good belief! I approve. Which, again, a strange use of the word ‘ rational’. That usage really unmoors the approval of the belief from the likelihood that the belief is true.

[Slide 57] There is religious disagreement. The major world religions disagree about all sorts of stuff. This raises different questions, depending on your interests, If your interest is in truth : Which religion(s) are true? If it’s in being rational : Which religion(s) are rational? If it’s being saved : Which religion(s) can save me? Recall the big picture here: all of these questions arise out of the fact of religious disagreement. There’s obviously a lot more to be said about those first two questions (about which religions are true and which rational), but this is a good place to stop. [Slide 1] Toleration

[Slide 2] On toleration Intolerance and unacceptable costs Tolerating bad ideas Mill’s defense of toleration The Inquisitor and Lewis’ objection to Mill Lewis’ defense of toleration

[Slide 3] There is religious disagreement. The major world religions disagree about all sorts of stuff. This raises different questions, depending on your interests, If your interest is in truth : Which religion(s) are true? If it’s in being rational : Which religion(s) are rational? If it’s being saved : Which religion(s) can save me? Recall this point… Today I want to talk a bit more about that third question. In particular, I want to explore some surprising philosophical complications that arise for people who are exclusivists about salvation – i.e. people who think that their own religion is the only way to be saved. That belief, it turns out, creates problems in social political philosophy concerning toleration . (as we’ll see, other non-religious beliefs also give rise to this same concern about toleration. So this should be relevant to the atheists, agnostics, and inclusivist theist among you, too.)

[Slide 4] What is toleration? The easiest way to get at it, is probably by looking a few cases – and by contrasting civility with mere toleration. Civility involves communicating in a way that signals respect and consideration . Toleration involves, simply, taking no active steps to suppress or eradicate something you dislike.

[Slide 3] There is religious disagreement. The major world religions disagree about all sorts of stuff. This raises different questions, depending on your interests, If your interest is in truth : Which religion(s) are true? If it’s in being rational : Which religion(s) are rational? If it’s being saved : Which religion(s) can save me? Recall this point… Today I want to talk a bit more about that third question. In particular, I want to explore some surprising philosophical complications that arise for people who are exclusivists about salvation – i.e. people who think that their own religion is the only way to be saved. That belief, it turns out, creates problems in social political philosophy concerning toleration . (as we’ll see, other non-religious beliefs also give rise to this same concern about toleration. So this should be relevant to the atheists, agnostics, and inclusivist theist among you, too.)

[Slide 4] What is toleration? The easiest way to get at it, is probably by looking a few cases – and by contrasting civility with mere toleration. Civility involves communicating in a way that signals respect and consideration . Toleration involves, simply, taking no active steps to suppress or eradicate something you dislike.

[Slide 5] You and an acquaintance are having a serious conversation when they say something you find offensive. You try to understand their point of view, but end up telling them you think they’re making a serious error. This is civil AND tolerant.

[Slide 6] You and an acquaintance are having a serious conversation when they say something you find offensive. You don’t think they can be reasoned with, so you give ‘em a “Fuck You” and excuse yourself from the conversation. This is uncivil but still basically tolerant.

[Slide 5] You and an acquaintance are having a serious conversation when they say something you find offensive. You try to understand their point of view, but end up telling them you think they’re making a serious error. This is civil AND tolerant.

[Slide 6] You and an acquaintance are having a serious conversation when they say something you find offensive. You don’t think they can be reasoned with, so you give ‘em a “Fuck You” and excuse yourself from the conversation. This is uncivil but still basically tolerant.

[Slide 7] You and an acquaintance are having a serious conversation when they say something you find offensive. To stop them from ever saying something like that again, you pull out a pistol and threaten to gun violence them. This is uncivil AND intolerant.

[Slide 8] Tolerance is a particular kind of attitude toward something you think is bad . (after all, we don’t tolerate pizza; we relish it.) Toleration is like Diet Civility – not full-on respect, but at least a willingness to let the bad thing continue on unimpeded. Toleration is, basically, non-interference.

[Slide 7] You and an acquaintance are having a serious conversation when they say something you find offensive. To stop them from ever saying something like that again, you pull out a pistol and threaten to gun violence them. This is uncivil AND intolerant.

[Slide 8] Tolerance is a particular kind of attitude toward something you think is bad . (after all, we don’t tolerate pizza; we relish it.) Toleration is like Diet Civility – not full-on respect, but at least a willingness to let the bad thing continue on unimpeded. Toleration is, basically, non-interference.

[Slide 9] The relevant opposite is intolerance . To be intolerant of a bad thing is to take active steps to suppress or eradicate it. By default, we are (and should be!) intolerant of bad things.

[Slide 10] We didn’t tolerate COVID. We immediately tried to suppress & eradicate it. We didn’t just shrug and say, “I guess this is our reality now” when COVID showed up. We immediately set about making vaccines to eliminate it.

[Slide 9] The relevant opposite is intolerance . To be intolerant of a bad thing is to take active steps to suppress or eradicate it. By default, we are (and should be!) intolerant of bad things.

[Slide 10] We didn’t tolerate COVID. We immediately tried to suppress & eradicate it. We didn’t just shrug and say, “I guess this is our reality now” when COVID showed up. We immediately set about making vaccines to eliminate it.

[Slide 11] We shouldn’t tolerate poverty. We should try to suppress and eradicate it.

[Slide 12] We are (correctly!) intolerant of crime, of cancer, of car crashes We have laws and policies and institutions aimed at eradicating and suppressing these bad things. We don’t simply agree to disagree with cancer or murder or traffic fatalities. We set about trying to stop them.

[Slide 11] We shouldn’t tolerate poverty. We should try to suppress and eradicate it.

[Slide 12] We are (correctly!) intolerant of crime, of cancer, of car crashes We have laws and policies and institutions aimed at eradicating and suppressing these bad things. We don’t simply agree to disagree with cancer or murder or traffic fatalities. We set about trying to stop them.

[Slide 13] By default, we are (and should be!) intolerant of bad things. Toleration , in contrast, is the appropriate response only when the costs of suppression or eradication are too high. EX | taxes mosquitos noisy neighbors seasonal influenza deaths (i) To eradicate taxes would be to leave ourselves with no government – and thus, none of the good things govts can give us (organizing, leading, defending, protecting, providing, conflict arbitrating, etc) (ii) Maybe someday we’ll be able to eradicate mosquitos but we’re not there yet. Any attempts would be overwhelming costly and overwhelmingly likely to fail. (To say nothing of the possible ecological catastrophes that might result) (iii) Nobody likes hearing their neighbors stomp around at 4am – and we might sometimes take steps to get them to stop – but permanent solutions are out of reach, at least until you can afford your own home. It’s too expensive to make all the wall in your apt building thicker OR to simply rent out all the apartments around yours so that you don’t have any neighbors (iv) We could have gone into lockdown every winter to save 10s of thousands of lives but we haven’t been doing that because we don’t judge the costs to be worth the gains.

[Slide 14] N.B. Toleration is not an all-or-nothing thing.

[Slide 15] Do we tolerate the North Korean regime? Sort of yes, sort of no. We do take some active steps to suppress and eliminate the oppressive North Korean state. But we haven’t gone to war over it yet. In contrast, we literally did go to war in Vietnam out of an intolerance for Communism. In that sense, we might say that we only sorta tolerate mosquitos. We aren’t directing that much effort at eradicating the whole species, but you and I will certainly take steps to kill those that lands on our necks. We’ll use bug spray and citronella.

[Slide 16] N.B. Toleration is not an all-or-nothing thing. At each stage, as we step-up our efforts at suppression / eradication, we must ask, “Is this extra step worth it? Do the benefits justify the costs?”

[Slide 17] On tolerating bad ideas

[Slide 18] Most people think we should tolerate bad views and that t he active suppression of bad ideas is a barbaric relic of the past. Even those people who think we should be rude to the pro-slavery folks or the white-supremacists mostly don’t advocate illegalizing their views, putting them in jail or killing them.

[Slide 17] On tolerating bad ideas

[Slide 18] Most people think we should tolerate bad views and that t he active suppression of bad ideas is a barbaric relic of the past. Even those people who think we should be rude to the pro-slavery folks or the white-supremacists mostly don’t advocate illegalizing their views, putting them in jail or killing them.

[Slide 19] Consider this statue of mary dyer. Mary Dyer was hanged in Boston in 1660 for preaching Quakerism. That was after twice being ordered to cease evangelizing or leave Massachusetts, and her twice returning and continuing to preach. Mary thought it was extremely good for people to be Quakers. The colony authorities thought it was extremely bad. They thought it was so bad that they literally killed to get her to stop. Historically speaking, this is not a unusual incident. Lots of people have been jailed or killed for having views that the people in power didn’t like. Probably none of you think we should start doing that again.

[Slide 20] Most people think we should tolerate bad views and that t he active suppression of bad ideas is a barbaric relic of the past. Quiz That most people aren’t down with that kind of intolerance these days is actually pretty interesting! Given that we are, by default, intolerant of bad things, the fact that basically everyone today is in favor of toleration when it comes to bad views suggests something important. What does that suggest? Something like: everyone agrees that the costs of suppressing / eradicating bad views is ASTRONOMICALLY HIGH – so high that no view is ever bad enough to justify suppression. Again, that’s interesting!

[Slide 21] Given this near-universal agreement, we might expect it to be easy to give a satisfying defense of toleration . Surely, we could just list all the terrible costs of intolerance , and it’d be obvious that tolerance is always better .

[Slide 22] So, what are some reasons to be tolerant , from a truth-neutral perspective? (Not looking for full-fledged arguments, here – just considerations in favor of tolerance.) Quiz Here by “truth-neutral”, I mean that we’re looking for reasons that everyone can appreciate – as opposed to reasons that are only moving if you have certain political or religious or philosophical beliefs. So, for example, “One reason to be tolerant is that the Pope says tolerance is good.” is not a truth-neutral reason. Non-Catholics mostly won’t care about that. On the other hand, basically everyone thinks killing is bad so it would count as a truth-neutral reason to point out that “Effective intolerance may sometimes require us to kill people.” You don’t have to belong to any particular ideological group to care about that. So: what are some truth-neutral reasons to like tolerance – some reasons that we can mostly all agree on? Think about that for a second; try to come up with a few.

[Slide 21] Given this near-universal agreement, we might expect it to be easy to give a satisfying defense of toleration . Surely, we could just list all the terrible costs of intolerance , and it’d be obvious that tolerance is always better .

So: what are some truth-neutral reasons to like tolerance – some reasons that we can mostly all agree on? Think about that for a second; try to come up with a few.

[Slide 23] What is probably the best known defense of toleration comes from John Stuart Mill (1806 - 1873). Mill’s version is the one you all probably heard in your civics or government classes in high school. Mill wants to give us a Neutralist Tally of all the great benefits of toleration, a list that will be acceptable to all people, regardless of their religious creed or political party. Mill’s idea is basically this, Let me list off to you all the great things we get from being tolerant. And since I’m trying to argue on behalf of society at large, I’ll restrict my list to only those benefits that we can all appreciate and enjoy – nothing controversial! Christians and atheists alike can agree on this list; Republicans and Democrats both. Then he argues that that list, when added together, clearly shows that the cost of intolerance is too high – that it’s best for society if we tolerate bad ideas.

[Slide 24] Mill’s list of reasons for toleration Here’s Mill’s list of reasons to be tolerant

[Slide 24] Mill’s list of reasons for toleration Here’s Mill’s list of reasons to be tolerant

[Slide 25] Risk of error One risk of intolerance is that we might mess up and suppress the truth on accident. That would be bad.

[Slide 26] Mixture Another risk is that we might suppress a partially true/partially false view. Truth and error may be found combined in one package deal, so that there's no way of suppressing the error without also suppressing the truth So for example, if you suppress Marx’s books because you think communism is false and dangerous, you also risk suppressing the parts of Marx where he says other stuff that might be true and interesting and important

[Slide 25] Risk of error One risk of intolerance is that we might mess up and suppress the truth on accident. That would be bad.

[Slide 26] Mixture Another risk is that we might suppress a partially true/partially false view. Truth and error may be found combined in one package deal, so that there's no way of suppressing the error without also suppressing the truth So for example, if you suppress Marx’s books because you think communism is false and dangerous, you also risk suppressing the parts of Marx where he says other stuff that might be true and interesting and important

[Slide 27] Dead dogma Another reason to like tolerance is that it forces us to debate and argue over which views are true. And one important reason that debate and argument are good is that they help us develop and deepen our understanding of our own positions. By being challenged, we are forced to think a little more carefully about why we believe what we believe. So Mill worries: If we suppress all the bad views, future generations may have true beliefs but without actually understanding why those beliefs are true. Having never had to defend their positions, they will have dead dogmas – true beliefs that are poorly understood and held for shallow reasons. Since it’s important both to have true beliefs and to understand them, we should be wary of suppressing all opposition to our own views. Opposition is good for us!

[Slide 28] Critical thinking Likewise, you might think that active debate helps instill critical thinking skills in citizens. And surely the state does better when it’s citizens are wise and good at thinking. A state that suppresses all false views – and, thus, suppresses lots of substantive debates – may be a state whose citizens have really poor critical thinking skills

[Slide 29] Diversity & individuality One might also think it’s simply a good thing to have a diversity of opinions. Or, put differently, that it’s bad for everyone to conform to the same narrow template, to be a cookie-cutter copy of everyone else. If mere diversity is good then intolerance is bad because it limits diversity

[Slide 30] State surveillance Another worry is that truly effective suppression is going to require some pretty invasive state surveillance. Like, how can we be sure the communists – or whomever– aren’t secretly holding meeting? Well we’re going to have to watch everyone all the time That’s a bad thing! That’s a serious cost to effective intolerance. Thus, another reason to favor tolerance

[Slide 31] Suffering of dissidents Last – but not at all least – the people who hold the views we are trying to suppress will suffer. (Maybe because they won’t be allowed to speak their mind; maybe because we’ll throw them in jail; maybe because we will kill them) Suffering is bad. We should want to prevent suffering whenever we can. The fact that intolerance requires some people to suffer is a really good reason to favor tolerance.

[Slide 32] Mill argues, taken together, these reasons clearly tell in favor of toleration . It’s best for everyone if we argue with the bad views rather than suppress/eradicate them, for surely the good views will triumph in the end. ... Mill’s position is basically the “marketplace of ideas” defense of toleration – though that particular phrase, the “marketplace of ideas” wasn’t coined until a century later.

[Slide 33] The Inquisitor & the case for intolerance This is all set-up for the main argument in the Lewis paper. Lewis’ main point is that this traditional defense is clearly inadequate. If toleration truly is justified, this cannot be the reason why. His strategy is to show that Mill’s list of benefits cannot stack up against the kinds of bad things that we may allow by being tolerant of bad views.

[Slide 34] “Imagine the Inquisitor : a thoughtful Christian, benevolent by his own lights. He claims no infallibility. Indeed his faith is infirm, and he is vividly aware that he just might be making a tragic mistake. He grants every item in [Mill’s list.] His only complaint is that the tally is incomplete. He believes, in fact, that the included items have negligible weight compared to the omitted item: heresy . The consequence [of which] is eternal damnation.]” -David Lewis He starts with an easy case.

[Slide 35] The Inquisitor is an exclusivist about salvation. He’s not cruel nor dogmatic nor self-righteous, but he does earnestly believe that heretics will suffer in hell forever. Since he wants what’s best for everyone, he is (understandably!) very concerned about heresy.

[Slide 36] Lewis argues, To the Inquisitor – who believes heretics burn forever in hell – Mill’s defense of toleration is laughably inadequate. Why? Why is the Inquisitor unmoved by Mill’s list? Quiz

[Slide 37] For the Inquisitor, the cost of toleration is unacceptably high. Benevolent and well-meaning, it is clear to him that the best thing to do is stamp out heresy . Basically, Lewis’ point is this:... By tolerating the heretic, he risks the spread of heresy – and the eternal damnation of yet more souls. Compared to those stakes, the costs of intolerance are insignificant. EVEN IF he can never convince the heretics to change their ways so long as he is able to prevent even one person from believing the heresy, that’ll pay for everything. One soul saved from hell is worth indefinitely many people thrown in jail or executed. So. That’s not great. Here’s an example of a person who does not agree that we should be tolerant of other people’s views . Kinda concerning!

[Slide 38] Well, so what? So what if one hypothetical man has a problem with toleration? Can’t we simply ignore this fringe extremist – and those like him? Can’t the rest of all agree that toleration is clearly the better option? But maybe you’re thinking something like this.

[Slide 38] Well, so what? So what if one hypothetical man has a problem with toleration? Can’t we simply ignore this fringe extremist – and those like him? Can’t the rest of all agree that toleration is clearly the better option? But maybe you’re thinking something like this.

[Slide 39] For starters, 58% of Americans do believe in hell, and many do believe that people can be damned for having the wrong beliefs. First of all: that’s actually just not true, that basically no one has the same views as the Inquisitor... I couldn’t find any good statistics on what Americans think is required for avoiding hell, but the point remains: most people around here think that hell is a a real place and that there are souls to be saved. And it is certainly very common for religious people to think that only those with certain beliefs can avoid hell. Thus, the Inquisitor is not some irrelevant relic of the past. A sizable chunk of our country has similar beliefs. All of those people should seemingly agree with the Inquisitor that intolerance is better than tolerance. Of course, almost all Christians are actually still tolerant of non-believers. Of the nearly 200 million believers in hell in our country, basically none of them murder heretics or advocate for throwing atheists in jail. But – and this is Lewis’ point – that seems quite clearly like a big ass mistake. (Like How dare they?! How. dare. they stand around and do nothing while heresies spread, while atheism grows, while eternal souls are lost to Satan and infinite suffering?! That is unbelievably fucked up, if you earnestly believe that only good Christians go to heaven.) Or – better – it’s unbelievably fucked up, if the best we can say in support of toleration is Mill’s story. If that’s all we got, then it is a truly horrendous mistake for Christians who believe in hell to be tolerant.

[Slide 40] Lewis also points out, we are all extremists about something. Most people have views like the Inquisitor’s – i.e. views with incredibly high stakes. Seemingly, most people should feel as the Inquisitor does about the adequacy of Mill’s list. But, this same problem also arises (though, admittedly in a somewhat less extreme form!) for other members of society. The Inquisitor is not some lone counter-example to Mill’s defense of toleration; he’s just a convenient example of a broader pattern of problem cases. We don’t have to stick to religious views to make Lewis’ point. There are plenty of other political positions for which the stakes are clearly, unequivocally huge.

[Slide 64] For these reasons, Lewis argues, it often makes more sense to tolerate . The alternative is just too risky. The risks of going to war are just too high. However bad things are now – when only some people are heretics or climate skeptics or socialists or pro-choice or whatever – think about how much worse it would be if the heretics won the war and your side is the one being suppressed and eradicated. Plus, Mill has correctly pointed out that there are real and serious costs of intolerance and, more than that, since I know that I’m standing on the side of truth and justice, surely I’ll eventually win the debate anyway. For these reasons, Lewis concludes, it makes most sense to tolerate my ideological opponents – at least, until my team and I get the decisive upper hand in public opinion and can be confident that we’ll succeed at suppressing and eradicating the opposition.

[Slide 65] But that only explains why we might be tolerant about one particular issue. How do we explain why we should be tolerant of basically all views ?

[Slide 66] “First, some toleration of dangerous opinions is justified as a quid pro quo; then other toleration is justified because it makes the first transaction feasible.” -David Lewis

[Slide 67] There are lots of high stakes issues, and ( i) a person may have different allies / opponents on different issues. (ii) a person in the majority on 1 issue may be in the minority on another This seems to justify a blanket treaty of toleration on every issue – rather than a more complex situation where issues X and Y are governed by norms of tolerance while issue Z being decided via a war of intolerance. So, sure: maybe you’ll succeed at eradicating the Pro-Lifers. But are you confident that you’ll survive the Christian Inquisition? And will your Pro-Choice allies speak up for you when they come for the socialists? Thus, Lewis argues, we can explain and justify the commonsense view that we should tolerate bad views. This is a rational thing for us to believe – but the story about why is much more complicated that we might have originally supposed.

[Slide 3] A shipowner is preparing one of his ships for a trans-Atlantic voyage, its cargo a host of emigrants seeking better economic opportunities in a new country. He knows the ship is old and was never well-built. Reliable employees have suggested she needs repairs before she sails again. Doubt gnaws at the shipowner, and he wonders if he should cancel the voyage and have her overhauled and refit. Is time infinite or did God create the universe at some point?

[Slide 4] With some effort he manages to put these concerns aside. He tells himself, “She’s already seen many safe journeys, weathered many storms, and surely God would not allow these poor emigrants to die at sea.” He fosters in himself a sincere conviction that the vessel is safe and seaworthy, and he watches her departure with a light heart. Is time infinite or did God create the universe at some point?

[Slide 31] Some objections

[Slide 32] Objection ‘Faith’ just means belief in a higher power . Thus, many theists both have faith and know that their religion is correct. One common worry about the argument is this: that it is employing the word ‘faith’ in a sneaky and unfair way. Maybe “faith” just means, like, “religious belief”. If that’s right, then you can’t infer that someone lacks evidence merely from the fact that they have faith.

[Slide 31] Some objections

[Slide 32] Objection ‘Faith’ just means belief in a higher power . Thus, many theists both have faith and know that their religion is correct. One common worry about the argument is this: that it is employing the word ‘faith’ in a sneaky and unfair way. Maybe “faith” just means, like, “religious belief”. If that’s right, then you can’t infer that someone lacks evidence merely from the fact that they have faith.

[Slide 33] Reply To know something, the thing known must be actually true . Most religions are false. So most theists don’t have knowledge . But that doesn’t obviously work! The problem is this... There are two key claims here. Let’s take them one at a time.

[Slide 34] Does your friend know that he’s a glass of orange juice? Let me illustrate with a few cases. Suppose one of your (human) friends tells you that he knows he is a glass of orange juice. He tells you about all the very convincing evidence he has for this. First of all, he feels like a glass of orange juice. Also, when he looks at himself, he sees a glass of orange juice. Finally, he is sure that were he to lie down, he would spill orange juice everywhere.

Finally, he is sure that were he to lie down, he would spill orange juice everywhere.

[Slide 47] Objection Some facts are “ junk facts ” that don’t matter. It’s surely not wrong to adopt beliefs about junk facts w/ insufficient evidence. So, Clifford’s principle is false. (Reject P1) Quiz This is probably true,. But it’s not enough to save the theist. Why doesn’t this objection help the theist defend her faith?

[Slide 48] Reply Granted: maybe we can believe whatever we want about junk facts . But facts about religion aren’t junk facts ! Recall, a junk fact is a fact that is so far removed from the circumstances of your life, at present, such that it seems clear to you that your beliefs about the fact will have no bearing on your behavior. But… That’s not true of religious beliefs. For pretty much everyone, what they believe about religion will absolutely have some impact or other on their behavior. It’ll affect what they do on Sundays, what they think about when they’re in pain, what they say to pollsters who ask about their religious beliefs, etc. Religious facts aren’t junk facts – they’re rather more important and impactful than facts about badger sightings 30 years ago in Ohio.

[Slide 50] Objection It’s fine to believe w/ insufficient evidence if the belief helps achieve some good result .

[Slide 51] Your BFF tells you she’s gonna quit smoking. This is the 10 th time she’s tried, and so far she’s never made it more than a week. But she seems really confident, and you want to be supportive! So you nod and will yourself to honestly believe that this is the one; this time she’ll quit! Plausibly, it’s morally good to believe in your friend, even though you have insufficient evidence. … Here’s what I want to say about the case. This seems plausible to me: far from being immoral, willing yourself to believe in your friend despite the lack of evidence is a morally good thing to do!

[Slide 52] Reply Maybe! But is that satisfying? Is that really the best place for theists to rest their defense? (“Yes, my faith is unjustified, but it’s not immoral to be a theist so long as my religion is good for me!”) This objection works if we think that believe in God leads to some good outcome. I’m not sure that’s obviously true – to my knowledge, there’s no good empirical evidence at present that religious people are better people, for instance – but set that aside. This still seems like a worrying response. This should feel pretty unsatisfying for the theist, I think.

[Slide 53] Objection This is as much a problem for atheists as for theists. The evidence for God is mixed ! Thus, surely any belief here is objectionable. Here’s one last objection.

[Slide 54] P1) It is always, everywhere, and for anyone wrong to believe anything on the basis of insufficient evidence. P2) Given the conflicting evidence about the existence of God, any firm belief on this matter is based on insufficient evidence. C) It is wrong to positively believe or disbelieve in God. The Anti-faith Argument (generalized) Meaning agnosticism is the morally best position. Notice, this, too, does not really defend theism. It just gets more people in trouble. Next time we’ll look at a more robust response to this concern. We’ll look at a defense of the rationality of faith.

[Slide 3] Our beliefs partly determine our actions . This means our beliefs are not always morally innocuous . It can be positively bad to have bad beliefs . Recall, this was Clifford’s insight. It is morally important not only what we do but also what we believe.

[Slide 4] “It is wrong always, everywhere, and for anyone, to believe anything upon insufficient evidence .” – William K. Clifford Here’s the lesson Clifford draws. (Notice this is pretty strong, a rather forceful statement of the view. We saw some reasons last time for thinking that we need to weaken this view in a few different places. It seems to get things wrong for junk facts and for beliefs that are necessary for achieving some good results (like your belief that your friend will successfully quit smoking).)

[Slide 7] Evidence

[Slide 8] First, a distinction: Some evidence is public evidence , which can be shared w/ other people. Some evidence is private evidence , which cannot directly be shared.

[Slide 7] Evidence

[Slide 8] First, a distinction: Some evidence is public evidence , which can be shared w/ other people. Some evidence is private evidence , which cannot directly be shared.

[Slide 9] Your evidence that Obama has hands VS My evidence that Obama has hands I’ve seen photos and video – in all of them he has hands. That’s public evidence! I can share those videos/photos with you! We can look at the same evidence.

[Slide 10] Your evidence that you have a headache VS My evidence that you have a headache Suppose you have a headache. How can you tell? You feel your headaching . That’s very good evidence! But notice, I can’t feel your headaching. I don’t have access to that evidence. At best, you can tell me that your headaches – but, notice, that evidence is different from (and less persuasive than ) your evidence. Your evidence is the feeling of your head aching . My evidence is your verbal report that your head aches.

[Slide 11] Your evidence that 94 × 7 = 658 vs My evidence that 94 × 7 = 658 Is this both? In one sense, it’s private since I can’t share my internal mental calculations with you. But we can share a written account of the calculation. You could write out the problem on a piece of paper and I could watch you complete it step by step, agreeing at each point along the way. So the paper is public evidence, even if the actual internal calculations are private

[Slide 14] Hick : Seemingly, you are justified in believing you just saw Bill Murray emerge from a dumpster.

[Slide 16] Why? Quiz

[Slide 15] Hick : Seemingly, your friends are not justified in believing you just saw Bill Murray emerge from a dumpster.

[Slide 16] Why? Quiz

[Slide 17] Your evidence You saw, with your own eyes, Bill Murray emerge from the dumpster. Your friends’ evidence They were told by a friend that Bill Murray emerged from a dumpster. Your evidence is better than theirs, and that’s why it’s rational for you to believe and for them not to.

[Slide 18] If I have private evidence that you lack, then it may be rational for me to believe something, while being irrational for you to believe the same thing. Seemingly, then, this is the lesson to be drawn from this case.

[Slide 19] Evidence and God

[Slide 20] Is there public evidence for God? Yes, but it’s mixed. This was the point of our discussion about religious disagreement and this is one of the pivots upon which the Anti-Faith argument turns: The public evidence for God is not very good - or is, at best, very conflicted.

[Slide 22] Mysticism & Religious Experiences

[Slide 23] The philosopher William James is famous for his 1902, Varieties of Religious Experience , a lengthy attempt to catalog and describe mystical states . He points out that these experiences arise in all religious traditions and seem to share some core features.

[Slide 24] “ From the beginning, revelations take place in so flagrant a shape that the Sufis see before them, whilst wide awake, the angels and the souls of the prophets. They hear their voices and obtain their favors. Then the transport rises from the perception of forms and figures to a degree which escapes all expression, and which no man may seek to give an account of without his words involving sin… in the prophetic, the sight is illumined by a light which uncovers hidden things and objects which the intellect fails to reach… the transport which one attains by the method of the Sufis is like an immediate perception, as if one touched the objects with one's hand.” – Al-Ghazzali

[Slide 23] The philosopher William James is famous for his 1902, Varieties of Religious Experience , a lengthy attempt to catalog and describe mystical states . He points out that these experiences arise in all religious traditions and seem to share some core features.

They hear their voices and obtain their favors. [Slide 25] “I was alone upon the seashore as all these thoughts flowed over me, liberating and reconciling; and now again... I was impelled to kneel down, this time before the illimitable ocean, symbol of the Infinite. I felt that I prayed as I had never prayed before, and knew now what prayer really is… Earth, heaven, and sea resounded as in one vast world-encircling harmony. It was as if the chorus of all the great who had ever lived were about me. I felt myself one with them, and it appeared as if I heard their greeting: ‘Thou too belongest to the company of those who overcome.’” – Mawilda von Meysenbug

[Slide 26] James suggests that mystical, religious experiences tend to have in common, Ineffability (i.e. seem indescribable in words) Noetic quality (i.e. seem like true insights into reality) Transiency (i.e. tend to pass in a few minutes or hours) Passivity (i.e. seem “forced upon” the experiencer) … And again, such experiences are essentially universal. People of all faiths report having experiences like these, experiences that share these same core features.

I was impelled to kneel down, this time before the illimitable ocean, symbol of the Infinite. It was as if the chorus of all the great who had ever lived were about me. I felt myself one with them, and it appeared as if I heard their greeting: ‘Thou too belongest to the company of those who overcome.’” – Mawilda von Meysenbug

[Slide 26] James suggests that mystical, religious experiences tend to have in common, Ineffability (i.e. seem indescribable in words) Noetic quality (i.e. seem like true insights into reality) Transiency (i.e. tend to pass in a few minutes or hours) Passivity (i.e. seem “forced upon” the experiencer) … And again, such experiences are essentially universal. People of all faiths report having experiences like these, experiences that share these same core features.

[Slide 27] Mystical states , James observes, tend to be “absolutely authoritative over the individuals to whom they come.” That is, by their very nature, these experiences tend to strike people as obviously true and real . James makes a few final and important observations about religious experiences or mystial states… To be clear, this is merely descriptive. James is just telling how people actually seem to behave. He’s not saying that they’re being rational. (nor is he saying they’re being irrational. ) He’s merely pointing out that people who have very clear religious experiences tend to insist that those experiences are accurate. The experiences themselves often come pre-packaged with a forceful sense of veridicality. (the experience itself seems to arrive screaming, I am real; I am true; this is reality laid bare. Again, that’s just a description of how people actually talk about these experiences and act.

[Slide 28] Why think that religious experiences are private evidence ? So, obviously, here’s the next question we should ask: Should we think of religious experiences as a kind of evidence? Do the people who have religious experiences have more evidence about religious matters than the rest of us?

[Slide 28] Why think that religious experiences are private evidence ? So, obviously, here’s the next question we should ask: Should we think of religious experiences as a kind of evidence? Do the people who have religious experiences have more evidence about religious matters than the rest of us?

[Slide 29] Let’s start with a non-religious case. Suppose you have a friend with a balloon fetish. One day, you’re both confronted with a balloon. She might say, “I’m having all the same perceptual experiences as you: I smell latex; I see a red, round thing. I hear it squeak when you rub it. BUT my experience also has an additional sexual component. I perceive the balloon as sexy. I can’t point to any particular part as the sexy part; I can’t show you how the balloon is sexy. I simply perceive it that way, and the best I can do is describe to you verbally what it’s like for me. Should you doubt that your friend is experiencing the balloon as sexy? Surely not. Why would they lie about that? You should believe, My friend is experiencing this balloon as sexy.

[Slide 30] Now compare that to this case: Suppose you and I go hiking. We arrive a magnificent natural vista and spend a few minutes in quiet contemplation. At one point, you turn to me and say, “I think I’m having a religious experience. It’s like… it’s like I can feel God’s presence her with us, surrounding us. I feel her love and comfort and peace.” “I can’t point to any particular part of this vista as the religious part; I can’t show you how this scene is religious. I simply perceive it that way, and the best I can do is describe to you verbally what it’s like for me.” Should you doubt that your friend’s experience includes a component that seems religious? Hick wants to say that, as in the balloon case, it doesn’t really make a lot of sense to doubt them. Why would they lie about that? You should believe, My friend is experiencing this scene as religious. They are having that experience. But is that experience evidence? Again, Hick wants to say yes . But only for that person. These experiences should be counted as a kind of private evidence – not really any different in kind than the feeling of a headache. It’s true that your friend can’t share their religious experience with you. But, then, neither can they share the aching of their head.

She might say, “I’m having all the same perceptual experiences as you: I smell latex; I see a red, round thing. I hear it squeak when you rub it. BUT my experience also has an additional sexual component. I perceive the balloon as sexy. I can’t point to any particular part as the sexy part; I can’t show you how the balloon is sexy. I simply perceive it that way, and the best I can do is describe to you verbally what it’s like for me. Should you doubt that your friend is experiencing the balloon as sexy? Surely not. Why would they lie about that? You should believe, My friend is experiencing this balloon as sexy.

[Slide 30] Now compare that to this case: Suppose you and I go hiking. We arrive a magnificent natural vista and spend a few minutes in quiet contemplation. At one point, you turn to me and say, “I think I’m having a religious experience. It’s like… it’s like I can feel God’s presence her with us, surrounding us. I feel her love and comfort and peace.” “I can’t point to any particular part of this vista as the religious part; I can’t show you how this scene is religious. I simply perceive it that way, and the best I can do is describe to you verbally what it’s like for me.” Should you doubt that your friend’s experience includes a component that seems religious? Hick wants to say that, as in the balloon case, it doesn’t really make a lot of sense to doubt them. Why would they lie about that? You should believe, My friend is experiencing this scene as religious. They are having that experience. But is that experience evidence? Again, Hick wants to say yes . But only for that person. These experiences should be counted as a kind of private evidence – not really any different in kind than the feeling of a headache. It’s true that your friend can’t share their religious experience with you. But, then, neither can they share the aching of their head.

[Slide 31] Why think that religious experiences are private evidence ? Because they seem like genuine experiences for the experiencer – just ones that can’t be shared with others. So here’s what Hick wants to say. In the same way that the aching of my head feels real to me – even though I can’t directly prove to other people that my head aches – my mystical experiences of God may feel real to me – even though I can’t directly prove to other people that I’m really having a religious experience. And, just as I wouldn’t tell my friend, “It’s irrational for you to believe you have a genuine headache right now b/c your “evidence” isn’t publicly available bro” Hick wants to say, we shouldn’t tell our religious friend, “It’s irrational for you to believe you’re having a genuine religious experience b /c your “evidence” isn’t publicly available ”

[Slide 32] Where might religious experiences be found? What kinds of things are we looking for?

[Slide 41] P1) It is always, everywhere, and for anyone wrong to believe anything on the basis of insufficient evidence. [Slide 43] Maybe the lesson we should draw is this, Theism seems like a rational response to profound religious experiences. Because such experiences seem to count as private evidence . BUT… It must equally be true that agnostics (and atheists!) without religious experiences are responding rationally to the public evidence . (c.f. The Problem of Divine Hiddenness ) Why atheists? Why does this story change what we say about atheists? The atheist might rationally reason this way, “If God exists, She would want me to know that She exists and she would have the power to let me know! This is just the Problem of Divine Hiddenness. For me, then, the fact that I haven’t had any religious experiences seems itself to be some evidence against the existence of god. (That is, the fact that there are no religious experiences in my life is some private evidence that I have have that there are some unnecessary absences in the world. Which means, I have some private evidence that the Problem of Divine Hiddenness argument is sound. So I’m rationally justified in rejecting the 3-O God.)”

[Slide 3] Theologians and philosophers have long acknowledge an apparent tension between God’s omniscience and human freedom.

[Slide 4] “You wonder how it can be that… God has foreknowledge of all future events and [also true] that we sin voluntarily and not by necessity . For if, you say, God foreknows that a man will sin, he must necessarily sin. But if there is necessity there is no voluntary choice of sinning, but rather fixed and unavoidable necessity.” – Saint Augustine Augustine was a 4th century, early christian philosopher and scholar

[Slide 7] Omniscience

[Slide 8] We often gloss ‘ omniscient ’ as all-knowing . (“ God knows everything there is to know ”) But w e can sensibly ask, When does God know everything?

[Slide 7] Omniscience

[Slide 8] We often gloss ‘ omniscient ’ as all-knowing . (“ God knows everything there is to know ”) But w e can sensibly ask, When does God know everything?

[Slide 9] Presumably, this could never happen, You – Who was that cute guy on the bus? God – Mmm… shit… I used to know that. If you think about it for a second, you’ll quickly realize the answer needs to be always... Presumably, God never forgets anything she used to know

[Slide 10] Or this, You – Hey, how do skates work anyway? God – Not sure yet. But I’ll know someday! Likewise, presumably God already knows anything that She will know in the future... God surely doesn’t have a list of thing that she still needs to figure out. In her omniscience, she already and always has everything figured out.

[Slide 9] Presumably, this could never happen, You – Who was that cute guy on the bus? God – Mmm… shit… I used to know that. If you think about it for a second, you’ll quickly realize the answer needs to be always... Presumably, God never forgets anything she used to know

[Slide 10] Or this, You – Hey, how do skates work anyway? God – Not sure yet. But I’ll know someday! Likewise, presumably God already knows anything that She will know in the future... God surely doesn’t have a list of thing that she still needs to figure out. In her omniscience, she already and always has everything figured out.

[Slide 11] God’s Omniscience plausibly includes knowledge of the future. First, it seems confused to imagine God as ever being ignorant . ( You – Should I expect rain tomorrow? God – No clue! Take an umbrella just in case? ) Second, our theodicies say that God knows how every evil will lead to future goods . ( You – And you’re sure this suffering is necessary ? God – Probably? It’s not like I can see the future! ) If God already and always has everything figured out, it seems to follow that God’s Omniscience includes knowledge about the future. We can give a couple quick arguments in support of this conclusion… I should note: there are theists who insist that God doesn’t know the future. Usually they say that’s because the future is unfixed and unknowable. On this view, there is literally no fact of the matter right now about what will happen tomorrow, and, thus, nothing for God to know. Again, this is a real view and this view really does avoid the problem we’re talking about today. But to say that the future is unknowable creates very serious problems for most theodicies, which require God to know in advance which bits of suffering are justified by future events. So I’ll set this view aside for now. We’ll assume that God’s Omniscience does include knowledge of the future.

[Slide 12] God’s omniscience seems to come to this, At every moment, God already has true beliefs about everything, including the future. ... Ok, that’s enough about omniscience for know. Keep in mind this idea, though: God’s omniscience seems to include knowledge of the future.

[Slide 13] Human Freedom Now let’s talk about freedom for a bit.

[Slide 14] What does it mean to say that we’re free ? What is it to act or choose freely ? This is a hard question! There is considerable philosophical disagreement. One thing we can do, however, is to start by pointing to clear cases of unfree choice and clear cases of free choice.

[Slide 13] Human Freedom Now let’s talk about freedom for a bit.

[Slide 14] What does it mean to say that we’re free ? What is it to act or choose freely ? This is a hard question! There is considerable philosophical disagreement. One thing we can do, however, is to start by pointing to clear cases of unfree choice and clear cases of free choice.

[Slide 15] After much deliberation, you settle on Applebee’s. You choose Applebee’s because your crappy town only has, like, one other restaurant (Arby’s.) At first you think you want Dave & Buster’s, but one of your friends presents you with excellent reasons for choosing Applebee’s instead. You think Dave & Busties would be the best choice, but you have a crippling addiction to Applebee’s Fiesta Lime Chicken. So… that’s that. You’re chained to an Applebee’s bathroom urinal. (It’s consensual but complicated) Your captor has your phone and texts, “Applebees?” to the group chat. At first you think you want some D&B action tonight, but then a wizard casts a spell to make you prefer Applebee’s. Which is where you end up. Suppose your friends ask you to choose where to go to dinner. Quiz ... So look at this list. I’ve tried to rank these options from most free to least free. Where, intuitively, does it make the most sense to you to draw the line between free choices and unfree choices ? (Like, a line such that everything above seems like a free choice, and everything below seems unfree.)

[Slide 16] After much deliberation, you settle on Applebee’s. You choose Applebee’s because your crappy town only has, like, one other restaurant (Arby’s.) You think Dave & Busties would be the best choice, but you have a crippling addiction to Applebee’s Fiesta Lime Chicken. So… that’s that. You’re chained to an Applebee’s bathroom urinal. (It’s consensual but complicated) Your captor has your phone and texts, “Applebees?” to the group chat. At first you think you want some D&B action tonight, but then a wizard casts a spell to make you prefer Applebee’s. Which is where you end up. Suppose your friends ask you to choose where to go to dinner. Here’s my inclination: to draw the line between 3 and 4. 3 seems unproblematically free to me. It doesn’t seem to undermine my freedom in any way that I’m persuaded by good reasons to choose something – as in #3. #4 is harder. I’m kinda inclined to think that people in the grip of a powerful addiction aren’t really making free choices when it comes to the source of their addiction. But that’s more controversial. I think if you were inclined to draw the line between 4 and 5 that that’s perfectly defensible. But 5 and 6 seem like really clear cases of unfreedom.

[Slide 17] A free choice is, at the very least, one that is under that agent’s control . One popular view that you make a free choice when (and only when) it was in your power to do otherwise . So what does it mean to choose freely? Looking at these case of free and unfree choice, I think it’s clear that freedom requires some sort of control. A free choice is one that you have the right sort of control over. There are a few different ways we might precisify that thought, a few different ways of filling out what it means to have control over your choices. Here’s one plausible view – and one that’s historically been very popular among philosophers… This “could have done otherwise” is also how Pike understands freedom in the reading assigned for this module.

[Slide 18] After much deliberation, you settle on Applebee’s. You choose Applebee’s because your crappy town only has, like, one other restaurant (Arby’s.) At first you think you want Dave & Busties, but one of your friends presents you with excellent reasons for choosing Applebee’s instead. You think Dave & Busties would be the best choice, but you have a crippling addiction to Applebee’s Fiesta Lime Chicken. So… that’s that. You’re chained to an Applebee’s bathroom urinal. (It’s consensual but complicated) Your captor has your phone and texts, “Applebees?” to the group chat. At first you think you want some D&B action tonight, but then a wizard casts a spell to make you prefer Applebee’s. Which is where you end up. Suppose your friends ask you to choose where to go to dinner. Look back at this case. The ‘in your power to do otherwise’ account seems to offer us a satisfying way to explain why #4, #5 and #6 all seem to be unfree choices to go to Applebees. In #4, there’s maybe a sense in which you could have done otherwise (like, you aren’t literally forced to go to Applebees), but a person with a full-blown addiction does seems unfree in the sense that their impulse control is badly fractured. The addicted person recognizes that they should be making certain choices and… they just don’t. They can’t help themselves. They aren’t, entirely, in control. They can’t, in a real sense, do otherwise. (but, again, they aren’t literally forced. So those of you who thought #4 was a free choice have some room on the “could have done otherwise” account to make sense of your intuition, too.) The ‘in your power to do otherwise’ account does even better in 5 and 6. In 5, you literally do not have the use of your arms or access to your phone. You aren’t in control of the decision; you couldn’t do otherwise. In 6, you literally don’t have control over your own preferences or mental states. You aren’t in control of the decision to go to Applebees; you couldn’t do otherwise.

[Slide 25] There are other things to buy online. (i.e. this isn’t your only option ) You’re physically able to buy those things. (i.e. you’re not tied down or anything) You’re psychologically able to buy those things (i.e. you’re not addicted / mind-controlled) Well, recall our account of freedom: a choice counts as freely made when the agent could have done otherwise . So… does this count as free, by that account? ... So yeah… it seems like you really could have done otherwise. Surely you were in control of this decision. Surely this decision is, paradigmatically, a freely made choice.

[Slide 26] But consider the same situation from God’s perspective, 1,000 years ago, before you were even born, God (being omniscient) knew that you’d buy a Handitaur today. She believed you would, and She was right. On the day of your birth, God still knew it. It was then already true that you’d buy a Handitaur. When you woke up this morning, God still knew – i.e. She believed, had good justification for that belief, and it was already true. And when you did finally “choose” to add a Handitaur to your cart, you were doing the very thing that God has always known you’d do. (Since, like, forever!)

[Slide 25] There are other things to buy online. (i.e. this isn’t your only option ) You’re physically able to buy those things. (i.e. you’re not tied down or anything) You’re psychologically able to buy those things (i.e. you’re not addicted / mind-controlled) Well, recall our account of freedom: a choice counts as freely made when the agent could have done otherwise . So… does this count as free, by that account? ... So yeah… it seems like you really could have done otherwise. Surely you were in control of this decision. Surely this decision is, paradigmatically, a freely made choice.

[Slide 26] But consider the same situation from God’s perspective, 1,000 years ago, before you were even born, God (being omniscient) knew that you’d buy a Handitaur today. She believed you would, and She was right. On the day of your birth, God still knew it. It was then already true that you’d buy a Handitaur. When you woke up this morning, God still knew – i.e. She believed, had good justification for that belief, and it was already true. And when you did finally “choose” to add a Handitaur to your cart, you were doing the very thing that God has always known you’d do. (Since, like, forever!)

[Slide 27] Was this a free choice? It kinda seems like it from our perspective but… Not so much from God’s. As far as God’s concerned, you really couldn’t have done otherwise. God has always known you’d buy that handitaur. There was never any doubt; there was never any choice – not really. God made you the kind of person who inevitably, inexorably buys that Handitaur. Thus, the problem: if God is omniscient, it doesn’t seem like humans can ever make genuinely free choices. Let me offer an alternative way of articulating the problem, one that some of you may find more intuitive.

[Slide 28] Can you freely choose to become a member of the Roman Senate? No. Only citizens of the Republic of Rome can be in the Senate. And becoming a citizen of Rome is not in your power . Start by considering this question. … You can’t choose to be a Roman Senator. Since before you were born it was true that you’d never be able to choose that for yourself. It is, simply, not in your power .

Start by considering this question. … You can’t choose to be a Roman Senator. Since before you were born it was true that you’d never be able to choose that for yourself. [Slide 29] Can you freely choose to join Boulder’s municipal dump truck fleet (as a truck)? No. Only dump trucks can join the municipal fleet. And becoming a dump truck is not in your power .

[Slide 30] Can you freely choose to do X? If it’s not in your power to do Y – where Y is some prerequisite for X – then No. There’s a general principle here, If X requires Y and Y is not in your power, it’s also not in your power to do X.

[Slide 31] Did you freely choose to buy Handitaur? To make any other choice would’ve require you to break God’s omniscience or make God wrong – which isn’t in your power . So… no . So this is another way (I think an even more intuitive way) of stating the problem. ...

[Slide 32] This applies to any choice ever made by any human. You ate Cap’n Crunch for breakfast, rather than toast. You got an 86 on the physics exam, instead of a 68. You marry and have kids with Terry rather than Robin. Were those free choices? Could you have done otherwise? Surely not – for God always knew that you’d do as exactly as you did. And, thus, had you done anything else, you would have surprised God and broken her omniscience. But you can’t surprise God. You can’t break God’s omniscience. So it’s never in your power to make any choice other than the one that God has always foreknown. Your choices are never freely made.

[Slide 31] Did you freely choose to buy Handitaur? To make any other choice would’ve require you to break God’s omniscience or make God wrong – which isn’t in your power . So… no . So this is another way (I think an even more intuitive way) of stating the problem. ...

[Slide 32] This applies to any choice ever made by any human. You ate Cap’n Crunch for breakfast, rather than toast. You got an 86 on the physics exam, instead of a 68. You marry and have kids with Terry rather than Robin. Were those free choices? Could you have done otherwise? Surely not – for God always knew that you’d do as exactly as you did. And, thus, had you done anything else, you would have surprised God and broken her omniscience. But you can’t surprise God. You can’t break God’s omniscience. So it’s never in your power to make any choice other than the one that God has always foreknown. Your choices are never freely made.

[Slide 33] God is omniscient OR human choices are sometimes free Pick One. To be clear, the point is that these two ideas appear to be in conflict. The argument isn’t that humans aren’t free, full stop. The argument is that IF God is omniscient, THEN humans cannot be free. ALTERNATIVELY… IF humans are free, then God cannot be omniscient Even better, we should say: the point of the argument is that we have to choose between human freedom and God’s omniscience. Those two things are incompatible, so we have pick one and leave the other: We can say either: God is omniscient. Since she already foreknows what we’ll do, it’s not in our power to do otherwise; our choices are never free. OR we can say: human choices are often genuinely free. God Herself does not know what we’ll do until we do it, meaning God is not omniscient. She lacks knowledge of the future.

[Slide 34] Why is this a problem?

[Slide 34] Why is this a problem?

[Slide 35] Why can’t we just say, “Oops! Turns out I’m not really free. But, no big deal. I still mostly feel free!” ? … There are two major problems I want to point to.

[Slide 36] “You wonder how it can be that… God has foreknowledge of all future events and [also true] that we sin voluntarily and not by necessity . For if, you say, God foreknows that a man will sin, he must necessarily sin. But if there is necessity there is no voluntary choice of sinning, but rather fixed and unavoidable necessity.” – Saint Augustine The first was actually already articulated in this quote from Augustine. He was trying to explain why it matters that divine omniscience and human freedom are incompatible... His worry is that we can’t make sense of sin, can’t hold people responsible for their own misbehaviors, unless people sin freely and voluntarily.

[Slide 35] Why can’t we just say, “Oops! Turns out I’m not really free. But, no big deal. I still mostly feel free!” ? … There are two major problems I want to point to.

[Slide 36] “You wonder how it can be that… God has foreknowledge of all future events and [also true] that we sin voluntarily and not by necessity . For if, you say, God foreknows that a man will sin, he must necessarily sin. But if there is necessity there is no voluntary choice of sinning, but rather fixed and unavoidable necessity.” – Saint Augustine The first was actually already articulated in this quote from Augustine. He was trying to explain why it matters that divine omniscience and human freedom are incompatible... His worry is that we can’t make sense of sin, can’t hold people responsible for their own misbehaviors, unless people sin freely and voluntarily.

[Slide 37] Our legal system and everyday moral practices assume that people deserve certain kinds of treatment, good or ill, on the basis of their behavior. But if we never act freely , then that attitude is incoherent at best. Put differently, how can we hold people responsible for actions over which they had literally zero control ? Put differently, … The thought is: If God infallibly foreknew all of the bad things I’d ever do, then none of my choices were really UP TO ME, in the right sort of way. Before I was born, God already knew cheat on my taxes or whatever. it was already true that i’d do that. But, then, surely I can’t be held responsible for a bad deed that was casually fixed before I was even born. That’s sounds like crazy talk! Compare: suppose I make an assassination robot from scratch. I program it to pick up a gun, walk next door, and kill my next door neighbor – scripting out all of those actions in detail and in advance. Such that: I foreknow exactly what my robot will do when I turn it on. Surely, the robot itself is not responsible for killing my neighbor. And that should be true, regardless of how cognitively sophisticated my robot is. Like, suppose I give it conscious experiences and a rich intelligence. It is AWARE of itself as a being as it marches to my neighbors house and shoot him. Still: my robot is not responsible for that killing. How could it be? Given its programming, it doesn’t have any control of that act. It couldn’t do otherwise. This is, essentially, the worry that Augustine is raising about God’s omniscience. If God foreknows everything we’ll do, then we are all like conscious little robots, following the hard-coded scripts that God has foreordained for us. And, if that’s right, no one is ever morally responsible for anything. No one ever deserves to be punished (or rewarded) – by us or by God. Because no one ever really chooses anything, neither righteous nor wicked.

[Slide 38] Another Problem – If human choices are never free , then we must abandon the Free Will Theodicy . And there’s a second problem… The whole point of the Free Will Theodicy was that humans are free and human freedom is super duper valuable. More valuable than preventing any amount of suffering that might result from a freely made choice. But… now it looks like a 3-O God – in particular a Omniscient God – can’t actually co-exist with freedom. A 3-O God never has to choose between preserving human freedom and preventing evil because… there is no human freedom to preserve.
