[Slide 39] Objections So, the problem is that it looks like the theist can’t have both divine omniscience AND human freedom . That leaves the theist with 3 options; (1) Accept that God is omniscient; deny that humans make free choices (2) Accept that humans make free choices; deny that God is omniscient (3) Find some way to argue that both can be true: God is omniscient and humans are free OBvs, most theists want to go for that last option. So I want to finish our discussion by looking at some objections from theists, some attempts to thread the needle and argue that Omniscience and Freedom are compatible.

[Slide 40] Objection We’ve gotten the explanation backwards, God’s beliefs don’t force us to act ; our free actions force God to believe . Recall, the concern is that you choose to buy the Handitaur BECAUSE God always and already knew that you would. First, God knows, THEN that knowledge determines and explains why you act the way that you. This objection reverses that explanation: First, you act the way that you do (you buy the Handitaur), THEN that action determines and explains why God believes what she does (i.e. believes that you choose to buy the handitaur)

[Slide 39] Objections So, the problem is that it looks like the theist can’t have both divine omniscience AND human freedom . That leaves the theist with 3 options; (1) Accept that God is omniscient; deny that humans make free choices (2) Accept that humans make free choices; deny that God is omniscient (3) Find some way to argue that both can be true: God is omniscient and humans are free OBvs, most theists want to go for that last option. So I want to finish our discussion by looking at some objections from theists, some attempts to thread the needle and argue that Omniscience and Freedom are compatible.

[Slide 40] Objection We’ve gotten the explanation backwards, God’s beliefs don’t force us to act ; our free actions force God to believe . Recall, the concern is that you choose to buy the Handitaur BECAUSE God always and already knew that you would. First, God knows, THEN that knowledge determines and explains why you act the way that you. This objection reverses that explanation: First, you act the way that you do (you buy the Handitaur), THEN that action determines and explains why God believes what she does (i.e. believes that you choose to buy the handitaur)

[Slide 41] Does Obama exist because you know that Obama exists? Or do you know that Obama exists because he does ? The theist might point out: this is normally how beliefs work anyway! Compare: ask yourself... The world isn’t Peter Pan , and Obama isn’t Tinkerbell. We didn’t and can’t bring Obama into the world just by believing in him hard enough. Rather, first he existed and that caused us to have the belief, Obama exists. Point being: beliefs are fundamentally RESPONSES to the world. First, something is true in the world, THEN that causes us to adopt a certain belief (the belief that the thing is true.) The reverse sounds very silly: it’s not as though first we believe something and THEN our believe causes that thing to be true. The theist’s point is: surely God’s beliefs are like that too! First, the thing happens, then God believes it, in response.

[Slide 42] Reply First, the claim was never that God’s knowledge forces us to act; it merely signals that we’re unable to do otherwise. Second, this objection requires backwards causation. (Which is probably incoherent!) Objection We’ve gotten the explanation backwards, God’s beliefs don’t force us to act ; our free actions force God to believe . … It’s not like God’s knowledge is some golem standing behind us holding our arms and making us do stuff. That’s was never the claim. Rather, the claim is that we can tell that our actions aren’t free from the fact that God infallibly foreknows what we’ll do. (COMPARE: you can tell that someone is asleep from the fact that they’re snoring – snoring signals sleep. But that doesn’t mean that snores cause people to sleep . ) Worse though, is the fact that this objection commits us to some pretty dubious metaphysics.

[Slide 43] I can’t die today from a bullet you fire next week . You can’t hear thunder ten minutes before the lightning strikes . Your choice today can’t cause God to foreknow it in 200 B.C.E. This is something we all seem pretty committed to: an effect cannot precede its cause. Nothing I do today can cause events to happen 400 years ago. Nothing that happens a millennium from now can reach back in time and cause me to do certain things today. ...

[Slide 44] Objection God doesn’t just foreknow our choices; She foreknows that we’ll choose freely . Quiz … if that’s right, then clearly our choices MUST BE free. God’s knows they are! Quiz Question: Why doesn’t think work? Why isn’t this a satisfying objection?

[Slide 45] Objection God doesn’t just foreknow our choices; She foreknows that we’ll choose freely . Reply This seems to ignore the problem – to wit, that one seemingly cannot infallibly foreknow the outcome of a free choice . This obj basically just asserts that there is no incompatibility w/o actually giving us any reasons for thinking that’s right. This objection isn’t an argument at all; it’s just a bald assertion that there is no problem. That’s… not how objections work. Objections are, fundamentally, arguments. This is just a bare statement of disagreement with the same rhetorical force as simply yelling ‘NO’ and shaking your head. COMPARE: Recall that assassination robot case from early. I programmed my robot, step-by-step, to pick up a gun, walk to my neighbor’s house, and shoot my neighbor. Obviously, when I’m standing before the judge and jury on trial for murder, I’m not going to persuade anyone that I’m innocent by arguing that not only programmed my robot to murder; I programmed it to freely murder my neighbor. (and, thus, it’s the robot’s responsibility not mine.)

[Slide 46] Objection God’s foreknowledge is like that of a friend who knows you really well. One more objection.

[Slide 46] Objection God’s foreknowledge is like that of a friend who knows you really well. One more objection.

[Slide 47] Surely your BFFLs predictive accuracy doesn’t undermine your freedom ! Here’s the thought. Suppose, for instance, that your best friend just, like, gets you. I mean REALLY gets you. It sometimes feels like she knows what you’re going to do before you know what you’re going to do. It’s creepy, almost. She can predict what you’re going to order at restaurants, what you’re going to wear in the morning, what you’re going to say in class. She knows which things you’ll like before you’ve even tried them. She’s good at predicting when your relationships will end, how you’ll do on tests, and how you’ll be feeling at any particular moment. Surely there’s a sense in which your BFF consistently foreknows what you’ll do, what choices you’ll make. But – look – clearly that doesn’t mean you’re unfree. How could it? Like, “Oh, if you predicted I might do that, then I guess I didn’t have a choice after all.” What? So maybe god is like that. A BFFL who knows you so well She can predict all your behaviors.

[Slide 48] Objection God’s foreknowledge is like that of a friend who knows you really well. (i.e. accurate predictions don’t make you unfree !) Reply Your friend’s foreknowledge isn’t really like God’s b/c your BFF is fallible. (It is in your power to surprise your friend!) … Your friend, while very accurate, can still be wrong. It is very much in your power to surprise your friend, to make her wrong. You could set out to be, like, so random one day, and if you tried hard enough and introduced some genuine uncertainty into your decision-making (like rolling some dice or something), you would successfully break your friend’s foreknowledge. She can’t predict that you’ll roll a 6 or whatever. She’s not Omniscient, just very observant In short, the problem is that this objection seems to misunderstand the problem. The problem isn’t that God expects us to do certain things and is right a lot. The problem is that God is ALWAYS and INFALLIBLY RIGHT. So much so that, it’s impossible for us to surprise God, to break her omniscience. It’s God’s infallibility which undermines our freedom, not the fact that She knows your pretty well.

[Slide 49] God is omniscient OR human choices are sometimes free Pick One. Zooming back out, recall the big picture… As always, there’s more to be said here! There are other possible replies open to the theist –- maybe she can argue that we’re understanding freedom wrong and that the true view is quite compatible with omniscience? Or maybe the theist can try to make sense of the backwards causation of our actions in present causing God’s beliefs in the past (perhaps by giving us a robust account of God’s timelessness – i.e. an account of what it means to say that God exists outside of time?) But I’ll end our conversation at this point.

[Slide 1] Petitionary Prayer

[Slide 2] On prayer and petitions The many uses of prayer An account of generic petitions The Problem Responding to the Problem God predetermined that our prayers are causally important God is like a parent, waiting for us to ask for help Petitionary prayers are merely devotional prayers

[Slide 3] Prayer To pray is, obviously, to attempt to speak directly with or to God. Prayer is thus a form of communication and, as with any form of communication, a prayer is capable of carrying a wide variety of messages.

[Slide 4] People pray to, among other things, Affirm their devotion Voice their admiration Indicate their contrition Express their gratitude Request God’s intercession At the point where one believes in a loving, 3-O God, one must surely have lots to say to such a being. And, accordingly, we can recognize lots of different kinds of prayers, lots of different messages that people routinely send to God.

[Slide 3] Prayer To pray is, obviously, to attempt to speak directly with or to God. Prayer is thus a form of communication and, as with any form of communication, a prayer is capable of carrying a wide variety of messages.

[Slide 4] People pray to, among other things, Affirm their devotion Voice their admiration Indicate their contrition Express their gratitude Request God’s intercession At the point where one believes in a loving, 3-O God, one must surely have lots to say to such a being. And, accordingly, we can recognize lots of different kinds of prayers, lots of different messages that people routinely send to God.

[Slide 5] People pray to, among other things, Affirm their devotion Voice their admiration Indicate their contrition Express their gratitude Request God’s intercession Most of these types of prayers are straightforwardly reasonable. In other words, they make sense for theists to perform , given their belief in a loving 3-O God. Praise is a little bit complicated since a loving 3-O God clearly doesn’t need our encouragement to keep going or our admiration to feel like She’s doing a good job (literally the best possible job, as we’ve already extensively discussed.). So, we might sensibly ask, what’s the point of our praising God? What role does praise serve in one’s spiritual life, given that God really, definitely doesn’t need our praise? We could have that conversation, and it would be an interesting one. But that’s not actually what I want to talk about today.

[Slide 6] We are interested in these. People pray to, among other things, Affirm their devotion Voice their admiration Indicate their contrition Express their gratitude Request God’s intercession Our actual target is that last category of prayers, the prayers of intercession or, more simply put, petitionary prayers. Very often, people pray to God when and because they want something. They pray for aid for themselves, for their loved ones and acquaintances, or for the world more generally. A prayer for healing is a petitionary prayer. A prayer for one’s atheist neighbor to find god is a petitionary prayer. A prayer for the hurricane victims or for world peace is a petitionary prayer. In each case, the person praying is asking God for help, asking God to bring about some particular, desired state of affairs . As we’ll see, it’s actually rather difficult to make sense of these sorts of prayers when one believes in a loving, 3-O God.

[Slide 5] People pray to, among other things, Affirm their devotion Voice their admiration Indicate their contrition Express their gratitude Request God’s intercession Most of these types of prayers are straightforwardly reasonable. In other words, they make sense for theists to perform , given their belief in a loving 3-O God. Praise is a little bit complicated since a loving 3-O God clearly doesn’t need our encouragement to keep going or our admiration to feel like She’s doing a good job (literally the best possible job, as we’ve already extensively discussed.). So, we might sensibly ask, what’s the point of our praising God? What role does praise serve in one’s spiritual life, given that God really, definitely doesn’t need our praise? We could have that conversation, and it would be an interesting one. But that’s not actually what I want to talk about today.

[Slide 6] We are interested in these. A prayer for healing is a petitionary prayer. A prayer for one’s atheist neighbor to find god is a petitionary prayer. A prayer for the hurricane victims or for world peace is a petitionary prayer. As we’ll see, it’s actually rather difficult to make sense of these sorts of prayers when one believes in a loving, 3-O God.

[Slide 7] Do petitionary prayers make sense? (Are they effective or pointless and futile ?) This, then, is our question. For the most part, people make petitionary prayers to God because they genuinely want God’s aid AND, they seem to think, praying for it is an effective way to get help. So our question is: Does that make sense? Is it plausibly true that making petitions to God, asking for GOd’s help, really works ?

[Slide 8] In general, when do petitions / requests for intercession make sense? I suggest, the best way to understand the looming problem for petitionary prayers is to take a step back and think about petitions in general. We ask: under what conditions does it normally make sense to petition another person? Then, once we have a better understanding of petitions in general, we can simply apply our newly acquired understanding to petitions to God in particular

[Slide 10] In Star Wars , the plot begins with Leia sending a message to Obi-Wan Kenobi, asking him for help delivering R2-D2 to the planet Alderaan. Why does she do this? Why is this something that makes sense to do ? Here’s an easy and well-known pop culture example for us to play with...

[Slide 11] In order for Leia’s decision to petition to be rational (i.e. to make sense ), it seems that she must hold a few beliefs. I submit...

[Slide 12] Obi-Wan will be able to help and will genuinely be of help . If Leia thought that Obi-Wan was literally unable to help or that his “help” would ultimately be unhelpful, it wouldn’t make sense to ask. One, Leia must believe that Obi-Wan, the person she’s petitioning, will be able to help and will be of help… … put differently, there’s a good reason why Leia petitions a powerful space wizard like Obi Wan and not one of her long dead ancestors or her 5 year old cousin. Her long dead ancestors can’t help her, and her 5 year old cousin wouldn’t really be of help .

[Slide 13] Obi-Wan will want to help . If Leia didn’t think Obi-Wan could be motivated to help her, then she would contact someone else, instead. Two, Leia must believe that... … again, it wouldn’t make sense of Leia to contact, say. Darth Vader about helping. She already knows that he’ll say No. So it doesn’t make sense to ask.

Two ways for this to be true: Her request gives Obi-Wan new info on a situation he wasn’t already aware of. Her request gives Obi-Wan new reasons to act that he didn’t have before. Three, Leia must believe that her request will genuinely make a difference...

[Slide 16] Do petitionary prayers make sense? (Are they effective or pointless and futile ?) So back to our original question… We can simply apply our general theory of petition!

[Slide 17] For a petitionary prayer to make sense , the person praying must think that, God is able to help. God will want to help. God will not help unless a petition is made. Why would this ever be true? … When all of these things are true, then praying to God for help makes good sense. When any or all of these things are not true, praying to God for help won’t really make sense. Will be futile, pointless, silly, absurd. So imagine a theist who is praying to a loving 3-O God for aid. Surely Condition 1 is satisfied; surely God can help. God is all-powerful! And surely Condition 2 is satisfied; since God is loving and morally perfect, surely She wants to be of assistance for her chosen companions. (assuming the person isn’t asking for something evil.) But what about Condition 3? As we’ll see, Condition 3 is what gives rise to our problem. Simply put, it is hard to explain why Condition 3 would ever be true. Why would a loving 3-O God ever make her assistance conditional on a prayer?

[Slide 18] The Problem That’s the general shape of the concern, but we can be more precise than that. And I think the best place to start is by looking at cases of everyday petition (between humans!) that evince the same problem.

[Slide 19] So imagine you’re standing in line at Chipotle. As you get near the end of the line, you ask the person serving you, “Hey, when you finish putting all the ingredients in my burrito, would you actually roll it closed for me? I’ve always found out that burritos are way easier to hold that way!” This is, clearly, a stupid and pointless petition. The person is already aware of the need to roll your burrito closed and is obviously already planning to do that. Your petition doesn’t make sense b/c it clearly won’t make a difference. The person is already obviously planning to do the thing you’re petitioning for.

[Slide 20] Similarly, suppose you’re about to go under general anesthesia for an appendectomy, and you say to the surgeon, “hey, um, can I ask that you actually wash your hands before the procedure? I’m worried about getting an infection!” Again, this is a pointless and stupid petition and for the same reason: The person is already aware of the need and obviously already planning to do that. This petition surely is not going to make a difference. (Making this request is, if anything, kind of insulting! When you ask the surgeon to wash her hands, you thereby imply that you weren’t expecting her to do that. Which is insulting! It would be monstrous for her to not wash her hands before surgery.)

[Slide 21] Here’s a case of the opposite sort: Suppose you’re at the DMV to get your license renewed. You finally finish the process, and the employee working with says you owe $35.68. You ask her, a complete stranger, “Hey, could you actually pay that for me? And also, I noticed that picture of a dog on your desk… would you be willing to change your dog’s name to be my name?” Obviously, this too is a deeply silly petition – but for the opposite reason. In this case, it’s blindly obvious that the answer is No. This random employee is not going to pay for your license nor will they name their dog after you. This petition is obviously not going to make a difference. Which is why it doesn’t make sense to petition for this.

[Slide 22] Quiz So, quiz question: Can you see the problem for petitionary prayer yet? Think about it for a second – why might we think that petitioning a loving, 3-O God is necessary silly and pointless?

[Slide 23] Here’s the worry: Petitioning a loving 3-O God for help is rather like petitioning a surgeon to wash their hands before surgery or petitioning a random DMV employee to re-name her dog after you. It’s hard to see how any of these requests could ever make a difference. Since God already knows everything and already has a perfect plan for the future, your petitions must surely all seem pointless and futile. There are 2 ways this might go: On the one hand, if you pray for something good and sensible, then God is obviously already aware of your need and obviously already planning to help. Your asking doesn’t make a difference. Obversely, if you’re asking God for something stupid or evil or impossible or just plain unwise or unworkable, then God has obviously already determined not to do that and your request is, again, completely pointless. Your asking cannot make a difference. Thus, either way – whether you ask for something sensible or wicked – surely your petition doesn’t make a difference. [next slide]

[Slide 24] Put simply, Since a 3-O God has already decided whether or not She’ll do whatever it is you’re requesting, surely a petitionary prayer can never make a difference . Here’s a tidier statement of the problem … It’s just really hard to see how any petition of yours would ever make a difference, would ever cause God to do something she would not otherwise have done. God, in her wisdom, isnt going to let you talk her into making a mistake, nor does she need your help in pointing out which actions are worth doing.

[Slide 26] Responding to the Problem What options does the theist have for making sense of petitionary prayer, then?

[Slide 27] Possible Response God has already decided that we’ll pray AND that our prayers will be a meaningful part of the causal pattern of the world. ... This is effectively Thomas Aquinas’ response to the problem. On this view, our petitionary prayers ARE NOT aimed at getting God to do something that She wouldn’t otherwise do. Rather the course of events is entirely predetermined and we have been assigned a role in those events: namely, our role is to offer prayers. God has woven our prayers into the causal fabric of the universe. Which means our question, “But do petitionary prayers actually DO anything?” doesn’t arise. God has already decided that there will be prayers . Praying is our assigned role. It’s true that our prayers for aid don’t persuade God . But they are still important.

[Slide 28] Replies (i) This doesn’t explain why our prayers are included in God’s plan as important causes. (ii) This is, essentially, just an assertion that there is no problem . (“ Pet. prayers make sense b/c God says they do!”) (iii) This seems to give up on free will . 1) The original problem is that praying for assistance from God seems futile and pointless. It’s not obvious that this response really helps. Why should we bother praying? Aquinas tells us, because God has already planned for us to pray. But… why though? Why does God want that in her plans? Why doesn’t God tell us instead to stop wasting our damn time on prayers and go help the poor? In other words, this proposal doesn’t actually explain what we’re trying to explain. At best, we’ve merely pushed the question back a step. Our original question, “Why should we bother praying for help?”, has now been replaced with a new question, “Why does God want us to bother praying for help?” 2) We’ve seen this issue over and over again in this course. Hopefully, you’ve picked up on the pattern by now. First, we observe some apparent contradiction lurking among our religious beliefs. “What up with that?” we ask. Then one of the theist’s first responses is to say, “Ahh, but there’s actually no contradiction here b/c of God and the way that She is.” Which… isn’t really a response at all. Replies like that don’t give us any reasons for thinking that the theist is right. As before, as always, this response sucks. It’s unsatisfying. It’s quitting on the problem, not resolving it. 3) A final concern with this reply is that it seems to require that God preordain our petitionary prayers. Aquinas tells us that God has already decided that we will pray! We don’t really have a choice! In that sense, this “solution” at best only gets rid of our present problem to create another one. Now we can make sense of prayer, but we don’t have any free will.

[Slide 29] Possible Response Maybe our petitionary prayers provide God with new information ? Reply This won’t work if God is truly Omniscient . What else might we say to defend the sensibleness of petitionary prayer? Well, recall, the problem before us is that petitionary prayers don’t seem to ever make a difference to what God decides to do. And, recall, there were two ways in which you might expect a petition to make a difference. First, your petition might give the person new information that they didn’t have before. Second, your petition might give the person new reasons to act that they didn’t have before. So, one option for making sense of petitionary prayer is to argue that our prayers provide God with new information. Our prayers matter and make sense b/c they apprize God of important new facts. But that won’t work. If God really does already and always know all of the facts, then we have nothing to teach Her.

[Slide 30] Possible Response Maybe our petitionary prayers provide God with new reasons to act? But what about the second way in which petitions make a difference? Maybe we don’t teach God anything, but, instead, we give Her new reasons. Maybe the mere fact of our asking gives God a new reason to act that She didn’t have before.

[Slide 31] Quiz The thought is something like this: Suppose you are a parent, and your child is struggling with their math homework. Do you immediately go to help them, as soon as you see them struggling? Probably not. You probably wait until they ask for your help. But why? Quiz question: Think about that for a second. Why might you hold off on helping your child with their math homework until they ask ? Lots of things we might say here: Maybe you hold off on helping b/c you’re trying to teach them independence. Or maybe they need to learn that sometimes we should ask others for the help we need. Or maybe your child simply prefers to work through hard problems on their own. And so when you offer to help w/o being asked, they resent it. (“I didn’t ask you yet!” your kid yells.) and thus, when your child does ask for help, you get a new reason to act that you didn’t have before: your child is ready for help. That wasn’t true until he asked!

[Slide 32] Can we tell the same story about God? Perhaps petitionary prayers are like that. Maybe God is trying to teach us independence, and so doesn’t rush to help us unasked. Or maybe God wants some of us to learn that we sometimes must ask for the help we need – and so doesn’t rush to help us unasked. Or maybe many of us simply prefer to work through hard problems on their own – and so God waits to be asked. On any of these stories, when a person does finally pray to God for help, God does thereby get a new reason to act that She didn’t have before: your child is ready for help. That wasn’t true until the prayer was made!

[Slide 33] “It will not be easy for there to be friendship between a [3-O God] and a fallible, finite, imperfect person… There are at least two dangers for the inferior member of the pair. First, he can be so overcome by the advantages or superiority of his “friend” that he becomes simply a slavish follower… [Second,] there is the danger of becoming spoiled in the way that members of a royal family [may be spoiled, becoming] tyrannical, willful, indolent, self-indulgent.” – Eleonore Stump Stump offers a very similar reply, suggesting that God may hold back on helping until asked because of some familiar problems that arise in unequal friendships – i.e. friendships between one very powerful person and one very unpowerful person… Stump’s suggestion is: God holds back because she doesn’t want to smother us. SHe doesnt’ want be so present in our lives that we don’t really have a chance to breathe and be our own people AND because she doesn’t want to spoil us, doesn’t want to give theists help so much and so often that they become bratty shitheads.

[Slide 34] Possible Response Our petitionary prayers provide God with new reasons to act – the fact that I have asked for help is a new reason to help me! So this is a more promising response! Maybe petitionary prayers make sense b/c God is intentionally holding back from helping us until asked.

[Slide 35] Replies (i) God, unlike a parent, will know that you’re ready for help w/o you needing to pray . (ii) If petitionary prayers do give God new reasons , then God seems to be unfair. A couple possible worries about this response: (1) One concern: In the parent case, the child’s request is doing two things at once: it is both giving the parent new reason AND giving the parent new information. The child’s request informs the parent, Okay, I’m ready for help! But that’s not true in the God case. God will know when you’re ready for help as soon as you’re ready. You don’t need to tell God. She already knows. So… what good is your prayer doing? Why do we need to announce our readiness with petitionary prayers? God knows when we’re ready, and seemingly that’s when She gets new reasons to act. But then, again, what’s the point of prayer? (2)... Let me give you a case to illustrate the thought.

[Slide 36] Imagine a small mountain town, nestled in a valley. Rains have been heavy this season and so has soil erosion. One day, two residents hear a loud rumbling. Running outside, they see a swarm of huge boulders, loosened by the rains, rolling down a ridge toward the town. One resident immediately throws up a prayer, “God, please spare my house and my family from the avalanche.” The other resident, not being a religious man, doesn’t pray. The boulders take some unlucky bounces and completely crush the house of the non-religious man but spare the property of the religious man. What are we to make of this?

[Slide 36] Imagine a small mountain town, nestled in a valley. Rains have been heavy this season and so has soil erosion. One day, two residents hear a loud rumbling. Running outside, they see a swarm of huge boulders, loosened by the rains, rolling down a ridge toward the town. One resident immediately throws up a prayer, “God, please spare my house and my family from the avalanche.” The other resident, not being a religious man, doesn’t pray. The boulders take some unlucky bounces and completely crush the house of the non-religious man but spare the property of the religious man. What are we to make of this?

[Slide 37] Two ways this might have gone... Option 1 The prayer was irrelevant . God’s morally perfect plan was always to crush the one house and spare the other. Option 2 The prayer was relevant . God’s plan was, originally, to crush both houses, but the prayer prevented one of the crushings. Now, obviously Option 1 does raise some serious concerns! Why would it ever be morally perfect for God to crush a house with a boulder? But, notice, that’s just the Problem of Evil – why would God let this bad thing happen? I don’t know, but we’ve already had that conversation. Nothing new here. Option 2, though, raises both Problem of Evil concerns and a genuinely new concern: If God answers prayers like this, if the theist’s prayers really do provide God with a new reason to act, then God now appears either unfair and vindictive. If prayers matter and make a difference, then people who don’t pray must thereby suffer more and suffer preventably and precisely because they don’t pray. On this view, it is part of God’s morally perfect plan that She will prevent bad things from happening to Her favorites at their request, but will let bad things befall everyone else. The non-believers are, effectively, punished for their failure to pray. (Obviously, God knows that the atheists need her help. But, on Option 2, She doesn’t actually help them unless they pray.) The point is: It’s very hard to imagine a morally perfect being adopting this strategy. God is supposed to love all us equally, to want what’s best for all of us all of the time. And it’s really hard to think that a being like that would punish people for not praying.

[Slide 38] Replies (i) God, unlike a parent, will know that you’re ready for help w/o you needing to pray . (ii) If petitionary prayers do give God new reasons , then God seems to be unfair. So that’s the second problem with this reply (i.e. the one that says “Petitionary Prayers give god new reasons that She didn’t have before.”) The problem is, briefly, that many of those who need help don’t ask God for it. So if God answers prayers, if prayers really do make a difference, there’s now a serious fairness problem. God apparently isn’t willing to count a person’s readiness for help as a good reason to act unless that person actually asks. And that seems pretty messed up.

[Slide 39] Possible Response Maybe our petitionary prayers aren’t really petitions at all but devotional prayers in disguise? One more response I want to consider… If that’s right, then the fact that petitionary prayers are ineffective is not important. If they’re just devotional prayers, they aren’t really supposed to be effective!

[Slide 40] Consider the phenomenon of fan mail . Sometimes when you really like, say, an author or musician, you may write them a letter telling them how much their art means to you. You might even suggest a lyric or story idea, or ask them to do an event in your town . Here’s a hopefully apt comparison ... Think about that for a second, tho. Does the letter writer really expect the author to use her book idea or the musician to schedule a tour date in her town MERELY because she asked? Probably not! The author or musician is going to keep doing what they were already going to do. Thus, although the fan mail may sound like it contains petitions (“Please right a book using my idea.”) it really doesn’t. The point is just to express appreciation. And, one might reason, maybe “petitionary” prayers are like that too: not really requests but more expressions of appreciation.

If they’re just devotional prayers, they aren’t really supposed to be effective!

[Slide 40] Consider the phenomenon of fan mail . Sometimes when you really like, say, an author or musician, you may write them a letter telling them how much their art means to you. You might even suggest a lyric or story idea, or ask them to do an event in your town . Here’s a hopefully apt comparison ... Think about that for a second, tho. Does the letter writer really expect the author to use her book idea or the musician to schedule a tour date in her town MERELY because she asked? Probably not! The author or musician is going to keep doing what they were already going to do. Thus, although the fan mail may sound like it contains petitions (“Please right a book using my idea.”) it really doesn’t. The point is just to express appreciation. And, one might reason, maybe “petitionary” prayers are like that too: not really requests but more expressions of appreciation.

[Slide 41] Possible Response A petitionary prayer is really just a way of saying, “ Thy will be done .” The prayer isn’t supposed to change God’s mind but is merely devotional . Thus, our response… On this view, all petitionary prayers are just devotional prayers. The person who prays isn’t actually trying to persuade God to help; she’s just expressing her admiration for God and her confidence in God’s perfect plan. That’s helpful b/c it’s easy to make sense of devotional prayers. Those are clearly sensible and rational. It makes sense for theists to periodically renew their devotion to God and to remind themselves that everything that happens is in accordance with God’s plan. … What are we to make of this reply? I think this response is potentially successful , but comes at a moderate cost. The cost is that we must now admit that most theists are simply confused when they make petitionary prayers. Most theists actually do believe that their prayers for assistance are effective petitions. But if we adopt this response, that’s not correct. Our prayers for aid don’t make a difference; they simply affirm our devotion to God. So this response is maybe successful but highly revisionary – we have to revise our understand of WHY we pray for God’s assistance.

[Slide 42] The Problem Since a 3-O God has already decided whether or not She’ll do whatever it is you’re requesting, surely a petitionary prayer can never make a difference . So let’s zoom out one more time. Recall the problem:... We’ve looked at a few different possible responses – some more promising than others. Obviously and as always, there’s a lot more to be said. But I’ll leave you with a couple suggestive thoughts.

[Slide 43] Two other issues surrounding petitionary prayer (1) We can’t tell whether our prayers made any difference, when answered (2) We can’t tell why our prayers are unanswered, when they are We’ve talked about A problem with petitionary prayers. But there seem to be a couple other, philosophically interesting ones. Suppose we decide that petitionary prayers really are effective and really do sometimes make a difference. If so, then I think the theist is still left with two interesting epistemological problems. The first is that it’s never going to be perfectly clear whether our prayers made a difference. Suppose you get the thing you prayed for. Sometimes that may be because you prayed. Other times is may be because God was already planning to bring about whatever thing you prayed for. But how do we tell those cases apart? Second, there will be plenty of other times where the theist’s prayers clearly don’t get answered by God. This too raises an interesting problem. Namely, it’s rarely going to be obvious WHY God has chosen not to answer our prayers. Is it because the thing we’ve prayed for is wicked or selfish? Maybe it’s because it would lead to some greater evil that we ourselves don’t forsee? Maybe it’s b/c God is trying to teach us not to rely to heavily on Her? ANd how do we tell which cases are which? I don’t know. Hard questions. Ok. I’ll leave off here. Hopefully in the discussions this week, you all will be able to advance some of these issues a bit further.

[Slide 4] The 3-O God God is a being who is O mnipotent, O mniscient, and O mnibenevolent (i.e. morally perfect .) This question has lurked in the background of a number of our discussions already. The 3-O God, after all, is Omnibenevolent – which I cashed-out at the time as “morally perfect.” I never actually spelled out what that means with any precision, though. I didn’t give you an account of moral perfection! I didn’t even say much what morality is supposed to be. I’ve just been relying on our everyday understanding of what it means to be morally good.

[Slide 5] I think that’s enough, at least when it comes to understanding the Problem of Evil. We all understand what it means, more-or-less, to assess someone as a good or bad person. I can say, for instance, Mr. Rogers was a good person, and you basically understand what I mean, even though I’ve never defined ‘goodness’ for you. You understand that Mr. Rogers was good in the sense that he was kind and caring and generally well-behaved. We’ve been saying the same thing about God: God is a good person. (The 3-O God is, in fact, the best possible person .) We might not know precisely what that entails – being the best possible person – but I think we can see the general outline.

[Slide 6] Saying that God is morally perfect is, I think, really informative – even if we never stop to define moral perfection! Like, consider Zeus. We know that Zeus is not a 3-O God because, among other reasons, he is not morally perfect . The ancient Greeks unequivocally describe Zeus as jealous and as petty and, worst of all, as a serial rapist. Obviously, no being like that could ever be considered the best possible person . Zeus is not the 3-O God.

[Slide 22] Divine Command Theory is the view that morality comes directly from God . It says, “ morality consists in acting in accordance with God’s Will, in following God’s List of Universal Rules of Behavior . ” Here’s the quick version of the view… The really short version of the view is this: “What’s right is right b/c God says it’s right. What’s wrong is wrong b/c God says it’s wrong.”... Let me draw your attention really quickly to two points: First, it’s important for the view that rules be universal. They’re like laws, but instead of applying only within a particular domain or state (like, say, Colorado) they apply everywhere. They apply throughout God’s domain. Second, it’s important for the view that the rules be fixed by God’s will. The rules are from God in a really intimate way. When God tells us not to kill, She’s saying that killing is not what she wants for the world. This is what Brunner was saying, “Morality consists in always doing what God wills at any particular moment.”

[Slide 27] On Realism, Relativism, & Subjectivism So. We’re about to start a VERY lengthy detour through metaphysics and metaethics. Bear with me.

[Slide 28] Consider the claim, Cilantro is delicious. Quiz Suppose you have two friends who disagree about this – maybe the two of them come from different countries. They keep arguing about it. One can’t believe the other likes cilantro; the other is skeptical that one doesn’t love it. What are you going to tell them to end the argument? Probably you’re going to say that taste is subjective. People experience food in different ways and that’s ok. And there’s just never going to be some universal fact of the matter about cilantro’s taste being either delicious or nasty. There’s no contradiction in saying it’s delicious for me and nasty for you.

[Slide 27] On Realism, Relativism, & Subjectivism So. We’re about to start a VERY lengthy detour through metaphysics and metaethics. Bear with me.

[Slide 28] Consider the claim, Cilantro is delicious. Quiz Suppose you have two friends who disagree about this – maybe the two of them come from different countries. They keep arguing about it. One can’t believe the other likes cilantro; the other is skeptical that one doesn’t love it. What are you going to tell them to end the argument? Probably you’re going to say that taste is subjective. People experience food in different ways and that’s ok. And there’s just never going to be some universal fact of the matter about cilantro’s taste being either delicious or nasty. There’s no contradiction in saying it’s delicious for me and nasty for you.

[Slide 29] Consider the claim, Tipping your waiter is rude. Quiz Suppose you have two friends who disagree about this – maybe the two of them come from different countries. They keep arguing about it. One insists that tipping a waiter is super- rude and against all social customs; the other insists that it’s actually polite and widely-expected. What are you going to tell them to end the argument? Here, you’ll probably point out that rudeness, politeness, and good manners are going to be different in different communities, for different cultures. In the U.S., the consensus view is that tipping is a polite thing to do. That’s what our culture has decided. But other culture can and have made other decisions. In Iceland, for instance, tipping is considered rude. Neither country is wrong! They just have different norms.

[Slide 30] Consider the claim, H 2 O is a polar molecule. Quiz Suppose you have two friends who disagree about this – maybe the two of them come from different countries. They keep arguing about it. One insists that H2O molecules are polar; the other insists that water molecules are non-polar. What are you going to tell them to end the argument? Here, I think, you’ll tell them to google it or will reach for your chemistry textbook. There is actually a fact of the matter here – namely, water molecules are in fact polar. That’s true. That’s not subjective; it’s not culturally constructed; thats just an accurate description of the distribution of electric charge on water molecules in our universe.

[Slide 31] Observation Some sorts of truths are individually determined , are fixed by our own attitudes. Other sorts of truths are culturally-bound, are determined by our social context . O ther sorts of truths are universal , are independent of personal/cultural status . Here’s the point. ... This seems to represent, I think, our commonsense opinions about how truth works, in different domains. When it comes to thinks like deliciousness , what’s true seems purely subjective – such that the deliciousness facts may be different for different people, depending entirely on their own personal experiences. When it comes to things like politeness facts most people agree that what’s true is relative, is fixed by one’s cultural or social circumstances – such that the politeness facts may be diff in diff places Finally when it comes to things like chemistry, most people agree that what’s true is neither subjective nor relative, is independent of human attitudes. Let me introduce some terms to streamline these points.

[Slide 32] Subjectivism | Truth is fixed by an individual’s own experiences (a claim may be true-for-me but false-for-you) Most people are Subjectivists about tastes ( “Cilantro is delicious ” ), about smells ( “Gasoline is profoundly stinky ” ), and about aesthetics in general ( “The Mona Lisa is a supremely beautiful painting” ). Let’s call ‘Subjectivism’ the view that truth (in some particular domain) is fixed by an individual’s own experiences.\ … NOTICE, each of these examples is restricted to a particular domain or area of life. To be a taste subjectivist is to think that claims about deliciousness in particular are subjective. To be a subjectivist about aesthetics is to believe that claims about beauty and art are subjective. One could also be a chemistry subjectivist – only, that’s not a very plausible view. It’s not very plausible that each individual gets to *find their own truth* about chemistry, gets to decide whether water molecules are polar-for-me, or whether chlorine exists-for-you.

It’s not very plausible that each individual gets to *find their own truth* about chemistry, gets to decide whether water molecules are polar-for-me, or whether chlorine exists-for-you.

[Slide 33] Relativism | Truth is fixed by the norms of a particular community (a claim may be true-for-us but false-for-ya’ll) Most people are Relativists about etiquette ( “It’s polite to tip your waiter” ), about language ( “ Chips are long, narrow wands of fried potato” ), and about about social norms in general ( “It is normal to have more than 5 children” ). Here’s a name for that second category… Again, relativism is a view that one has about particular domains or areas of life. Most people are relativists about language – most people accept that what a word means is fixed by whatever linguistic community one finds oneself in. Most people are not relativists about taste, nor are people relativists about physics. Intuitively, my community doesn’t get to decide whether cabbage is tasty; *I* get to decide that. For myself. Obversely, neither my community nor I get to decide whether atoms have protons. They do. That’s just true everywhere, independent of our attitudes. If our community decides otherwise, we’re simply wrong about atoms

[Slide 34] Realism | Truth is independent of individual experiences/social norms (each claim is either true everywhere or false everywhere) Most people are Realists about math claims ( “The number 16 is divisible by 2” ), about logical claims ( “It’s impossible for there to be a married bachelor” ), and about about scientific claims in general ( “H 2 O is a polar molecule” ). Finally, here’s name for our third category… Realism, like Subjectivism and Relativism, is domain-specific. Most people are realists about chemistry but not realists about taste or about etiquette. There are no universal deliciousness facts nor any universal politeness facts.

[Slide 35] Subjectivism | Truth is fixed by an individual’s own experiences (a claim may be true-for-me but false-for-you) EX | “Cilantro is delicious. ” “‘Baby Shark is the greatest song of all time. ” “The smell of lilacs is disgusting .” Relativism | Truth is fixed by the norms of a particular community (a claim may be true-for-us but false-for ya’ll) EX | “It is rude to tip your waiter.” “It is normal to have more than 5 children.” “The word ‘fuck’ has a sexual meaning and is obscene. ” Realism | Truth is independent of individual experiences/social norms (each claim is either true everywhere or false everywhere) EX | “H 2 O is a polar molecule.” “A car tire has more mass than an electron.” “The number 16 is divisible by 2.” So, here are our three views. Each view is plausible in some domains and implausible in others. I think most people understand all this pretty well! Also most people have never explicitly worked through these ideas. Now, this is just a Cliff Notes version of the metaphysics at issue here. So if you’re thinking, “Wait, aren’t there other views than just these 3? And can’t these views sometimes combine in complex ways?” Yes! Totally. But this detour has already been long enough.

[Slide 37] The Big Question , Which of these views is most plausible in the moral domain ? Here’s our Big QUestion… We make moral claims all the time. We say things like, The prison system is unjust and inhumane or Masturbation is morally fine, totally unobjectionable or One should always get consent before intimately touching another person. How should we think about those moral claims? Should we Subjectivists about those claims? Or Relativists? Or Realists?

[Slide 38] Let me you an example to illustrate what we’re asking. So consider: lots of cultures, historically, practiced child sacrifice. That is, they would ritualistic kill young children in order to please (they thought) some supernatural entity. This happened all over the world, in all sorts of culture. We have, for instance, credible contemporary reports and solid archaeological evidence that the Phoenicians and Carthaginians – who were sea-traders on the Mediterranean – practiced child sacrifice. They would periodically kill a bunch of their own young children to appease their gods. This is common anymore of course. But let’s suppose there’s some small country today that takes up this historically common practice. Say, Denmark. The whole country of Denmark gets swept up in a Punic and Phoenician religious revival, and they start enthusiastically sacrificing young children to Baal Hammon.

[Slide 39] EX | “ Child sacrifice is immoral. ” Moral Subjectivism ? (“ I find child sacrifice repulsive; he thinks it’s good. Neither of us is wrong . We just like different things. ”) Moral Relativism ? (“ Our society thinks child sacrifice is monstrous; theirs thinks it’s great. No one’s wrong . It’s just cultural diversity. ”) Moral Realism ? (“ Child sacrifice is, simply, immoral. Some people think otherwise, but those people are confused and wrong. ”) Now, obviously, if you were to conduct a survey in the US on this issue, pretty much everyone would agree with this moral claim, that child sacrifice is immoral. So here’s our Big Question: how should we understand the truth conditions for that claim? Should we be relativists? Subjectivists? Realists? … (I should note in passing, there are other possible views here. If you want to learn about them, take a Metaethics course! It’s good stuff. We’ll stick to these 3 b/c they’re the most popular. Of the hundreds of students I’ve had, roughly 99% accept one or the other of these views.)

[Slide 42] “ We know objective moral values exist because we clearly apprehend some of them . [Slide 45] Moral Subjectivism ? A better way to defend moral realism is to point out some of the deeply unappealing consequences of moral subjectivism and moral relativism. Let’s start with subjectivism. Recall, pretty much everyone is a subjectivist about tastes and smells. Like, I think vinegar smells terrible – just literally I think it’s the worst smell in the world. But maybe you like the smell of vinegar; maybe it’s your favorite. We disagree. But… it’s a funny sort of disagreement, since it’s not like either of us thinks the other is genuinely wrong . Rather, we think the other has different preferences when it comes to the smell of vinegar. Each of us has our own subjective experience of the smell, and my experience isn’t true-er or better than yours. It’s just mine. My experience of vinegar is authoritative over me, but it shouldn’t be authoritative over you. And vice versa.

[Slide 46] Moral Subjectivism ? Should we have the same view about ethics? Is ethics also subjective? Well, if you listen to how people talk, they mostly don’t sound like they think it’s all just a matter of taste. People don’t say, “My own personal experience is that policing in America is deeply evil and unjust. But I know that it doesn’t bother you. And – hey! – that’s your truth. If you like police brutality, then I’m not going to yuck your yum.” Nor do people say, “I personally would never kick my dog or sexually assault my child. The very thought of doing that makes me sick to my stomach. But my neighbor is really into dog kicking and child assaulting! While *I* obviously don’t like that, my own moral subjectivity can never be authoritative over him. Dog kicking is like pineapple on pizza – awful and not my thing, but also, you know, not like OBJECTIVELY awful. Different strokes and all that!” Again, I pretty much never hear anyone say anything like that. Which is strong evidence that most people don’t actually believe that ethics is merely a matter of taste.

[Slide 45] Moral Subjectivism ? A better way to defend moral realism is to point out some of the deeply unappealing consequences of moral subjectivism and moral relativism. Let’s start with subjectivism. Recall, pretty much everyone is a subjectivist about tastes and smells. Like, I think vinegar smells terrible – just literally I think it’s the worst smell in the world. But maybe you like the smell of vinegar; maybe it’s your favorite. We disagree. But… it’s a funny sort of disagreement, since it’s not like either of us thinks the other is genuinely wrong . Rather, we think the other has different preferences when it comes to the smell of vinegar. Each of us has our own subjective experience of the smell, and my experience isn’t true-er or better than yours. It’s just mine. My experience of vinegar is authoritative over me, but it shouldn’t be authoritative over you. And vice versa.

[Slide 46] Moral Subjectivism ? Should we have the same view about ethics? Is ethics also subjective? Well, if you listen to how people talk, they mostly don’t sound like they think it’s all just a matter of taste. People don’t say, “My own personal experience is that policing in America is deeply evil and unjust. But I know that it doesn’t bother you. And – hey! – that’s your truth. If you like police brutality, then I’m not going to yuck your yum.” Nor do people say, “I personally would never kick my dog or sexually assault my child. The very thought of doing that makes me sick to my stomach. But my neighbor is really into dog kicking and child assaulting! While *I* obviously don’t like that, my own moral subjectivity can never be authoritative over him. Dog kicking is like pineapple on pizza – awful and not my thing, but also, you know, not like OBJECTIVELY awful. Different strokes and all that!” Again, I pretty much never hear anyone say anything like that. Which is strong evidence that most people don’t actually believe that ethics is merely a matter of taste.

[Slide 47] Moral Relativism ? What about relativism? I think is a far more common (and more plausible) view. Recall, most people are relativists about language and meaning. By convention, the middle finger gesture means screw you. That’s what our language community has decided is the symbolic content of that gesture. That’s not subjective. It doesn’t depend on your feelings or perceptual experiences. The middle finger simply and truly means screw you in our community. Now, how that gesture makes you feel, is subjective. You may find it offensive, or you may not. That’s a matter of taste. But the meaning isn’t a matter of taste. It’s fixed by convention. Which implies, of course, that it could have meant something else if we’d settled on a different convention. There’s a nearby possible world where that same gesture could be used to mean Good morning, best friend! Or Care for a spot of tea and a biscuit? But that’s not the meaning it actually has around here.

[Slide 48] Moral Relativism ? Should we have the same view about ethics? Is ethics also just a social convention? We could say, “Our community has made certain decisions about which actions are allowed and which aren’t. Those rules are real and true; they aren’t matters of taste. But they are nevertheless only true by convention. Those rules could have been different; we could easily have settled on a different convention. Different communities ultimately do settle on different conventions and that’s why there’s cross-cultural disagreement about ethics.” Except, I don’t think many people actually believe this either. Again, if you listen to how people talk about ethics, they mostly don’t sound like they think it’s all mere convention .

[Slide 49] Consider sexual harassment and the #MeToo movement. By convention, sexual harassment was normal and permissable behavior in basically all Western cultures until about 60 years ago. The phrase ‘sexual harassment’ didn’t even exist until the early 1970s. But when you listen to the way that people talk about the #MeToo movement, they don’t say that sexual harassment used to be ethically permissible by convention. They claim that sexual harassment has always been sexist, immoral, and unjust. Likewise w/ spousal rape and domestic violence. There have been plenty of cultures where, by convention, husbands were permitted have sex with their wives whenever they wanted and didn’t need consent. (This was true in all of the English speaking world until about the 1950s.) And there have been plenty of cultures (including parts of the historical US) where, by convention, husbands were permitted to engage in non-lethal domestic violence against their wives. Moral relativism is clear on these cases. All of the men who sexually and physically assaulted their wives in those times and places did not do anything immoral. Because, by convention, that was a thing that husbands were permitted to do. Again, my impression is that most people think that’s false. Most people think that those men were engaging in very, very serious moral misbehavior. Thus, most people aren’t moral relativists.

[Slide 50] Here’s another quick case. This graph is a historical accounting of US public opinion on interracial marriage. As you can see, it wasn’t until 1997 (!!!!) that more than half of Americans approved of inter-racial marriage. (That’s within my lifetime. That’s crazy.) The conventional opinion in 1980 clearly was that interracial was deeply inappropriate. So ask yourself, Do you agree that interracial couples in 1980 were doing something morally inappropriate ? Again, my impression is that pretty much everyone thinks, No. Meaning, pretty much everyone does not have the proper intuitions to count themselves a moral relativist.

[Slide 51] Divine Command Theory consists of two independent claims: Moral Realism : there are moral facts , and those facts are objective – i.e. are true independent of our attitudes about them Divine Dependence : objective moral facts can exist only if there’s a God to make them (i.e. moral facts can only arise from God’s will ) Alright. That’s a defense of Moral Realism – an attempt to improve on Craig’s rather disappointing argument. There is, of course and as always, a lot more to be said here – in support of Realism, in defense of subjectivism and relativism, and in support of yet other views that we don’t have time to talk about. But moral realism is only ½ of Divine Command Theory. The other half is the claim I’m calling Divine Dependence – the idea that objective moral facts – whether there are any or not – could only ever come from God.

[Slide 52] Why should we think Divine Dependence is true?

[Slide 79] We obey the law to avoid punishment. But... W hy should we obey the moral rules ? Most of these things you can get away with not doing. No one’s going to shame you. No one’s going to punish you. So … why should you bother? Relatedly, think about other sorts of wrongdoing that a person might know they can get away with (though they would be punished and shamed, if other people found out) Think about, e.g., peeping in someone’s window as they undress, embezzling money from a young child, abusing someone in a coma. Those things clearly violate common sense moral rules. But why should we care? What’s the motivation not to violate the moral rules when we know we’ll get away with it?

[Slide 80] If God is the author of morality , we have a ready answer: God cares. God will punish / reward us . … on the other hand, if there isn’t a God, it’s not clear what answer we can give here. That’s potentially trouble for the atheists who want to be moral realists.

[Slide 83] Divine Command Theory consists of two independent claims: Moral Realism : there are moral facts , and those facts are objective – i.e. are true independent of our attitudes about them Divine Dependence : objective moral facts can exist only if there’s a God to make them (i.e. moral facts can only arise from God’s will ) So that’s Divine Command Theory. DCT is the view that there are objective moral facts and that those facts flow from God’s will – could only ever come from God’s will. As an aside: Craig points out that this view can be wielded as an argument for the existence of God. And that’s true! If you can persuade an atheist that DCT is true – if you convince him that God is the author of morality – then you will have thereby convinced him that God is real. But as a practical matter, I’ve never heard of anyone taking that particular path to theism ( First you realize that ethics is objective and then you realize that God is only explanation for ethics. Finally, you reluctantly accept that God exists.) That’s why I’m not talking about that argument per se .

[Slide 84] The Euthyphro Dilemma As we’ve seen Divine Command Theory is a really common view worldwide. However, it’s not common among philosophers. That’s b/c most philosophers think the view was put to bed over 2,000 years ago by the greek philosopher, Plato. So I want to close by looking at that famous argument of Plato’s. The Euthyphro Dilemma, an objection to Divine Command Theory. (The Euthryphro is one of Plato’s dialogues. Plato’s writings are all in this form. He wrote what are, effectively, plays or dramatic conversations. The main character in his dialogues is Socrates (who was Plato’s IRL mentor) and in the dialogues, we find Socrates chatting about philosophy with other various Athenians – real people usually. The Euthyprho is a short dialogue where Socrates talks to a man called Euthyphro about goodness and piety and morality.)

DCT is the view that there are objective moral facts and that those facts flow from God’s will – could only ever come from God’s will. As an aside: Craig points out that this view can be wielded as an argument for the existence of God. And that’s true! He wrote what are, effectively, plays or dramatic conversations. The main character in his dialogues is Socrates (who was Plato’s IRL mentor) and in the dialogues, we find Socrates chatting about philosophy with other various Athenians – real people usually. The Euthyprho is a short dialogue where Socrates talks to a man called Euthyphro about goodness and piety and morality.)

[Slide 85] Socrates asks, “What is morality ? What makes something right or wrong?” Euthyphro suggests, “What’s right is doing the things that the gods love and command of us; what’s wrong is doing the things that the gods hate and forbid .” … Hopefully, you can see: this just is Divine Command Theory. Euthyphro is sayings, morality consists in acting in accordance with God’s (or THE gods’) will.

[Slide 86] Socrates asks, “Are the right things right because God says they’re right , OR does God say they’re right because they are right ?” In the dialog, Socrates isn’t satisfied with this answer because he thinks DCT has an unresolved ambiguity concerning the direction of explanation between the things that God likes and the things that are morally correct. So he asks for clarification… As we’ll see, this question poses a problem for DCT b/c neither answer is particularly. (Hence, why it’s called a dilemma. A hard choice between two bad options.) But we need to unpack that question. Even just grammatically, that’s a doozy of a sentence. SO I wanna make sure we’re all on the same page about what’s being asked.

[Slide 87] Consider something that’s good to do . Maybe – g iving aid to those who need it. Here’s the thought.

[Slide 88] DCT claims both of these things are true, It is good to aid the needy. God wants and commands us to aid the needy. One of these explains the other. But which way does it go? … That’s not a coincidence that both of those things are true – and it’s not brute co-variance either! DCT says that there’s a tight, explanatory relationship between the goodness of an act and what God’s wants/commands of us... Plato asks, Are the good things because God says so, or does God says so because they are good?

[Slide 89] Here’s an easier example, with the same formal structure. Here’s a flagpole. it exists. The flagpole-shadow also exists. And one of these explains the other. We can ask, Does the flagpole exist because of the shadow, or does the shadow exist because of the flagpole. Clearly the latter. The flagpole explains the existence of the shadow. And not the other way round. The shadow does NOT explain the flagpole.

[Slide 90] Plato isn’t asking about things and their shadows, obvs. He’s asking about the explanatory relationship between mental attitudes and facts about the world. This is sometimes called the direction of fit question. Do the mental attitudes come first and do they EXPLAIN why the world is the way that it is? OR Does the world first come to be a certain and then THAT EXPLAIN why we have the attitudes we have?

[Slide 91] Here’s an example Suppose it’s raining and you believe that it is raining. Do you believe that it’s raining b/c it is raining, or is it raining b/c you believe it is? (next slide)

[Slide 100] Socrates asks, “Are the right things right because God says they’re right , OR does God say they’re right because they are right ?” This is Plato’s question about DCT. The Divine Command Theorist claims, “God is the source of morality. The morally right things are just those things that God approves of.” And Plato asks: “Does God approve of the morally right things because they’re morally right or are they morally right only because he approves them?” Which is it? Which comes first – the approval or the moral rightness?

[Slide 101] Option #1 – God says that the right things are right b/c they are (independently) right. Option #2 – The right things are right (merely) b/c God says so. This is a dilemma because the theist has only two choices and both appear unattractive:

[Slide 102] Option #1 – God says that the right things are right b/c they are (independently) right. Implies that Divine Dependence is false – and, thus, that DCT is false. If Option #1 is correct, then God is not actually directly necessary for morality. Option #1 amounts a rejection of the Divine Dependence claim. The moral facts don’t exist merely as an expression of God’s will. They are, instead, independent facts about the world. Option #1 says: first, there are moral facts in the world. First it’s true that, say, that murder is wrong. AND THEN those independent facts explain God’s attitudes, explain God’s dislike of murder. On Option #1, God is noticing out-in-the-world facts about morality. If we adopt that picture, we have abandoned Divine Command THeory.

[Slide 101] Option #1 – God says that the right things are right b/c they are (independently) right. Option #2 – The right things are right (merely) b/c God says so. This is a dilemma because the theist has only two choices and both appear unattractive:

[Slide 102] Option #1 – God says that the right things are right b/c they are (independently) right. Implies that Divine Dependence is false – and, thus, that DCT is false. If Option #1 is correct, then God is not actually directly necessary for morality. Option #1 amounts a rejection of the Divine Dependence claim. The moral facts don’t exist merely as an expression of God’s will. They are, instead, independent facts about the world. Option #1 says: first, there are moral facts in the world. First it’s true that, say, that murder is wrong. AND THEN those independent facts explain God’s attitudes, explain God’s dislike of murder. On Option #1, God is noticing out-in-the-world facts about morality. If we adopt that picture, we have abandoned Divine Command THeory.

[Slide 103] Option #2 – The right things are right (merely) b/c God says so. Compatible with DCT , but implies that morality is arbitrary. ( and Subjective in an important sense!) On the other hand, if Option #2 is correct, then we are saying that God’s attitudes really do come first. If God is the source of all moral reasons, then God had no reasons for saying that certain things were wrong and other things right. The picture is: FIRST, God made some subjective, arbitrary judgment calls (literally for no reasons ) and then those divine preferences gave rise to morality. It is bad to murder b/c God arbitrarily decided to dislike murder. It’s good to help the needy because God arbitrarily decided that that’s dope. Notice, that implies, if God had commanded us to kick the needy, then that would have been a good thing to do. Now, we might want to say, “hold on! That’s not how it went at all! God would never command us to kick the needy! That’s inconceivable, given Her moral perfection.” Maybe! But this is Plato’s point. WHY? Why is it that God would never command us to kick the needy? Presumably b/c… kicking the needy is bad. And God would never tell us to do something bad. But notice, now we’ve retreated back to Option #1 and abandoned DCT. We’re saying: God would never tell us to kick the needy b/c God’s preferences aren’t arbitrary. We’re saying that God knows that kicking the needy is wrong. Wrong not b/c God says so, but for some other reason. The reason that God says it’s wrong is because it is wrong. That’s option #1. And that might be true, but that story clearly appears to be incompatible with DCT. Option #2 says, in contrast, that God’s Will is the true source of morality, is the only thing grounding and propping-up the moral rules.

[Slide 104] Child sacrifice is against the rules of morality Traveling is against the rules of basketball Compare: the rule against travelling in basketball. Why is it against the rules to travel? Is there some independent, universal fact about the badness of traveling – of walking without constantly bouncing the game ball? Of course not. There’s no deep justification for the rule against traveling. If you ask, BUt WHY can’t I travel? The answer is: that’s just not part of James Naismith’s original design. That’s just a rule he came up with! At best, maybe that rule makes the game more interesting and fun. That seems to be more-or-less what we’re saying about the moral rules on Option #2. If the right thing are right only because God says so, then there no deep justification why God decided that child sacrifice is against the rules. BUT WHY can’t I sacrifice children? The answer is: that’s just not part of God’s original design. That’s just a rule She came up with! At best, maybe that rule makes life more interesting and fun.

[Slide 103] Option #2 – The right things are right (merely) b/c God says so. Compatible with DCT , but implies that morality is arbitrary. ( and Subjective in an important sense!) On the other hand, if Option #2 is correct, then we are saying that God’s attitudes really do come first. If God is the source of all moral reasons, then God had no reasons for saying that certain things were wrong and other things right. The picture is: FIRST, God made some subjective, arbitrary judgment calls (literally for no reasons ) and then those divine preferences gave rise to morality. It is bad to murder b/c God arbitrarily decided to dislike murder. It’s good to help the needy because God arbitrarily decided that that’s dope. Notice, that implies, if God had commanded us to kick the needy, then that would have been a good thing to do. Now, we might want to say, “hold on! That’s not how it went at all! God would never command us to kick the needy! That’s inconceivable, given Her moral perfection.” Maybe! But this is Plato’s point. WHY? Why is it that God would never command us to kick the needy? Presumably b/c… kicking the needy is bad. And God would never tell us to do something bad. But notice, now we’ve retreated back to Option #1 and abandoned DCT. We’re saying: God would never tell us to kick the needy b/c God’s preferences aren’t arbitrary. We’re saying that God knows that kicking the needy is wrong. Wrong not b/c God says so, but for some other reason. The reason that God says it’s wrong is because it is wrong. That’s option #1. And that might be true, but that story clearly appears to be incompatible with DCT. Option #2 says, in contrast, that God’s Will is the true source of morality, is the only thing grounding and propping-up the moral rules.

[Slide 104] Child sacrifice is against the rules of morality Traveling is against the rules of basketball Compare: the rule against travelling in basketball. Why is it against the rules to travel? Is there some independent, universal fact about the badness of traveling – of walking without constantly bouncing the game ball? Of course not. There’s no deep justification for the rule against traveling. If you ask, BUt WHY can’t I travel? The answer is: that’s just not part of James Naismith’s original design. If the right thing are right only because God says so, then there no deep justification why God decided that child sacrifice is against the rules. BUT WHY can’t I sacrifice children? The answer is: that’s just not part of God’s original design. That’s just a rule She came up with! At best, maybe that rule makes life more interesting and fun.

[Slide 105] Option #1 – God says that the right things are right b/c they are (independently) right. Option #2 – The right things are right (merely) b/c God says so. This is the Euthyphro dilemma. Either the moral rules are independent of God (and so Divine Command Theory is false. Morality is more than God’s Will) OR the moral rules are no more and no less than the unreasoned and arbitrary preferences of the divine. That’s compatible with Divine Command Theory, but that strikes a lot of people as a deeply implausible characterization of morality.

[Slide 106] Divine Command Theory consists of two independent claims: Moral Realism : there are moral facts , and those facts are objective – i.e. are true independent of our attitudes about them Divine Dependence : objective moral facts can exist only if there’s a God to make them (i.e. moral facts can only arise from God’s will ) That’s Plato’s objection to DCT. The view, if taken seriously, seems to undermine the importance of the very thing it’s supposed to explain. It makes morality appear arbitrary and largely uninteresting. Obviously and as always, There’s a lot more to be said about DCT and about the Euthyphro, but I’ll leave off her. There’re also other ways we might make sense of the connection between god and morality. We’ll look at those in the next module.

[Slide 13] Teleology Let’s start with that first claim, Teleology. For a lot of you, this may be your first time encountering this concept. But it’s an important idea. I’d hazard: one really can’t understand this history of Western thought without understand Teleology.

[Slide 14] ‘ Telos’ (τέλος) is the Greek word for ‘purpose’ or ‘goal’. Hence, ‘teleology’ literally means, the study of things’ purposes . … That’s the literal meaning. The view is just that all natural things have purposes. (Thus, someone who says, “I’m a teleologist” isn’t saying “I study purposes.” They’re saying “I believe that each natural thing has an intrinsic purpose.”) This idea that all natural things have purposes dates back to Aristotle . (Aristotle was a Greek philosopher around the year 300 BCE, and was t he student of Plato, whom we read last week.) And, it’s historically important b/c it was THE dominant view of the natural world in the West for 2000+ years. (From Aristotle until, basically, Darwin)

[Slide 23] Teleology all natural things possess a specific & fixed purpose or function (and that function explains why each particular thing exists!) Each of these things has a specific and unchanging function – each things exists for a reason and has a particular job to do, a specific role to play in the natural order. The role of rain is to nourish living things – plants and animals. That’s why there’s rain. The role of trees is to flourish and grow and to provide food, shelter, and resources to other living things. That’s why there are trees. Etc. Teleology tells us everything has a job to do , and each thing exists because that job needs doing. That’s what the view says. But why would anyone think that’s true?

[Slide 24] The fundamental motivation for teleology is an analogy between human artifacts and natural objects. Well...

[Slide 25] Quiz Think about the design of a stapler for a second. Think about what it’s purpose is; think about how it’s design facilitates its accomplishing that. QUIZ QUESTION: Contemplate this stapler. What are some things you notice? And how do those things facilitate the stapler’s accomplishing its function of stapling? ... e.g. there’s a rough surface on top for no-slip grip! The staple-head is close enough to the bottom that it would be hard to get a finger in there. The metal plate that the staples are pushed into has two settings, but you have to push up on the plate to switch between t hem. That way, it won’t accidentally flip while you’re stapling The handle is long to give you more torque for pushing through larger stacks of paper. There’s a little window on the side of the staple-head so you can see if there are any staples in there Etc Obviously, there’s nothing special about staplers in this respect! Pick any human-crafted artifact – a piano, a blender, a trowel, a semi-truck – and you can do this same exercise. You can make sense of its various features as intentional design decisions, aimed at improving the artifact’s functionality.

[Slide 26] Here’s something interesting: We can even do this with things that we’ve never seen before and don’t know the function of. Probably you don’t recognize this thing in the image. BUT you do know it’s man-made, that it was intentionally and carefully designed with some function in mind . This thing cleary has a clear purpose – you just probably don’t know what that is. In this case, t his is a watchmaker’s staking set (for punching holes and other fine detail work)

And, Aristotle thought, if you look at natural objects (i.e. one’s not made by people), a lot of them give off the same vibe. … We might not know, yet, what exactly everything is for, but it seems likely that they’re for something
