[Slide 33] “ If therefore purposes [are] present in man-made things, [they’re] present also in nature. The best illustration is a doctor doctoring himself: nature is like that. ” – Aristotle … A perfect doctor could keep herself alive forever. Nature is like that. Nature is both agent and patient; is self-organizing; self-perpetuating. All natural objects have a function, a purpose, and their ultimate purpose is to perpetuate the natural order. E ach part of nature has a job to play in sustain nature as a whole.

[Slide 34] “ If a house, e.g., had been a thing made by nature, it would have been made in the same way as it is now by [humans]; and if things made by nature were made not only by nature but also by [humans], they would come to be in the same way as by nature. ” – Aristotle … In other words, Aristotle thinks, if nature grew its own houses, they would look just like ours. They would have doors and insulated walls and water-proof roofs. Those things are all necessary for a house to serve it’s intended function.

[Slide 35] Teleology all natural things possess a specific & fixed purpose or function (and that function explains why each particular thing exists!) That’s how Aristotle got to Teleology. First, he notices some facts about human artifacts. (a saw’s job is to cut. It is carefully designed to fulfill that function. Saws only exist because there is some cutting that needs doing.) Then, he notices that natural objects seem to have the same flavor. So he concludes they follow the same principles. (An eye’;s job is to see. It is carefully designed to fulfill that function. Eyes only exist because there is a need for vision.)

[Slide 36] Christian and Islamic theology took this idea and added God . Aristotle wasn’t a Christian. He was a Macedonian living 300 years before Christ. But Christians (and Muslims!) really liked Aristotle. They thought he was right about pretty much everything. (If you read any old Islamic philosophy, you’ll quickly notice that they refer to Aristotle as simply, ‘ THE Philosopher’ (!!!) And so it was that Teleology got enshrined in Western thought for a couple thousand years. The only major change was the addition of God to the story.

[Slide 37] “ We see that things which lack knowledge, such as natural bodies, act for an end , and this is evident from their acting always, or nearly always, in the same way, so as to obtain the best result. Hence it is plain that they achieve their end, not fortuitously, but designedly . Now whatever lacks knowledge cannot move towards an end, unless it be directed by some being endowed with knowledge and intelligence; as the arrow is directed by the archer. Therefore, some intelligent being exists by whom all natural things are directed to their end; and this being we call God . ” — St. Thomas Aquinas … This is a pretty obvious an easy extension of Teleology. Aristotle insists that everything in nature has a specific and fixed purpose. Where did those come from? He says, Nature itself put them there to perpetuate itself. Later theists said, Nah. God put those functions there.

[Slide 38] The lower-order beings exist for the sake of the higher-order beings. That’s their function . What results is this sort of hierarchical picture of the universe. The rains fall to nourish the plants. The plants grow to nourish the animals. The animals and plants grow to nourish humans. Humans thrive to bring glory to God.

[Slide 39] Teleology all natural things possess a God-given purpose or function Morality from Function morality consists in things acting in accordance with their function The Theory of Natural Law consists of two independent claims: So that’s Teleology. [Slide 54] Theory of Natural Law What’s morally right is for each thing to fulfill its assigned role , to act only in accordance with its natural function . Zooming back out, these two claims together form the view commonly known as the theory of natural law.

[Slide 55] Some quick objections to TNL While the Theory of Natural Law was historically very popular, very few people accept it today. It has, shall we say, a few problems. I’ll run through some, but I’ll try to be quick about it. Dead horses and all that.

[Slide 54] Theory of Natural Law What’s morally right is for each thing to fulfill its assigned role , to act only in accordance with its natural function . Zooming back out, these two claims together form the view commonly known as the theory of natural law.

[Slide 55] Some quick objections to TNL While the Theory of Natural Law was historically very popular, very few people accept it today. It has, shall we say, a few problems. I’ll run through some, but I’ll try to be quick about it. Dead horses and all that.

[Slide 56] Objection The Theory of Natural Law is, on its face, hella ableist. … According to TNL, there’s a particular way that each thing in the universe ought to be and any deviations from those ideals are not just unfortunate but morally bad. That includes human bodies. On TNL, human bodies ought to be capable of sight, of hearing, of bipedal locomotion. And human bodies that can’t do those things are defective and in a morally bad way. Which is… not a view that’s likely to appeal to your average, 21st century college student

[Slide 57] Objection The claim, each natural thing is good and intended , has lots of counter-examples. (e.g. rabies, tribalism, selfishness) … Recall, the theory of natural law tells us that the world is carefully designed such that everything naturally and by default tends towards the good, toward doing its own important function. Except… there are lots of natural things that suck. Rabies is natural, but it’s surely not morally good for rabies to do its thing. Likewise, there are a bunch of natural human tendencies that seem positively bad – tendencies that we must fight to overcome, if we want our world to be a minimally decent place. Things like tribalism and selfishness.

[Slide 58] Objection The claim, anything contrary-to-function is wicked , has lots of counter-examples. (e.g. holding things in your mouth, having sex while infertile/gay/etc., harvesting solar power) … Historically, christian thinkers have used this claim as a reason to condemn homosexuality and sorts non-procreative sexual activities. That’s, again, not a great look here in the 21st century! There are other less grave counterexamples, too. (mouths aren’t for holding, but it’s not wicked if you use them that way. Sunlight isn’t for charging batteries but it’s not wicked to use it that way) The general concern is this: finding a surprising and unintended function for an old thing does not seem intrinsically wicked. That’s a serious problem for Morality-from-Function.

[Slide 59] Objection It’s not obvious what counts as natural (droughts? cancer?) or that everything has a function (vestigial structures?) or that the world is well-designed . (c.f. the Problem of Evil) Another issue (or, really, 3 separate issues)....

[Slide 60] Objection TNL conflicts with our best science (indeed, offers explanations that are backwards! ) Finally, the Big Objection is that the Theory of Natural Law is incompatible with our best science. Most notably, it reverses the explanations we get from the Theory of Evolution – which is the lynchpin of our best biology

[Slide 61] Trait : Long necks Function : Reach foliage that other herbivores can’t Quiz Here’s a concrete example. Consider functionality of the giraffe’s neck. It’s genuinely useful for the species to have this trait. But the story we get from biology about how this highly functional trait arose and the story we get from The Theory of Natural Law are, basically, opposites. They reverse the order of explanation. Can you see why? THink about it for a sec: why is the story from the TNL backwards from the story we get from modern evolutionary biology?

[Slide 61] Trait : Long necks Function : Reach foliage that other herbivores can’t Quiz Here’s a concrete example. Consider functionality of the giraffe’s neck. It’s genuinely useful for the species to have this trait. But the story we get from biology about how this highly functional trait arose and the story we get from The Theory of Natural Law are, basically, opposites. They reverse the order of explanation. Can you see why? THink about it for a sec: why is the story from the TNL backwards from the story we get from modern evolutionary biology?

[Slide 62] Modern Biology – “ First, some mammals randomly ended up with a long-neck mutation. That trait turned out to be f itness-enhancing – i.e. found a function . So it was passed on. ” Theory of Natural Law – “ First, there was a need for animals to make use of that high foliage . It was to fill that role that giraffes were created and given such long necks.” … Notice these explanations point in opposite directions. The modern biology explanation says that the trait occurred first, and then stuck around because there happened to be a function it could serve. The TNL explanation says that there was first a function that needed to be filled and that’s why the trait was brought about.

[Slide 63] Objection TNL conflicts with our best science (indeed, offers explanations that are backwards! ) … TNL also conflicts with our best science in other ways. For instance, our best science tells us that speciation is not end-directed – i.e there is no goal or designer guiding the creation of different species, just random and statistical processes and differential fitness success. TNL says otherwise,. Likewise, our best science tells us that bad design is going to be quite common – with systems that don’t work quite right or are overly complicated or are finicky or cost-prohibitive or simply inefficient. That’s inevitable given how evolution works, a series of ever-agglomerating small changes. TNL says the opposite. So yeah. The Theory of Natural Law has some problems; not too many people these days accept it. But it’s historically super significant, and I think that alone make it worth the price of admission.

[Slide 5] Can we give a secular (i.e. non-religious) account of moral realism ? But h ere’s the question I want to consider today …

[Slide 6] Divine Dependence : objective moral facts can exist only if there’s a God to make them (i.e. moral facts can only arise from God’s will ) Can an atheist make sense of objective moral facts ? Recall, part of Divine Command theory was this idea that moral realism requires God, that the only way to make sense of non-relative, non-subjective moral facts is to say that God made them. So our question today is: Is that true? Can an atheist really not make sense of objective moral facts?

[Slide 7] Argument #1 – The Law maker Argument Argument #2 – The Weirdness Argument Argument #3 – The Motivation Argument Divine Dependence – objective moral facts can exist only if there’s a God to make them, only if they stem from God’s Will . Also recall, we got 3 different arguments from Craig for Divine Dependence. Each of these arguments gives independent support to this idea that Moral Realism can be true only if God makes it true.

[Slide 8] 3 Challenges for Secular Ethics (i) Don’t the moral laws require a lawmaker ? (ii ) Isn’t morality too weird to exist without God? (iii) Isn’t God required to explain the force/importance of morality? So this, then, is the challenge before us. To make sense of moral realism w/o God, we’ll want to respond to all three of Craig’s arguments. Each is a challenge to the atheist, saying, You, the atheist, aren’t allowed to believe in objective moral facts unless you also believe in God. Thus, to defend a secular account of moral realism, we’ll need to answer these challenges.

[Slide 11] “Objective laws – of the sort we find in mathematics, or astronomy, or hydrology – are not of our own creation. We have identified them and given them names, but we have not invented the truths that they represent. So in these cases [atheists already] have instances of laws without lawmakers …” He writes,

[Slide 16] The Lawmaker Argument P1) All laws require a lawmaker (to write them / give them authority) P2) If Moral Realism is true, there are moral laws C) If Moral Realism is true, there is a moral lawmaker ( viz. God ) Shafer-Landau is giving us an objection to P1. It’s just not true, he says, that all laws require lawmakers. Or, more precisely, his point is that all atheists already reject P1. Atheists are already happy to believe in universal laws of electromagnetism w/o a lawmaker, so they shouldn't balk at believing in universal laws of morality, just because there’s no lawmaker. And so The Lawmaker Argument, which is supposed to persuade atheists that God is required for morality, fails. No atheist is going to accept P1.

[Slide 31] The Point The laws of morality seem no more weird than the laws of chemistry . Morality is, at worst, believably weird. (as believably weird as chemistry, physics, etc.) … In other words, the atheist can (and, for consistency’s sake, should ) reject the claim that moral realism is too weird to be believed. There’s no extra weirdness involved in morality; it’s just same normal everyday weirdness we already see in the sciences, from other domains with abstract, universal rules. Of course, some theists may like that Weirdness argument I just satirically suggested, thinking, “Yes. I agree. Atheists aren’t allowed to be believe in science.” Which, sure, you can say that, but that misses the point. That’s like pointing at a poster of the periodic table and saying, “Chemistry exists; therefore, God. Checkmate atheists.” That argument is, shall we say, very very ungreat . That argument doesn’t say anything new or anything interesting to anyone. Atheists already know that chemistry exists. And they already know that denying the existence of God does require them to make sense of chemistry w/o appealing to the divine. That shit is all old news; it’s already priced in; it’s not changing minds and hearts And that’s precisely why it’s helpful for the atheist’s position to point out that the weirdnesses of morality are roughly the same as the weirdnesses of chemistry. It means that accepting the existence of objective moral facts doesn’t create much extra work for atheists. They were already gonna have to explain, like, the existence of chemistry laws in the early universe. And now it looks like they’ll be able to re-use that explanation (whatever it is) for moral laws.

[Slide 35] The Grounding Argument If atheism is true, there is no ultimate or final explanation for why the moral facts are what they are; the moral facts are ungrounded . If the moral facts are ungrounded , moral realism is false. So, if atheism is true, moral realism is false. I submit, the deeper concern is about the source of morality, about which features of the world give rise to or ground moral facts.

[Slide 37] Some things are more fundamental than other things. Let’s make some quick observations about how the universe is put together. First, this seems true:... [Slide 39] Atoms are more fundamental than inflation. Both things are real, of course, (atoms do exist and so does inflation – prices do sometimes increase!), but atoms are more fundamental. Atoms are part of the basic building blocks of the universe. They are part of our universe’s fundament it’s basement it’s foundation. Inflation is not. Inflation is an emergent property of markets – which are themselves composes of products and people – which are made of chemicals – which are made of atoms.

[Slide 41] This is a diagram of a Folic acid molecule. Notice, the molecule is made up of at oms of Carbon, Nitrogen, Oxygen, and Hydrogen. Those atoms are more fundamental than the molecule and they give rise to the Folic Acid mole cule. The molecules are essential parts of the whole. There’s an important asymmetry here. Those Carbon and nitrogen atoms can exist w/o Folic acid but folic acid can’t exist without carbon and nitrogen.

[Slide 42] Similarly, your blood cells give rise to your blood. Hemoglobin is more fundamental than the blood it composes. There could be no blood without hemoglobin, but the reverse isn’t true.

[Slide 43] Some things are more fundamental than other things. The fundamental things give rise to the non-fundamental things. Less-fundamental things are grounded in more-fundamental things . One final point. Sometime, instead of using the “gives rise to” locution, philosophers will talk about grounding. Those two phrases are supposed to pick out the same relationship. To say that one thing grounds another is to say that the one gives rise to the other, supports the existence of the other.

[Slide 44] We’ve seen this example a couple times already this semester: We might say, for instance, that a pole grounds its shadow. The pole is more fundamental than its shadow AND t he pole gives rise to its shadow. The shadow exists because the pole exists. You can’t give any sort of ultimate explanation for the shadow without appealing to the pole

[Slide 53] Suppose that the follow claim is an objective moral fact , t orturing innocent people for fun is wrong. Q: What’s the best explanation for that fact? What grounds it? (a) The fact that God dislikes torture (b) The fact that torture isn’t part of man’s natural function (c) The fact that torture victims have rights and can suffer (before advancing) To see the point, we’ll need to get into the moral realism mindset. (So if you aren’t already sympathetic with moral realism, try to pretend that you are for a bit.) Think about some moral claims that strike you as plausibly universally ture, and think about what the best, truest, and ultimate explanation is for those more claims. Here’s an example… Let me offer three different explanations... Hopefully you can see the problem. If we’re looking for satisfying explanations, the ones we’re getting from DCT and TNL are rather lacking. (at least, I find explanations a and b unsatisfying – and, judging from past experience, so do a lot of my students… though, certainly not all of them.)

[Slide 54] Suppose that the follow claim is an objective moral fact , denying civil liberties to people just b/c of their skin tone is wrong. Q: What’s the best explanation for that fact? What grounds it? (a) The fact that God dislikes racism (b) The fact that racism is unnatural (c) The fact that people have comparable rights and interests, irrespective of the color of their skin Here’s another example… Again, just ask yourself: which of these explanations is most satisfying? Seems like the best, deepest explanation for our proposed moral fact? I, personally, only feel the pull of (c). And, again, judging from past experience, I’m not alone in that.

[Slide 55] Suppose I wanted to convince you that, it’s immoral to allow teens under the age of 16 to watch porn. Q: Which of these arguments would you find most persuasive? (a) I show you that God hates it when young people watch porn (b) I show you that young people watching porn is unnatural (c) I show you that watching porn before age ~16 is consistently and profoundly detrimental to a person’s long term well-being Here’s a helpful variation on the same thought. Think about some moral issue that’s hard or at least new to contemporary society. Maybe digital porn. Think about what a persuasive argument would look like for this claim. What would I need to show you to convince you that this is true? (I’m not saying it is true! Just ask yourself what the best sort of evidence would be for its truth.) … As before, i personally really only feel the pull of (c). I don’t think the other two argument would actually persuade me. Like, suppose (a) and (c) came apart. Suppose it’s clear that God hates it when young people watch porn BUT it’s also clearly not harmful to them Or consider the opposite case. Suppose it’s clear that God doesn’t mind when young people watch porn BUT that it’s also clear that it’s super harmful in the long run In both cases, I myself am inclined to go with the harm facts. Insofar as I’m persuaded that it’s genuinely not harmful, then I’m inclined to think it’s morally ok – regardless of GOd’s attitudes. And insofar as I’m persuaded that it IS super harmful, then I’m inclined to think it morally isn’t ok – again, regardless of God’s attitudes. That suggests that we think harm considerations explain the moral facts better than God’s attitudes. Of course, it’s plausible, that (a) and (c) would never come apart, That God would never approve of something super harmful nor would God ever disapprove of something completely harmless. But notice: that’s basically the point Plato was making last module. God’s moral attitudes aren’t, we don’t think, random and arbitrary. Rather morality is grounded independently from God’s attitudes. And God merely noticing which things are right or wrong.

[Slide 57] Here’s a possible story … Maybe morality is ultimately grounded in the fact that we each have a life that can go better or worse for us , in the fact that each of us has interests and desires and feelings, and in the fact that it’s bad for us when those things are ignored or thwarted and good for us when those things are attended to & promoted. Morality is simply a set of guidelines concerning how best to promote those important, good things . So, e.g., the reason why morality forbids slavery is precisely that slavery profoundly undermines the important interests of the ones enslaved. And the reason morality demands kindness is that kindness plays a consistent & important role in the promotion of other people’s interests. … The point is, the atheist seemingly can offer a satisfying, secular ethics – can give a robust explanation for where morality comes from, for its force and importance. Namely, People have interests and lives and well-being. People can suffer and can feel joy. All that stuff matters. That stuff can be the source of morality. (next)

[Slide 58] What’s the deep explanation for why you shouldn’t go outside when your sports team wins and shoot a gun into the air? It’s that you might kill some innocent bystander with rights and interests that matter. Facts about that person’s interests are what the moral fact that you shouldn’t fire your gun into the air in celebration.

[Slide 59] 3 Challenges for Secular Ethics (i) Don’t the moral laws require a lawmaker ? (ii ) Isn’t morality too weird to exist without God? (iii) Isn’t God required to explain the force/importance of morality? So… to summarize: Here were the three challenges we distilled from Craig’s article on Divine Command Theory. (and, recall, by “secular ethics” here, I mean a version of moral realism that’s compatible with atheism, where there are universal moral facts without a need, necessarily, for God.) We’ve seen one reply to each of these challenges. As ever and always, there’s a lot more to be said here. Other challenges, other replies, objections to our replise, etc. If you like this stuff, I recommend taking a metaethics course. ... For now though, I want to fill in a gap that I perceive in the Shafer-Landau piece I had you read for this module. Recall, our question in this module and the last is, What is the connection between God and morality? I want to talk about what that connection might look like, on this sort of view – where moral realism comes apart from God.

[Slide 70] A Direct Approach – Divine Command Theory (“ God willed the moral rules into being. ”) An Indirect Approach – Theory of Natural Law (“ God designed nature. Morality follows from Her design. ”) An Indirect Approach – Secular Ethics (“ God created beings with interests. She got morality for free. ”) Theist : God is ultimately responsible for morality This, I think, is the gap left in the Shafer-Landau article. Recall, the theist wants to say that God is ultimately responsible for morality. My point is that the secular approach we’ve been considered heretofore very much does seem compatible with that view. To say that ethics is grounded in human interests is not necessarily to push God out of the picture! The theist can, instead, make room for God as the direct creator of the universes basic building blocks and the indirect creator of anything that those building blocks naturally give rise to on their own. ALright. SO this concludes our 2-module discussion of God morality. Our final topic, for the next two modules, is death.

[Slide 9] “You could never step into the same river twice” – Heraclitus All of this so far is, I take it, common sense. But that’s not to say it’s uncontroversial. The Presocratic philosopher Heraclitus famously thought that NOTHING ever survived a change – a view he famously encapsulated with this quote. (Here the thought is: by the time you go to step into the river a second time, everything’s different: the banks have shifted a little, different waters swirl around your feet. And since everything’s different, it’s not really the same river.) To change, he thought, is necessarily to be destroyed. No changes are survivable.

[Slide 10] But we mostly don’t think that. We say things like “Look at how this river formed that oxbow” and we MEAN it. *This* river did that! The river survived the oxbow formation. Most people are not Heracliteans. Most people think that survival through change is possible.

[Slide 11] Each of these change-surviving things is said to persist through time . The later version of the thing is not literally the same as the earlier thing. (it’s been changed!) But it’s still the same thing .

[Slide 12] Suppose you paint a smokestack. After the paint job, it is not literally the same as it was before. It used to be grey all over, but now it’s red and white and grey. The smokestack is not literally the same but it is still, in a looser sense, the same smokestack. The paint job didn’t destroy the smokestack. That’s what we mean when we say of an object that it survives a change: it’s different than it was before, but that difference doesn’t mean the object was destroyed.

[Slide 13] We can ask of any persisting thing, What must stay-the-same for this entity to persist through time ? Which parts are the essential parts? Today we ask this question of people .

[Slide 14] To illustrate: Here are 3 pics of Drew Barrymore. We all think, I take it, that these three photos are photos of one single person. Lots of things have changed in the time between when these photos were taken, but none of those changes caused Drew Barrymore to pass out of existence. So… why not? What part (or parts) of Drew were preserved such that she, the person, was able to persist? And what sorts of events, if they had happened to her, would have resulted in her ceasing to be?

[Slide 15] Three Candidate Accounts of Personal Identity Two persons existing at different times count as the same (as identical ) iff ... they have the same soul . they have the same body . they have the same mental content . (esp. a stable personality and shared memories) Here’s a list of three plausible candidate accounts of personal identity through time. (these aren’t nec ALL the accounts there could possibly be, but these cover most of the views that the average person on the street is likely to endorse.)

So I’ll just leave it at this: the same view says that there’s one part of you (the soul part) that’s the essential bit. As long as your soul remains intact, you continue to exist.

[Slide 19] On the same body view, the persistence conditions for persons are like the persistence conditions for clubs. Imagine a sorority. Every year, 25% of its members graduate and a new class of roughly the same size enters. That is to say, year-to-year, the club is mostly the same, but some of its parts are swapped out for others. In the long run, of course, none of the members stays the same. But that doesn’t mean the sorority is destroyed every 4 years or so. Rather, we might say that the sorority persists through time because there is a continuity of membership. The members are swapped out slowly: as a few old members are lost, a few new members are brought in and integrated into the club alongside the remaining members. It’s the continuity that makes the sorority count as the same from one year to another. (Compare: suppose there was no continuity. Each year, all the old members are kicked out, and an entirely new group brought in. That seems like a situation in which the old sorority is destroyed and a new one created every year. No continuity of membership, so no survival.) This is what the Same Body view says about personal identity over time. Obviously, we know that human bodies are constantly undergoing change and replacement. Every minute of every day, old cells are dying and new cells are being born. Over the course of a decade, nearly all of your old atoms have been exchanged for new ones. But that doesn’t destroy you. You survive the change because there is a continuity of membership. As a few old parts are lost, a few new parts are brought in and integrated into the body alongside the remaining bits. It’s the continuity that makes the body count as the same from one year to another.

[Slide 20] Person 1 Person 2 On the Same Mental Content view, these are the same person b/c there is psychological continuity between them. … On this view, then, any other

[Slide 21] Person 1 Person 2 remembers being have shared, stable personality traits have shared goals, projects, interests This is actually a family of views, depending on which parts of the mind we want to emphasize. We might think, for instance, that what matters is a stable personality. If Person 1 and Person 2 act and think alike, then they are the same person Or, we might think that what matters is some stable set of interests or goals. Or, we might think that what matters is some memory connection – that Person 2 is Person 1 just in case Person 2 remembers being person 1. OR we might choose 2 or 3 of these together. Maybe persistence require stable personality and memory and goals. Or, maybe it just requires stable personality and memory. Etc.

[Slide 22] Person 1 Person 3 remembers being Person 2 remembers being Quiz One final wrinkle: Suppose Person 2 remembers being Person 1. And Person 3 remembers being Person 2. But Person 3 does not remember being Person 1. Quiz question: Why is this potentially a problem? It’s a problem b/c it seems to lead to a contradiction! We have to say: Person 3 is NOT the same as person 1 b/c 3 doesn’t remember being 1. But also, 3 IS the same as 1 b/c 3 = 2 and 2 = 1 and identity is a transitive relationship. So Person 3 both is and is not the same as person 1. That’s a bad result! How do we fix this problem? Well, sophisticated versions of the mental content view usually have a continuity condition, similar to the one used by the Same Body view. We say: the memory connection is also transitive. What matters isn’t whether 3 remembers 1 directly but whether there’s a chain of memory connecting 3 to 1. (if 3 remembers 2 and 2 remembers 1, 3 is the same as 1) So the view is usually stated like this: Person 3 is identical to anyone she remembers being AND anyone that any of those people remember being AND anyone that THOSE people remember being, and so it.

[Slide 21] Person 1 Person 2 remembers being have shared, stable personality traits have shared goals, projects, interests This is actually a family of views, depending on which parts of the mind we want to emphasize. We might think, for instance, that what matters is a stable personality. If Person 1 and Person 2 act and think alike, then they are the same person Or, we might think that what matters is some stable set of interests or goals. Or, we might think that what matters is some memory connection – that Person 2 is Person 1 just in case Person 2 remembers being person 1. OR we might choose 2 or 3 of these together. Maybe persistence require stable personality and memory and goals. Or, maybe it just requires stable personality and memory. Etc.

[Slide 22] Person 1 Person 3 remembers being Person 2 remembers being Quiz One final wrinkle: Suppose Person 2 remembers being Person 1. And Person 3 remembers being Person 2. But Person 3 does not remember being Person 1. Quiz question: Why is this potentially a problem? It’s a problem b/c it seems to lead to a contradiction! We have to say: Person 3 is NOT the same as person 1 b/c 3 doesn’t remember being 1. But also, 3 IS the same as 1 b/c 3 = 2 and 2 = 1 and identity is a transitive relationship. So Person 3 both is and is not the same as person 1. That’s a bad result! How do we fix this problem? Well, sophisticated versions of the mental content view usually have a continuity condition, similar to the one used by the Same Body view. We say: the memory connection is also transitive. What matters isn’t whether 3 remembers 1 directly but whether there’s a chain of memory connecting 3 to 1. (if 3 remembers 2 and 2 remembers 1, 3 is the same as 1) So the view is usually stated like this: Person 3 is identical to anyone she remembers being AND anyone that any of those people remember being AND anyone that THOSE people remember being, and so it.

[Slide 23] Three Candidate Accounts of Personal Identity Two persons existing at different times count as the same (as identical ) iff ... they have the same soul . they have the same body . they have the same mental content . (esp. a stable personality and shared memories) So, here are our 3 views.

[Slide 24] Death and Survival That’s the big picture question we’re dealing with today. However, our more specific interest is in the relationship between personal persistence through time and the afterlife.

[Slide 24] Death and Survival That’s the big picture question we’re dealing with today. However, our more specific interest is in the relationship between personal persistence through time and the afterlife.

[Slide 25] According to many religions, your death is an event that you survive. When you think about it, this is kind of a surprising claim! (for simplicity, let’s say that death occurs when your body has been damaged (whether by decay, force, or disease) to the point that it cannot support conscious life, when your brain no longer supports conscious thoughts.) According to many religions, then, the cessation of your own brain activity is an event that you survive! You die, but also don’t! This view requires us to persist through our own death.

[Slide 26] Some say you go to heaven. That’s key: YOU go to heaven. It’s really you up there singing Hosannah or whatever. It’s not a look-a-like, not a twin or a doppelganger. YOU. You were living on earth; then you died; now YOU are up there with the angels. Your death did not end you. It only ended...something else. (The Earth-part of your life maybe?) You, the person, persist through your own death.

[Slide 26] Some say you go to heaven. That’s key: YOU go to heaven. It’s really you up there singing Hosannah or whatever. It’s not a look-a-like, not a twin or a doppelganger. YOU. You, the person, persist through your own death.

[Slide 27] Some people think that sometimes when individuals die, they are sent to Hell - a place of eternal torment. Maybe! But again, that you is a very important part of the story. It has to BE YOU that God sends to Hell or else She’s torturing an innocent person for YOUR sins. Which is deeply inappropriate. God would surely never do such a terrible thing. So, again, people who believe in hell are committed to people persisting through their own death .

[Slide 28] Other religious traditions say that you’re reincarnated. Again, fine; maybe! But, also again, it’s important that it really be YOU in the new body. The story has to be this: there’s this thing (YOU) and at one point in time you were in one human body and now that very same thing (YOU) are in another body – like in the body of a bear maybe. If that’s not the story, if YOU don’t literally survive, then we don’t really have reincarnation. What we have you dying at one point in time – permanently dying, ceasing to be – and then at some later we have a bear. Just a bear. Put differently, for reincarnation to work, you and the bear have to have something in common. (Not just anything! You and the bear both like pizza but that doesn’t make the bear the same person as you. Rather, you and the bear need to share the essential you-part. Whatever that is. Your memories, your soul, whatever.)

[Slide 29] Our Question How does God do that? How does She bring you into the afterlife? What parts of you does She preserve to ensure it’s really YOU in heaven? (... or in hell or in your next reincarnated life?) … She probably doesn’t need to keep your fingernails and hair safe. It’s ok if those rot away and are gone forever. But what about your memories? Your body as a whole? Your soul? Your personality?

[Slide 32] Souls The average american probably thinks this is the correct view. (“Which parts of you make it heaven?” I ask the man on the street. “ Oh, my soul for sure.” he says.) So let’s start here.

[Slide 33] What even is a soul? We immediately run into a problem. If you’re going to hang personal identity on this funny entity, THE SOUL, you definitely owe us a story about what that entity is, what it’s like, and how we know it’s real. (COMPARE: I know what bodies are! I already believe that they exist; I already know what they’re like. And I know what memories are! I already believe that they exist and know what they are like!) But I’m not sure I know what a soul is. I’m not sure I know what a soul is like. After all, people don’t use that word in any single, consistent way – they use it to pick out pretty radically different ideas. [next]

[Slide 34] The word ‘soul’ is massively ambiguous. Among other things, it can mean: an immaterial spirit (a ghost , basically) the animating “life force” of person the essential part of a person, the self … The first is certainly a very common religious view. Lots of religious people believe that humans are composed of two very different kinds of stuff. We have a material body, made of atomic matter, and an immaterial soul, made of… ghost stuff? Idk exactly. But that’s a common view, anyways: humans are made of atoms and ghosts. ( I think the word ‘ghost’ is preferable to ‘spirit’ in this case because spirit has mostly the same ambiguity that soul does. The word ‘ghost’, in contrast, is used more univocally.) The second usage is more aristotelian – which is to say, old-school - but you do still hear people talk about souls this way. My soul is maybe my life essence or my animating principle. You hear this talk a lot in fantasy books and video games – I think b/c it’s vague and mystical sounding? The “life force” use of ‘soul’ is I think uncommitted on the metaphysics of souls. Could be material, could be immaterial, could be abstract or concrete. That third usage is simply unhelpful for our current purposes. If ‘soul’ just means that part of you which makes you, you, then Same Soul view is trivially true and uninformative. We ask, “What part of you does God need to bring into heaven to let you survive your own death?” And this view says, “The important part! The essential person-making part!” Which, yeah! THat’s right. But our question is, Which part is that?? So: to assess the Same Soul view, we need to get clear on what souls are. Let me suggest a helpful way of organizing the conversation.

[Slide 35] Non-physical Things (have neither mass nor energy) The number one The definition of ‘dinosaur’ The letter ‘A’ Dante’s Inferno Facts Physical Things (have mass and energy) A table Dinosaurs A copy of the letter ‘A’ written in chalk A paper copy of Dante’s Inferno Photons Roughly speaking, there are two kinds of entities in the world: There are the physical, material things – with mass and energy, composed of elementary particles and subject to the laws of physics. And there are the non-physical things – with neither mass nor energy, not composed of any physical stuff nor subject to the laws of physics. If souls exist, they presumably belong in one or the other of these categories.

[Slide 37] Some people claim that the human soul masses 21 grams. This is a view that some people do actually hold. FOr instance… This is based on an experiment performed in 1907, where a scientist put some people on an industrial scale as they died and watched what happened. For the one of the people, the scale seemed to measure a drop in weight of 21 grams at the time of death. This experiment is widely considered to have been comically shitty science, but the idea is pretty popular in the public imagination. Lots of people seem to think this is true: The human soul has mass. So… ok. Lets consider that view. Because HOO BOY do i have some questions about this mass-having soul.

[Slide 38] Since souls have mass, they had better be subject to gravity. … do they fall out of our bodies when we die? … are there any souls in orbit around the sun? … can souls get trapped in a black hole? ...

[Slide 45] Non-physical souls either interact with matter, or they don’t. As soon as we start trying to imagine non-physical souls, we are confronted with a choice: We either grant that those souls interact with matter or we deny that they do.

[Slide 46] If there’s no interaction , then nothing in your soul ever changes anything in the physical world, and vice versa. That means your soul has no information about what’s going on in your body, and doesn’t change, update, or store information about worldly events. Suppose we deny interaction. Clearly this isn’t a good way to go. If this is the view, then God, when She saves your soul, doesn’t actually save you. That’s because your soul doesn’t have any information about anything that ever happened in your life – it’s exactly the same, blank slate baby soul that you got when you were born, unchanged by time and happenstance. (b/c again, on this view, your soul never interacts with the world)

[Slide 45] Non-physical souls either interact with matter, or they don’t. As soon as we start trying to imagine non-physical souls, we are confronted with a choice: We either grant that those souls interact with matter or we deny that they do.

(b/c again, on this view, your soul never interacts with the world)

[Slide 47] Alternatively, suppose souls do interact with our bodies. Since souls are non-physical, those must be non-physical interactions. Physical things interact physically with other physical things – and we basically understand those interactions. That’s what the entire discipline of physics is all about. We must now try to imagine a brand-new sort of interaction, a kind unstudied by physicists: a non-physical interaction between matter located in space and time on the one hand and immaterial, unlocated, non-physical soul-stuff on the other. Since our best science only has physical instruments with which to record and measure physical interactions, we must anticipate that these non-physical interactions will be, in themselves, undetectable. They’ll be no way to directly examine them, watch them, measure them, except in their results. If souls cause things to happen in the real world, then we should see their effects in the real world – even if we can’t directly investigatethe causation events themselves. These effects should appear to us to be uncaused . (That is to say, we’d expect to find some event that occurs but not for any discernable, material reason.) Since souls are primarily thought to interact with our brains, we would expect, in particular, to observe uncaused brain events.

[Slide 48] Here’s what we’d expect. Rather than a continuous pattern of neural firings (THIS neuron fires, causing THAT neuron to fire, causing THIS OTHER neuron to fire, etc), we’d expect sometimes to see neurons firing for literally no reason. No neighboring neurons going off. No proximate environmental stimuli. Just ZAP out of the blue without explanation: a neuron firing and literally no way to explain that using our best physics. Seemingly, that’s how it would have to look if the soul is causing brain events while being a non-physical thing. Souls would have to reach into the material world and change things. To our material eyes, that should look like a tiny miracly, like an inexplicable violation of the laws of physics.

[Slide 49] COMPARE: How do we know that neutrinos exist? Originally, they were postulated as a way to explain why beta decay processes actually do obey the laws of conservation of energy, momentum, and angular momentum. But we couldn’t find them for a long time! Turns out, the reason we couldn’t find them is because they don’t causally interact with other things like, hardly at all. Neutrinos mostly pass straight through ordinary matter, having no interaction. As long as that’s going on, there’s no way to tell they’re there. Eventually, we set up these huge neutrino detections chambers to detect them. Very occasionally there will be a causal interaction in these chambers, where the only explanation for the event is a neutrino. We should find something similar if souls occasionally interact with the material world. We should, in principle, be able to identify particular causal events that cannot be explained except by postulating souls.

[Slide 50] Objection If immaterial souls interact with matter, there should be apparently uncaused brain events. But we haven’t found any. So this is the first problem with our view. Our best neuroscience has yet to find any uncaused brain events. That’s a reason to worry! The proponent of this view apparently either needs to say: that we havent looked hard enough OR needs to tell a different story about how souls interact with matter such that we wouldn’t expect to find apparently uncaused brain events.

[Slide 55] We get 2 different results, depending on whether souls contain mental content. Case #1 | P2 has... JC’s soul + Tom’s body + Tom’s mind Case #2 | P2 has... JC’s soul + Tom’s body + JC’s mind Quiz Option 1: JC’s soul doesn’t contain any mental content – doesn’t have anything from Julius Caesar’s mind in it. If so, then P2 will act exactly like Tom used to. He has all the same memories as Tom. Same personality, etc. No one notices a change. That’s option #1. Option 2: Julius Caesar’s soul contains Julius’ mental content. If so, then P2 will act like Julius Caesar, will talk like Julius Caesar, will remember having been Julius Caesar. After all, in case #1, P2 does have Julius Caesar’s soul. But that’s make P2 Julius Caesar b/c P2 doesn’t act or think like Julius Caesar.

[Slide 58] Does a person survive a soul-swap ? Yes, if they retain their mental content . (e.g. memories, personality, etc.) No, if they don’t retain that content . But then… that implies that, mental content is what matters. So here’s the problem (again, insofar as you share my intuitions about those cases). Our question is... Most people who like the same soul view DO actually believe that their soul includes all of their mental content – their memories, personality, beliefs, goals etc. That’s fine! That’s a coherent view. BUT the point is this: On that view, it seems kinda misleading to say that God needs to preserve your soul to bring you to heaven. She does, maybe, but the soul isn’t what matters. The is just the vessel for the important stuff. Think of it this way: on Option 2 – when the resulting person is really just Julius Caesar reincarnated – Julius Caesar’ soul is no more and no less the cup that holds his mind. When we put the cup and its contents into a new body, we thereby put Julius Caesar in a new body. But JC isn’t essentially the cup; that’s confused. He’s the content. It seems like we could imagine God pouring that content into a different cup. If She wanted, God could cut and paste your mental content from your current soul into a new soul. And, intuitively I think, that new soul would be you. Maybe that happens every night. Maybe human souls are weak and crumbly like Dixie cups, and so God has to copy our content into a new one every night when we sleep! That would be ok though! The important bits would still be saved; you would still be saved. We can tell, it seems, because you wake up each morning with the exact same memories and personality.

[Slide 60] Three Candidate Accounts of the Afterlife For you to survive your own death ... God must preserve your soul . God must preserve your body . God must preserve your mental content . (esp. your personality and memories) So the first option didn’t look very promising. Let’s talk about that third option next.

[Slide 61] Mental Content Given what we just said about souls, you might be feeling the pull of this view. Maybe what god needs to do to bring you into the afterlife is make sure that there’s an entity in heaven, or wherever, who remembers being you, who act like you, and who shares all your other tendencies, values, and goals. Turns out, this view also has a very serious problem.

[Slide 62] Again, we can get at the problem with a case. Suppose you step into a Star Trek-style teleporter – a machine can move you about the universe at the speed of light. Here’s how it works: you step in the machine and press the TELEPORT button. The machine quickly scans your body, noting the exact location and arrangement of every one of your sub-atomic parts, and then transmits that information to another teleportation machine (on say Mars). The first machine then disassembles your body into its component atoms while the second machine perfectly re-assembles your body on Mars (using a different batch of atoms of course. Your old atoms are still on Earth) BOOM – you’re on Mars now. So far so good. That seems basically coherent – if technologically unfeasible at present. But now imagine that the transporter machine malfunctions. The copy is still created on Mars BUT the machine on Earth fails to disassemble the original copy. So now there are two humans running around with the exact same arrangements of atoms, the exact same memories, personalities, etc.

Maybe what god needs to do to bring you into the afterlife is make sure that there’s an entity in heaven, or wherever, who remembers being you, who act like you, and who shares all your other tendencies, values, and goals. Turns out, this view also has a very serious problem.

Your old atoms are still on Earth) BOOM – you’re on Mars now. So far so good. That seems basically coherent – if technologically unfeasible at present. But now imagine that the transporter machine malfunctions. The copy is still created on Mars BUT the machine on Earth fails to disassemble the original copy. So now there are two humans running around with the exact same arrangements of atoms, the exact same memories, personalities, etc.

[Slide 63] God could create a duplicate-you right now , in heaven. But that person wouldn’t be you . After all, you’re you. But that means sharing memories and personality is insufficient for identity . Here’s the punchline. Now imagine God doing that very same thing to you right now in heaven… In other words, the mere fact that two persons have all the same memories and personality doesn’t make them literally one single person. Since it’s possible for God to create cognitive duplicates of you (beings who have your memories/personality but aren’t you) , there must be more to you than just your personal content. (And it won’t work to simply say, “But God would never do that! She would wait until I’m dead to create a copy in heaven.” That might be true, but it’s beside the point. The point is that it’s not enough to simply create a duplicate of you in heaven. Since the duplicate isn’t you in the case where you’re still alive on Earth, surely it’s not you in the case where you’ve just died on earth.

[Slide 64] O bjection Identity seems like a 1-to-1 relation: in the future, there will be either zero or exactly 1 person who is identical to you . But on the mental content view, there can be 2 or more people identical to you . Here’s the more general worry: The mental content view is concerning b/c it violates a core belief we have about identity: that each of us is, essentially, exactly one person. In future, either I won’t exist at all or there will be exactly one person who is me. The mental content view doesn’t abide by this principle. Since multiple future people could have my same mental content (could have my personality and remember being me) multiple future people could, on the mental content, view be me. That seems wrong!

[Slide 65] Three Candidate Accounts of the Afterlife For you to survive your own death ... God must preserve your soul . God must preserve your mental content . God must preserve your body . So the second option didn’t look very promising either.

[Slide 66] Bodies So maybe we think that God needs to bring literally your exact brain and body up into heaven. That might fix all these issues! That will guarantee that there’s exactly one of you in the future – and it will preserve your mental content. Except, it turns out, this view ALSO has some serious problems.

[Slide 67] Objection 1 : The tower analogy

[Slide 68] Suppose your 3-year-old son builds a fancy block tower. He is very proud of it (and so are you!), and you leave it up in the living room to show to your spouse when she gets home. But while your son is out of the room, you accidentally knock the tower down. Panicking, you pull up a picture of the tower that you took on your phone and try to reconstruct it before your wife gets home. Suppose you get the blocks back in more-or-less the same order.

[Slide 67] Objection 1 : The tower analogy

[Slide 68] Suppose your 3-year-old son builds a fancy block tower. He is very proud of it (and so are you!), and you leave it up in the living room to show to your spouse when she gets home. But while your son is out of the room, you accidentally knock the tower down. Panicking, you pull up a picture of the tower that you took on your phone and try to reconstruct it before your wife gets home. Suppose you get the blocks back in more-or-less the same order.

[Slide 69] Plausibly, this isn’t your son’s tower. It’s a mere duplicate . Your son’s tower ceased to exist when it was knocked down. Quiz This case really relies on your having the right sort of intuitions about the tower. So: quiz question, Give me some intuitions: Do you think that the recreated tower is literally the very same tower as the one that was knocked down?

[Slide 70] Plausibly, this isn’t your son’s tower. It’s a mere duplicate . Your son’s tower ceased to exist when it was knocked down. Maybe this is how you and I work. God can’t recreate us in heaven. She can only fashion mere duplicates . ... But it gets worse!

[Slide 69] Plausibly, this isn’t your son’s tower. It’s a mere duplicate . Your son’s tower ceased to exist when it was knocked down. Quiz This case really relies on your having the right sort of intuitions about the tower. [Slide 70] Plausibly, this isn’t your son’s tower. It’s a mere duplicate . Your son’s tower ceased to exist when it was knocked down. Maybe this is how you and I work. God can’t recreate us in heaven. She can only fashion mere duplicates . ... But it gets worse!

[Slide 71] Also, what happens if your body and, say, Queen Elizabeth I’s body were made using the same blocks? Who gets to keep that atom? Or does God just use new blocks? We might note: Your physical brain will rot and decay in the ground when you die. We know this. You will disintegrate into dust and soil and ash and become inter-mixed with the other components of Earth’s crust. So if we like the brain view, we have to ask: “When exactly does God reassemble your brain in heaven and with what parts? Since she doesn’t do that right away, it’s pretty much guaranteed to be the case that some of those atoms from your brain will be inside a different person’s brain when he or she dies. But if you were both using that atom when you died, who gets it in heaven? This will be true of millions and millions of your atoms. At least in the tower case, you really did use the same blocks to remake the tower. It doesn’t look like God is going to be able to do that. She’s going to have to remake your brain with some new atoms. But then… why think that’s you? At that point, where she’s using new atoms, she could create thousands of copies of your brain. They wouldn’t all be literally you. This just runs into the same problem we had with the mental content view.

[Slide 72] Objection 2 : Body-swap

[Slide 73] Pop culture contains quite a few body swap stories. Freaky Friday involves a mother and daughter swapping bodies and LEARNING IMPORTANT LIFE LESSONS. Body swaps are weird but they seem basically coherent. (That is to say, we understand the premise! We can follow along with story no problem. It’s Lindsay Lohan walking around in a Jamie Lee Curtis suit, and vice versa.)

[Slide 74] There’s also Kafka’s Metamorphosis, in which a man wakes up in the body of an insect.

[Slide 1] The Badness of Death

[Slide 2] Set-up & review On well-being From the Lawmaker Argument From the Weirdness Argument The presumptive badness of death A case The deprivation account The No Subject Argument Justification Objections / replies The Symmetry Argument

[Slide 3] Last module… According to many religions, your death is an event that you survive – death is just the transitional event between this life and the afterlife. … Last module we talked about this idea and what philosophical complications it gives rise to. We asked, “How is it that YOU, the person, survive the corruption and decay of your earthly body? What essential parts of you are surviving that process such that your death doesn’t actually kill you?” That’s the religious perspective on death. Today I want to shift gears and talk about the atheistic perspective on death.

[Slide 4] Today, let’s suppose that’s wrong, that your death is actually the end of you. Death , we’ll say, is the event in which a person permanently ceases to be .

[Slide 3] Last module… According to many religions, your death is an event that you survive – death is just the transitional event between this life and the afterlife. … Last module we talked about this idea and what philosophical complications it gives rise to. We asked, “How is it that YOU, the person, survive the corruption and decay of your earthly body? What essential parts of you are surviving that process such that your death doesn’t actually kill you?” That’s the religious perspective on death. Today I want to shift gears and talk about the atheistic perspective on death.

[Slide 4] Today, let’s suppose that’s wrong, that your death is actually the end of you. Death , we’ll say, is the event in which a person permanently ceases to be .

[Slide 5] I want to look at a couple surprising arguments about this notion of death , first introduced by the Epicureans. (circa ~300 BCE) They argued, Your death is not bad for you . … Here, again, death is to be understood as the event in which you permanently cease to be. [Slide 6] On well-being

[Slide 7] The Steppe-child You are a female child born in 1693 to a desperately poor family in the Eurasian Steppe. From the moment of your birth, you receive very little affection or attention from your parents, who wanted a son rather than a daughter. You are malnourished, under-stimulated, and largely neglected. This continues until age 4, at which point you take ill with smallpox and die. Here’s an easy case to get us started. Imagine the following... … Now consider the course of your actual life, and compare your real life to that of the Steppe-child. Assuming your life has not been much, much worse than the life of the average American, you probably agree with the following: My life is better than the life of the Steppe-child Given a choice, any sensible person would choose a life like mine over a life like hers. For pretty much all of us, I suspect, this is an easy judgment to make. We are well-practiced at evaluating our own lives and the lives of other people; we are used to thinking about what we want (and DON’T want) for ourselves and for our loved ones.

[Slide 8] Some observations Some things that befall us in life are good and beneficial while other things are bad and harmful; all else equal, a life with lots of good things and few bad things is better than a life with lots of bad things and few good things; we all want for ourselves, for our children, for our friends and loved ones to have good lives and not bad lives. The Steppe-child case points us toward some more general, commonsensical observations… … I take it none of these observations are groundbreaking or particularly controversial. Notice: each of these facts involves a central notion: that of human well-being – or of welfare, or of a good life, a life that goes well for the person whose life it is. Many other familiar expressions in everyday English involve the same notion – we talk about our ‘quality of life’, about having ‘a life worth living’, and about things being ‘in one’s best interest’. There’s a whole branch of philosophy concerned with this idea, with human well-being – with these sorts of evaluations of how well our lives are going.

[Slide 9] Quick terminological note We’ll say an event harms X (is bad for X) when it has a negative impact on X’s well-being . (e.g. X’s being injured, sick, robbed, neglected, disappointed) And an event benefits X (is good for X) when it has a positive impact on X’s well-being. (e.g. X’s being healthy, loved, successful, happy, comfortable) Real quick: let me define a couple terms that will be useful for our conversation today. ... I think these definitions more-or-less track our everyday English uses of these two words, but I wanted to explicit about how I’ll be using them.

[Slide 11] Commonsense Dying is (usually) harmful / bad for us The Epicureans Dying is never harmful / bad for us …. But, as it turns out, there are some provocative – and not at all silly – arguments against this idea. So this is the central philosophical puzzle we’re talking about today: most people think death is bad-for us; we’ll look at a couple arguments that seem to show otherwise. (Recall, death is, for today, a permanent ceasing to be. So the thought is: it’s usually bad for people to permanently pass out of existence. )

[Slide 12] The presumptive badness of death Before we look at those arguments, though, I want us to try our best to explain that commonsense intuition that death, permanently ceasing to be, is usually bad for us.

[Slide 13] Your best friend has been diagnosed with terminal cancer. You’re very upset about this. You feel bad for her that she will die soon. She sees that you’re upset and tries to comfort you, “ Don’t worry. I’ve been given some medicine to make sure my last few months will be pain-free and, indeed, rather pleasant. ” Let’s start with a case...

[Slide 14] This cheers you up a little, but you still feel terrible. “ Don’t worry! ” She says again. “ I really don’t fear dying at all. I’ve come to terms with it. So I won’t waste my last few months feeling depressed or anxious. ” Again that’s a little comforting – but only a little.

[Slide 13] Your best friend has been diagnosed with terminal cancer. You’re very upset about this. You feel bad for her that she will die soon. She sees that you’re upset and tries to comfort you, “ Don’t worry. I’ve been given some medicine to make sure my last few months will be pain-free and, indeed, rather pleasant. ” Let’s start with a case...

[Slide 14] This cheers you up a little, but you still feel terrible. “ Don’t worry! ” She says again. “ I really don’t fear dying at all. I’ve come to terms with it. So I won’t waste my last few months feeling depressed or anxious. ” Again that’s a little comforting – but only a little.

[Slide 15] Finally she says, “ Look, I know it’s going to be miserable for you when I’m gone. You’ll have lost a great friend! But that’s not a reason to feel bad for me . I’ll be dead! I won’t be missing you ; I won’t be feeling or thinking anything. So, honestly, don’t feel bad for me. It’s not a misfortune for me that I’m going to die. ”

[Slide 16] Seemingly, your friend’s death is still bad for her , even though she won’t feel bad . (won’t be in pain / feel anxious / miss her friends) So death must be bad for another reason. … There must be, in other words, some other sort of harm that your friend is overlooking, some other respect in which her death will be legitimately bad for her.

[Slide 17] Proposal Some events are bad for us not b/c they hurt or b/c they cause future bad things but b/c they block us from getting good things. Let’s call these ‘ harms of deprivation ’ Here’s the standard suggestion in the philosophy literature, among the people who work on well-being and death: What your friend is missing is an entire category of harm… Here’s a case: suppose you lie down for a quick nap before some event that you’ve been really looking forward to – maybe a concert or a party. Only, you accidentally sleep through the whole event. It seems right to say that your being asleep during the concert is bad for you – and it would have been good for you for someone to wake you up. But why is tha? Why is it bad for you to be napping? It obvs doesn’t feel bad – and it doesn’t lead to any future bad events (like, no one’s mad at you or anything; it doesn’t cause you to be depressed or homeless or whatever). Rather, it seems like your nap is comparatively bad for you. Being asleep was bad for you because you would otherwise have been doing something really great. Your nap deprived you of all the good experiences you would have otherwise had at the concert or the party. That’s the sense in which the nap was bad for you.

[Slide 18] Or here’s a different case: suppose you are randomly selected to win one of those Publishers Clearing House awards – you know, that thing were they show up at your door with a video camera and a comically large check. However, before you can be presented with the winnings, I steal that giant-ass check from the giant-ass checkbook and cash it myself. There is no replacement check. You win nothing. But you never find out that any of this has happened! You didn’t enter any contests, and you have no idea that you’ve won. As far as you know, nothing bad has happened to you. Clearly, what I did was bad for you. But, Why was it bad for you? Well not because it caused you pain or disappointment, since you didn’t know the money was coming. Rather, it was bad because I DEPRIVED you of something that would have been good for you.

[Slide 19] “ Why is death bad? Because when I’m dead I don’t exist. And if we ask, how can it be the case that non-existence is bad ? The answer is, because of the lack of the good things in life. When I don’t exist, I am not getting the things that I could have otherwise gotten, if only I were still alive . Death is bad because it deprives me of the good things in life . This is nowadays known as the deprivation account of the… badness of death, since it holds that what is centrally bad about death is that it deprives you of the goods of life you might otherwise be getting. ” – Shelly Kagan In the reading for this Module, Shelly Kagan proposes that death is like this, too...

[Slide 20] Proposal Death is one of these deprivation harms – bad for us not b/c of how it feels but b/c of what it makes us miss out on . This is the Deprivation Account – it’s a popular explanation for why death is bad for us. … The Deprivation Account says that death is bad in pretty much exactly the same way that napping through a party is bad. Death is like the ultimately, final nap. Once you die, you miss out on all of the parties and all the concerts forever. This is, I think, a nice commonsensical account of the badness of death. It can explain a number of our intuitions about death.

This is nowadays known as the deprivation account of the… badness of death, since it holds that what is centrally bad about death is that it deprives you of the goods of life you might otherwise be getting. ” – Shelly Kagan In the reading for this Module, Shelly Kagan proposes that death is like this, too...

[Slide 21] Quiz According to the Deprivation Account , why is it worse for a 22-year-old to die than for an 82-year-old? … b/c the 22 year is deprived of many more good things, suffers a much greater loss . I think this is a plausible explanation. And, thus, I think this is a reason to like the Deprivation Account.

[Slide 22] Josef works as a janitor in a govt “intelligence” facility. A political dissident has just been brought in for interrogation. Josef’s knows how this ends: the prisoner will be tortured – cruelly, ceaselessly, for years – until his body gives out. That’s how this always goes, for prisoners. For weeks, Josef hears the man screaming and pleading for death as unspeakably awful things are done to him. One night, Josef slips a poison into the man’s food. He dies quietly in his sleep. Quiz QUIZ: Why, according to the Deprivation Account, is the prisoner’s death a benefit rather than a harm? … Since the prisoner’s future contains mostly torture and suffering, killing the prisoner’s deprives him of mostly bad things. And, thus, his early demise is not bad for him. It is , we might say, a benefit of deprivation to poison him, rather than a harm of deprivation. I also think this is a plausible explanation. And, thus, I think this too is a reason to like the Deprivation Account.

[Slide 25] “ The most terrifying bad thing, death , is [not bad for] us, since as long as we exist, death is not there, and when death is there, then we do not exist. [Slide 43] If I have a headache from 7am to 4pm, I am harmed during that time frame.

[Slide 45] 1. Death isn’t bad for me before I die b/c one cannot be presently harmed by future events. 2. Death isn’t bad for me after I die b/c only existing things can be subjects of harm 3. If my death is bad for me, there must be some particular time at which it’s bad C. My death isn’t bad for me More generally, we might say: to do harm is to have a particular sort of effect on a person and on their life. But people exist in time, as do lives. When you affect someone (whether by harming them or in some other way), you don’t affect them timelessly or always, you affect them during their lifespan. This is the intuitive thrust of that third claim.

[Slide 46] The No Subject Argument P1) Death isn’t bad for me before I die b/c one cannot be presently harmed by future events. P2) Death isn’t bad for me after I die b/c only existing things can be subjects of harm P3) If death is bad for me , there must be some particular time at which it’s bad C) Death isn’t bad for me To summarize then, this is the argument – what I’ll call the ‘No Subject’ Argument, after P2 which is the innovative premise.

[Slide 47] Responding to the No Subject Argument

[Slide 48] Objection What about the moment of death ? Maybe that’s when death is bad for me. Quiz We’ve said something about why death isn’t bad before you die and about why death isn’t bad after you die, but – you might be thinking – what about in between? Maybe there’s a particular moment of death and that moment – being neither before nor after – is when death is bad for you. QUIZ: Can you see why this probably isn’t going to work? What’s the problem with this proposal?

[Slide 47] Responding to the No Subject Argument

[Slide 48] Objection What about the moment of death ? Maybe that’s when death is bad for me. Quiz We’ve said something about why death isn’t bad before you die and about why death isn’t bad after you die, but – you might be thinking – what about in between? [Slide 49] Objection What about the moment of death ? Maybe that’s when death is bad for me. Reply In that moment , are you alive or dead? If alive, death hasn’t happened yet; if dead, you don’t exist to be harmed. It seems like we can just re-run the original argument, applied to that particular moment....

[Slide 50] Objection What about posthumous harm ? If *I* can do things after you’ve died that are bad for you, surely your death is bad for you. Here’s another possible objection. You might think, there’s no puzzle here, since we already believe in the existence of posthumous harm. That is, we already agree that people can harm us after we’ve died! Here’s a plausible case: Suppose, you tell me on your deathbed that you want me to scatter your ashes on your favorite mountain. This is really important to you. You make me promise to do that. Then when the time comes, I don’t feel like climbing a mountain, so I flush your cremation remains down my toilet. We might think about this case, This is a clear instance of harm: there was a thing you wanted and that I promised to do for you. THen i broke my promise, violated your trust, and thwarted your desire. You were harmed, even though you’d already died. But hey! Since I can harm you after you’ve died, surely your own death can be harming you after you’ve died.

[Slide 49] Objection What about the moment of death ? Maybe that’s when death is bad for me. Reply In that moment , are you alive or dead? If alive, death hasn’t happened yet; if dead, you don’t exist to be harmed. It seems like we can just re-run the original argument, applied to that particular moment....

[Slide 50] Objection What about posthumous harm ? If *I* can do things after you’ve died that are bad for you, surely your death is bad for you. Here’s another possible objection. You might think, there’s no puzzle here, since we already believe in the existence of posthumous harm. That is, we already agree that people can harm us after we’ve died! Here’s a plausible case: Suppose, you tell me on your deathbed that you want me to scatter your ashes on your favorite mountain. This is really important to you. You make me promise to do that. Then when the time comes, I don’t feel like climbing a mountain, so I flush your cremation remains down my toilet. We might think about this case, This is a clear instance of harm: there was a thing you wanted and that I promised to do for you. THen i broke my promise, violated your trust, and thwarted your desire. You were harmed, even though you’d already died. But hey! Since I can harm you after you’ve died, surely your own death can be harming you after you’ve died.

[Slide 51] Objection What about posthumous harm ? If *I* can do things after you’ve died that are bad for you, surely your death is bad for you. Reply True – IF posthumous harm is possible, death can be a harm. But is it? The No Subject Argument applies here, too. Here’s the problem, though. The possibility of posthumous isn’t a solution, it’s part of what’s at issue... Epicurus’ point is that you can’t be harmed by your own death once you’re already dead. He’s going to say that same exact thing about my flushing your ashes. You can’t harmed by that; you’re already dead. [next]

[Slide 52] The No Subject Argument (Posthumous Harm Edition) P1) Flushing your ashes isn’t bad for you before you die b/c you can’t be presently harmed by future events. P2) Flushing your ashes isn’t bad for you after you die b/c only existing things can be subjects of harm P3) If flushing your ashes is bad for you , there must be some particular time at which it’s bad C) Flushing your ashes isn’t bad for me To make the point more vivid: Look! We can simply revise the original argument to show that my flushing your ashes isn’t bad for you. So I don’t know whether posthumous harm is possible or not. The point is that this issue is hostage to the exact same argument. We can’t reject the No Subject Argument simply by invoking posthumous harm.

[Slide 53] Objection Maybe non-existing people can be harmed. Death harms me by depriving me of good things, though I don’t exist. Reply That implies that the bajillions of people who could have been born (but weren’t) are harmed by their non-birth. (are deprived of good things despite not existing) Here’s a different possibility. Maybe we should abandon this idea that non-existing people are unable to be harmed. Maybe harms don’t require existing subjects. Death is still a very serious deprivation for me. By dying I lose out on all the good stuff in life. Maybe we should just be comfortable w/ saying that not existing is bad for me when I don’t exist. We’ve already seen, though, where this suggestions runs us into trouble... We dont want to say that abstinence is exactly as harmful as infant-murdering. It’s true that both actions deprive a being of a good life. But clearly those aren’t the same thing. Infanticide seems like a real harm. Abstinence doesn’t. It doesn’t because unconceived children don’t exist. But… now we’re back where we started. Apparently, if you don’t exist, you can’t be harmed.

[Slide 54] Objection Maybe non-existing people can be harmed but only if they exist at some point or other . One final suggestion raised by Kagan in the article i had you read for this module, seeks to thread the gap between these two claims. Maybe, he says... … This seems to solve the problem. Since our unborn children never exist, we don’t have to count them as harmed by never-having-been born. They are deprived of good things, but it’s not a harm b/c they never existed. But since the dead did once exist, they do count as harmed. THeir death deprives them of good things and it IS a harm b/c they did exist.

[Slide 55] Objection Maybe non-existing people can be harmed but only if they exist at some point or other . Reply Maybe? This sounds rather ad hoc . (“What if we solve the problem by saying death is the exception to the rule ?” Compare : “Maybe all harms happen at particular times, except not death, that’s the one exception.”) I’m not totally sure what to say about this proposal. It works, technically, but it also strikes me as a bit suspicious...

[Slide 56] The Symmetry Argument As ever, there’s much more to be said here about the No Subject Argument. HOwever, I want to close by looking at another argument we get from the Epicureans for the conclusion that death isn’t bad for us. This argument picks up the intuitive idea that we were just playing with: namely, the idea that the non-existence of the unborn isn’t bad for them.
